q: Which of the following is the correct sequence for adult BLS?
opts: ["A-B-C-D-R","D-R-C-A-B","R-D-A-B-C","C-A-B-D-R"]
exp: Danger first for your own safety, then Response, then Compressions immediately, then Airway, then Breathing.

q: The compression rate for adult CPR should be:
exp: Fast enough to move blood, not so fast the chest can't recoil fully between compressions.

q: Agonal breathing in an unresponsive patient should be treated as:
opts: ["Normal breathing","Cardiac arrest — start CPR","Choking","Recovery position only"]
exp: Agonal gasping looks like breathing but is actually a sign the heart has stopped.

q: In infant choking, abdominal thrusts are avoided because:
opts: ["Too painful","The liver is large and unprotected","Infants can't choke on solids","No effect on infants"]
exp: An infant's liver sits low and unprotected, so hard abdominal thrusts risk injuring it.

q: An AED pad should be placed from an implanted pacemaker:
opts: ["Directly over it","At least 1 inch away","Opposite side of body only","Distance doesn't matter"]
exp: Placing the pad too close can damage the device or reduce shock delivery.

q: In a drowning victim, initial management begins with:
opts: ["30 chest compressions","5 rescue breaths","Pulse check for 30 seconds","Immediate defibrillation"]
exp: Drowning collapse is caused by hypoxia, not a primary cardiac event, so oxygen (breaths) comes first.

q: The following are links in the Chain of Survival EXCEPT:
opts: ["Early recognition","Early defibrillation","Delayed advanced life support","Post-resuscitation care"]
exp: Every real link is about acting EARLY — 'delayed' is the opposite of what the chain requires.

q: After ROSC, targeted temperature management is used to protect:
opts: ["The kidneys","The brain","The liver","The skin"]
exp: The brain remains vulnerable to injury after the heart restarts; controlling temperature helps protect it.

q: For a conscious adult with mild choking (still able to cough), the correct action is:
opts: ["5 back slaps immediately","Encourage coughing, do not intervene yet","Start abdominal thrusts","Begin CPR"]
exp: A patient who can still cough effectively is still moving air — coughing is the body's own most effective clearing mechanism, so you let it work first.

q: For severe choking in a conscious adult, management alternates between:
opts: ["5 back slaps and 5 abdominal thrusts","10 chest compressions and rescue breaths","Head-tilt chin-lift and jaw thrust","Recovery position and CPR"]
exp: Alternating back slaps and abdominal thrusts gives two different mechanical ways to dislodge the obstruction.

q: In pregnant or obese choking victims, thrusts should be delivered as:
opts: ["Abdominal thrusts as normal","Chest thrusts instead of abdominal","No thrusts at all","Back blows only, no thrusts"]
exp: An enlarged abdomen (pregnancy/obesity) makes abdominal thrusts less effective and less safe, so chest thrusts are used instead.

q: For an unconscious choking adult, the correct approach is to:
opts: ["Start CPR, checking the mouth before each breath","Wait for the ambulance without doing anything","Perform abdominal thrusts only","Place immediately in recovery position"]
exp: Once unconscious, CPR is started, and the mouth is checked for a visible object before each rescue breath is attempted.

q: CPR may be stopped in all of the following situations EXCEPT:
opts: ["Return of spontaneous circulation","A qualified person takes over","The rescuer feels the situation is hopeless","A physician pronounces death"]
exp: A rescuer's personal feeling that it's 'hopeless' is not on its own a valid reason to stop — CPR continues until one of the defined stopping criteria is met.

q: An ETCO₂ reading below what value after 20 minutes of CPR may prompt consideration of stopping resuscitation?
exp: A persistently very low ETCO₂ after prolonged good-quality CPR suggests very poor circulation is being generated and survival is unlikely.

q: In an intubated patient during CPR, ventilation should be given at:
opts: ["30:2 ratio, same as non-intubated","Continuous compressions + 10 breaths/min","1 breath every compression","No breaths, compressions only"]
exp: Once the airway is secured with a tube, compressions no longer need to pause for breaths — they continue non-stop while breaths are added at a steady rate of about 1 every 6 seconds.

q: The origin of modern intensive/critical care, including artificial ventilation, is credited to the:
opts: ["Influenza pandemic of 1918","Polio epidemic of the early 1950s","World War II battlefield medicine","HIV epidemic of the 1980s"]
exp: During the polio epidemic, patients with paralysed breathing muscles needed artificial ventilation, and this need is what led to the development of the modern ICU.

q: The correct compression-to-ventilation ratio for a single rescuer performing adult CPR is:
exp: For a single rescuer on an adult, 30 compressions are followed by 2 breaths, repeated in cycles.

q: For a 2-rescuer child (not infant) CPR, the compression-to-ventilation ratio is:
exp: With 2 trained rescuers present for a child, the ratio changes to 15 compressions to 2 breaths, allowing more frequent ventilation for the smaller patient.

q: Pulse check location in an infant during CPR assessment is the:
opts: ["Carotid artery","Radial artery","Brachial artery","Femoral artery"]
exp: An infant's short, chubby neck makes the carotid pulse hard to feel reliably, so the brachial pulse (inner upper arm) is used instead.

q: The compression depth recommended for an infant during CPR is approximately:
exp: An infant's chest is much smaller and more fragile, so the target compression depth is shallower, at about 4cm, compared to 5–6cm in an adult.

topic: TOPIC 2: ANATOMY & PHYSIOLOGY OF THE SKIN
q: The skin is considered the body's:
opts: ["Smallest organ","Largest organ","Only avascular organ","Most vascular organ"]
exp: By surface area and weight, the skin is the body's single largest organ.

topic: TOPIC 2: ANATOMY & PHYSIOLOGY OF THE SKIN
q: Which layer of the skin is avascular?
opts: ["Dermis","Hypodermis","Epidermis","Subcutaneous fat"]
exp: The epidermis has no blood vessels of its own; it gets nutrients by diffusion from the dermis below.

topic: TOPIC 2: ANATOMY & PHYSIOLOGY OF THE SKIN
q: The thinnest skin on the body is found at the:
opts: ["Palms","Soles","Upper eyelid","Back"]
exp: Eyelid skin is under 0.1mm thick, allowing rapid, easy blinking.

topic: TOPIC 2: ANATOMY & PHYSIOLOGY OF THE SKIN
q: Thick skin differs from thin skin by having an extra layer called the:
opts: ["Stratum basale","Stratum lucidum","Stratum corneum","Stratum granulosum"]
exp: The extra stratum lucidum toughens high-friction areas like palms and soles.

topic: TOPIC 2: ANATOMY & PHYSIOLOGY OF THE SKIN
q: Keratinocytes make up approximately what percentage of the epidermis?
exp: They are the dominant epidermal cell type, at about 95%.

topic: TOPIC 2: ANATOMY & PHYSIOLOGY OF THE SKIN
q: Which of the following is NOT a function of the skin?
opts: ["Heat regulation","Protection against microorganisms","Vitamin D synthesis","Production of red blood cells"]
exp: Red blood cells are made in bone marrow, not skin.

topic: TOPIC 2: ANATOMY & PHYSIOLOGY OF THE SKIN
q: The approximate turnover time for keratinocytes is:
opts: ["7 days","28–42 days","6 months","2 years"]
exp: It takes roughly a month for a new keratinocyte to form at the base and be shed at the surface.

topic: TOPIC 2: ANATOMY & PHYSIOLOGY OF THE SKIN
q: Melanocyte activity declines by approximately what amount per decade after age 30?
exp: This decline in pigment-producing cells is part of normal skin ageing, contributing to greying and reduced sun protection with age.

topic: TOPIC 2: ANATOMY & PHYSIOLOGY OF THE SKIN
q: The dermis contains all of the following EXCEPT:
opts: ["Blood vessels","Nerve endings","Hair follicles","Keratinocytes"]
exp: Keratinocytes are found in the epidermis, not the dermis — the dermis instead holds vessels, nerves, follicles, and glands.

topic: TOPIC 2: ANATOMY & PHYSIOLOGY OF THE SKIN
q: The two sub-layers of the dermis are the:
opts: ["Papillary and reticular","Basale and corneum","Superficial and deep fascia","Granulosum and lucidum"]
exp: The papillary layer (loose connective tissue, upper) and reticular layer (dense connective tissue, deeper) make up the dermis.

topic: TOPIC 2: ANATOMY & PHYSIOLOGY OF THE SKIN
q: Total body weight contributed by the skin is approximately:
exp: At 4.5–5kg, the skin makes up roughly 12–15% of an adult's total body weight.

topic: TOPIC 2: ANATOMY & PHYSIOLOGY OF THE SKIN
q: With intrinsic (natural) ageing, the dermis loses approximately what percentage of its thickness?
exp: The dermis thins by roughly a fifth as part of the normal, non-sun-related ageing process.

topic: TOPIC 2: ANATOMY & PHYSIOLOGY OF THE SKIN
q: The total surface area of adult human skin is approximately:
exp: Roughly 2 square metres — about the size of a small blanket — covers the average adult body.

topic: TOPIC 2: ANATOMY & PHYSIOLOGY OF THE SKIN
q: Using the memory device 'Bad Skin Gets Lighter Constantly', the epidermal layers from DEEPEST to most SUPERFICIAL are:
opts: ["Corneum, Lucidum, Granulosum, Spinosum, Basale","Basale, Spinosum, Granulosum, Lucidum, Corneum","Spinosum, Basale, Corneum, Lucidum, Granulosum","Granulosum, Basale, Spinosum, Corneum, Lucidum"]
exp: New cells are born at the base (basale) and get pushed upward and outward through spinosum, granulosum, lucidum (thick skin only), and finally corneum, where they are shed.

topic: TOPIC 2: ANATOMY & PHYSIOLOGY OF THE SKIN
q: The stratum corneum, the outermost epidermal layer, is mainly made up of:
opts: ["Living, actively dividing cells","Dead, flattened, keratin-filled cells","Blood vessels","Nerve endings"]
exp: By the time cells reach the surface layer, they have died and filled with tough keratin protein, forming a protective, waterproof outer layer.

topic: TOPIC 2: ANATOMY & PHYSIOLOGY OF THE SKIN
q: The stratum basale (deepest epidermal layer) is significant because it:
opts: ["Contains no living cells","Is where new skin cells are continuously produced","Is only found in thick skin","Is the layer responsible for sweating"]
exp: This is the 'production line' layer where new keratinocytes are constantly generated to replace cells lost at the surface.

topic: TOPIC 2: ANATOMY & PHYSIOLOGY OF THE SKIN
q: The epidermis receives its nutrients via:
opts: ["Its own dedicated blood vessels","Diffusion from the dermis below","Direct absorption from the external environment","The subcutaneous fat layer only"]
exp: Since the epidermis has no blood vessels of its own, nutrients and oxygen must diffuse upward from the vascular dermis beneath it.

topic: TOPIC 2: ANATOMY & PHYSIOLOGY OF THE SKIN
q: Which skin appendage structures are found in the dermis?
opts: ["Hair follicles and glands","Keratinocytes only","Melanocytes only","The stratum corneum"]
exp: Hair follicles, sweat glands, and sebaceous glands all originate in the dermis, even though parts of them extend up through the epidermis.

topic: TOPIC 2: ANATOMY & PHYSIOLOGY OF THE SKIN
q: Palms and soles are examples of:
opts: ["Thin skin","Thick skin","Mucous membrane","Non-keratinised skin"]
exp: These high-friction, weight-bearing areas have thick skin with an extra layer (stratum lucidum) for added durability.

topic: TOPIC 2: ANATOMY & PHYSIOLOGY OF THE SKIN
q: The primary function of melanocytes in the skin is to:
opts: ["Produce keratin","Produce pigment (melanin) for UV protection","Sense touch and pressure","Regulate body temperature directly"]
exp: Melanocytes make melanin, the pigment that helps protect deeper skin layers from UV radiation damage.

topic: TOPIC 3: OVERVIEW OF BURNS & CCU MANAGEMENT
q: A burn injury is best defined as:
opts: ["Liquefactive necrosis","Coagulative necrosis","Caseous necrosis","Fat necrosis"]
exp: Heat denatures/cooks tissue proteins — this specific process is coagulative necrosis.

topic: TOPIC 3: OVERVIEW OF BURNS & CCU MANAGEMENT
q: The burn zone that is the main target of treatment, since it may still be saved, is the:
opts: ["Zone of coagulation","Zone of stasis","Zone of hyperaemia","Zone of necrosis"]
exp: The centre is already dead and the outer zone heals alone — the middle zone can go either way depending on care quality.

topic: TOPIC 3: OVERVIEW OF BURNS & CCU MANAGEMENT
q: Using the Rule of Nines, each arm accounts for:
exp: Each whole arm is 9% of total body surface area.

topic: TOPIC 3: OVERVIEW OF BURNS & CCU MANAGEMENT
q: Using the Parkland formula for a 70kg patient with 40% TBSA, total 24-hour fluid required is:
exp: 4 × 70 × 40 = 11,200ml, split half in the first 8 hours and half over the next 16.

topic: TOPIC 3: OVERVIEW OF BURNS & CCU MANAGEMENT
q: Systemic burn shock risk begins above what %TBSA?
exp: Beyond 20% TBSA, inflammation becomes whole-body rather than local.

topic: TOPIC 3: OVERVIEW OF BURNS & CCU MANAGEMENT
q: The leading cause of death in burns patients after 48 hours is:
opts: ["Hypovolaemic shock","Sepsis","Airway obstruction","Electrolyte imbalance"]
exp: The open burn wound becomes an entry point for bacteria once the acute fluid-shift phase has passed.

topic: TOPIC 3: OVERVIEW OF BURNS & CCU MANAGEMENT
q: The Curreri formula for nutritional requirements in burns patients is:
opts: ["25kcal×kg + 40kcal×%TBSA","10kcal×kg only","4ml×kg×%TBSA","2000kcal fixed daily"]
exp: This formula accounts for both baseline needs (per kg) and the extra hypermetabolic demand created by the burn itself (per %TBSA).

topic: TOPIC 3: OVERVIEW OF BURNS & CCU MANAGEMENT
q: The target urine output in an adult burns patient during fluid resuscitation is:
exp: Urine output is the most practical bedside marker of adequate fluid resuscitation, and 0.5–1.0ml/kg/hr is the target range in adults.

topic: TOPIC 3: OVERVIEW OF BURNS & CCU MANAGEMENT
q: Wound care in major burns is typically initiated within:
opts: ["1–2 hours","24–72 hours","1 week","1 month"]
exp: Wound care begins once the patient is stabilised from the initial resuscitation phase, generally within this window.

topic: TOPIC 3: OVERVIEW OF BURNS & CCU MANAGEMENT
q: Front of trunk (chest + abdomen) accounts for what percentage of TBSA under the Rule of Nines?
exp: The front of the trunk is counted as 18%, matching the back of the trunk, also 18%.

topic: TOPIC 3: OVERVIEW OF BURNS & CCU MANAGEMENT
q: The two drugs used together to manage the hypermetabolic response in burns are:
opts: ["Propranolol and oxandrolone","Adrenaline and atropine","Insulin and glucagon","Furosemide and spironolactone"]
exp: A beta-blocker (propranolol) reduces the hyperdynamic cardiovascular response, while an anabolic steroid (oxandrolone) helps counter the muscle-wasting hypermetabolic state.

topic: TOPIC 3: OVERVIEW OF BURNS & CCU MANAGEMENT
q: DVT prophylaxis in burns patients typically includes:
opts: ["Aspirin only","LMWH plus compression stockings","Warfarin only","No prophylaxis is used"]
exp: Low molecular weight heparin combined with mechanical compression reduces the clot risk that comes with immobility and the inflammatory state of a major burn.

topic: TOPIC 3: OVERVIEW OF BURNS & CCU MANAGEMENT
q: Special areas of the body that require CCU-level attention in burns include all of the following EXCEPT:
opts: ["Face","Hands and feet","Perineum and genitalia","Upper back"]
exp: Face, hands, feet, perineum, genitalia, and joints are the specific 'special areas' due to functional/cosmetic risk — general upper back is not singled out in the same way.

topic: TOPIC 3: OVERVIEW OF BURNS & CCU MANAGEMENT
q: In modern burns care, mortality for a 50% TBSA burn has fallen to approximately:
exp: Advances in fluid resuscitation, wound care, and critical care have dramatically improved survival compared to roughly 25 years ago, when mortality at this size of burn was around 50%.

topic: TOPIC 3: OVERVIEW OF BURNS & CCU MANAGEMENT
q: The incidence of burns in the general population is approximately:
exp: This is the commonly cited population incidence figure for burn injuries.

topic: TOPIC 3: OVERVIEW OF BURNS & CCU MANAGEMENT
q: The group most commonly affected by burns is:
opts: ["Elderly women","Males aged 21–40 years","Infants under 1 year only","Females aged 60–80 years"]
exp: Economically active young to middle-aged men are the group most frequently affected by burns.

topic: TOPIC 3: OVERVIEW OF BURNS & CCU MANAGEMENT
q: The basal metabolic rate (BMR) in a major burns patient can increase by:
exp: The body's energy demands roughly double or triple after a major burn, driving the hypermetabolic state that must be matched with adequate nutrition.

topic: TOPIC 3: OVERVIEW OF BURNS & CCU MANAGEMENT
q: Back of trunk burns are calculated as what percentage of TBSA using the Rule of Nines?
exp: Like the front of the trunk, the back is also counted as 18% under the Rule of Nines.

topic: TOPIC 3: OVERVIEW OF BURNS & CCU MANAGEMENT
q: For small or patchy burns, a quick estimate of surface area can be made using:
opts: ["The patient's own palm ≈ 1% TBSA","The Rule of Nines only","Lund and Browder chart only in adults","No estimation method exists"]
exp: The palm method is a convenient bedside estimate: the patient's own palm (including fingers) is roughly equal to 1% of their total body surface area.

q: Inhalational injury is responsible for approximately what percentage of burns deaths?
exp: Even though only around a third of burns patients have inhalation injury, it drives roughly 90% of burns deaths.

q: Carbon monoxide is dangerous in burns patients because it:
opts: ["Directly burns the lungs","Binds haemoglobin 240 times more strongly than oxygen","Causes immediate cardiac arrest","Has no significant effect"]
exp: CO occupies the oxygen-carrying site on haemoglobin far more tightly than oxygen does.

q: A patient with an electrical burn should have cardiac monitoring for at least:
opts: ["2–4 hours","12 hours","24–48 hours","1 week"]
exp: Dangerous rhythms may not appear immediately, so monitoring continues even with an initially normal ECG.

q: Dark brown urine following an electrical burn most likely indicates:
opts: ["Dehydration only","Myoglobinuria from muscle breakdown","Urinary tract infection","Normal finding"]
exp: Damaged muscle releases myoglobin, filtered by the kidneys, turning urine dark and risking kidney injury if untreated.

q: The correct first aid for a chemical burn is:
opts: ["Apply a neutralising agent immediately","Copious irrigation with water","Cover with a dry dressing only","Apply ice directly"]
exp: Water dilutes and washes away the chemical; neutralisers generate extra heat and worsen the burn.

q: Low voltage electrical injury (<1000V) mainly carries the risk of:
opts: ["Massive tissue destruction","Cardiac arrest with little tissue destruction","No risk at all","Guaranteed limb amputation"]
exp: Low voltage doesn't cook tissue the way high voltage does, but it can still disrupt the heart's normal rhythm and cause arrest.

q: Treatment for myoglobinuria after electrical injury includes double fluids plus:
opts: ["Mannitol and sodium bicarbonate","Insulin and glucose","Furosemide alone","No additional treatment needed"]
exp: Mannitol promotes urine flow (osmotic diuresis) and bicarbonate alkalinises the urine, both helping flush myoglobin out before it clogs the kidneys.

q: Compartment syndrome following an electrical burn is treated with:
opts: ["Fasciotomy","Amputation immediately","Ice packs only","Compression bandaging"]
exp: Cutting open the tight fascia (fasciotomy) relieves the dangerous pressure building up inside the swollen muscle compartment.

q: Unique long-term complications of high-voltage electrical burns include:
opts: ["Cataracts and hearing loss","Improved vision","No lasting effects","Hair regrowth"]
exp: The electrical current can damage the lens of the eye and inner ear structures, causing cataracts and hearing loss well after the acute injury.

q: The correct rewarming temperature range for frostbite is:
exp: Warm (not hot) water in this range rewarms tissue effectively without causing additional thermal injury.

q: Rubbing frozen tissue during frostbite management is:
opts: ["Recommended to restore circulation","Never done — it worsens tissue damage","Only done on the hands","Only done if pain-free"]
exp: Ice crystals inside and around the cells make the tissue extremely fragile; rubbing causes further mechanical damage.

q: Supraglottic inhalational injury is primarily caused by:
opts: ["Heat","Chemical irritants only","Carbon monoxide only","Bacterial infection"]
exp: Direct heat causes swelling above the vocal cords, which can progress to airway obstruction.

q: Infraglottic inhalational injury is mainly caused by:
opts: ["Direct heat","Noxious chemical combustion products in smoke","Blunt trauma","Radiation exposure"]
exp: Chemicals carried in smoke reach below the vocal cords and injure the lower airway and lungs, potentially progressing to ARDS.

q: Systemic carbon monoxide poisoning in burns patients is best treated with:
opts: ["Humidified 100% oxygen","Room air only","Bronchodilators alone","Antibiotics"]
exp: High-concentration oxygen helps displace CO from haemoglobin faster than room air would.

q: High voltage electrical injury causes tissue damage mainly through:
opts: ["Electrothermal heating, cooking tissue from the inside","Direct mechanical trauma only","Chemical reaction with the skin","Freezing of the tissue"]
exp: Unlike low voltage, high voltage current generates enough heat as it passes through the body to cook tissue internally, like a microwave.

q: Voltage above what threshold is classified as 'high voltage' in electrical burns?
exp: Voltage above 1000V is classified as high voltage, with a very different injury pattern (deep, deceptive tissue damage) compared to low voltage.

q: Head elevation of 30–45° is recommended in inhalational injury management to:
opts: ["Improve appetite","Reduce airway oedema","Prevent pressure sores","Improve hearing"]
exp: Elevating the head helps reduce swelling around the airway, lowering the risk of obstruction.

q: Ventilation strategy for a patient with significant inhalational injury favours:
opts: ["High tidal volumes to maximise oxygenation","Low tidal volumes with permissive hypercapnia","No ventilatory support is needed","Maximum PEEP at all times"]
exp: Protecting the already-injured lung from further pressure damage is prioritised, even if it means tolerating a slightly higher CO2 level.

q: Chemical burns should NEVER be treated with a neutralising agent because:
opts: ["Neutralising agents are too expensive","The chemical reaction generates extra heat and worsens the burn","Neutralising agents are illegal","It has no real effect either way"]
exp: Mixing the neutraliser with the original chemical produces an exothermic reaction, adding a thermal burn on top of the chemical injury.

q: The classic triad of fat embolism syndrome is:
opts: ["Fever, cough, chest pain","Confusion, dyspnoea, petechiae","Hypotension, tachycardia, pallor","Jaundice, ascites, confusion"]
exp: Fat droplets from fractured bone marrow travel to lungs, brain, and skin capillaries, causing this classic combination.

q: An SpO₂ below what value is generally used as an ICU admission threshold?
exp: Below 90% signals inadequate oxygenation needing closer, higher-level care.

q: The 3 Ms of ICU management are:
opts: ["Monitoring, Medication, Movement","Manpower, Machine, Money","Mobility, Meals, Medication","Man, Machine, Method"]
exp: Staff, equipment, and funding all need to be present together for an ICU to function.

q: To prevent pressure ulcers, a critically ill patient should be turned every:
opts: ["30 minutes","1 hour","2 hours","6 hours"]
exp: Regular 2-hourly turning relieves pressure before tissue damage develops.

q: The most accurate day-to-day measure of fluid balance in a critically ill patient is:
opts: ["Urine colour","Daily weight","Skin turgor","Thirst"]
exp: Weight change closely tracks fluid gain or loss — about 1kg change equals roughly 1 litre of fluid.

q: Compared to general ward care, ICU care costs approximately:
opts: ["The same","2× more","4× more","10× more"]
exp: The extra staffing, equipment, and monitoring involved make ICU care roughly four times more expensive.

q: A heart rate below what value is used as an ICU admission threshold?
exp: A heart rate this slow may signal a dangerous underlying problem needing closer monitoring.

q: In ICU monitoring, which principle is emphasised as more important than a single reading?
opts: ["The most recent value alone","Trends over time","Only the highest recorded value","Only the lowest recorded value"]
exp: A single number can be misleading, but watching how a value changes over time reveals whether a patient is truly improving or deteriorating.

q: Nutrition should ideally be started in a critically ill orthopaedic patient within:
opts: ["2–4 hours","24–48 hours","1 week","2 weeks"]
exp: Early nutrition (24–48 hours) helps counter the hypermetabolic, catabolic state of critical illness.

q: CVP that is low most likely indicates the need for:
opts: ["Diuretics","Fluid administration","Vasoconstrictors only","No action needed"]
exp: A low central venous pressure suggests the patient is relatively underfilled and needs more fluid.

q: A GCS below what value is generally used as an indication for intubation?
exp: A GCS under 8 suggests the patient can no longer reliably protect their own airway, so intubation is considered.

q: Which of the following is an orthopaedic-specific indication for ICU admission?
opts: ["Isolated ankle sprain","Fat embolism syndrome","Minor wrist fracture","Uncomplicated cast application"]
exp: Fat embolism is a systemic, potentially life-threatening complication of fracture, unlike the other minor orthopaedic issues listed.

q: A heart rate above what value is used as an ICU admission threshold?
exp: A sustained heart rate above 110bpm may reflect significant physiological stress requiring closer monitoring.

q: Orthopaedic conditions are classically grouped into how many pairs of categories?
exp: The 7 pairs cover congenital/developmental, infection/inflammation, arthritis/rheumatic, metabolic/endocrine, tumours/lesions, neurological/muscle weakness, and injury/mechanical conditions.

q: DVT and pulmonary embolism are significant ICU risks in orthopaedic patients because:
opts: ["They never occur after fractures","Immobility after fracture increases clot risk, and a dislodged clot can travel to the lungs","They only occur in upper limb fractures","They are unrelated to orthopaedic injury"]
exp: Reduced mobility after a fracture increases the risk of a leg vein clot forming, which can break off and travel to the lungs, causing sudden collapse.

q: Post-cardiac arrest patients are managed in the ICU:
opts: ["Only if they show ongoing symptoms","Always, regardless of apparent recovery","Never — ward care is sufficient","Only if under age 40"]
exp: Even a patient who seems to have recovered after cardiac arrest needs close monitoring for delayed complications, so ICU admission is routine.

q: Multiple organ failure (MOF) in an orthopaedic trauma patient reflects:
opts: ["A single organ system affected in isolation","Kidneys, liver, lungs, and heart failing together","A benign, self-limiting condition","An issue confined to the injured limb only"]
exp: MOF is a systemic collapse of multiple organ systems simultaneously, often triggered by severe trauma or its complications, and requires intensive multi-system support.

q: ARDS in the context of orthopaedic trauma most often develops as a complication of:
opts: ["Isolated finger fractures","Fat embolism or severe polytrauma","Routine elective joint replacement with no complications","Simple plaster cast application"]
exp: Severe systemic insults like fat embolism or major polytrauma can trigger widespread lung inflammation, leading to ARDS.

q: Bolus sedation doses in ICU are typically timed to be given:
opts: ["Randomly throughout the day","Before physio, suctioning, turning, or debridement","Only at bedtime","Never, continuous infusion is always used instead"]
exp: Giving an extra bolus just before a painful or uncomfortable procedure ensures the patient is adequately comfortable for that specific intervention.

q: The anatomical landmark dividing upper from lower GI bleeding is the:
opts: ["Pylorus","Ileocaecal valve","Ligament of Treitz","Duodenal bulb"]
exp: This landmark at the duodenojejunal junction is the accepted dividing line.

q: Melaena (black, tarry stool) is most suggestive of bleeding from:
opts: ["The rectum","The upper GI tract","Haemorrhoids","The appendix"]
exp: Blood digested over a long distance turns black and tarry — about 90% of the time this means an upper GI source.

q: The correct order of initial management in acute GI bleeding is:
opts: ["Drugs → Look → Drip → Blood","Drip → Blood → Drugs → Look","Look → Drip → Blood → Drugs","Blood → Drip → Look → Drugs"]
exp: IV access/fluids first, transfuse as needed, give medications, then endoscopy to find and treat the source.

q: The preferred endoscopic treatment for bleeding oesophageal varices is:
opts: ["Sclerotherapy only","Endoscopic variceal ligation (EVL)","Surgical resection","Aggressive over-transfusion"]
exp: Band ligation is the current preferred method; over-transfusion is avoided as it raises portal pressure.

q: The single best predictor of rebleeding after an initial GI bleed is:
opts: ["Patient's age alone","The magnitude of the initial bleed","Time of day of the bleed","Blood group"]
exp: A larger first bleed usually indicates a more severe underlying lesion, making a repeat bleed more likely.

q: The most common cause of lower GI bleeding is:
opts: ["Haemorrhoids","Colorectal cancer","Diverticular disease","Angiodysplasia"]
exp: Diverticular disease accounts for roughly a quarter of lower GI bleeds and up to 60% of the most severe ones.

q: A Mallory-Weiss tear classically occurs after:
opts: ["Repeated vomiting","Chronic constipation","A single episode of coughing","Eating spicy food"]
exp: Forceful, repeated retching tears the mucosa at the gastro-oesophageal junction.

q: Dieulafoy's lesion is characterised by:
opts: ["A small, insignificant vessel","An abnormally large artery in the gastric submucosa","A benign gastric polyp","A normal anatomical variant with no bleeding risk"]
exp: This unusually large, superficial artery can cause sudden, massive, recurrent bleeding and is notoriously hard to diagnose.

q: Portal gastropathy is best described as:
opts: ["Simple gastritis unrelated to the liver","A mosaic stomach pattern from portal hypertension","A bacterial stomach infection","An autoimmune condition"]
exp: It's a direct consequence of portal hypertension causing congestion in the stomach lining, not ordinary gastritis, and is managed by lowering portal pressure rather than surgery.

q: Massive upper GI bleeding is generally defined as blood loss of:
opts: [">100ml",">1000ml or >3g/dL Hb drop in 2–4hrs",">50ml over 24 hours","Any visible blood in vomit"]
exp: This threshold indicates haemodynamically significant bleeding requiring urgent transfusion.

q: Propranolol prophylaxis for variceal bleeding aims to reduce heart rate by approximately:
opts: ["10% or to 90bpm","25% or to 55bpm","50% or to 40bpm","No specific target"]
exp: This specific target reflects an adequate reduction in portal pressure achieved through beta-blockade.

q: The concentration of epinephrine used for endoscopic injection in bleeding ulcers is:
exp: This diluted concentration is strong enough to cause local vasoconstriction and stop bleeding without excessive systemic effect.

q: Hb levels in an acute GI bleed should be interpreted with caution because:
opts: ["Hb rises immediately with bleeding","Hb lags — blood hasn't yet been diluted","Hb is not affected by bleeding","Hb only changes after 1 week"]
exp: Immediately after a bleed, the remaining blood hasn't yet mixed with replacement fluid, so the Hb can look falsely reassuring — vitals are watched more closely than an early Hb value.

q: GI bleeding is approximately how many times more common in men than women?
opts: ["Equal in both sexes","About 2 times more common in men","About 10 times more common in men","More common in women"]
exp: Men are affected roughly twice as often as women, reflecting differences in risk factors like alcohol use and NSAID intake.

q: The target haematocrit (HCT) during resuscitation for variceal bleeding is:
exp: This moderate target avoids both under-resuscitation and over-transfusion, which can raise portal pressure and worsen variceal bleeding.

q: H. pylori eradication is significant in peptic ulcer disease because it:
opts: ["Has no effect on ulcer recurrence","Provides a non-surgical cure for many peptic ulcers","Is only relevant to gastric cancer, not ulcers","Increases the risk of rebleeding"]
exp: Treating the underlying H. pylori infection removes the main driver of many peptic ulcers, curing the disease without surgery.

q: A patient with cirrhosis and a GI bleed carries higher rebleeding risk mainly because of:
opts: ["Improved clotting function","Portal hypertension and poor clotting","Lower blood pressure at baseline only","No increased risk compared to others"]
exp: Cirrhosis both raises pressure in the portal venous system (feeding varices) and impairs the liver's ability to make clotting factors — a dangerous combination for rebleeding.

q: Angiodysplasia as a cause of lower GI bleeding is most common in which group?
opts: ["Children","Elderly patients and those with chronic renal failure","Pregnant women only","Young healthy athletes"]
exp: Fragile, abnormal blood vessels (angiodysplasia) become more common with age and are also associated with chronic kidney disease.

q: Coagulopathy or low platelet count in a patient with GI bleeding:
opts: ["Reduces the risk of rebleeding","Increases the risk of rebleeding","Has no bearing on bleeding risk","Only matters in upper GI bleeds, not lower"]
exp: If the blood can't clot effectively, any bleeding point is far more likely to continue or restart.

q: Fulminant hepatic failure is defined as encephalopathy developing within:
opts: ["<2 weeks in a previously normal liver","2–12 weeks in a previously normal liver","Months to years in a diseased liver","Any timeframe, in any liver"]
exp: Speed (under 2 weeks) plus a previously healthy liver together define FHF.

q: The most common cause of fulminant hepatic failure is:
opts: ["Hepatitis B infection","Paracetamol overdose","Alcohol use","Amanita mushroom poisoning"]
exp: Paracetamol overdose accounts for roughly half of all FHF cases.

q: Chronic hepatic encephalopathy (PSE) typically develops in a liver that is:
opts: ["Previously completely normal","Already diseased (cirrhotic)","Transplanted within the last week","Unaffected by the current illness"]
exp: PSE builds slowly on top of existing cirrhosis and portal hypertension.

q: Lactulose helps reduce ammonia levels in hepatic encephalopathy by:
opts: ["Increasing ammonia absorption","Trapping ammonia in the colon as NH4+ for excretion","Directly neutralising ammonia in the blood","Increasing protein digestion"]
exp: An acidic colon converts NH3 into NH4+, which is trapped and excreted in stool rather than absorbed.

q: Which viral hepatitis is the most common viral cause of fulminant hepatic failure?
opts: ["Hepatitis A","Hepatitis B","Hepatitis C","Hepatitis E only"]
exp: Among the viral causes, hepatitis B is the most frequently implicated in fulminant liver failure.

q: The main complication of FHF, occurring in about 80% of cases, is:
opts: ["Cerebral oedema","Renal calculi","Pancreatitis","Peptic ulcer"]
exp: Swelling of the brain is the dominant, most dangerous complication driving mortality in FHF.

q: A PT (prothrombin time) greater than what value is used as a liver transplant criterion in FHF?
opts: [">15 seconds",">30 seconds",">50 seconds",">100 seconds"]
exp: A PT this prolonged reflects severe loss of the liver's clotting-factor production, signalling the need to consider transplant.

q: The recommended dose of neomycin in the management of PSE is:
opts: ["100mg every 6 hours","1g every 6 hours","10g once daily","500mg once weekly"]
exp: Neomycin reduces ammonia-producing gut bacteria; this is the standard dosing used in PSE management.

q: Protein reintroduction in PSE patients is generally recommended at:
opts: ["0–10g/day","20–50g/day","100–150g/day","No restriction needed"]
exp: Protein is carefully reintroduced at a moderate level to balance nutritional needs against the risk of worsening ammonia production.

q: The memory device 'FAST + FLAT' for fulminant hepatic failure refers to:
opts: ["Rapid onset, with no ascites and no enlarged spleen","Slow onset with big spleen","A dietary recommendation","A ventilator setting"]
exp: FHF develops quickly ('fast') in a liver that hasn't had time to develop the chronic signs of ascites or splenomegaly ('flat').

q: Subacute (subfulminant) hepatic failure develops over a timeframe of:
opts: ["<2 weeks","2–12 weeks","Months to years","Decades"]
exp: This intermediate category falls between the rapid fulminant type and the slow chronic type, and is relatively rare.

q: A liver transplant is considered in FHF when the patient's age is:
opts: ["Between 20–30 years only","<10 or >40 years","Only over 65 years","Age is not a factor"]
exp: Very young or older patients tend to have a worse prognosis with FHF, making transplant assessment a priority in these age groups.

q: A bilirubin level above what value is used as a transplant criterion in FHF?
opts: [">50 micromol/L",">100 micromol/L",">300 micromol/L",">1000 micromol/L"]
exp: A bilirubin this high reflects severe liver dysfunction and is one of the recognised criteria for considering urgent transplant.

q: Amanita mushroom (death cap) poisoning as a cause of FHF is notable because:
opts: ["Symptoms appear immediately","Symptoms are delayed 2–3 days before liver collapse","It never causes liver failure","It only affects the kidneys"]
exp: The delayed onset of symptoms means the poisoning is often not suspected until significant liver damage has already occurred.

q: Encephalopathy toxins implicated in hepatic failure include all of the following EXCEPT:
opts: ["Ammonia","False neurotransmitters","GABA","Insulin"]
exp: Ammonia, free fatty acids, mercaptans, false neurotransmitters, and GABA are all implicated toxins in hepatic encephalopathy — insulin is not part of this mechanism.

q: Which of the following commonly precipitates hepatic encephalopathy in a patient with chronic liver disease?
opts: ["GI bleeding","Constipation","Infection","All of the above"]
exp: GI bleeding, constipation, infection, high protein intake, fluid imbalance, and certain drugs can all tip a cirrhotic patient into overt encephalopathy.

q: Compared to FHF, chronic hepatic encephalopathy (PSE) is best described as:
opts: ["Fast and flat (no ascites/splenomegaly)","Slow and swollen (ascites and splenomegaly present)","Identical in presentation to FHF","Never associated with cirrhosis"]
exp: Unlike the fast-onset, previously-normal-liver picture of FHF, PSE develops slowly in a liver already showing chronic signs like ascites and an enlarged spleen.

q: The overall goal of lactulose therapy in hepatic encephalopathy is to:
opts: ["Increase blood ammonia levels","Lower blood ammonia levels by trapping it in the gut","Treat the underlying viral infection","Replace lost clotting factors"]
exp: By acidifying the colon, lactulose converts absorbable ammonia into a trapped, non-absorbable form that leaves the body in stool.

q: Which of the following liver failure types occurs in a liver that was previously completely normal, over 2–12 weeks?
opts: ["Fulminant","Subacute/subfulminant","Chronic (PSE)","None of the above"]
exp: This is the defining feature that separates subacute failure from both the faster fulminant type and the pre-existing-disease chronic type.

q: A 'silent chest' in an acute asthma attack indicates:
opts: ["The attack is resolving","Extreme danger — very little air is moving","A normal finding in mild asthma","Successful bronchodilation"]
exp: Wheeze is the sound of air squeezing through narrowed airways; silence means almost no air is moving — a pre-arrest sign.

q: The target SpO₂ in acute severe asthma treatment is:
exp: Aiming for 100% is unnecessary; 93–95% is the recommended range.

q: Magnesium sulphate in acute severe asthma is given as:
opts: ["1g IV bolus over 5 minutes","2g IV over 20 minutes","4g IM stat","500mg orally"]
exp: The standard dose is 2g IV over 20 minutes, used after an hour of poor response to first-line treatment.

q: A key electrolyte disturbance to monitor after giving salbutamol is:
opts: ["Hyperkalaemia","Hypokalaemia","Hypercalcaemia","Hyponatraemia"]
exp: Salbutamol pushes potassium into cells, which can lower blood potassium.

q: In acute severe asthma, steroids should ideally be administered within:
opts: ["10 minutes","1 hour","6 hours","24 hours"]
exp: Early steroid administration gives more time for the anti-inflammatory effect to take hold before the attack worsens.

q: Using more than how many SABA canisters per month is considered a danger sign?
exp: Needing more than one reliever canister a month suggests poorly controlled asthma at high risk of a severe attack.

q: The following are all features of life-threatening (not just severe) asthma EXCEPT:
opts: ["Silent chest","PEF 33–50%","SpO₂ <92%","Cyanosis"]
exp: A PEF of 33–50% describes 'severe' asthma; life-threatening asthma has an even lower PEF, below 33%.

q: The main pathophysiological problem in acute severe asthma is:
opts: ["Excess mucus production only","Gas trapping with dynamic hyperinflation","Complete airway closure with no air movement at baseline","Primary cardiac dysfunction"]
exp: Air gets into the lungs through narrowed airways but can't fully get back out, causing progressive overinflation.

q: Ipratropium bromide is used in acute severe asthma as:
opts: ["A replacement for oxygen","An addition to SABA for extra bronchodilation","A steroid substitute","An antibiotic"]
exp: It's added alongside salbutamol (SABA) to provide additional bronchodilating effect through a different mechanism.

q: A history of previous ICU admission or intubation for asthma indicates:
opts: ["No increased future risk","High risk of a fatal or near-fatal future attack","The patient no longer needs controller medication","Asthma has likely resolved"]
exp: A prior near-fatal episode is one of the strongest predictors of future severe or fatal attacks.

q: Antibiotics in acute severe asthma should be given:
opts: ["Routinely to all patients","Only if bacterial infection is confirmed","Instead of steroids","Before oxygen"]
exp: Asthma exacerbations are usually triggered by viral infections or allergens, not bacteria, so antibiotics are reserved for confirmed bacterial cases.

q: The typical oral prednisolone dose in acute severe asthma is:
opts: ["5–10mg/day for 1 day","30–50mg/day for 3–5 days","200mg/day for 2 weeks","1mg/day indefinitely"]
exp: This dose and short duration is enough to reduce airway inflammation without needing a prolonged steroid course for most patients.

q: The dexamethasone dose sometimes used as an alternative to prednisolone in acute asthma is:
opts: ["0.06mg/kg/day","0.6mg/kg/day","6mg/kg/day","60mg/kg/day"]
exp: This weight-based dose provides an equivalent anti-inflammatory effect to standard prednisolone dosing.

q: The most common trigger for an asthma exacerbation is:
opts: ["Viral respiratory infection","Food allergy","Exercise","Cold weather alone"]
exp: Common colds and other viral infections are the single most frequent trigger for worsening asthma.

q: Poor adherence to inhaled corticosteroids (ICS) is significant because it is:
opts: ["An unavoidable risk factor","The most preventable cause of severe exacerbations","Unrelated to exacerbation risk","Only relevant in children"]
exp: Since ICS keeps the airways less inflamed day-to-day, skipping it leaves the airways constantly vulnerable to a severe flare-up — and this is a modifiable, preventable risk factor.

q: IV hydrocortisone in acute severe asthma is typically given at a dose of:
opts: ["20mg/day","200mg/day in divided doses","2000mg/day","2mg/day"]
exp: This is the standard IV steroid dose used when a patient cannot take oral steroids.

q: Heliox (helium-oxygen mixture) may be considered in acute severe asthma:
opts: ["As the very first treatment given","After 1 hour of failed improvement with standard treatment","Only in children under 2","Instead of oxygen entirely"]
exp: Its lower density can improve gas flow through narrowed airways, and it is considered as an additional option once initial treatment hasn't worked.

q: A patient using their reliever (SABA) inhaler more than once a month should be assessed for:
opts: ["Improved asthma control","Poor asthma control and increased risk","Discontinuing all asthma medication","No action is needed"]
exp: Frequent reliever use signals the underlying inflammation isn't controlled, putting the patient at higher risk of a severe attack.

q: Which of the following is classified as a trigger for asthma exacerbation?
opts: ["Thunderstorms (pollen particle bursting)","Air pollution","Food allergy","All of the above"]
exp: Viral infections, allergens, air pollution, poor ICS adherence, food allergy, and even thunderstorms can all trigger an exacerbation.

topic: TOPIC 9: ICU MANAGEMENT OF LIFE-THREATENING ASTHMA
q: Drowsiness (somnolence) in a patient with severe asthma most likely indicates:
opts: ["The patient is simply tired","Rising CO2 and impending respiratory arrest","Over-sedation from medication","Improved oxygenation"]
exp: Rising CO2 sedates the brain — this is a red flag for impending arrest, not ordinary tiredness.

topic: TOPIC 9: ICU MANAGEMENT OF LIFE-THREATENING ASTHMA
q: Mechanical ventilation settings in life-threatening asthma use a LOW tidal volume of:
exp: A small tidal volume avoids adding more pressure to already-overinflated lungs.

topic: TOPIC 9: ICU MANAGEMENT OF LIFE-THREATENING ASTHMA
q: PEEP settings in ventilated life-threatening asthma should be:
opts: ["High (>15cmH2O)","Minimal (<5cmH2O)","Zero at all times","Equal to peak inspiratory pressure"]
exp: Because intrinsic PEEP is already present from trapped air, adding a lot more external PEEP worsens the trapping.

topic: TOPIC 9: ICU MANAGEMENT OF LIFE-THREATENING ASTHMA
q: The following are absolute indications for intubation in life-threatening asthma EXCEPT:
opts: ["Cardiopulmonary arrest","Decreasing level of consciousness","Mild wheeze with normal speech","Severe refractory hypoxaemia"]
exp: Mild wheeze with normal speech describes a mild attack, not one requiring emergency intubation.

topic: TOPIC 9: ICU MANAGEMENT OF LIFE-THREATENING ASTHMA
q: The recommended I:E ratio in ventilated life-threatening asthma is:
opts: ["1:1",">1:2 (longer expiratory time)","2:1","1:4"]
exp: A longer expiratory phase relative to inspiration gives trapped air more time to leave the lungs.

topic: TOPIC 9: ICU MANAGEMENT OF LIFE-THREATENING ASTHMA
q: The peak inspiratory pressure safety limit during mechanical ventilation for asthma is:
opts: ["<20cmH2O","<40cmH2O","<60cmH2O","No limit needed"]
exp: Exceeding this pressure risks rupturing the already overinflated, fragile lungs.

topic: TOPIC 9: ICU MANAGEMENT OF LIFE-THREATENING ASTHMA
q: The initial FiO₂ setting when starting mechanical ventilation for life-threatening asthma is:
exp: Starting at maximum oxygen ensures the patient is well oxygenated while the situation is stabilised, then titrated down as tolerated.

topic: TOPIC 9: ICU MANAGEMENT OF LIFE-THREATENING ASTHMA
q: Cardiovascular compromise (falling blood pressure) in a severe asthmatic is considered:
opts: ["A reason to delay intubation","A relative indication to strongly consider intubation","Unrelated to the respiratory condition","Only relevant in children"]
exp: Hyperinflated lungs can compress the heart and reduce cardiac output, so falling blood pressure in this context is a serious warning sign.

topic: TOPIC 9: ICU MANAGEMENT OF LIFE-THREATENING ASTHMA
q: The development of a pneumothorax in a ventilated asthmatic patient is:
opts: ["An expected, harmless finding","A relative indication to strongly consider intubation/escalation","Only relevant before ventilation started","Treated by increasing PEEP further"]
exp: A pneumothorax in this setting can rapidly worsen the patient's condition and signals the need for urgent escalation of care.

topic: TOPIC 9: ICU MANAGEMENT OF LIFE-THREATENING ASTHMA
q: Intrinsic PEEP (PEEPi/auto-PEEP) develops in life-threatening asthma because:
opts: ["Air cannot fully leave the lungs before the next breath","The ventilator is set incorrectly in all cases","The patient is over-oxygenated","It only occurs in healthy lungs"]
exp: With narrowed airways and not enough time to exhale fully, air becomes progressively trapped, generating pressure inside the lungs independent of the ventilator's set PEEP.

topic: TOPIC 9: ICU MANAGEMENT OF LIFE-THREATENING ASTHMA
q: Humidification of inspired gases during ventilation for asthma is used to:
opts: ["Cause bronchospasm","Reduce thick secretions","Increase airway resistance","Replace the need for bronchodilators"]
exp: Moist air helps keep mucus thinner and easier to clear, reducing the risk of mucus plugging.

topic: TOPIC 9: ICU MANAGEMENT OF LIFE-THREATENING ASTHMA
q: A rising PaCO₂ in an asthmatic patient not yet ventilated is a sign of:
opts: ["Improving ventilation","Approaching respiratory muscle exhaustion and failure","Overtreatment with oxygen","A normal, reassuring finding"]
exp: As the respiratory muscles tire from the increased work of breathing, CO2 starts to build up — a late and dangerous sign that ventilatory failure is approaching.

topic: TOPIC 9: ICU MANAGEMENT OF LIFE-THREATENING ASTHMA
q: Fatigue combined with somnolence in a severe asthmatic is classified as:
opts: ["An absolute indication for intubation","A relative indication to strongly consider intubation","A sign of successful treatment","Not clinically significant"]
exp: While not an automatic emergency intubation on its own, this combination strongly suggests the patient is deteriorating and needs close reassessment for possible intubation.

topic: TOPIC 9: ICU MANAGEMENT OF LIFE-THREATENING ASTHMA
q: Cardiopulmonary arrest in an asthmatic patient is classified as which type of intubation indication?
opts: ["Relative","Absolute","Contraindicated","Not an indication at all"]
exp: Cardiac or respiratory arrest is one of the clear-cut absolute indications requiring immediate intubation, with no room for a 'wait and see' approach.

topic: TOPIC 9: ICU MANAGEMENT OF LIFE-THREATENING ASTHMA
q: Decreasing level of consciousness in a severe asthmatic patient is classified as:
opts: ["A relative indication for intubation","An absolute indication for intubation","Not related to asthma severity","A sign of improvement"]
exp: A patient who is losing consciousness can no longer protect their airway, making this an absolute, immediate indication for intubation.

topic: TOPIC 9: ICU MANAGEMENT OF LIFE-THREATENING ASTHMA
q: Ventilator rate settings in life-threatening asthma are deliberately kept LOW to:
opts: ["Increase air trapping","Allow more time for full exhalation, reducing air trapping","Reduce oxygen delivery","Match the patient's own respiratory rate exactly"]
exp: A slower rate leaves more time between breaths for the trapped air to escape before the next breath is delivered.

topic: TOPIC 9: ICU MANAGEMENT OF LIFE-THREATENING ASTHMA
q: Severe refractory hypoxaemia in an asthma patient is classified as which type of intubation indication?
opts: ["Absolute","Relative","Contraindicated","Delayed only"]
exp: When oxygen levels cannot be corrected despite maximal treatment, this is an absolute indication requiring immediate intubation.

topic: TOPIC 9: ICU MANAGEMENT OF LIFE-THREATENING ASTHMA
q: The goal of using LOW tidal volume, LOW rate, and LOW PEEP together in ventilated asthma can be summarised as:
opts: ["Maximising lung volumes at all costs","'Less trapping' — minimising further air trapping and barotrauma","Achieving the highest possible peak pressures","Ensuring the patient breathes exactly like a healthy person"]
exp: Every one of these settings is chosen with the same underlying goal: protecting the already over-inflated lungs from further trapped air and pressure damage.

topic: TOPIC 9: ICU MANAGEMENT OF LIFE-THREATENING ASTHMA
q: In a ventilated life-threatening asthma patient, an inspiratory pressure target is generally set at:
exp: This range is used as the pressure control target while staying safely under the 40cmH2O peak pressure ceiling.

q: The 5 Ps of compartment syndrome are:
opts: ["Pain, Pulse, Pallor, Puffiness, Pyrexia","Pain, Pallor, Paraesthesia, Pulselessness, Pain on passive stretch","Pressure, Pulse, Pain, Paralysis, Puncture","Pallor, Pyrexia, Paraesthesia, Pain, Pulselessness only"]
exp: These are the classic warning signs of dangerously rising pressure inside a muscle compartment.

q: The first clinical symptom of tetanus is typically:
opts: ["Generalised convulsions","Risus sardonicus","Trismus (lockjaw)","Neck stiffness"]
exp: Jaw muscle spasm preventing mouth opening is classically the earliest sign.

q: Tetanus toxin causes muscle spasm by:
opts: ["Directly destroying muscle fibres","Blocking inhibitory neurotransmitters (glycine and GABA)","Increasing acetylcholinesterase activity","Causing muscle fibre paralysis"]
exp: Without the normal 'off switch' signals, muscles keep contracting with nothing telling them to relax.

q: The first-choice drug for controlling spasms in tetanus is:
opts: ["Tubocurarine","Diazepam","Chlorpromazine","Vecuronium"]
exp: Diazepam enhances GABA activity, directly compensating for the toxin's effect.

q: A tetanus patient should ideally be nursed in an environment that is:
opts: ["Bright and stimulating","Noisy, for constant communication","Quiet and dark, avoiding unnecessary stimulation","Cold, to reduce metabolic rate"]
exp: Even small stimuli can trigger a massive, dangerous spasm in a hypersensitive nervous system.

q: The average incubation period for tetanus is:
opts: ["1–2 days","6–10 days","2 months","1 year"]
exp: Symptoms usually appear within about a week to ten days after the wound, though this can extend up to 20–30 days.

q: Which form of tetanus is the most common overall?
opts: ["Generalised","Localised","Cephalic","Neonatal"]
exp: The generalised form, involving the whole body, is by far the most frequently seen presentation.

q: Neonatal tetanus is classically associated with:
opts: ["Unclean umbilical cord cutting","Vaccination reaction","Maternal diabetes","Premature birth alone"]
exp: Cutting the umbilical cord with unsterile instruments introduces the tetanus organism, causing this very high-mortality form in newborns.

q: The recommended dose of diazepam for tetanus spasms is:
opts: ["1–2mg every 6–8 hours","10–30mg every 6–8 hours","100mg once daily","500mg IV stat"]
exp: This is the standard therapeutic range used to control spasms while avoiding excessive sedation.

q: Tubocurarine, used for severe tetanus spasms, requires which additional intervention?
opts: ["No additional support needed","Mechanical ventilation (IPPV)","Oral feeding only","Discontinuation of all other drugs"]
exp: Tubocurarine paralyses muscles, including those needed for breathing, so ventilator support is mandatory whenever it's used.

q: Passive immunisation for tetanus (ATS) works by:
opts: ["Giving ready-made antibodies for immediate but short-lived protection","Stimulating the body to make its own long-lasting antibodies","Directly killing the Clostridium organism","Providing lifelong immunity in a single dose"]
exp: Unlike the tetanus vaccine (active immunisation, which builds long-term immunity), ATS gives immediate but temporary protection using pre-formed antibodies.

q: Compartment syndrome causing pain on passive stretch of the affected muscle is significant because it is:
opts: ["A late, unreliable sign","Often one of the earliest warning signs","Never associated with compartment syndrome","Only seen after the compartment has already been decompressed"]
exp: Pain that is disproportionate to the injury, especially when a muscle is passively stretched, is often the earliest clue that compartment pressure is dangerously high, before pulses are even lost.

q: The mechanism by which tetanus toxin travels to the central nervous system is via:
opts: ["The bloodstream only","Motor nerves, travelling upward to the spinal cord","The lymphatic system exclusively","Direct diffusion through skin"]
exp: Tetanospasmin specifically travels along motor nerve fibres in a retrograde direction up to the spinal cord, where it exerts its blocking effect.

q: Cephalic tetanus, occurring after head or facial wounds, is notable for:
opts: ["Being the mildest form with lowest mortality","Having high mortality","Never causing trismus","Only affecting the legs"]
exp: Because of its location near vital structures, this form of tetanus carries a particularly high mortality rate.

q: Risus sardonicus, seen in tetanus, refers to:
opts: ["A fixed, sinister facial grin from facial muscle spasm","Loss of facial sensation","Drooping of one side of the face","Excessive facial sweating"]
exp: Sustained spasm of the facial muscles pulls the face into a characteristic fixed grin, an early and recognisable sign of tetanus.

q: Wound debridement in tetanus management serves the purpose of:
opts: ["Halting further toxin production at the source","Providing pain relief only","Improving cosmetic appearance","Reducing the need for antibiotics"]
exp: Removing dead and infected tissue removes the environment the Clostridium bacteria need to keep producing toxin.

q: Antibiotic choice for treating the underlying Clostridium infection in tetanus typically includes:
opts: ["Penicillin or metronidazole","Aspirin","Insulin","No antibiotics are used"]
exp: These antibiotics are effective against Clostridium tetani, helping to stop further toxin production at the source.

q: The 5 treatment goals in tetanus management include all of the following EXCEPT:
opts: ["Halt toxin production","Neutralise circulating toxin","Encourage active mobility and stimulation","Control convulsions and rigidity"]
exp: Active mobility and stimulation are the opposite of what's needed — tetanus care specifically minimises stimulation to avoid triggering dangerous spasms.

q: Localised tetanus differs from generalised tetanus in that it:
opts: ["Affects the whole body equally","Confines spasms to muscles near the wound only","Only occurs in newborns","Has the highest mortality of all forms"]
exp: In localised tetanus, the toxin's effect remains confined to the region near the wound rather than spreading throughout the body.

q: The structures immediately posterior to the thyroid gland include the:
opts: ["Parathyroid glands","Submandibular glands","Parotid glands","Thymus gland"]
exp: Their position directly behind the thyroid is why they're at risk during thyroid surgery.

q: Accidental removal of the parathyroid glands during thyroidectomy results in:
opts: ["Hyperthyroidism","Hypocalcaemia","Hyperkalaemia","Hypoglycaemia"]
exp: Without the parathyroids, blood calcium drops, causing Trousseau's and Chvostek's signs.

q: Approximately what percentage of circulating T3/T4 is in the free (active) form?
exp: Only about 2% is free and active; the rest is bound to carrier proteins.

q: The most common cause of hyperthyroidism is:
opts: ["Toxic multinodular goitre","Solitary toxic adenoma","Grave's disease","Thyroiditis"]
exp: Grave's disease accounts for about 75% of hyperthyroidism cases.

q: In Grave's disease, the antibody responsible for overstimulating the thyroid is called:
opts: ["Anti-nuclear antibody (ANA)","TSI (Thyroid Stimulating Immunoglobulin)","Rheumatoid factor","Anti-thyroglobulin antibody only"]
exp: TSI mimics TSH but is never switched off by normal feedback control, driving continuous overactivity.

q: Toxic multinodular goitre (Plummer's disease) accounts for approximately what percentage of hyperthyroidism cases?
exp: It's the second most common cause of hyperthyroidism after Grave's disease.

q: The steps of thyroid hormone synthesis begin with:
opts: ["Trapping iodine","Releasing TSH","Forming T3/T4 directly from tyrosine without iodine","Activating the pituitary gland"]
exp: Iodine must first be actively trapped by the thyroid cell before it can be used to build thyroid hormone.

q: The central control pathway for thyroid hormone production follows the sequence:
opts: ["Thyroid → TSH → Pituitary → TRH → Hypothalamus","Hypothalamus → TRH → Pituitary → TSH → Thyroid","Pituitary → Hypothalamus → TSH → TRH → Thyroid","TSH → Hypothalamus → TRH → Pituitary → Thyroid"]
exp: The hypothalamus releases TRH, which tells the pituitary to release TSH, which then stimulates the thyroid itself.

q: Solitary toxic adenoma, the least common cause of hyperthyroidism, accounts for approximately:
exp: A single nodule functioning autonomously is a rare cause, making up only about 1% of hyperthyroidism cases.

q: The typical dose range of propylthiouracil (PTU) used in hyperthyroidism is:
exp: This is the standard dosing range used to reduce thyroid hormone production in hyperthyroid patients.

q: The typical dose range of carbimazole in hyperthyroidism management is:
opts: ["1–5mg/day","15–60mg/day","150–600mg/day","No standard dose exists"]
exp: This range is used to control excess thyroid hormone production, with the dose often adjusted based on response.

q: Anti-thyroid drug treatment is typically continued for how long before a patient may be considered cured?
opts: ["2 weeks","3 months","18 months","10 years"]
exp: A treatment course of around 18 months gives the best chance of long-term remission in Grave's disease before considering the patient cured.

q: Following a subtotal thyroidectomy, the amount of thyroid tissue typically left on each side is approximately:
opts: ["1g","5g","20g","The entire lobe is always left intact"]
exp: Leaving a small remnant (about 5g on each side) preserves some thyroid function while removing the bulk of the overactive tissue.

q: Mortality associated with thyroidectomy surgery is approximately:
exp: Thyroidectomy is a very safe procedure in modern practice, with an extremely low mortality rate.

q: Pre-operative anti-thyroid drug (ATD) treatment before thyroid surgery is typically given for:
opts: ["1–2 days","4–6 weeks","1–2 years","No pre-operative treatment is needed"]
exp: This preparation period helps bring thyroid hormone levels down to a safer level before the stress of surgery.

q: Lugol's iodine is used pre-operatively in thyroid surgery at a dose of:
opts: ["2–5 drops for 10–15 days","50 drops for 1 day","1 drop for 1 year","It is not used pre-operatively"]
exp: This short course helps reduce the vascularity and firm up the thyroid gland before surgery, making the operation safer.

q: The pyramidal lobe of the thyroid, when present, is a remnant of the:
opts: ["Thyroglossal duct","Parathyroid gland","Carotid sheath","Trachea"]
exp: This variable extra lobe is a leftover from the embryological migration path the thyroid took during development, and extends toward the hyoid bone.

q: The lymph node most associated with suspicion of thyroid malignancy when enlarged is the:
opts: ["Delphian node","Inguinal node","Axillary node","Popliteal node"]
exp: This midline lymph node overlying the thyroid, when enlarged, raises specific suspicion for thyroid cancer.

q: Peripheral (negative feedback) control of thyroid hormone means that:
opts: ["High T3/T4 stimulates more TSH release","High T3/T4 suppresses TSH release","TSH has no effect on thyroid hormone levels","Peripheral control only applies to TRH, not TSH"]
exp: This is a classic negative feedback loop — when hormone levels are already high, the pituitary reduces TSH release to avoid overproduction; when levels are low, TSH increases to stimulate more production.

q: Non-toxic goitre is characterised by:
opts: ["Hyperthyroidism","Normal thyroid function (euthyroid)","Hypothyroidism","Absent thyroid tissue"]
exp: The gland is enlarged but hormone levels remain normal.

q: The most important cause of endemic goitre is:
opts: ["Excess iodine intake","Iodine deficiency","Viral infection","Autoimmune destruction"]
exp: Low dietary iodine forces TSH up, driving gland enlargement.

q: Pendred syndrome is classically associated with goitre plus:
opts: ["Congenital deafness","Congenital blindness","Cardiac defects","Renal failure"]
exp: This inherited enzyme defect affects both the thyroid's iodine handling and inner ear development.

q: The correct order of the natural history of non-toxic goitre is:
opts: ["Nodular → Colloid → Parenchymatous","Parenchymatous → Colloid → Nodular","Colloid → Parenchymatous → Nodular","Nodular → Parenchymatous → Colloid"]
exp: The gland first swells generally (still reversible), fills with colloid, then forms permanent nodules over repeated cycles.

q: What percentage of solitary thyroid nodules are malignant?
exp: High enough that every solitary nodule warrants proper investigation.

q: The definitive investigation for a solitary thyroid nodule is:
opts: ["Ultrasound alone","FNAC (Fine Needle Aspiration Cytology)","Chest X-ray","Serum TSH alone"]
exp: FNAC provides tissue diagnosis, classifying the nodule as malignant, benign, indeterminate, suspicious, or inadequate.

q: Approximately what additional percentage of solitary nodules are follicular adenomas (benign but significant)?
exp: Beyond the 15% that are malignant, a further 30–40% are follicular adenomas, which is why every solitary nodule needs proper work-up.

q: Which of the following is a goitrogenic food capable of blocking iodine uptake?
opts: ["Cassava","Rice","Chicken","Eggs"]
exp: Cassava contains substances that interfere with iodine uptake, having a similar effect to true iodine deficiency.

q: In the parenchymatous stage of goitre development, the condition is:
opts: ["Permanent and irreversible","Reversible with thyroxine treatment","Only treatable by surgery","Already cancerous"]
exp: This earliest stage can still be reversed by correcting the underlying iodine/hormone deficiency.

q: The nodular stage of goitre is best managed by:
opts: ["Simple observation only","Surgery if needed, as it is not reversible","Thyroxine alone, which fully reverses it","No treatment is ever required"]
exp: By this stage, permanent structural changes have occurred that medical treatment alone cannot reverse.

q: Prevention of endemic iodine-deficiency goitre at a population level is achieved through:
opts: ["Iodination of salt","Universal thyroidectomy","Avoiding all dairy products","Routine radioiodine treatment"]
exp: Adding iodine to salt is a simple, effective, population-wide public health measure to prevent deficiency.

q: Which of the following drugs is known to act as a goitrogen?
opts: ["Paracetamol","Thiocyanates","Amoxicillin","Furosemide"]
exp: Thiocyanates interfere with the thyroid's ability to take up and use iodine, promoting goitre formation.

q: The drug of choice to prevent further convulsions in eclampsia is:
opts: ["Diazepam","Magnesium sulphate","Phenytoin","Hydralazine"]
exp: MgSO4 is specifically proven for preventing and treating eclamptic seizures.

q: The loading dose of magnesium sulphate in eclampsia is:
opts: ["1–2g IV","4–6g IV slowly","10g IV bolus","500mg IM"]
exp: 4–6g given slowly over 5–10 minutes, followed by a maintenance infusion.

q: Which of the following is monitored to detect magnesium sulphate toxicity?
opts: ["Blood glucose","Tendon reflexes, respiratory rate, urine output","Liver enzymes only","Body temperature"]
exp: These three ('TRU') are the earliest and most reliable warning signs of magnesium toxicity.

q: The antidote for magnesium sulphate toxicity is:
opts: ["Naloxone","Calcium gluconate","Vitamin K","Flumazenil"]
exp: Calcium directly opposes magnesium's effects at the neuromuscular junction.

q: Which drug is contraindicated for controlling postpartum bleeding in a woman with eclampsia?
opts: ["Oxytocin","Ergometrine","Misoprostol","Carboprost"]
exp: Ergometrine causes vasoconstriction that can dangerously raise already-high blood pressure.

q: The maintenance infusion rate of magnesium sulphate after the loading dose is:
opts: ["0.1g/hour","1–2g/hour","10g/hour","No maintenance is needed"]
exp: This continuous lower-dose infusion keeps magnesium at a therapeutic level to prevent further seizures.

q: A minimum respiratory rate of what value should be maintained during MgSO4 therapy?
opts: [">6/min",">12/min",">30/min","No specific value is required"]
exp: A respiratory rate falling below this threshold suggests magnesium toxicity is depressing breathing.

q: A minimum urine output of what value should be maintained during MgSO4 therapy?
opts: [">5ml/hr",">25ml/hr",">100ml/hr","Urine output is not relevant"]
exp: Magnesium is excreted by the kidneys, so poor urine output risks the drug accumulating to toxic levels.

q: Warning signs that may precede an eclamptic convulsion include:
opts: ["Severe headache and blurred vision","Improved appetite","Reduced blood pressure","Increased urine output only"]
exp: These symptoms reflect the brain and eyes being affected by the rising blood pressure, warning that a seizure may follow.

q: ACE inhibitors are contraindicated in pregnancy-related hypertension mainly because they can cause:
opts: ["Maternal hair loss","Fetal renal toxicity","Improved placental blood flow","No significant risk"]
exp: These drugs can damage the developing fetal kidneys, making them unsafe throughout pregnancy.

q: HELLP syndrome, a complication of severe pre-eclampsia, stands for:
opts: ["Haemolysis, Elevated Liver enzymes, Low Platelets","High Eclampsia, Low Labour Progress","Hypertension, Elevated Lipids, Low Protein","Haematoma, Embolism, Liver failure, Pulmonary oedema"]
exp: This life-threatening variant combines red blood cell breakdown, abnormal liver function, and a dangerously low platelet count.

q: The recommended position for nursing a patient during and after an eclamptic convulsion is:
opts: ["Flat on the back","Side-lying, to prevent aspiration","Fully upright/sitting","Prone with head down"]
exp: Turning the patient onto her side helps keep the airway clear and reduces the risk of inhaling vomit or secretions.

q: During an eclamptic seizure, bed rails should be:
opts: ["Left completely open","Padded to prevent injury","Removed entirely","Irrelevant to patient safety"]
exp: Padding protects the patient from injuring herself against the rails during violent convulsive movements.

q: Median sternotomy is the standard approach for:
opts: ["Lung lobectomy","Open heart surgery","Oesophagectomy","Splenectomy"]
exp: Splitting the sternum gives direct access to the heart.

q: The most common complication following thoracotomy is:
opts: ["Wound dehiscence","Atelectasis","Bronchopleural fistula","Pericardial tamponade"]
exp: Pain-limited shallow breathing and poor cough lead to mucus build-up and lung collapse.

q: Bronchopleural fistula is a particularly dangerous complication after which surgery?
opts: ["Appendectomy","Pneumonectomy for TB","Cholecystectomy","Thyroidectomy"]
exp: A leak between airway and empty pleural space after whole-lung removal can flood the remaining lung.

q: The most common cardiac irregularity seen after thoracotomy, especially in patients over 50, is:
opts: ["Ventricular fibrillation","Atrial fibrillation","Complete heart block","Sinus bradycardia"]
exp: Surgical manipulation near the heart commonly triggers AF in older patients.

q: The posterolateral thoracotomy incision provides the best access to:
opts: ["The heart for cardiac surgery","Pleura, bronchus, lungs, and posterior mediastinum","The pelvis","The abdomen only"]
exp: This is the most commonly used incision, particularly for lung and oesophageal procedures.

q: The anterolateral thoracotomy incision provides best access to:
opts: ["The pericardium, anterior mediastinal tumours, and mitral valve","The kidneys","The posterior spine","The lower limbs"]
exp: This incision approaches structures at the front of the chest, including the heart's mitral valve region.

q: A thoraco-abdominal incision on the right side gives access to a:
opts: ["Lacerated liver","Ruptured spleen","Oesophagogastric injury","None of the above"]
exp: The right-sided extension of this incision is used specifically to reach liver injuries.

q: Sputum retention after thoracotomy may require which intervention?
opts: ["Bronchoscopy, intubation, or tracheostomy","No intervention is ever needed","Immediate re-operation only","Discontinuing all pain relief"]
exp: When a patient cannot clear secretions effectively despite physiotherapy, more invasive airway clearance may be required.

q: Bronchospasm following thoracotomy is treated with:
opts: ["Bronchodilators and steam inhalation","Antibiotics only","Immediate surgery","No treatment is available"]
exp: These measures help relax the airway muscles and loosen secretions.

q: Wound disruption (dehiscence) after thoracotomy is managed with:
opts: ["Debridement, resuture, and antibiotics","Observation only, no intervention","Immediate discharge","Reducing all wound care"]
exp: An actively disrupted wound needs active surgical management to heal safely and avoid infection.

q: Thoracostomy differs from thoracotomy in that it is:
opts: ["A large incision for major intrathoracic surgery","A small opening for placing a chest drain","Identical to thoracotomy","Only used for cardiac surgery"]
exp: Thoracostomy is a much smaller procedure aimed specifically at inserting a drainage tube, not full surgical access.

q: Haemorrhage after thoracotomy can be classified as:
opts: ["Primary, reactionary, or secondary","Only ever primary","A trivial, non-life-threatening complication","Unrelated to the type of surgery performed"]
exp: Recognising which type of bleeding is occurring (immediate, early post-op, or delayed/infection-related) helps guide the appropriate management.

q: Post-pneumonectomy empyema is a recognised complication that may occur:
opts: ["With or without an accompanying bronchopleural fistula","Only if a fistula is also present","Only within the first hour after surgery","Exclusively in paediatric patients"]
exp: Infection can develop in the empty pleural space left after pneumonectomy, whether or not a fistula is also present.

q: What percentage of chest injuries are managed WITHOUT surgery?
exp: Most chest trauma is managed with supportive measures like chest tubes rather than an operation.

q: In tension pneumothorax, the trachea deviates:
opts: ["Toward the affected side","Away from the affected side","Remains midline","Deviates upward only"]
exp: Trapped air under pressure pushes the mediastinum toward the uninjured side.

q: Beck's Triad, indicating pericardial tamponade, consists of:
opts: ["Hypertension, bradycardia, loud heart sounds","Raised JVP, hypotension/tachycardia, muffled heart sounds","Fever, cough, chest pain","Cyanosis, wheeze, stridor"]
exp: Blood compressing the heart raises neck vein pressure, drops cardiac output, and muffles heart sounds.

q: In a trauma patient, the airway should be opened using:
opts: ["Head-tilt chin-lift","Jaw thrust","Recovery position only","No airway manoeuvre needed"]
exp: Jaw thrust opens the airway without moving the neck, protecting a possibly injured cervical spine.

q: An open (sucking) chest wound should be managed with:
opts: ["A fully sealed 4-sided occlusive dressing","A 3-sided occlusive dressing","No dressing at all","Direct pressure only, no dressing"]
exp: A 3-sided dressing acts as a one-way valve, releasing trapped air while preventing new air entry.

q: Paradoxical chest wall movement, where the flail segment moves inward on inspiration, is characteristic of:
opts: ["Simple pneumothorax","Flail chest","Pericardial tamponade","Pulmonary contusion"]
exp: When multiple adjacent ribs are broken in two places, that segment loses its normal structural connection and moves opposite to the rest of the chest wall.

q: Thoracotomy is generally indicated for massive haemothorax when drainage exceeds:
opts: ["100ml total",">1500ml or >200ml/hr for 3 hours","50ml/hr for 1 hour","Any amount of blood at all"]
exp: These specific volume thresholds indicate ongoing significant bleeding that chest tube drainage alone won't control.

q: A widened mediastinum after chest trauma raises suspicion of:
opts: ["Simple rib fracture","Thoracic aortic disruption","Minor bruising only","Normal anatomical variant"]
exp: This is considered the most important X-ray warning sign for a potentially fatal aortic tear after trauma.

q: Pulmonary contusion after blunt trauma is significant because it:
opts: ["Resolves immediately with no risk","Can worsen over 24–48 hours and predispose to ARDS","Only affects children","Requires immediate thoracotomy in all cases"]
exp: The injury often gets worse before it gets better, and excess IV fluids can make the lung injury worse, so fluid management is done carefully.

q: Diaphragmatic injury should be suspected with penetrating trauma at or below the:
opts: ["2nd intercostal space","5th intercostal space","10th intercostal space","Diaphragmatic injury is never related to wound location"]
exp: Penetrating wounds at this level or lower are anatomically close enough to the diaphragm to raise suspicion of injury.

q: Severe subcutaneous emphysema after chest trauma raises suspicion of:
opts: ["Simple rib fracture","Tracheobronchial injury","Normal healing","Pericardial tamponade"]
exp: Air leaking from a damaged airway tracks under the skin, producing this characteristic crackling swelling.

q: Needle decompression for tension pneumothorax is performed at the:
opts: ["5th ICS anterior axillary line","2nd ICS midclavicular line","2nd ICS posterior axillary line","Xiphoid process"]
exp: This is the standard, quickest access point for emergency needle decompression before a formal chest tube can be placed.

q: Primary postpartum haemorrhage after a vaginal delivery is defined as blood loss of:
exp: 500ml or more within 24 hours of vaginal delivery meets the definition of primary PPH.

q: The most common cause of postpartum haemorrhage, among the 4 Ts, is:
opts: ["Tone (uterine atony)","Trauma","Tissue","Thrombin"]
exp: A uterus failing to contract firmly is by far the most frequent cause of excessive postpartum bleeding.

q: The classic triad seen in Amniotic Fluid Embolism is:
opts: ["Fever, rash, joint pain","Hypotension, hypoxia, DIC","Headache, blurred vision, hypertension","Jaundice, ascites, confusion"]
exp: AFE causes a sudden collapse pattern combining low blood pressure, poor oxygenation, and abnormal clotting.

q: The most common underlying cause of DIC is:
opts: ["Sepsis","Dehydration","Anaemia","Hypertension"]
exp: Severe infection is the single most common trigger for DIC.

q: The first-line vasopressor of choice in septic shock is:
opts: ["Dopamine","Adrenaline","Norepinephrine","Vasopressin"]
exp: Norepinephrine is the preferred first-choice drug to raise blood pressure in septic shock.

q: Secondary postpartum haemorrhage occurs within what timeframe after delivery?
