opts: ["First 24 hours only","Day 1 up to 6 weeks postpartum","Only after 3 months","Only in the first hour"]
exp: Unlike primary PPH (within 24 hours), secondary PPH can occur any time from day 1 up to 6 weeks after birth.

q: Amniotic fluid embolism is definitively confirmed by:
opts: ["Clinical symptoms alone","Autopsy","Routine blood test","Ultrasound"]
exp: There is no reliable test to confirm AFE while the patient is alive — diagnosis is only confirmed at autopsy.

q: On a peripheral blood smear, DIC characteristically shows:
opts: ["Normal red blood cells","Schistocytes (fragmented red cells)","Increased platelets","Target cells only"]
exp: Red cells get physically damaged as they pass through vessels clogged with abnormal clots, producing fragmented schistocytes.

q: In DIC, fibrinogen levels are typically:
opts: ["Increased","Decreased","Unchanged","Not measurable"]
exp: Fibrinogen is consumed faster than it can be replaced as widespread abnormal clotting uses it up.

q: Peripartum cardiomyopathy (PPCM) is diagnosed using:
opts: ["Chest X-ray alone","Echocardiogram showing reduced LVEF","Blood culture","Urinalysis"]
exp: An echo directly visualises the heart's pumping function, confirming the reduced ejection fraction that defines PPCM.

q: The mortality rate associated with amniotic fluid embolism is approximately:
exp: AFE is one of the most lethal obstetric emergencies, with mortality reported as high as 90%.

q: What proportion of maternal deaths are estimated to occur in the postpartum period?
exp: This substantial proportion highlights why vigilant postpartum monitoring is so important, not just monitoring during labour and delivery.

q: Primary postpartum haemorrhage following a caesarean section is defined as blood loss of:
exp: The threshold is higher after a C-section (1000ml) compared to a vaginal delivery (500ml), reflecting the naturally greater blood loss expected with surgery.

q: Among the 4 Ts of PPH, 'Tissue' refers to:
opts: ["Retained placental tissue","Uterine muscle tone","Genital tract lacerations","Clotting factor deficiency"]
exp: Retained fragments of placenta or membranes prevent the uterus from contracting down fully, causing ongoing bleeding.

q: Among the 4 Ts of PPH, 'Thrombin' refers to bleeding caused by:
opts: ["Uterine atony","An underlying clotting disorder","Cervical tears","Retained placenta"]
exp: When the blood itself cannot clot properly, due to a pre-existing or acquired coagulopathy, bleeding continues despite a well-contracted uterus.

q: The most common type of thyroid carcinoma is:
opts: ["Follicular","Medullary","Papillary","Anaplastic"]
exp: Papillary carcinoma makes up about 85% of thyroid cancers and has the best prognosis.

q: Follicular thyroid carcinoma characteristically spreads via the:
opts: ["Lymphatic system","Bloodstream (haematogenous route)","Direct local invasion only","Cerebrospinal fluid"]
exp: Follicular cancer travels through the blood, often producing distant bone or lung metastases.

q: Calcitonin is a tumour marker specifically associated with which type of thyroid cancer?
opts: ["Papillary","Follicular","Medullary","Anaplastic"]
exp: Medullary carcinoma arises from calcitonin-producing C-cells.

q: Thyroxine is given after papillary thyroidectomy mainly to:
opts: ["Replace lost hormone only","Suppress TSH and reduce recurrence risk","Increase TSH production","Treat hypocalcaemia"]
exp: Suppressing TSH removes the stimulus that could drive any leftover cancer cells to grow.

q: Psammoma bodies on histology are a classic feature found in what percentage of papillary carcinoma cases?
exp: These characteristic calcified structures are seen in roughly 60% of papillary thyroid cancers.

q: Medullary thyroid carcinoma is familial (hereditary) in approximately what percentage of cases?
exp: About a quarter of medullary carcinomas run in families, making genetic counselling relevant for affected patients.

q: Anaplastic thyroid carcinoma most typically affects:
opts: ["Young men","Elderly women","Children","Pregnant women"]
exp: This aggressive, almost always fatal cancer typically presents in older women.

q: Despite its very poor prognosis, anaplastic thyroid carcinoma is notable for being:
opts: ["Curable with surgery alone","Radiosensitive","Never associated with airway obstruction","The most common type of thyroid cancer"]
exp: While surgery is rarely possible, radiotherapy can help relieve tracheal obstruction even though it doesn't cure the disease.

q: Radiation exposure increases thyroid cancer risk for how long afterward?
opts: ["1 year","5 years","At least 30 years","No increased risk after radiation"]
exp: The elevated cancer risk from radiation exposure persists for decades, not just the following year or two.

q: Management of medullary thyroid carcinoma differs from papillary/follicular types in that:
opts: ["Radioiodine and radiotherapy are not helpful","Radioiodine is always curative","Surgery is never performed","It requires no lymph node assessment"]
exp: Because medullary carcinoma doesn't arise from the iodine-concentrating follicular cells, radioiodine treatment (unlike in papillary/follicular cancer) is not effective.

q: The standard surgical management of papillary thyroid carcinoma is:
opts: ["Observation only","Total thyroidectomy with node dissection","Radiotherapy alone, no surgery","Partial lobectomy only, regardless of size"]
exp: Removing the whole gland along with affected lymph nodes gives the best chance of clearing the cancer, especially given how well papillary carcinoma responds to this approach.

q: A pre-existing goitre shows a positive correlation with which types of thyroid carcinoma?
opts: ["Papillary only","Follicular and anaplastic","Medullary only","No correlation with any type"]
exp: Longstanding goitre is specifically linked to a higher risk of these two carcinoma types.

q: Follicular thyroid carcinoma is managed with total thyroidectomy plus block dissection, and for metastases:
opts: ["Radioiodine and radiotherapy","No further treatment is available","Chemotherapy is always first-line","Observation only"]
exp: Unlike medullary carcinoma, follicular cancer cells still take up iodine, so radioiodine (plus radiotherapy where needed) remains an effective treatment for metastatic disease.

topic: TOPICS 18–19: RADIOLOGY, CONTRAST MEDIA & X-RAY INTERPRETATION
q: Positive contrast agents (e.g. iodine, barium) appear on X-ray as:
opts: ["Dark/lucent","Opaque/white","Invisible","Same density as air"]
exp: High atomic number agents block X-rays, showing up as bright white areas.

topic: TOPICS 18–19: RADIOLOGY, CONTRAST MEDIA & X-RAY INTERPRETATION
q: The mnemonic IPPR, used to check chest X-ray quality before interpretation, stands for:
opts: ["Inspiration, Position, Penetration, Rotation","Identity, Projection, Positioning, Report","Inflation, Pressure, Perfusion, Rate","Inspiration, Perfusion, Position, Result"]
exp: These four checks must be confirmed before trusting what's seen on the film.

topic: TOPICS 18–19: RADIOLOGY, CONTRAST MEDIA & X-RAY INTERPRETATION
q: The air bronchogram sign on chest X-ray is most associated with:
opts: ["Pneumothorax","Consolidation/pneumonia","Rib fracture","Cardiomegaly"]
exp: Air-filled bronchi show as dark branching lines against white consolidated lung tissue.

topic: TOPICS 18–19: RADIOLOGY, CONTRAST MEDIA & X-RAY INTERPRETATION
q: A widened mediastinum on chest X-ray after trauma is most suggestive of:
opts: ["Simple rib fracture","Thoracic aortic disruption","Pleural effusion","Diaphragmatic hernia"]
exp: This is the single most important X-ray warning sign after trauma.

topic: TOPICS 18–19: RADIOLOGY, CONTRAST MEDIA & X-RAY INTERPRETATION
q: The minimum amount of pleural fluid usually needed to be visible on a standard erect chest X-ray is:
exp: Smaller amounts of fluid can hide without being visible on standard films.

topic: TOPICS 18–19: RADIOLOGY, CONTRAST MEDIA & X-RAY INTERPRETATION
q: Before any contrast media examination, a test dose of how much is given?
opts: ["0.1ml","1ml, then wait 5 minutes","10ml stat","No test dose is needed"]
exp: This small test dose, followed by observation, helps identify an early reaction before the full dose is given.

topic: TOPICS 18–19: RADIOLOGY, CONTRAST MEDIA & X-RAY INTERPRETATION
q: Steroid prophylaxis for a patient with a previous contrast reaction includes prednisolone at what dose/schedule?
opts: ["5mg once, no repeat","50mg every 6 hours for 24 hours before the exam","500mg once, 1 week before","No steroids are used"]
exp: This regimen, started well before the exam and continued after, significantly reduces reaction risk in high-risk patients.

topic: TOPICS 18–19: RADIOLOGY, CONTRAST MEDIA & X-RAY INTERPRETATION
q: Which of the following is classified as a MAJOR/SEVERE contrast reaction?
opts: ["Mild urticaria","Metallic taste","Laryngeal oedema and respiratory arrest","Mild nausea"]
exp: These life-threatening reactions require immediate resuscitation, unlike the mild reactions listed in the other options.

topic: TOPICS 18–19: RADIOLOGY, CONTRAST MEDIA & X-RAY INTERPRETATION
q: In a tension pneumothorax on X-ray, the trachea deviates to the:
opts: ["Same (ipsilateral) side","Opposite (contralateral) side","Remains exactly midline","Cannot be assessed on X-ray"]
exp: Trapped air under pressure pushes the mediastinal structures, including the trachea, away from the affected side.

topic: TOPICS 18–19: RADIOLOGY, CONTRAST MEDIA & X-RAY INTERPRETATION
q: After a pneumonectomy, the trachea characteristically shifts:
opts: ["Toward the operated (empty) side","Away from the operated side","Never shifts after pneumonectomy","Shifts downward only"]
exp: Unlike tension pneumothorax, the empty space left after removing a whole lung pulls the trachea toward that side over time.

topic: TOPICS 18–19: RADIOLOGY, CONTRAST MEDIA & X-RAY INTERPRETATION
q: Negative contrast agents used in radiology include:
opts: ["Barium and iodine","Carbon dioxide, nitrous oxide, and air","Gadolinium only","Water only"]
exp: These low atomic number agents let X-rays pass through, appearing dark/lucent on the image.

q: Which type of ICU delirium is most commonly MISSED by staff?
opts: ["Hyperactive","Hypoactive","Mixed","All types are equally easy to spot"]
exp: A quiet, withdrawn patient is easy to mistake for simply resting, but this pattern is actually delirium and can be more dangerous because it's overlooked.

q: Physical restraints in a delirious ICU patient should be:
opts: ["Used routinely to prevent line removal","Avoided, as they worsen agitation and delirium","Applied only at night","Used instead of reorientation strategies"]
exp: Restraining a confused patient tends to increase distress and agitation rather than calm it.

q: An effective nursing strategy to reduce ICU delirium includes:
opts: ["Frequent changes of caregiving staff","Reorientation using a clock, calendar, and family photos","Keeping the environment brightly lit and noisy at all times","Withholding explanations to avoid overstimulation"]
exp: Helping the patient understand where they are and what day it is reduces confusion.

q: What percentage of mechanically ventilated ICU patients may experience delirium?
exp: Delirium is remarkably common in this population, affecting the large majority of ventilated ICU patients at some point.

q: Use of 3 or more medications in ICU is a risk factor for delirium because it can:
opts: ["Generate inflammatory markers (CRP, IL-1, TNF)","Have no effect on brain function","Only affect the kidneys","Improve cognitive function"]
exp: Polypharmacy is linked to increased inflammatory activity, which is one of the pathways contributing to delirium.

q: Bladder catheter use is a delirium risk factor through its effect on:
opts: ["Cerebral oxidative metabolism","Skin integrity only","Bone density","Vision"]
exp: Catheter-related factors can reversibly impair the brain's oxidative metabolism, contributing to delirium.

q: Hyperactive delirium is characterised by:
opts: ["Quiet withdrawal","Agitation, restlessness, and pulling out tubes/lines","No behavioural change at all","Improved cooperation with care"]
exp: This type is easy for staff to notice because the patient is visibly agitated and disruptive.

q: Mixed delirium is defined as:
opts: ["A type that never resolves","Alternating between hyperactive and hypoactive features","Only affecting elderly patients","A type with no defining features"]
exp: Patients with mixed delirium show both agitated and withdrawn phases at different times.

q: The most severe form of alcohol withdrawal syndrome is:
opts: ["Simple tremor","Delirium tremens (DTs)","Mild anxiety","Insomnia only"]
exp: DTs involves severe confusion, hallucinations, and autonomic instability, and carries a mortality risk if untreated.

q: Alcohol withdrawal symptoms typically begin how long after the last drink?
opts: ["Immediately, within minutes","6–24 hours","1–2 weeks","Only after 1 month"]
exp: This onset window is typical for a heavy drinker who abruptly stops alcohol intake.

q: The first-line drug class used to prevent seizures and DTs in alcohol withdrawal is:
opts: ["Antibiotics","Benzodiazepines","Antihistamines","NSAIDs"]
exp: Benzodiazepines like diazepam or lorazepam are the mainstay of preventing the dangerous progression of alcohol withdrawal.

q: Thiamine (B vitamin) is given during alcohol withdrawal management specifically to prevent:
opts: ["Delirium tremens","Wernicke's encephalopathy","Liver failure","Pancreatitis"]
exp: Chronic alcohol use often causes thiamine deficiency, and replacing it early prevents this serious, preventable brain complication.

q: The antidote for paracetamol overdose is:
opts: ["Flumazenil","Acetylcysteine","Naloxone","Atropine"]
exp: Acetylcysteine replenishes liver glutathione, helping detoxify the harmful paracetamol breakdown product.

q: The antidote used to reverse benzodiazepine sedation is:
opts: ["Naloxone","Flumazenil","Acetylcysteine","Protamine sulphate"]
exp: Flumazenil blocks the same receptor benzodiazepines act on, though caution is needed in dependent patients.

q: Gastric lavage is contraindicated in which of the following situations?
opts: ["General sedative overdose within 1 hour","Corrosive/acid ingestion","Barbiturate overdose while still conscious","Within 2 hours of a large tablet overdose"]
exp: Passing a tube for a corrosive substance risks further damage to the oesophagus and stomach.

q: According to global statistics, a suicide occurs approximately every:
opts: ["4 seconds","40 seconds","4 hours","4 days"]
exp: This frequently cited WHO-derived statistic highlights the scale of the global suicide burden.

q: For every 1 completed suicide, approximately how many attempts occur?
exp: This ratio highlights how many non-fatal attempts occur for every death, emphasising the importance of early intervention.

q: Suicide is a leading cause of death particularly in which age group?
opts: ["0–5 years","14–29 years","70–80 years","Suicide is not age-related"]
exp: This younger age range is disproportionately affected, making suicide a leading cause of death in this group.

q: What percentage of suicide victims had visited their GP in the month before the attempt?
exp: This statistic highlights a significant missed window of opportunity for healthcare intervention.

q: Activated charcoal is used in overdose management primarily to:
opts: ["Induce vomiting","Adsorb the drug and prevent further absorption","Directly neutralise all poisons","Replace the need for antidotes"]
exp: Charcoal binds to the drug in the gut, preventing it from being absorbed into the bloodstream.

q: For a conscious patient after barbiturate overdose who is still breathing adequately, initial management is:
opts: ["Immediate intubation regardless of breathing","Observation","No monitoring required","Discharge immediately"]
exp: If breathing remains adequate, close observation is appropriate rather than jumping straight to invasive airway support.

q: According to WHO data, approximately what proportion of completed suicides occur in developing countries?
exp: About half of all completed suicides worldwide occur in developing countries, reflecting the global scale of this issue.

q: Patients presenting after self-harm should be treated by healthcare staff with:
opts: ["Judgement about their choices","Dignity and respect","Delayed care until psychiatric review","Minimal communication"]
exp: Regardless of the circumstances, treating the patient with dignity and respect is a core principle of care after self-harm, both ethically and to support their recovery.

q: Status epilepticus is defined as a seizure lasting longer than:
opts: ["30 seconds","2 minutes","5 minutes","15 minutes"]
exp: Beyond 5 minutes (or repeated seizures without recovery), it's classified as status epilepticus, a medical emergency.

q: The first-line drug of choice for status epilepticus is:
opts: ["Phenytoin","Lorazepam","Magnesium sulphate","Levetiracetam"]
exp: IV lorazepam acts quickly to stop the seizure and is the preferred first choice.

q: In a pregnant patient having eclamptic seizures, the first-line treatment is:
opts: ["Lorazepam","Phenytoin","Magnesium sulphate","Diazepam"]
exp: Eclamptic seizures respond specifically to magnesium sulphate, used first-line in this particular situation.

q: The mortality rate associated with status epilepticus is approximately:
exp: This significant mortality range underlines why status epilepticus is treated as a true medical emergency.

q: Epilepsy is defined as:
opts: ["A single isolated seizure","2 or more seizures at least 24 hours apart without known cause","Any seizure lasting under 1 minute","Seizures only occurring in children"]
exp: A diagnosis of epilepsy requires recurrent, unprovoked seizures separated by at least a day.

q: The second-line drug of choice for status epilepticus, given as an IV loading dose, is:
opts: ["Lorazepam","Phenytoin","Salbutamol","Adrenaline"]
exp: Phenytoin is used when first-line benzodiazepines alone haven't stopped the seizure.

q: Diazepam, as an alternative first-line agent for status epilepticus, can be given via all of the following routes EXCEPT:
opts: ["IV","Rectal","Buccal or nasal","Oral tablet during active seizure"]
exp: During an active seizure, a patient cannot safely swallow a tablet — diazepam is given via routes that don't require swallowing.

q: A seizure that stops on its own after 3 minutes and the patient regains consciousness would be classified as:
opts: ["Status epilepticus","Not status epilepticus","Epilepsy by definition","Cannot be determined"]
exp: Since it resolved before the 5-minute threshold and the patient recovered consciousness, this does not meet the definition of status epilepticus.

q: If a patient continues seizing despite first- and second-line drugs, the next step generally involves:
opts: ["No further treatment is available","Escalating to general anaesthesia/ICU-level care","Simply waiting for the seizure to stop on its own","Discharging the patient home"]
exp: Refractory status epilepticus that hasn't responded to standard drug therapy requires escalation to intensive, anaesthetic-level seizure control.

topic: TOPIC 23: CONDITIONS AMENABLE IN ICU — PHYSIOTHERAPY
q: How soon after the start of inactivity does muscle protein synthesis begin to decline?
opts: ["Within 6 hours","After 3 days","After 1 week","After 1 month"]
exp: Muscle breakdown starts within just 6 hours of inactivity, emphasising the importance of early mobilisation.

topic: TOPIC 23: CONDITIONS AMENABLE IN ICU — PHYSIOTHERAPY
q: Which of the following is a contraindication to chest physiotherapy?
opts: ["Cystic fibrosis","Acute myocardial infarction","Post-operative atelectasis","Mechanical ventilation"]
exp: During an acute heart attack, the extra physical stress of physiotherapy could worsen cardiac strain.

topic: TOPIC 23: CONDITIONS AMENABLE IN ICU — PHYSIOTHERAPY
q: A patient is considered ready for ventilator weaning when their RSBI (RR/Vt) is:
opts: [">120","<120",">200","Not a useful measure"]
exp: An RSBI below 120 predicts a good chance of successful weaning.

topic: TOPIC 23: CONDITIONS AMENABLE IN ICU — PHYSIOTHERAPY
q: After 10 days of bed rest, even a healthy elderly person can be expected to lose approximately how much muscle strength?
opts: ["1%","15%","50%","No measurable loss"]
exp: This surprisingly rapid decline underscores why prolonged immobility is avoided whenever possible.

topic: TOPIC 23: CONDITIONS AMENABLE IN ICU — PHYSIOTHERAPY
q: The minimum vital capacity generally required for ventilator weaning readiness is:
opts: ["1ml/kg","10ml/kg predicted body weight","50ml/kg","Vital capacity is not assessed"]
exp: This threshold suggests the patient has enough respiratory reserve to breathe adequately once support is withdrawn.

topic: TOPIC 23: CONDITIONS AMENABLE IN ICU — PHYSIOTHERAPY
q: The negative inspiratory force required for weaning readiness is at least:
opts: ["-5cmH2O","-20cmH2O","-100cmH2O","Not relevant to weaning"]
exp: This measure of inspiratory muscle strength needs to reach at least this level to suggest adequate strength for weaning.

topic: TOPIC 23: CONDITIONS AMENABLE IN ICU — PHYSIOTHERAPY
q: What percentage of long-term ICU survivors experience some degree of weakness?
exp: The vast majority of long-term ICU survivors are affected by weakness to some degree, reflecting how common ICU-acquired weakness is.

topic: TOPIC 23: CONDITIONS AMENABLE IN ICU — PHYSIOTHERAPY
q: An FiO₂ below what value is generally required before considering weaning?
opts: ["<0.2","<0.5","<0.8","FiO₂ is irrelevant to weaning"]
exp: Needing a lower oxygen concentration suggests the lungs are functioning well enough to attempt weaning.

topic: TOPIC 23: CONDITIONS AMENABLE IN ICU — PHYSIOTHERAPY
q: Over 21 days of critical illness, total body protein loss can reach approximately:
opts: ["1%","16%, mostly from skeletal muscle","50%","No measurable loss occurs"]
exp: A substantial proportion of this loss comes directly from skeletal muscle, contributing significantly to ICU-related weakness.

topic: TOPIC 23: CONDITIONS AMENABLE IN ICU — PHYSIOTHERAPY
q: What percentage of mechanically ventilated patients develop significant weakness?
exp: A meaningful proportion, roughly a quarter, of ventilated patients go on to develop significant weakness.

topic: TOPIC 23: CONDITIONS AMENABLE IN ICU — PHYSIOTHERAPY
q: Raised intracranial pressure above what value is a contraindication to physiotherapy?
opts: [">5mmHg",">20mmHg",">50mmHg","ICP is not a consideration"]
exp: Physiotherapy manoeuvres can transiently raise ICP further, so it's avoided once pressure is already significantly elevated.

q: The 3 phases of the Active Cycle of Breathing Techniques (ACBT) are:
opts: ["Unstick, Collect, Evacuate","Breath Control, Thoracic Expansion Exercises, Forced Expiration Technique","Inhale, Hold, Exhale","Percussion, Vibration, Drainage"]
exp: ACBT cycles through recovery, deep expanding breaths, then forced huffs to move secretions.

q: Vibration, as an airway clearance technique, is applied:
opts: ["During inspiration only","During expiration only","Throughout the whole breathing cycle","Only during coughing"]
exp: Vibration is timed to the outward breath, helping shake secretions loose as air moves out.

q: Regarding drug timing with airway clearance techniques, the correct order is:
opts: ["Antibiotics before ACT, bronchodilators after","Bronchodilators before ACT, antibiotics after","Both given only after ACT","Timing does not matter"]
exp: Bronchodilators open the airways first, making clearance more effective; antibiotics then treat the now better-ventilated airways.

q: The 3 phases of Autogenic Drainage are:
opts: ["Unstick, Collect, Evacuate","Breath Control, Expansion, Forced Expiration","Percuss, Shake, Suction","Inhale, Pause, Exhale"]
exp: This technique moves through low, mid, then high lung volumes to progressively move secretions upward, without needing any equipment.

q: Percussion, as an airway clearance technique, is applied:
opts: ["During inspiration only","During expiration only","During both inspiration and expiration","Only while the patient is standing"]
exp: Unlike vibration, percussion with cupped hands is applied throughout the whole breathing cycle.

q: HFCC (High-Frequency Chest Wall Oscillation) uses an air-filled vest that squeezes the chest at a rate of:
opts: ["1–2 times/second","5–20 times/second","50–60 times/second","Once per minute"]
exp: This relatively fast oscillation rate helps loosen and move secretions toward the central airways.

q: Percussion as an airway clearance technique is contraindicated in a patient with:
opts: ["Retained secretions","Platelet count <20,000","Post-operative atelectasis","Cystic fibrosis"]
exp: Very low platelets increase the risk of bruising or bleeding from the repeated impact of percussion.

q: Vibration as an airway clearance technique is contraindicated in:
opts: ["Bronchospasm and wheezing","Retained secretions","Atelectasis","Cystic fibrosis"]
exp: Applying vibration to already-narrowed, irritable airways can worsen bronchospasm.

q: Postural drainage is contraindicated in a patient with raised ICP above:
opts: ["5mmHg","20mmHg","100mmHg","ICP is not relevant to PD"]
exp: Head-down positioning used in some PD positions can further raise ICP, which is dangerous once it's already elevated.

q: A key sign that airway clearance techniques are working effectively includes:
opts: ["Worsening breath sounds","Improved breath sounds and productive cough","Rising temperature","Increasing SpO2 has no relevance"]
exp: Clearer breath sounds and an effective, productive cough are practical bedside signs that secretions are actually being mobilised and cleared.

q: Percussion during airway clearance techniques is performed using:
opts: ["Flat, open-palm slapping","Cupped hands","A closed fist","Mechanical hammers only"]
exp: Cupping the hands traps a cushion of air that transmits a rhythmic vibration into the chest wall without causing direct pain from a flat strike.

q: Chest wall mobilisation as an airway clearance component works by:
opts: ["Expanding the thorax and loosening secretions","Directly killing bacteria in the lungs","Replacing the need for coughing entirely","Only working on the left lung"]
exp: Moving the chest wall through its range helps loosen secretions and improve overall lung expansion.

q: ICU-Acquired Weakness differs from Guillain-Barré Syndrome in that ICU-AW involves:
opts: ["Demyelination","Axonal degeneration without demyelination","No nerve involvement at all","Only facial muscle weakness"]
exp: GBS damages the myelin sheath, while ICU-AW damages the axon directly without demyelination.

q: Which muscle group is almost always SPARED in ICU-Acquired Weakness?
opts: ["Proximal limb muscles","Respiratory muscles","Facial and ocular muscles","Hip girdle muscles"]
exp: Unlike the proximal limb and respiratory muscles, the face and eyes are almost never involved in ICU-AW.

q: The single most important management strategy for ICU-Acquired Weakness is:
opts: ["Prolonged bed rest","Early mobilisation","Heavy sedation","Strict immobilisation of all joints"]
exp: Getting the patient moving as early as safely possible makes the biggest difference in preventing and treating ICU-AW.

q: Women are how many times more likely than men to develop ICU-AW?
opts: ["Equally likely","About 2 times","About 4 times","About 10 times"]
exp: This is a well-documented sex-based difference in ICU-AW risk.

q: ICU-AW can begin to develop after as little as:
opts: ["1 day of mechanical ventilation","4–7 days of mechanical ventilation","1 month of mechanical ventilation","1 year of mechanical ventilation"]
exp: The process can start surprisingly quickly, within under a week of ventilation.

q: On the MRC muscle strength scale, a score of 0 indicates:
opts: ["Normal power","Movement against gravity only","Complete paralysis, no movement","Flicker of movement only"]
exp: The MRC scale runs from 5 (normal) down to 0, with 0 representing total absence of movement.

q: Corticosteroids contribute to ICU-AW risk through:
opts: ["Directly strengthening muscle","Directly causing muscle breakdown (steroid myopathy)","Having no effect on muscle","Preventing muscle wasting entirely"]
exp: Steroids can directly break down muscle tissue, adding to the risk of ICU-acquired weakness.

q: Hyperglycaemia is linked to ICU-AW because it:
opts: ["Has no effect on nerves or muscle","Damages muscle and nerve tissue","Only affects the kidneys","Prevents muscle wasting"]
exp: High blood glucose is directly damaging to nerve and muscle tissue, which is why tight glucose control is protective.

q: 6-minute walk test distance in ARDS survivors at 1 year is typically:
opts: ["100% of expected, fully recovered","~66% of expected, even after lungs normalise","0% — unable to walk at all","Not a relevant outcome measure"]
exp: Even once lung function returns to normal, muscle weakness from ICU-AW often leaves patients with reduced walking capacity well into recovery.

q: At 2 years after ARDS, 6-minute walk distance in survivors remains at approximately:
opts: ["100% of expected","~68% of expected — still not fully recovered","10% of expected","Cannot be assessed"]
exp: Recovery from ICU-AW is often slow and incomplete, with functional deficits persisting even years later.

q: Recovery from ICU-Acquired Weakness is generally:
opts: ["Rapid and always complete","Often slow and incomplete, especially in the elderly","Never possible","Complete within 48 hours"]
exp: Many patients, particularly older ones, continue to have residual weakness for a long time after the acute illness resolves.

q: Neuromuscular blocking agents used during mechanical ventilation contribute to ICU-AW risk by:
opts: ["Strengthening muscles","Prolonging weakness","Having no lasting effect","Only affecting the heart"]
exp: These drugs paralyse muscles for ventilator management, and their use is linked to prolonged weakness afterward.

q: Postural drainage works primarily by using:
opts: ["Positive pressure ventilation","Gravity and positioning","Chemical mucolytics only","Electrical stimulation"]
exp: Positioning a lung segment uppermost lets gravity help drain secretions toward larger airways.

q: How many postural drainage positions are used, corresponding to the lung's bronchopulmonary segments?
exp: 12 positions match the anatomical segments of both lungs so every part can be drained.

q: How many bronchopulmonary segments does the right lung have?
exp: The right lung has 3 lobes and 10 segments total (3 upper + 2 middle + 5 lower).

q: How many bronchopulmonary segments does the left lung have?
exp: The left lung, having only 2 lobes, has 8 segments in total (4 upper + 4 lower).

q: Postural drainage was first described in what year?
exp: S.H. Quincke first described intermittent postural drainage for thick secretions in 1898.

q: Postural drainage was introduced specifically for cystic fibrosis care in which decade?
exp: This established the technique as part of standard care for the growing population of cystic fibrosis patients.

q: Chest physiotherapy (CPT), as a broader category, includes all of the following EXCEPT:
opts: ["Airway clearance techniques","Breathing exercises","Thoracic mobility exercises","Antibiotic prescription"]
exp: CPT covers physical and physiotherapeutic modalities, not drug prescribing, which is a separate medical intervention.

q: Postural drainage was accepted by the wider medical community in what year?
exp: William Ewart's endorsement in 1901 helped establish postural drainage as a recognised medical technique, a few years after it was first described.

q: The definition of postural drainage specifically emphasises the use of:
opts: ["Chemical agents to break down mucus","Gravity and positioning to move secretions into central airways","Electrical stimulation of the diaphragm","High-dose antibiotics"]
exp: Positioning the patient so gravity assists secretion movement toward the larger, more easily cleared central airways is the defining principle of PD.

q: Postural drainage positions are based specifically on:
opts: ["Patient preference alone","Bronchopulmonary segmental anatomy","Random rotation regardless of anatomy","Blood pressure readings"]
exp: Each of the 12 positions is designed to match a specific lung segment's anatomical drainage pathway.

q: A key difference between chest physiotherapy (CPT) and postural drainage (PD) is that PD is:
opts: ["A completely separate, unrelated field","One specific technique within the broader category of CPT","Always performed without any positioning","Only used outside the ICU"]
exp: CPT is the overall umbrella term covering many techniques, and postural drainage is one specific gravity-based technique within it.

q: The Belmont Report's 3 core ethical principles are:
opts: ["Autonomy, Beneficence, Justice","Consent, Privacy, Fairness","Honesty, Accuracy, Transparency","Safety, Dignity, Respect"]
exp: These three principles form the foundational framework most modern research ethics guidelines build upon.

q: The Nuremberg Code primarily established the requirement for:
opts: ["Peer review of all studies","Voluntary consent and subject welfare","Statistical significance testing","Government funding approval"]
exp: This code emerged after WWII specifically to ensure research subjects freely agree to participate and that their welfare is protected.

q: The Helsinki Declaration establishes that:
opts: ["Science's interests always come first","Subject wellbeing takes precedence over the interests of science/society","Consent is optional if the research benefits many people","Only adult subjects may ever be studied"]
exp: This declaration puts the individual research subject's welfare above any broader scientific or societal gain.

q: According to Polit and Hungler (1991), Respect for Human Dignity includes:
opts: ["Self-determination and full disclosure","Freedom from harm only","Fair distribution of resources only","Data accuracy only"]
exp: This principle is about the subject's free choice to participate and being told everything relevant before agreeing.

q: Vulnerable research subjects include all of the following EXCEPT:
opts: ["Children","Prisoners","Healthy working adults","The physically/mentally challenged"]
exp: Healthy, competent adults are generally able to give full informed consent themselves, unlike the groups needing extra protection.

q: Plagiarism in research is best described as:
opts: ["Only copying text word-for-word","Using others' work or ideas as one's own, without crediting the source","A minor issue with no ethical significance","Only applicable to published books"]
exp: Both direct quotation and paraphrasing of someone else's ideas must be credited — failing to do so, in any form, is plagiarism.

q: Non-falsification of data means that a researcher must:
opts: ["Report findings truthfully, even if they contradict the hypothesis","Only report results that support their theory","Adjust data to make results look more significant","Avoid publishing negative results"]
exp: Ethical research requires reporting the true relationship between variables, whatever the outcome turns out to be.

q: The right to withdraw from a research study at any time is part of the subject's right to:
opts: ["Self-determination","Anonymity only","Compensation","Data ownership"]
exp: Being free to change one's mind and leave a study without penalty is a core aspect of a subject's autonomy over their own participation.

q: Risk/Benefit Ratio is a consideration under which ethical principle?
opts: ["Beneficence","Justice","Confidentiality","Plagiarism avoidance"]
exp: Beneficence requires weighing potential harm against potential benefit before proceeding with research.

q: An institutional ethical review committee's main role is to:
opts: ["Fund research projects","Check a research plan against ethical guidelines before it starts","Write the research report for the researcher","Only review studies after they are completed"]
exp: These committees act as a gatekeeper, reviewing proposed studies in advance to ensure ethical standards will be met.

q: The Results chapter of a research report should primarily:
opts: ["Interpret and generalise findings","Report the data objectively, arranged to answer the study's objectives","Contain the researcher's personal opinions","Repeat the literature review"]
exp: Results are meant to be a factual, objective account of what was found, organised around what the study set out to answer.

q: Compared to the Results chapter, the Discussion chapter is generally considered:
opts: ["Easier to write","The most difficult part of the research report","Optional and rarely included","Identical in content to the Results"]
exp: Discussion requires synthesis and interpretation of data, which demands a deeper level of thinking than simply reporting facts.

q: If a study used both a questionnaire and an observation checklist, the Discussion chapter should:
opts: ["Combine both instruments' findings into one unstructured section","Follow the same two-section structure as the Results chapter","Only discuss the questionnaire findings","Ignore the observation checklist entirely"]
exp: Keeping the same structural order as the Results section makes the Discussion easier to follow and directly comparable.

q: A 'Reference,' as distinct from a Bibliography, refers to:
opts: ["Every source read during background research","A source actually cited or quoted in the text","Only sources published in the last 5 years","Only journal articles"]
exp: Reference specifically means sources you directly used (quoted or paraphrased) in your writing, unlike a bibliography which includes all consulted works.

q: One of the key reasons for including references in a research report is to:
opts: ["Increase the report's page count","Enable readers to verify the accurate source of information used","Avoid the need for a literature review","Replace the need for a methodology section"]
exp: References let readers trace claims back to their original source and check accuracy for themselves.

q: When selecting how to present a research objective's data, a researcher should:
opts: ["Use the same chart type for every objective regardless of data type","Choose the best visual (table, bar chart, pie chart, line graph) for that specific finding","Avoid using any visuals","Present all data purely in narrative form only"]
exp: Different types of data are best communicated by different visual formats — matching the visual to the data makes the story clearer.

q: The 'back matter' of a research report typically includes:
opts: ["Reference, Bibliography, and Appendix","Only the title page","The introduction and literature review","The abstract only"]
exp: These three sections together make up what comes after the main body chapters of the report.

q: In the Harvard (APA) referencing system, sources are arranged:
opts: ["Alphabetically","By order of appearance in the text","By publication length","Randomly"]
exp: The Harvard/APA system organises the reference list alphabetically by author surname.

q: In the Numbering System (Turabian/Chicago), sources are arranged:
opts: ["Alphabetically","In the order they first appear in the text","By page count","By year only, ignoring author"]
exp: Each source gets a number the first time it's cited, and the reference list follows that same numerical order.

q: The Latin term 'Ibid' is used when citing:
opts: ["The same work, the same page as just cited","The same work, a different page from the last citation","A completely different, unrelated work","Only works published this year"]
exp: Ibid tells the reader you're citing the same source again but from a different page than your previous citation.

q: The Latin term 'Op cit' is used when:
opts: ["A source already cited is referenced again, with other citations in between","Citing a source for the very first time","No source is being cited at all","Citing an unpublished thesis only"]
exp: Op cit ('already cited') is used to point back to a source you cited earlier, once other references have come between.

q: In a bibliography, the correct group order for arranging sources is:
opts: ["Journals → Books → Magazines/Newspapers → Other publications","Books → Journals → Other publications → Magazines/Newspapers","Random order within each letter of the alphabet","Newest to oldest publication date"]
exp: This is the fixed, expected grouping order for organising a bibliography's different source types.

q: A key formatting difference between Reference and Bibliography is that in Bibliography:
opts: ["The author's first name is written first","The author's surname is written first","No author details are included at all","Sources are never listed alphabetically"]
exp: Bibliography entries conventionally list the surname first (e.g., 'Nightingale, F.'), unlike normal prose naming.

q: Material such as a full questionnaire or consent form used in a study is best placed in the:
opts: ["Results chapter","Appendix","Abstract","Title page"]
exp: Bulky supporting material that would clutter the main chapters is moved to the Appendix instead, often labelled by section (A, B, C).

topic: TOPIC 30: PRINCIPLES OF JUSTICE AND FAIRNESS
q: In nursing ethics, the principle of Justice specifically requires:
opts: ["Treating all patients equally regardless of background","Giving preferential treatment to paying patients","Prioritising patients by social status","Ignoring resource limitations entirely"]
exp: Justice in nursing means a patient's background should never influence the quality of care they receive.

topic: TOPIC 30: PRINCIPLES OF JUSTICE AND FAIRNESS
q: When triaging multiple patients, a nurse practising justice should prioritise based on:
opts: ["Personal characteristics such as wealth","Clinical need","Order of arrival only, regardless of severity","Insurance status"]
exp: The sickest patient is treated first based on medical urgency, not on unrelated personal factors.

topic: TOPIC 30: PRINCIPLES OF JUSTICE AND FAIRNESS
q: Distributive Justice, as a concept, is most associated with the philosopher:
opts: ["John Rawls","Aristotle","Sigmund Freud","Florence Nightingale"]
exp: Rawls is closely linked with theories of fair allocation of resources and benefits across society.

topic: TOPIC 30: PRINCIPLES OF JUSTICE AND FAIRNESS
q: The key distinction between Justice and Fairness is that:
opts: ["Justice = rightness/what's deserved; Fairness = impartiality/equal treatment","They mean exactly the same thing with no difference","Justice only applies to law, never to nursing","Fairness only applies to financial matters"]
exp: Justice is about the rightness of an outcome, while fairness is specifically about impartial, unbiased treatment in the process.

topic: TOPIC 30: PRINCIPLES OF JUSTICE AND FAIRNESS
q: Applying the exact same rule to everyone regardless of their different starting points is:
opts: ["Always guaranteed to be fair","Justice, but not necessarily truly fair to everyone","Never a valid ethical approach","Only relevant in legal settings"]
exp: Equal treatment (justice) doesn't always account for people's different disadvantages or starting points, which is where fairness comes in.

topic: TOPIC 30: PRINCIPLES OF JUSTICE AND FAIRNESS
q: Fairness, as distinct from strict justice, aims to:
opts: ["Ignore differences between individuals entirely","Close the gaps opened by injustice and inequality","Only benefit those who are already advantaged","Apply identical resources regardless of need"]
exp: Fairness may mean giving different amounts of support so that everyone ends up with a genuinely equal chance.

topic: TOPIC 30: PRINCIPLES OF JUSTICE AND FAIRNESS
q: Justice in resource allocation involves ensuring that:
opts: ["No patient is unfairly disadvantaged in access to medication, treatment, or staff time","Resources always go to the first patient who asks","Resource allocation is kept secret from patients","Only wealthy patients receive full resources"]
exp: Equitable distribution of limited resources, without unfairly disadvantaging any patient, is central to justice in nursing practice.

topic: TOPIC 31–32: ARTERIAL BLOOD GAS (ABG) ANALYSIS — PARTS I & II
q: The normal pH range for arterial blood is:
exp: This narrow range must be maintained for normal cell and enzyme function.

topic: TOPIC 31–32: ARTERIAL BLOOD GAS (ABG) ANALYSIS — PARTS I & II
q: A rise in PaCO2 causing acidosis indicates the disorder is:
opts: ["Metabolic","Respiratory","Neither respiratory nor metabolic","Always compensated"]
exp: CO2 is controlled by the lungs, so a CO2-driven pH problem is classified as respiratory in origin.

topic: TOPIC 31–32: ARTERIAL BLOOD GAS (ABG) ANALYSIS — PARTS I & II
q: A fall in HCO3 causing acidosis indicates the disorder is:
opts: ["Respiratory","Metabolic","Purely a lab error","Always fatal"]
exp: Bicarbonate is controlled by the kidneys, so a HCO3-driven pH problem is classified as metabolic.

topic: TOPIC 31–32: ARTERIAL BLOOD GAS (ABG) ANALYSIS — PARTS I & II
q: The normal anion gap range is:
exp: Values above this range suggest a high-anion-gap metabolic acidosis, caused by extra unmeasured acid in the blood.

topic: TOPIC 31–32: ARTERIAL BLOOD GAS (ABG) ANALYSIS — PARTS I & II
q: A high anion gap metabolic acidosis, such as in DKA, is caused by:
opts: ["Loss of bicarbonate from diarrhoea","Extra unmeasured acid (e.g. ketones) in the blood","Excess vomiting","Hyperventilation alone"]
exp: Ketone bodies act as an unmeasured acid load, widening the anion gap, unlike bicarbonate-loss acidosis which keeps the gap normal.

topic: TOPIC 31–32: ARTERIAL BLOOD GAS (ABG) ANALYSIS — PARTS I & II
q: Type 1 Respiratory Failure is characterised by:
opts: ["Low PaO2 with normal or low PaCO2","High PaCO2 only","Normal PaO2 and PaCO2","High PaO2 with low PaCO2"]
exp: Type 1 failure is primarily an oxygenation problem; Type 2 failure adds a CO2 retention problem on top.

topic: TOPIC 31–32: ARTERIAL BLOOD GAS (ABG) ANALYSIS — PARTS I & II
q: In metabolic acidosis, the expected respiratory compensation (fall in PaCO2) is calculated as:
opts: ["0.1 × the HCO3 fall","1.25 × the HCO3 fall","No compensation ever occurs","2 × the pH change"]
exp: The lungs blow off CO2 to help correct the acidosis, and this expected compensation follows this specific formula.

topic: TOPIC 31–32: ARTERIAL BLOOD GAS (ABG) ANALYSIS — PARTS I & II
q: A Modified Allen's Test that shows NO flushing after releasing the ulnar artery indicates:
opts: ["Safe to proceed with radial puncture","Inadequate collateral circulation — avoid that site","The patient is dehydrated","A normal, expected finding"]
exp: A negative result means the ulnar artery alone can't adequately supply the hand, so radial puncture on that wrist should be avoided.

topic: TOPIC 31–32: ARTERIAL BLOOD GAS (ABG) ANALYSIS — PARTS I & II
q: A completely normal pH on an ABG:
opts: ["Always rules out any acid-base disorder","Does NOT rule out a mixed disorder","Only occurs in healthy people","Means PaCO2 and HCO3 must also be normal"]
exp: Two opposing disorders (e.g. respiratory acidosis plus metabolic alkalosis) can cancel each other out, producing a falsely reassuring normal pH — always check all three values.

topic: TOPIC 31–32: ARTERIAL BLOOD GAS (ABG) ANALYSIS — PARTS I & II
q: The first step in the recommended 5-step ABG interpretation approach is to assess:
opts: ["Compensation","Oxygenation","Anion gap","Base excess only"]
exp: Checking oxygenation (PaO2/SO2) first ensures you don't miss a life-threatening hypoxaemia while focused on acid-base status.

topic: TOPIC 31–32: ARTERIAL BLOOD GAS (ABG) ANALYSIS — PARTS I & II
q: Radial artery sampling for ABG is typically performed at what needle angle?
exp: A 45° angle, bevel up, is the standard technique for accessing the relatively superficial radial artery.

topic: TOPIC 31–32: ARTERIAL BLOOD GAS (ABG) ANALYSIS — PARTS I & II
q: In respiratory acidosis, the kidneys compensate by:
opts: ["Decreasing HCO3 reabsorption","Increasing HCO3 reabsorption and H+ excretion","Having no role in compensation","Immediately normalising pH within minutes"]
exp: The kidneys respond to a CO2-driven acidosis slowly, over time, by retaining more bicarbonate to help buffer the excess acid.

topic: TOPIC 33: EVALUATION PHASE OF THE NURSING PROCESS
q: Evaluation in the nursing process is best described as:
opts: ["A one-time final step only","An ongoing procedure of re-assessment","Something done only by physicians","Unrelated to the original care plan"]
exp: Evaluation happens continuously throughout care, essentially repeating the assessment step to check progress against the plan.

topic: TOPIC 33: EVALUATION PHASE OF THE NURSING PROCESS
q: The 4 skills used in evaluation are:
opts: ["Observation, Interviewing, Record keeping, Interpretation","Diagnosis, Planning, Implementation, Discharge","Palpation, Auscultation, Percussion, Inspection","Prescribing, Administering, Charting, Billing"]
exp: These four skills together let the nurse gather and make sense of information about how the patient is progressing.

topic: TOPIC 33: EVALUATION PHASE OF THE NURSING PROCESS
q: Formative evaluation is best described as:
opts: ["A one-time review done only at discharge","Ongoing judgment of the intervention as it is being implemented","Only relevant to research studies","The same thing as summative evaluation"]
exp: Formative evaluation happens in real-time, continually checking and adjusting during the course of care.

topic: TOPIC 33: EVALUATION PHASE OF THE NURSING PROCESS
q: Summative evaluation is best described as:
opts: ["A continuous, real-time check during care","A final review of the whole care plan against stated objectives","Something done before any care is given","Only used for formative goals"]
exp: Summative evaluation looks back at the entire plan once care is complete (or at a defined endpoint) to judge overall success.

topic: TOPIC 33: EVALUATION PHASE OF THE NURSING PROCESS
q: If a goal states a patient will gain 2kg in 4 weeks, and the patient gains only 1kg, the goal is classified as:
opts: ["Met","Partially Met","Not Met","Cannot be evaluated"]
exp: Some progress was made toward the goal, but the full target wasn't reached, which is exactly what 'partially met' describes.

topic: TOPIC 33: EVALUATION PHASE OF THE NURSING PROCESS
q: A subtle but important principle of re-assessment is that:
opts: ["A goal that appears met always means the problem is fully solved","A goal may be met on paper while the underlying problem remains unsolved","Re-assessment is unnecessary once a goal is achieved","Only unmet goals require re-assessment"]
exp: This is a classic exam trap — documented 'success' doesn't always mean the real clinical problem has actually been resolved.

topic: TOPIC 33: EVALUATION PHASE OF THE NURSING PROCESS
q: One of the stated advantages of the nursing process is that it:
opts: ["Promotes decisions based on routine, unquestioned habits","Promotes individualised care and evidence-based decisions","Removes the need for documentation","Eliminates the need for reassessment"]
exp: The structured, data-driven nature of the nursing process supports care tailored to the individual patient, avoiding purely routine-based decisions.

topic: TOPIC 33: EVALUATION PHASE OF THE NURSING PROCESS
q: Which of the following is one of the skills specifically used during the evaluation phase?
opts: ["Prescribing medication","Interviewing","Performing surgery","Billing insurance"]
exp: Talking with the patient (interviewing) is one of the direct ways a nurse gathers information to judge how care is progressing.

q: Conflict that occurs within a single individual, such as being torn between two duties, is classified as:
opts: ["Interpersonal conflict","Intrapersonal conflict","Intergroup conflict","Role conflict"]
exp: This type of conflict is an internal struggle happening inside one person's mind, not between two separate people.

q: Conflict arising from unclear or overlapping job expectations between two staff members is known as:
opts: ["Intragroup conflict","Role conflict","Intrapersonal conflict","Distributive conflict"]
exp: When it's unclear who is responsible for what, that ambiguity itself becomes a source of conflict.

q: According to the Thomas-Kilmann model, the conflict resolution style with both high assertiveness and high cooperation is:
opts: ["Competing","Avoiding","Collaborating","Accommodating"]
exp: Collaborating aims for a genuine win-win outcome, requiring both parties to actively push for and cooperate toward a shared solution.

q: A nurse overriding a hesitant colleague's objection to start immediate CPR during a cardiac arrest is an example of which conflict style?
opts: ["Avoiding","Competing","Accommodating","Collaborating"]
exp: High assertiveness with low cooperation — pursuing the necessary action decisively — is appropriate in a genuine time-critical emergency.

q: The conflict resolution style where one party gives in to preserve the relationship is called:
opts: ["Competing","Compromising","Accommodating","Avoiding"]
exp: Accommodating means prioritising the relationship or the other person's concern over your own, often because the issue matters more to them.

q: The stage of conflict where the disagreement becomes visible through behaviour such as arguing or withdrawal is the:
opts: ["Latent stage","Perceived stage","Manifest stage","Aftermath stage"]
exp: This is the stage where everyone else around can actually observe that a conflict is happening.

q: When mediating conflict between staff, a nurse manager should ideally:
opts: ["Take sides with the more senior staff member","Act as a neutral mediator","Resolve the conflict publicly in front of patients","Avoid addressing it entirely"]
exp: A manager who is seen to favour one side loses the trust needed to mediate fairly between the two parties.

q: Which conflict resolution style involves each party giving up something to reach a middle-ground solution?
opts: ["Collaborating","Compromising","Competing","Avoiding"]
exp: Compromising is a 'split the difference' approach where both sides get some, but not all, of what they originally wanted.

q: Conflict left unresolved over time can lead to:
opts: ["Improved teamwork automatically","Reduced staff morale and compromised patient safety","No lasting effect on the team","Guaranteed resolution eventually"]
exp: Conflict that festers doesn't stay contained to the two people involved — it can erode team functioning and ultimately affect patient care.

topic: TOPIC 35: ASEPSIS IN CRITICAL CARE UNIT
q: Medical asepsis ('clean technique') is primarily used for:
opts: ["Central line insertion","Non-invasive procedures like routine care and wound dressing","Open heart surgery","Intubation"]
exp: Clean technique reduces germs to a safer level without eliminating every single microorganism, suitable for non-invasive care.

topic: TOPIC 35: ASEPSIS IN CRITICAL CARE UNIT
q: Surgical asepsis ('sterile technique') aims to:
opts: ["Reduce germs to a safe level","Eliminate ALL microorganisms and spores","Only target visible dirt","Replace the need for hand hygiene"]
exp: Because invasive procedures breach the body's natural defences, a zero-tolerance approach to microorganisms is required.

topic: TOPIC 35: ASEPSIS IN CRITICAL CARE UNIT
q: ICU patients account for approximately what proportion of all hospital-acquired infections, despite occupying fewer than 10% of beds?
exp: This striking disparity highlights how disproportionately vulnerable ICU patients are to hospital-acquired infection.

topic: TOPIC 35: ASEPSIS IN CRITICAL CARE UNIT
q: According to WHO's 5 Moments of Hand Hygiene, hand hygiene should be performed:
opts: ["Only after touching a patient","Before touching a patient, before an aseptic procedure, after body fluid exposure risk, after touching a patient, and after touching patient surroundings","Only once per shift","Only when hands are visibly soiled"]
exp: This WHO framework identifies five specific moments during patient care where hand hygiene must be performed.

topic: TOPIC 35: ASEPSIS IN CRITICAL CARE UNIT
q: The preferred method of hand hygiene, except when hands are visibly soiled, is:
opts: ["Soap and water","Alcohol-based hand rub","No hand hygiene is needed between patients","Antibacterial wipes only"]
exp: Alcohol-based rub is faster and equally or more effective than soap and water, except when there is visible soiling requiring a physical wash.

topic: TOPIC 35: ASEPSIS IN CRITICAL CARE UNIT
q: Ventilator-associated pneumonia (VAP) is a hospital-acquired infection specifically linked to:
opts: ["Urinary catheters","Mechanical ventilation","Central lines","Surgical wounds"]
exp: As the name suggests, this infection is directly associated with the presence of an endotracheal tube and mechanical ventilation.

topic: TOPIC 35: ASEPSIS IN CRITICAL CARE UNIT
q: CLABSI stands for an infection associated with:
opts: ["Central lines","Chest tubes","Catheters in the bladder","Surgical incisions"]
exp: Central Line-Associated BloodStream Infection is directly linked to the presence of a central venous catheter.

topic: TOPIC 35: ASEPSIS IN CRITICAL CARE UNIT
q: Antisepsis specifically refers to:
opts: ["The overall state of being germ-free","The use of chemical solutions for disinfection","Sterilisation of surgical instruments only","A synonym for medical asepsis"]
exp: Antisepsis is one specific tool within the broader concept of asepsis — using chemical agents to reduce or kill microorganisms.

topic: TOPIC 35: ASEPSIS IN CRITICAL CARE UNIT
q: The target hand hygiene compliance rate in ICU settings is generally considered to be:
opts: [">50%",">90%",">10%","Compliance targets are not tracked"]
exp: Given how critical hand hygiene is to preventing HAIs, a high compliance target (over 90%) is the goal, even though actual ICU compliance is often suboptimal.

topic: TOPIC 36: RENAL TRANSPLANT — CRITICAL CARE UNIT MANAGEMENT
q: Among renal transplant types, the best expected outcome is generally seen with:
opts: ["Cadaveric donation","Living-related donation","Living non-related donation","All types have identical outcomes"]
exp: A closer genetic match, as seen with living-related donors, is associated with the best transplant outcomes.

topic: TOPIC 36: RENAL TRANSPLANT — CRITICAL CARE UNIT MANAGEMENT
q: Hyperacute transplant rejection occurs within:
opts: ["Minutes to hours","1–4 days","Days to weeks","Months to years"]
exp: This is the fastest and most immediate type of rejection, driven by pre-formed antibodies already present in the recipient.

topic: TOPIC 36: RENAL TRANSPLANT — CRITICAL CARE UNIT MANAGEMENT
q: Chronic transplant rejection typically develops over:
opts: ["Minutes to hours","1–4 days","Days to weeks","Months to years"]
exp: Chronic rejection is a slow, multifactorial process that unfolds over a much longer timeframe than the other rejection types.

topic: TOPIC 36: RENAL TRANSPLANT — CRITICAL CARE UNIT MANAGEMENT
q: The standard triple immunosuppression regimen after renal transplant includes:
opts: ["Cyclosporine/Tacrolimus + Azathioprine/Mycophenolate + Prednisolone","Insulin + Metformin + Glipizide","Aspirin + Clopidogrel + Warfarin","Only prednisolone alone"]
exp: This three-drug combination targets the immune response through different mechanisms to prevent rejection.

topic: TOPIC 36: RENAL TRANSPLANT — CRITICAL CARE UNIT MANAGEMENT
q: Post-operative monitoring of cyclosporine levels in a renal transplant recipient is performed:
opts: ["Once daily","Every hour","Once weekly","Only if symptoms develop"]
exp: This unusually frequent monitoring reflects how tightly cyclosporine levels must be controlled to avoid both rejection and drug toxicity.

topic: TOPIC 36: RENAL TRANSPLANT — CRITICAL CARE UNIT MANAGEMENT
q: At 10 years, survival with a living-related renal transplant is approximately:
exp: This figure dramatically outperforms both cadaveric transplant and dialysis-alone survival at the same time point.

topic: TOPIC 36: RENAL TRANSPLANT — CRITICAL CARE UNIT MANAGEMENT
q: At 10 years, survival on dialysis alone (without transplant) is approximately:
exp: This strikingly low figure demonstrates why transplant, when possible, is so strongly preferred over long-term dialysis alone.

topic: TOPIC 36: RENAL TRANSPLANT — CRITICAL CARE UNIT MANAGEMENT
q: Which of the following is an absolute contraindication to renal transplant?
opts: ["Well-controlled hypertension","Active uncontrolled infection","Age over 60 with good health","Mild anaemia"]
exp: An active, uncontrolled infection makes the immunosuppression required after transplant dangerously unsafe.

topic: TOPIC 36: RENAL TRANSPLANT — CRITICAL CARE UNIT MANAGEMENT
q: The best timing for renal transplant relative to dialysis is:
opts: ["Only after years of dialysis","Pre-emptive (before dialysis) or with minimal dialysis time","Timing makes no difference to outcomes","Only after complete kidney failure with no urine output"]
exp: Transplanting before or very early in dialysis is associated with better outcomes than waiting through prolonged dialysis first.

topic: TOPIC 37: NEPHRECTOMY — INDICATIONS AND CCU MANAGEMENT
q: The first successful nephrectomy, performed in 1869, demonstrated that:
opts: ["Kidney removal is always fatal","One healthy kidney is sufficient for urine excretion","Both kidneys must always be removed together","Nephrectomy was not possible until modern times"]
exp: Gustav Simon's pioneering operation proved a fact now taken for granted — a person can live normally with just one functioning kidney.

topic: TOPIC 37: NEPHRECTOMY — INDICATIONS AND CCU MANAGEMENT
q: Nephroureterectomy involves removal of:
opts: ["The kidney only","The kidney, the entire ureter, and a cuff of bladder","Only the ureter","Both kidneys and the bladder entirely"]
exp: This more extensive procedure removes the whole urinary tract pathway when cancer could be anywhere along it.

topic: TOPIC 37: NEPHRECTOMY — INDICATIONS AND CCU MANAGEMENT
q: For a cancer nephrectomy performed laparoscopically, the kidney must be removed:
opts: ["Broken into pieces through small incisions","Whole, inside a protective bag","Left inside the body","Through the mouth"]
exp: Removing the kidney whole, contained in a bag, prevents cancer cells from spreading during extraction.

topic: TOPIC 37: NEPHRECTOMY — INDICATIONS AND CCU MANAGEMENT
q: For a non-cancer nephrectomy performed laparoscopically, the kidney can be:
opts: ["Only removed whole, never morcellated","Morcellated (broken up) and removed through small incisions","Left inside the abdominal cavity","Removed only via open surgery"]
exp: Without cancer risk, the kidney can be safely broken into smaller pieces for removal through the small laparoscopic incisions.

topic: TOPIC 37: NEPHRECTOMY — INDICATIONS AND CCU MANAGEMENT
q: Post-nephrectomy donor investigations typically include:
opts: ["Chest X-ray, FBC, Electrolytes/Urea/Creatinine, eGFR","Only a urine dipstick","No pre-operative investigations are needed","Bone marrow biopsy"]
exp: These tests assess both general fitness for surgery and specifically the kidney function relevant to donation.

topic: TOPIC 37: NEPHRECTOMY — INDICATIONS AND CCU MANAGEMENT
q: The complication rate for partial nephrectomy is approximately:
exp: This is a clinically significant rate, with bleeding, infection, and urinary leak being the most common complications.

topic: TOPIC 37: NEPHRECTOMY — INDICATIONS AND CCU MANAGEMENT
q: Post-nephrectomy hospital stay typically ranges from:
opts: ["2–7 days","2–4 weeks","6 months","Discharge is always same-day"]
exp: The length of stay depends on the specific procedure and the patient's recovery, generally falling within this range.

topic: TOPIC 37: NEPHRECTOMY — INDICATIONS AND CCU MANAGEMENT
q: Nephrectomy for living donor kidney transplantation is performed on:
opts: ["The recipient's diseased kidney","The donor, to remove the kidney being given","Neither donor nor recipient","Only cadaveric donors"]
exp: This links directly to renal transplant — a living donor's surgery to remove their kidney is itself a nephrectomy.

topic: BONUS: THE PRINCIPLES OF BARRIER NURSING
q: Barrier nursing, as classically defined, aims to:
opts: ["Protect the patient from others","Protect others from the patient","Have no protective purpose","Only apply to surgical patients"]
exp: Standard barrier nursing (source isolation) is about preventing an infected patient from spreading disease to others.

topic: BONUS: THE PRINCIPLES OF BARRIER NURSING
q: Reverse barrier nursing (protective isolation) is used to:
opts: ["Protect others from the patient","Protect a vulnerable/immunocompromised patient from external infection","Punish non-compliant patients","Replace the need for hand hygiene"]
exp: This flips the usual direction of isolation — here, the patient's weak immune system needs protecting from outside germs.

topic: BONUS: THE PRINCIPLES OF BARRIER NURSING
q: A patient with confirmed tuberculosis requires which type of transmission-based precaution?
opts: ["Contact precautions","Airborne precautions","Droplet precautions only","No special precautions needed"]
exp: TB spreads via tiny airborne particles, requiring a negative-pressure room and fit-tested N95 respirator.

topic: BONUS: THE PRINCIPLES OF BARRIER NURSING
q: 'Protective Environment' precautions, used for severely immunocompromised patients, rely on:
opts: ["Negative pressure rooms","Positive pressure rooms","No special ventilation","Open-door policy"]
exp: Positive pressure keeps outside contaminated air from entering the room, protecting the vulnerable patient inside — the opposite of airborne precaution rooms.

topic: BONUS: THE PRINCIPLES OF BARRIER NURSING
q: The correct order for donning PPE is:
opts: ["Gloves → Mask → Gown → Goggles","Hand hygiene → Gown → Mask → Goggles → Gloves","Mask → Gloves → Gown → Goggles","Goggles → Gloves → Gown → Mask"]
exp: Gloves go on last, since they'll be the item most exposed to contamination during patient contact.

topic: BONUS: THE PRINCIPLES OF BARRIER NURSING
q: The correct order for doffing (removing) PPE is:
opts: ["Gloves → Goggles → Gown → Mask → Hand hygiene","Mask → Gown → Goggles → Gloves → Hand hygiene","Hand hygiene → Gloves → Gown → Mask → Goggles","Goggles → Mask → Gloves → Gown"]
exp: The most contaminated item (gloves) is removed first, and hand hygiene is performed last, after everything else is off.

topic: BONUS: THE PRINCIPLES OF BARRIER NURSING
q: Standard precautions differ from transmission-based precautions in that standard precautions:
opts: ["Are only used for confirmed infections","Apply to ALL patients, all the time","Are optional depending on staff preference","Replace the need for PPE entirely"]
exp: Standard precautions form the baseline of care for every patient, regardless of known infection status.

topic: BONUS: THE PRINCIPLES OF BARRIER NURSING
q: Which of the following is one of the 5 types of transmission-based precautions?
opts: ["Contact precautions","Standard precautions","Universal precautions","General precautions"]
exp: Contact precautions (used for organisms like MRSA and C. difficile) is one of the 5 named transmission-based precaution types, alongside droplet, airborne, enhanced barrier, and protective environment.

q: Bactericidal antibiotics work by:
opts: ["Stopping bacterial growth only","Actively killing bacteria, e.g. by attacking the cell wall","Only affecting viruses","Having no mechanism of action"]
exp: Drugs like penicillins directly destroy bacteria, typically by disrupting their cell wall structure.

q: Bacteriostatic antibiotics, such as tetracyclines, work by:
opts: ["Killing bacteria outright","Stopping bacterial growth, often via protein synthesis inhibition","Boosting bacterial reproduction","Only working against fungi"]
exp: Rather than killing bacteria directly, these drugs halt their ability to grow and multiply, giving the immune system time to clear the infection.

q: The 'golden age' of antibiotic discovery occurred during which period?
exp: Most major antibiotic classes still in use today were discovered during this productive mid-20th-century period.

q: Prophylactic antibiotic use refers to:
opts: ["Treating an already established infection","Preventing infection before it starts, e.g. before surgery","Only used for viral infections","A synonym for therapeutic use"]
exp: Prophylactic antibiotics are given to prevent an infection from developing in the first place, most commonly around the time of surgery.

q: A 'dirty' surgical wound carries an infection risk of approximately:
exp: Dirty wounds (with established infection or gross contamination) carry the highest baseline infection risk of the four wound classification categories.

q: For surgical prophylaxis, the antibiotic dose should ideally be given:
opts: ["More than 2 hours before surgery","Just before surgery","Only after the surgery is completed","Timing does not matter"]
exp: Giving the dose just before incision ensures adequate drug levels are present in the tissue during the highest-risk period; more than 2 hours before is considered wasted timing.

q: The preferred route for surgical antibiotic prophylaxis is:
opts: ["Oral","Systemic (IV)","Topical only","Intramuscular only"]
exp: IV administration achieves reliable, adequate blood and tissue levels quickly, right when they're needed.

q: Antibiotic stewardship refers to:
opts: ["Using the maximum possible dose for every infection","Selecting the right drug, dose, and duration based on local resistance data","Avoiding antibiotics entirely","Prescribing broad-spectrum antibiotics for all infections regardless of type"]
exp: Stewardship is about using antibiotics thoughtfully and appropriately to maximise effectiveness while minimising resistance development.

topic: BONUS: BLOOD VOLUME & CENTRAL VENOUS PRESSURE (CVP)
q: The normal CVP range is:
exp: This is the accepted normal range for pressure in the central veins, reflecting venous return to the right heart.

topic: BONUS: BLOOD VOLUME & CENTRAL VENOUS PRESSURE (CVP)
q: A low CVP most commonly indicates:
opts: ["Fluid overload","Hypovolaemia","Right heart failure","Normal fluid status always"]
exp: When venous return is reduced due to inadequate circulating volume, CVP drops.

topic: BONUS: BLOOD VOLUME & CENTRAL VENOUS PRESSURE (CVP)
q: The most commonly cannulated site for central line insertion is the:
opts: ["Femoral vein","Internal jugular vein","Radial artery","Brachial vein"]
exp: The internal jugular vein offers reliable, relatively safe access and is the most frequently chosen site.

topic: BONUS: BLOOD VOLUME & CENTRAL VENOUS PRESSURE (CVP)
q: After central line insertion, a chest X-ray is performed to:
opts: ["Check catheter tip position and exclude pneumothorax","Assess bone density","Check for appendicitis","Replace the need for blood tests"]
exp: Confirming the tip sits correctly in the superior vena cava and ruling out a pneumothorax (a recognised insertion complication) is essential before using the line.

topic: BONUS: BLOOD VOLUME & CENTRAL VENOUS PRESSURE (CVP)
q: The Seldinger technique for central line insertion involves:
opts: ["Direct large-bore needle insertion with no guidewire","Passing a catheter over a guidewire after initial needle access","Surgical cutdown only","No use of any wire or catheter"]
exp: This stepwise, guidewire-based technique is the preferred, safer method for central venous access.

topic: BONUS: BLOOD VOLUME & CENTRAL VENOUS PRESSURE (CVP)
q: A patient with sepsis presenting with a CVP of 9mmHg (slightly elevated) should be interpreted with caution because:
opts: ["Sepsis always causes low CVP","Sepsis can present with low, normal, OR high CVP","A CVP of 9 always confirms fluid overload","CVP is irrelevant in sepsis"]
exp: Unlike straightforward hypovolaemia, sepsis is unpredictable and its CVP reading must be interpreted alongside the full clinical picture.

topic: BONUS: BLOOD VOLUME & CENTRAL VENOUS PRESSURE (CVP)
q: CVP should be measured at which point in the respiratory cycle?
opts: ["End of inspiration","End of expiration","Mid-inspiration","Timing doesn't matter"]
exp: Measuring at end-expiration minimises the effect of intrathoracic pressure changes on the reading, giving a more consistent value.

topic: BONUS: BLOOD VOLUME & CENTRAL VENOUS PRESSURE (CVP)
q: Over-insertion of the guidewire during central line placement risks causing:
opts: ["Improved cardiac function","Arrhythmias","No clinical consequence","Immediate cure of the underlying condition"]
exp: If the wire is advanced too far, it can irritate the heart's conduction system and trigger an arrhythmia.

topic: BONUS: NUTRITION IN THE SURGICAL PATIENT
q: Marasmus is characterised by:
opts: ["Low albumin and oedema","Normal albumin, a 'walking skeleton' appearance","Only occurs in sepsis","Excess body fat"]
exp: Marasmus results from an inadequate but nutritionally balanced diet, and unlike kwashiorkor, albumin stays relatively normal.

topic: BONUS: NUTRITION IN THE SURGICAL PATIENT
q: Kwashiorkor is characterised by:
opts: ["Normal albumin and no oedema","Low albumin, with oedema masking underlying wasting","Only occurs in well-fed, healthy patients","Excess protein intake"]
exp: An inadequate AND unbalanced diet, often combined with sepsis or trauma, causes low albumin and fluid retention that hides the true extent of muscle/fat loss.

topic: BONUS: NUTRITION IN THE SURGICAL PATIENT
q: Keto-adaptation, which spares muscle during starvation, fails to protect muscle in:
opts: ["Simple fasting","Sepsis and trauma","Healthy individuals on a diet","It never fails"]
exp: In sepsis/trauma, inflammatory cytokines drive protein breakdown for fuel instead of allowing the normal fat-sparing ketone adaptation to protect muscle.

topic: BONUS: NUTRITION IN THE SURGICAL PATIENT
q: The Finger-thumb test at the bedside is used to assess:
opts: ["Protein stores","Fat stores","Hydration status","Blood glucose"]
exp: This simple physical test gives a quick bedside impression of the patient's remaining fat reserves.

topic: BONUS: NUTRITION IN THE SURGICAL PATIENT
q: The Tendon-bone test at the bedside is used to assess:
opts: ["Fat stores","Protein stores","Vitamin levels","Electrolyte balance"]
exp: This test helps estimate how much muscle/protein mass the patient has lost.

topic: BONUS: NUTRITION IN THE SURGICAL PATIENT
q: Enteral nutrition is generally preferred over parenteral nutrition when:
opts: ["The gut is functioning","The gut has completely failed","Never — parenteral is always preferred","Only in paediatric patients"]
exp: If the gastrointestinal tract can be used safely, enteral feeding is preferred as it's more physiological and carries fewer complications than IV feeding.

topic: BONUS: NUTRITION IN THE SURGICAL PATIENT
q: The catheter sepsis rate associated with TPN (parenteral nutrition) is approximately:
exp: This is a clinically meaningful infection risk directly tied to having an indwelling central line for TPN delivery.

topic: BONUS: NUTRITION IN THE SURGICAL PATIENT
q: Fat provides approximately how many kilocalories per gram, compared to carbohydrate and protein?
opts: ["Fat 4kcal/g, same as CHO/protein","Fat 9kcal/g, more than double CHO/protein (4kcal/g each)","Fat 1kcal/g, far less than CHO/protein","Fat provides no calories"]
exp: Fat is the most calorie-dense macronutrient, providing more than twice the energy per gram compared to carbohydrate or protein.

q: According to 2017 ACC/AHA criteria, Stage II hypertension is defined as:
exp: This is the highest defined category, indicating significant hypertension requiring active management.

q: Elective surgery is generally considered for cancellation when blood pressure exceeds:
opts: ["SBP >180 AND DBP >110","SBP >120 AND DBP >80","SBP >100 alone","Any blood pressure above normal"]
exp: This combined threshold (or SBP ≥200 alone) reflects a level of risk considered too high for elective proceeding without first optimising blood pressure.

q: Primary (essential) hypertension accounts for approximately what percentage of hypertension cases?
exp: The vast majority of hypertension has no single identifiable cause and is classified as primary/essential.

q: Malignant hypertension is defined as a medical emergency involving:
opts: ["SBP>180, DBP>120, plus organ damage","Any blood pressure reading above normal","Only diastolic pressure elevation","Low blood pressure with organ damage"]
exp: The combination of very high pressures with evidence of organ damage is what elevates this from simple hypertension to a true emergency.

q: On the day of surgery, a patient's regular β-blocker medication should generally be:
opts: ["Stopped abruptly","Continued","Doubled in dose","Replaced with a diuretic"]
exp: Suddenly stopping a β-blocker can cause dangerous rebound effects, so it is continued through the perioperative period.

q: On the day of surgery, diuretics are generally:
opts: ["Continued as normal","Usually withheld","Doubled in dose","Given intravenously regardless of oral status"]
exp: Diuretics are typically held on the day of surgery to avoid compounding fluid shifts and electrolyte changes during the perioperative period.

q: Hypertension is the leading cause of end-stage renal disease after which condition?
opts: ["Diabetes","Asthma","Hepatitis","Osteoarthritis"]
exp: Diabetes is the single leading cause, with hypertension close behind as the second most common cause of ESRD.

q: Secondary hypertension, unlike primary hypertension, is characterised by:
opts: ["Having no identifiable underlying cause","Having an identifiable underlying cause, such as renal artery stenosis","Never being treatable","Only occurring in children"]
exp: Because secondary hypertension has a specific identifiable cause, treating that underlying condition can sometimes resolve the high blood pressure entirely.

topic: BONUS: FLUID THERAPY — FLUIDS IN DIFFERENT CLINICAL CONDITIONS
q: Using the 4-2-1 rule, the hourly maintenance fluid for a 50kg adult is:
opts: ["50mL/hour","90mL/hour","150mL/hour","200mL/hour"]
exp: 4×10 + 2×10 + 1×30 = 40+20+30 = 90mL/hour, applying the rule in tiers by weight.

topic: BONUS: FLUID THERAPY — FLUIDS IN DIFFERENT CLINICAL CONDITIONS
q: To replace blood loss using crystalloid fluid, the required volume ratio compared to the blood lost is approximately:
opts: ["1:1","1:3 (three times the lost volume)","1:10","No replacement fluid is needed"]
exp: Crystalloids distribute throughout the body's fluid compartments, so roughly three times the lost blood volume is needed to achieve the same intravascular effect as a 1:1 colloid replacement.

topic: BONUS: FLUID THERAPY — FLUIDS IN DIFFERENT CLINICAL CONDITIONS
q: The four broad categories of shock, using the 'pump-pipe-water' framework, are:
opts: ["Hypovolaemic, Cardiogenic, Distributive, Obstructive","Septic, Anaphylactic, Neurogenic, Spinal","Mild, Moderate, Severe, Critical","Only Hypovolaemic and Cardiogenic exist"]
exp: This framework groups shock causes by what's failing: not enough water (hypovolaemic), a failing pump (cardiogenic), leaky pipes (distributive), or a blockage (obstructive).

topic: BONUS: FLUID THERAPY — FLUIDS IN DIFFERENT CLINICAL CONDITIONS
q: Neurogenic shock is distinguished from other types of shock by:
opts: ["Tachycardia","Bradycardia","No change in heart rate","Always presenting with fever"]
