exp: Unlike most shock states which trigger a compensatory tachycardia, neurogenic shock characteristically causes bradycardia due to loss of sympathetic tone.

topic: BONUS: FLUID THERAPY — FLUIDS IN DIFFERENT CLINICAL CONDITIONS
q: Pre-operative fasting guidelines typically allow clear fluids up until how long before surgery?
opts: ["30 minutes","2 hours","8 hours","24 hours"]
exp: Clear fluids empty from the stomach quickly, so they are permitted closer to surgery time than solid food or milk.

topic: BONUS: FLUID THERAPY — FLUIDS IN DIFFERENT CLINICAL CONDITIONS
q: Pre-operative fasting guidelines typically allow milk up until how long before surgery?
opts: ["1 hour","4 hours","12 hours","No milk is ever allowed before surgery"]
exp: Milk takes longer to clear from the stomach than clear fluids, so a longer fasting window is required.

topic: BONUS: FLUID THERAPY — FLUIDS IN DIFFERENT CLINICAL CONDITIONS
q: In the perioperative period, the most common cause of fluid-related morbidity/mortality is:
opts: ["Fluid deficit","Fluid excess","Perfectly balanced fluid status, which is always harmful","Fluid status has no bearing on outcomes"]
exp: Contrary to what might be assumed, giving too much fluid is a more common and significant cause of harm than under-hydration in the perioperative period.

topic: BONUS: FLUID THERAPY — FLUIDS IN DIFFERENT CLINICAL CONDITIONS
q: Using the Parkland formula, the fluid given in the FIRST 8 hours after a burn is:
opts: ["The full 24-hour calculated volume","Half of the total 24-hour calculated volume","A quarter of the total volume","No fluid is given in the first 8 hours"]
exp: Half of the total calculated 24-hour fluid requirement is front-loaded into the first 8 hours, with the remainder given over the following 16 hours.

q: A 35-year-old woman from the Jos Plateau presents with an anterior neck swelling that moves with swallowing. She has no systemic symptoms. What is the most likely diagnosis?
opts: ["Thyrotoxicosis","Simple non-toxic goitre","Hashimoto's thyroiditis","Follicular carcinoma"]
exp: Painless, moving-with-swallowing neck swelling with no systemic symptoms is the classic picture of a simple non-toxic goitre.

q: A region is said to have endemic goitre when:
opts: ["5% of the population have thyroid enlargement","10% or more of the population have goitre","More than 50% have thyroid nodules","Only females are affected"]
exp: The WHO/epidemiological threshold for calling a region "endemic" for goitre is 10% or more of the population affected.

q: The most common cause of endemic goitre is:
opts: ["Excess iodine","Iodine deficiency","Hypercalcaemia","Thyroid carcinoma"]
exp: Low dietary iodine is overwhelmingly the most common driver of endemic goitre worldwide.

q: A patient with diffuse toxic goitre presents with heat intolerance, palpitations, and fine tremors. Which hormone is excessively produced?
opts: ["Parathyroid hormone","Thyroid-stimulating hormone","Triiodothyronine and thyroxine","Calcitonin"]
exp: Excess T3/T4 causes the hypermetabolic features of thyrotoxicosis.

q: A farmer presents with goitre and reports eating cassava regularly. Which mechanism explains his condition?
opts: ["Cassava inhibits iodine trapping","Cassava increases TSH secretion","Cassava enhances thyroglobulin coupling","Cassava reduces TRH secretion"]
exp: Cassava contains cyanogenic compounds that interfere with the thyroid's ability to trap iodine.

q: In simple goitre, the neck swelling moves with swallowing because:
opts: ["It is attached to the hyoid bone","The thyroid gland moves with the trachea","The swelling is cystic","The lesion is subcutaneous"]
exp: The thyroid is fixed to the trachea via the pretracheal fascia, so it rises and falls with swallowing.

q: A patient presents with palpitations, tremor, exophthalmos, and weight loss. The most probable diagnosis is:
opts: ["Simple goitre","Toxic goitre","Hashimoto's disease","Thyroid cyst"]
exp: Palpitations, tremor, exophthalmos and weight loss together point to toxic (hyperfunctioning) goitre, classically Grave's disease.

q: Which of the following drugs inhibits thyroid hormone synthesis preoperatively?
opts: ["Adrenaline","Carbimazole","Dopamine","Propanolol"]
exp: Carbimazole blocks thyroid peroxidase, reducing new hormone synthesis before surgery.

q: A client on propylthiouracil develops a sore throat and fever. What should the nurse suspect?
opts: ["Hypocalcaemia","Agranulocytosis","Thyroid storm","Myxoedema"]
exp: Agranulocytosis (low neutrophils causing sore throat/fever) is the serious, classic side effect to watch for with propylthiouracil/carbimazole.

q: The recurrent laryngeal nerve lies in relation to which structure?
opts: ["Carotid sheath","Tracheo-esophageal groove","Prevertebral fascia","Submandibular duct"]
exp: The recurrent laryngeal nerve runs in the tracheo-oesophageal groove, close to the thyroid, which is why it's at risk during surgery.

q: Toxic multinodular goitre is also called:
opts: ["Jod-Basedow disease","Plummer's disease","Hashimoto's thyroiditis","Reidel's thyroiditis"]
exp: Toxic multinodular goitre is also known as Plummer's disease.

q: The female-to-male ratio in goitre incidence is approximately:
exp: Goitre is markedly more common in women, with ratios around 10:1 commonly cited.

q: Which of the following best explains the pathophysiology of goitre formation?
opts: ["Excess T3 and T4 stimulate TRH","Deficiency of T3/T4 increases TSH, leading to hypertrophy","Calcitonin excess suppresses parathyroid activity","TRH inhibits TSH release"]
exp: Low circulating T3/T4 removes negative feedback, so TSH rises and drives gland hypertrophy — the core mechanism of goitre formation.

q: Which investigation confirms thyroid function?
opts: ["Neck X-ray","Thyroid function test (T3, T4, TSH)","Ultrasound scan","FNAC"]
exp: Direct measurement of T3, T4, and TSH is how thyroid function itself is confirmed.

q: Fine needle aspiration cytology (FNAC) of thyroid is mainly used to:
opts: ["Assess hormone levels","Determine histological type","Detect tracheal deviation","Evaluate iodine uptake"]
exp: FNAC provides a tissue/cellular sample, used mainly to determine the histological type of a nodule (benign vs malignant).

q: A patient with Grave's disease has exophthalmos due to:
opts: ["Parathyroid hyperplasia","Retro-orbital tissue infiltration","Carotid artery compression","Calcitonin secretion"]
exp: Exophthalmos in Grave's disease results from autoimmune infiltration and swelling of the tissue behind the eye.

q: In the pathogenesis of goitre, coupling of iodotyrosine residues occurs in:
opts: ["Parafollicular cells","Follicular cells","Parathyroid cells","Pituitary cells"]
exp: Iodotyrosine coupling to form T3/T4 happens within the thyroid follicular cells.

q: Which of the following is a physiological cause of thyroid hormone deficiency?
opts: ["Pregnancy","Pituitary tumour","Malnutrition","Drug toxicity"]
exp: Pregnancy is a recognised physiological (non-pathological) state associated with changes in thyroid hormone demand/levels.

q: Toxic goitre symptoms of weight loss, tremor, and tachycardia occur due to:
opts: ["Decreased basal metabolic rate","Increased catecholamine sensitivity","Reduced cardiac output","Decreased tissue perfusion"]
exp: Increased catecholamine (adrenaline-type) receptor sensitivity in hyperthyroidism produces the tremor, tachycardia, and weight loss.

q: A patient from a mountainous area with an enlarged thyroid but normal thyroid function test likely has:
opts: ["Toxic goitre","Simple endemic goitre","Hashimoto's disease","Thyroid carcinoma"]
exp: Enlarged thyroid with normal T3/T4/TSH in an iodine-poor (mountainous) area is classic simple endemic goitre.

q: Preoperative preparation for thyroidectomy includes administering Lugol's iodine to:
opts: ["Increase hormone release","Decrease gland vascularity","Induce euthyroid state","Reduce laryngeal spasm"]
exp: Lugol's iodine reduces the blood supply and firms up the gland, making surgery safer.

q: The purpose of preoperative carbimazole therapy before thyroidectomy is to:
opts: ["Reduce gland firmness","Achieve euthyroid state","Decrease calcium level","Reduce postoperative infection"]
exp: Carbimazole is given pre-operatively to bring the patient to a euthyroid (normal hormone) state before anaesthesia and surgery.

q: A thyroidectomy patient complains of palpitations and anxiety preoperatively. The nurse should administer:
opts: ["Adrenaline","Propanolol","Dopamine","Morphine"]
exp: Propranolol controls the adrenaline-driven symptoms (palpitations, anxiety, tremor) of residual hyperthyroidism.

q: Before thyroidectomy, potassium iodide is given for about:
opts: ["24 hours","2 weeks","6 weeks","2 months"]
exp: Potassium iodide (Lugol's) is typically given for about 2 weeks before surgery.

q: The most life-threatening intraoperative complication of thyroidectomy is:
opts: ["Hypocalcaemia","Hemorrhage","Hoarseness","Hypothyroidism"]
exp: Haemorrhage during thyroid surgery is life-threatening because of the gland's rich blood supply and proximity to the airway.

q: Preoperative teaching for thyroidectomy includes:
opts: ["Neck flexion exercises","Neck support when turning","Deep mouth breathing only","Avoidance of coughing"]
exp: Supporting the neck (avoiding strain) when turning or moving protects the fresh suture line.

q: The position for a thyroidectomy patient immediately after surgery is:
opts: ["Supine with head flat","Semi-Fowler's with neck supported","Trendelenburg","High Fowler's"]
exp: Semi-Fowler's with neck support reduces swelling/tension and aids venous drainage after neck surgery.

q: The nurse ensures a tracheostomy set is available postoperatively because of:
opts: ["Hypocalcaemia","Airway obstruction","Hypertension","Hypoglycemia"]
exp: A tracheostomy set is kept ready because airway obstruction (from swelling, bleeding, or laryngeal spasm) is a feared post-op emergency.

q: During thyroid surgery, which structure must be preserved to prevent hypocalcaemia?
opts: ["Recurrent laryngeal nerve","Parathyroid glands","Carotid artery","Submandibular duct"]
exp: The parathyroid glands sit directly behind/within the thyroid and must be preserved to avoid post-op hypocalcaemia.

q: The anesthetist notes increased heart rate and hyperthermia during thyroidectomy. The nurse suspects:
opts: ["Thyroid storm","Tetany","Myxoedema","Hematoma"]
exp: Rising heart rate and hyperthermia during/after thyroid surgery are the hallmark signs of thyroid storm.

q: A thyroidectomy patient becomes restless, cyanosed, and has a neck swelling. The nurse should first:
opts: ["Reassure the patient","Remove sutures and call the surgeon","Start IV fluids","Elevate the foot of the bed"]
exp: Restlessness, cyanosis, and neck swelling suggest airway-compromising haematoma — sutures must be released immediately and the surgeon called.

q: Post-thyroidectomy, hoarseness of voice indicates:
opts: ["Laryngeal edema","Recurrent laryngeal nerve injury","Tracheal collapse","Hemorrhage"]
exp: Hoarseness after thyroid surgery points to recurrent laryngeal nerve injury or irritation.

q: Early postoperative complication of thyroidectomy includes:
opts: ["Keloid","Tracheomalacia","Hemorrhage","Hypothyroidism"]
exp: Haemorrhage is a recognised early (not late) complication after thyroidectomy.

q: The nurse monitors for tetany after thyroidectomy by checking:
opts: ["Trousseau's and Chvostek's signs","Kernig's sign","Babinski's sign","Lhermitte's sign"]
exp: Trousseau's and Chvostek's signs are the standard bedside tests for latent tetany from hypocalcaemia.

q: Positive Chvostek's sign indicates:
opts: ["Hypercalcaemia","Hypocalcaemia","Hypokalemia","Hypernatremia"]
exp: A positive Chvostek's sign (facial twitching on tapping) indicates hypocalcaemia.

q: The definitive treatment for severe postoperative tetany is:
opts: ["Vitamin D","10% Calcium gluconate IV","Antithyroid drug","Potassium chloride"]
exp: IV calcium gluconate is the definitive emergency treatment for severe symptomatic tetany.

q: When administering calcium IV, the nurse should avoid giving:
opts: ["Normal saline","Calcium with digitalis therapy","Glucose solution","Oral fluids"]
exp: Calcium can dangerously potentiate digitalis toxicity, so the combination is avoided.

q: A patient post-thyroidectomy suddenly develops stridor and dyspnea. The most likely cause is:
opts: ["Hypocalcaemia","Laryngeal edema","Hyperkalaemia","Anemia"]
exp: Sudden stridor and dyspnoea after thyroidectomy is most often due to laryngeal oedema narrowing the airway.

q: The nurse identifies thyroid storm by which symptom?
opts: ["Bradycardia","Hyperthermia up to 105°F","Muscle twitching","Hypotension only"]
exp: A very high temperature (up to 105°F) is a hallmark sign distinguishing thyroid storm from ordinary post-op fever.

q: Treatment of thyroid storm includes:
opts: ["IV insulin","Tepid sponging and propranolol","Glucagon","Sodium bicarbonate"]
exp: Cooling measures plus beta-blockade (propranolol) are core treatments for thyroid storm.

q: A patient develops tingling of fingers, muscle cramps, and carpopedal spasm after thyroidectomy. The nurse should suspect:
opts: ["Hypocalcaemia","Hyponatremia","Hyperkalemia","Hypercalcaemia"]
exp: Tingling, cramps, and carpopedal spasm are the classic early signs of hypocalcaemic tetany.

q: Postoperative nutrition for thyroidectomy patient begins with:
opts: ["Soft solid diet","Fluids as tolerated","Regular diet","NPO for 5 days"]
exp: Oral intake is cautiously restarted with fluids first once swallowing is confirmed safe.

q: The nurse places the thyroidectomy patient in semi-Fowler's position to:
opts: ["Reduce edema and venous congestion","Increase venous pressure","Promote calcium absorption","Enhance secretion drainage"]
exp: Semi-Fowler's position reduces venous congestion and swelling at the operative site.

q: After thyroidectomy, the nurse avoids neck flexion to:
opts: ["Prevent hypoxia","Avoid tension on suture lines","Reduce ICP","Decrease aspiration risk"]
exp: Avoiding neck flexion protects the fresh suture line from tension and disruption.

q: Tetany may develop due to injury of which gland?
opts: ["Thyroid","Parathyroid","Pituitary","Adrenal"]
exp: Damage to or removal of the parathyroid glands during thyroid surgery causes tetany.

q: The nurse suspects tracheal obstruction when the patient shows:
opts: ["Weakness","Frequent swallowing and choking","Hypotension","Cough suppression"]
exp: Frequent swallowing and choking sensation are classic early signs of tracheal compression from swelling/bleeding.

q: Post-thyroidectomy, normal calcium metabolism is maintained by:
opts: ["Parathyroid hormone","Calcitonin","Aldosterone","Insulin"]
exp: Parathyroid hormone is the primary regulator of calcium homeostasis after thyroid surgery.

q: Which ECG finding may occur with hypercalcaemia?
opts: ["Prolonged QT interval","Short QT interval","ST depression","T wave inversion"]
exp: Hypercalcaemia classically shortens the QT interval on ECG.

q: Post-thyroidectomy, a nurse observes hypotension and tachycardia. The most probable cause is:
opts: ["Hemorrhage","Hypothyroidism","Laryngospasm","Anxiety"]
exp: Ongoing bleeding causing hypovolaemia explains both hypotension and reflex tachycardia post-op.

q: To detect hemorrhage early after thyroidectomy, the nurse observes for:
opts: ["Neck swelling and frequent swallowing","Jaundice","Cyanosis","Tremor"]
exp: Neck swelling with frequent swallowing is the earliest clue of a developing haematoma/bleed.

q: Late complication of thyroidectomy includes:
opts: ["Hypothyroidism","Hypocalcaemia","Hemorrhage","Tetany"]
exp: Hypothyroidism (needing lifelong hormone replacement) is a recognised late complication, especially after total thyroidectomy.

q: In thyroid storm, the main cause of death is:
opts: ["Cardiac failure and shock","Tetany","Laryngeal paralysis","Renal failure"]
exp: In thyroid storm, the extreme hypermetabolic state can precipitate fatal cardiac failure and shock.

q: A nurse monitors a thyroidectomy patient for thyroid crisis. The most important vital sign to observe is:
opts: ["Pulse rate","Respiratory rate","Temperature","Blood pressure"]
exp: Pulse rate is the most immediately telling vital sign for detecting the tachycardia of an evolving thyroid crisis.

q: Post-thyroidectomy patient is connected to a cardiac monitor primarily to detect:
opts: ["Arrhythmias from calcium imbalance","Myocardial infarction","Hypovolemia","Thyroid storm"]
exp: Calcium imbalance from parathyroid injury can trigger dangerous cardiac arrhythmias, hence continuous monitoring.

q: Excess manipulation of the gland during surgery predisposes to:
opts: ["Tetany","Thyroid storm","Hypocalcaemia","Hematoma"]
exp: Excessive handling of the gland can release stored hormone suddenly, precipitating thyroid storm.

q: The nurse should suspect hypocalcaemia when the patient reports:
opts: ["Tingling in lips and fingers","Excessive sweating","Polyuria","Abdominal pain"]
exp: Tingling in the lips and fingers is an early sensory symptom of developing hypocalcaemia.

q: A patient on digoxin develops hypercalcaemia. The nurse should:
opts: ["Continue infusion","Stop calcium infusion immediately","Administer more fluids","Give adrenaline"]
exp: Hypercalcaemia combined with digoxin risks serious arrhythmia, so any calcium infusion is stopped immediately.

q: The safest IV fluid for thyroidectomy patient post-op is:
opts: ["Dextrose 5%","Normal saline","Ringer's lactate with calcium","Plasma"]
exp: Normal saline avoids the calcium content found in some other IV fluids, which could worsen hypercalcaemia risk.

q: The main goal of postoperative thyroidectomy care is:
opts: ["Maintaining airway patency","Preventing dehydration","Promoting sleep","Reducing anxiety"]
exp: Protecting the airway is always the top priority in post-thyroidectomy nursing care.

q: Which sign differentiates respiratory obstruction from tetany?
opts: ["Tingling of fingers","Neck swelling with dyspnea","Carpopedal spasm","Positive Chvostek sign"]
exp: Neck swelling with breathing difficulty points to an airway problem, distinguishing it from the muscular spasms of tetany.

q: The nurse should encourage postoperative coughing and deep breathing to:
opts: ["Clear secretions and prevent atelectasis","Stimulate thyroid secretion","Prevent tetany","Decrease oxygen demand"]
exp: Coughing and deep breathing clear secretions and help prevent post-op atelectasis/pneumonia.

q: The nurse detects hypocalcaemia within:
opts: ["6 hours","2--5 days after surgery","10 days","Immediately post-op"]
exp: Hypocalcaemia from parathyroid disruption classically becomes apparent 2-5 days after surgery.

q: Hoarseness lasting beyond 3 days after thyroidectomy suggests:
opts: ["Expected recovery","Recurrent laryngeal nerve injury","Edema","Anxiety"]
exp: Hoarseness persisting beyond a few days suggests actual nerve injury rather than simple post-op swelling.

q: When hypocalcaemia occurs, the nurse avoids disturbing the client because:
opts: ["Anxiety worsens spasms","Movement increases bleeding","Rest prevents infection","Pain relief is priority"]
exp: Reducing stimulation avoids triggering worse spasms while calcium levels are corrected.

q: The nurse includes in discharge teaching:
opts: ["Avoid hot environments","Take high-calorie diet","Avoid iodized salt","Stop exercise"]
exp: Heat can worsen residual hyperthyroid-type symptoms and should be avoided during recovery teaching.

q: The patient post-thyroidectomy should report immediately if:
opts: ["Voice becomes hoarse","Appetite increases","Sleep improves","Weight increases"]
exp: New or worsening hoarseness should be reported promptly as a possible sign of nerve injury.

q: Lifelong hormone replacement therapy is required after:
opts: ["Total thyroidectomy","Subtotal thyroidectomy","Hemithyroidectomy","Lobectomy"]
exp: Total removal of the gland leaves no thyroid tissue, requiring hormone replacement for life.

q: A thyroidectomy patient develops dyspnea and cyanosis. The immediate nursing action is:
opts: ["Administer calcium gluconate","Call the surgeon and prepare for tracheostomy","Give bronchodilator","Administer sedative"]
exp: Sudden dyspnoea with cyanosis signals critical airway compromise, requiring surgical review and tracheostomy readiness immediately.

q: The nurse reassures a patient that mild hoarseness after thyroidectomy:
opts: ["Is permanent","Resolves within 3--4 days","Indicates nerve injury","Requires tracheostomy"]
exp: Mild transient hoarseness from surgical swelling typically resolves within a few days.

q: Post-thyroidectomy, the nurse evaluates for hypothyroidism by assessing for:
opts: ["Weight gain and cold intolerance","Tremor and heat intolerance","Hyperactivity","Palpitations"]
exp: Weight gain and cold intolerance are the classic early signs of developing hypothyroidism.

q: Which one of the following is not a complication related to mechanical ventilation?
opts: ["hemothorax","MI","pneumothorax","ventilator associated pneumonia"]
exp: MI is not a direct ventilator complication; barotrauma (pneumothorax), haemothorax, and VAP are the classic ones.

q: What are the characteristics of stable unstable angina?
opts: ["Chest pain that occurs with exertion and/or emotional stress","Severe and crushing chest pain (\\>20 mins)","Chest pain that occurs at rest","Bradycardia"]
exp: Stable angina is chest pain triggered by exertion/emotional stress, relieved by rest.

q: Which of the following pain scale is best to be used for assessment of pain of in adults with pain?
opts: ["Simple description pain intensity scale","0-10 numeric pain scale","Faces pain-rating scale","McGill-Melzack pain questionnaire"]
exp: The 0-10 numeric scale is the most widely used, quick, and validated tool for adults who can self-report.

q: Which route of administration is preferred routine use of analgesia?
opts: ["Intraspinal","oral","Intravenous (IV)","Sublingual"]
exp: Oral is the preferred route for routine analgesia — least invasive, effective for most pain.

q: A client appears upset and tearful because of pain, however refuse to take medication because he says "I do not know anything about painkillers." What is the priority intervention for this client?
opts: ["Encourage expression of fears on past experiences","Provide accurate information about use of pain medication","Explain that addiction is unlikely among acute care clients","Seek family assistance in resolving this problem"]
exp: Her fear stems from a knowledge gap about painkillers, so accurate information directly addresses the cause.

q: A client with chronic pulmonary disease has an increased respiratory rate and signs of oxygen deficiency. ABGs of the patients shows increase in the blood CO2 level, the nurse charts which term to most accurately describe the client's condition?
opts: ["Hypercapnia","Hypoxemia","Cyanosis","Dyspnea"]
exp: Hypercapnia specifically means elevated blood CO2.

q: After having an I.V. line in place for 72 hours, a patient complains of tenderness, burning, and swelling with fever and light yellowish discharge form the site of insertion. This indicates:
opts: ["Infection","Phlebitis","Infiltration","Bleeding"]
exp: Fever plus purulent/yellowish discharge points to infection, not just simple phlebitis.

q: A Nurse at HMC asked a patient to verbalise if he understood the information given during nursing care to the client according to a devised nursing care plan. During which step of the nursing process does such action take place?
opts: ["Assessment","Planning","Implementation","Evaluation"]
exp: Checking whether the patient understood and achieved the planned outcome is the Evaluation step.

q: A male client is admitted to the hospital with RTA who is bleeding and crying from pain, however vital signs are stable. The nursing priority intervention for this client would be to:
opts: ["Assess the client's airway","Provide pain relief","Stop bleeding","Splint the chest wall with a pillow"]
exp: Airway is always assessed first (ABCs), even when vital signs look stable.

q: The nurse is providing care to an adult patient with acute MI. Which of the following should the nurse document as objective data?
opts: ["Patient history","Nonverbal clues during pain","Patient's description of pain","Electrocardiographic (ECG) waveforms"]
exp: ECG waveforms are measurable, observable data — objective. Pain descriptions/history are subjective.

q: Which of the following behaviour of a patient who is recently diagnosed with lung Cancer indicate that the patient is in the stage of denial?
opts: ["Going for another lung scan","Going to shrine for prayer to get cure","Patience","Preparatory grief"]
exp: Seeking a miracle cure rather than accepting the diagnosis is classic denial.

q: A 50 years old male admitted in medical ward B at HMC was injected with an IV antibiotic that has caused severe irritation, pain at the site and respiratory distress generally. What can be the possible diagnosis?
opts: ["Side effect","skin allergy","adverse reaction","Local inflammatory reaction"]
exp: Systemic reaction (irritation + respiratory distress) to a drug is an adverse drug reaction.

q: A patient is vomiting fresh blood in the emergency room. Which of the following best categorize this condition in the medical triage management?
opts: ["code green","code yellow","code red","code black"]
exp: Actively vomiting fresh blood is an immediate life threat — Code Red.

q: Which of the following condition is coded as yellow in medical triage management?
opts: ["vomiting fresh blood","high grade fever","closed fracture","persistant vomiting"]
exp: A closed fracture is urgent but not immediately life-threatening — Yellow.

q: A patient aged 40 has been received at the Emergency department who has multiple penetrating head trauma. The patient is hypoxic and gasping and about to die. What colour code will you give to this patient?
opts: ["Red","Yellow","Green","Black"]
exp: Hypoxic, gasping, about to die in the ED (not mass-casualty) = Red/immediate.

q: Another female patient has just arrived at the Emergency who is 50 years old and has got acute chest pain radiating towards left arm with vital signs as 160/110, HR 105 and Respiratory Rate 20/min. What color code will you give to this patient?
opts: ["Red","Yellow","Green","Black"]
exp: Stable vital signs with concerning symptoms = Yellow/urgent, not immediately life-threatening.

q: The main aim of the Intra-aortic balloon pump (IABP) include all except:
opts: ["increase renal blood","increasing cardiac output","increase myocardial O2 demand","decrease myocardial O2 demand"]
exp: IABP reduces (not increases) myocardial oxygen demand — that is the whole point of counterpulsation.

q: A ventilator delivers breath through which of the following mechanism?
opts: ["collapsing alveoli.","low oxygen levels.","negative pressure.","positive pressure"]
exp: Ventilators push air in using positive pressure (unlike natural negative-pressure breathing).

q: A patient is on the AC volume ventilation after open heart surgery, which of the following is to be taken care of during this mode to pneumothorax?
opts: ["keeping the patient sedated","delivering low PEEP","high peak inspiratory pressure","patient effort to breath more comfortable"]
exp: A high peak inspiratory pressure is the warning sign of barotrauma/pneumothorax risk.

q: What is the function of PEEP in the ventilator?
opts: ["it is the pressure maintained in the airway at the end of inhalation","it is the pressure maintained in the airway at the end of exhalation","is the pressure maintained in the airway at the end of inhalation & exhalation both","it is the pressure applied with each breath"]
exp: PEEP keeps some pressure/volume in the lungs at the end of exhalation, preventing alveolar collapse.

q: A patient is inhaling 500 ml of air with 75% Fio2, which of the following term the nurse should document for the amount of air inhaled?
opts: ["minute volume","residual volume","tidal volume","total lung capacity"]
exp: The volume of one single breath is tidal volume.

q: a 62 years old male patient has an ECG done that shows PR interval to be fixed, but drop QRS randomly. It indiciates?
opts: ["first degree block","second degree type-I block","second degree type-II block","third degree block"]
exp: Fixed PR interval with randomly dropped QRS complexes is the hallmark of Mobitz Type II.

q: 2 sec what does it indiciates?
opts: ["first degree block","second degree type-I block","second degree type-II block","third degree block"]
exp: A pause of this length in the described tracing best fits complete (third-degree) block; question stem is incomplete, answered on best available context.

q: Naloxone is administered to a patient in the Emergency room. This is an antidote for for which of the following type of poisoning?
opts: ["Chlorine poisoning","Opiate poisoning","Tricyclic antidepressant use","Alcohol overdose"]
exp: Naloxone is the specific opioid antagonist/antidote.

q: A femmale patient who is young is in the ER with suspected drug overdose or poisoning. Assessment reveals that the patient has mixed symptoms of physiologic excitation and depression. Which of the following drug can possibly produce such symptoms?
opts: ["cholinergic (parasympathomimetic), sympatholytic, opiate, or sedative-hypnotic agents, or alcohols","anticholinergic, sympathomimetic, or central hallucinogenic agents","polydrugs, hypoglycemic agents, tricyclic antidepressants, salicylates, cyanide","None of the above"]
exp: Polydrug and mixed toxidromes (e.g. TCAs, salicylates) classically produce both excitatory and depressive features together.

q: Which of the following is a priority intervention for patient with frostbite at the Emergency department?
opts: ["Cover area with woolen material and restore temperature slowly","Place area in hot water (100 degree Celsius)","rub frostbitten area quickly","none of above"]
exp: Slow rewarming with insulating material is safer than hot water or rubbing, which worsen tissue damage.

q: A 30 years old male admitted to ICU with RTA (road traffic accident), she is unconscious and is on ventilator with Tidal volume=550 and FiO2=90%. She has CVP line inserted because of failure to insert I/V cannula, IV bolus of 1 litre and blood was infused. You are measuring his CVP which is 12cm of H2O, what does this indicate?
opts: ["Hypovolemia","Mixed state","Hypervolemia","Normal volume"]
exp: CVP of 12 (above the normal 2-8 range) indicates volume overload — hypervolaemia.

q: A nurse is assessing Blood pressure of the patient, she knows that the invasive method of measuring Blood pressure is:
opts: ["Blood pressure cuff","Arterial line","Cardiac Monitor","PA catheter"]
exp: An arterial line gives continuous, direct (invasive) blood pressure measurement.

q: Preload can be measured by which of following:
opts: ["CVP","SVR","PA pressure","Cardiac Output"]
exp: CVP reflects right-heart filling pressure, used as a preload surrogate.

q: The serum creatine kinase (CK) rises in a patient with myocardial infarction with in the first:
opts: ["4-6 hours","2-4 days","12-18 hours","3-6 days"]
exp: CK begins rising within about 4-6 hours of MI onset.

q: A type of AV block characterised by progressive lengthening of the PR interval until the P wave fails to conduct is:
opts: ["Second degree AV-Block : Mobiz Type II","Second degree AV block: Mobiz Type I","First degree AV block","Third degree block"]
exp: Progressive PR lengthening before a dropped beat is Mobitz Type I (Wenckebach).

q: A condition characterised by a prolonged PR interval more than 0.2 secs in ECG is called:
opts: ["Second degree AV block","Mobiz Type II AV block","First degree Av block","Complete block"]
exp: PR interval >0.2 sec with every beat still conducting = first-degree AV block.

q: Intraaortic baloon pump therapy is most often used in the management of:
opts: ["Congestive cardiac failure","Cardiogenic shock","Pulmonary edema","all of the above"]
exp: IABP is most classically used to support patients in cardiogenic shock.

q: Which nursing intervention is appropriate to prevent pulmonary embolus in a patient who is prescribed bed rest?
opts: ["Limit the client\\'s fluid intake","Encourage deep breathing and coughing","Use the knee gatch when the client is in bed","Teach the patient to move legs in bed"]
exp: Leg movement/exercises promote venous return and reduce clot formation, directly targeting DVT/PE prevention.

q: An ABG analysis report shows: pH - 7.30; PCO2 - 35mmHg; HCO3- -18mEq/L. These findings are suggestive of
opts: ["Metabolic acidosis","Metabolic alkalosis","Respiratory alkalosis","Respiratory acidosis"]
exp: Low pH with normal PCO2 and low HCO3 points to a metabolic (not respiratory) acidosis.

q: Lactated Ringer's solution is contraindicated in
opts: ["Hypovolemia","Burns","Lactic acidosis","Fluid lost as bile or diarrhea"]
exp: Lactated Ringer's contains lactate that requires liver metabolism to bicarbonate — unsafe if lactic acidosis is already present.

q: Which of the following is the appropriate nursing intervention for a patient with a terminal illness who is passing through the acceptance stage?
opts: ["Allowing the patient to cry","Encouraging unrestricted visiting","Explaining the patient what is being done","Being around though not speaking"]
exp: In acceptance, patients often want quiet companionship rather than active talk or intervention.

q: What is the primary purpose of administering potassium chloride infusion to patient with diabetic ketoacidosis recieving IV insulin?
opts: ["Treatment of hypepnea","Prevention of fluccid paralysis","Replacement of potassium losses","Treatment of cardiac arrythmias"]
exp: Insulin drives potassium into cells, so KCl is given to replace/prevent depletion of total body potassium.

q: A nurse inadvertently infuses an IV solution containing potassium chloride too rapidly. Which of the following is an appropriate intervention advised by the physician?
opts: ["Rapid infusion of IV Ringer\\'s solution","Rapid infusion of IV NS 0.9%","Insulin added to 10% dextrose in water solution","Administration of albumin"]
exp: Insulin plus dextrose shifts excess potassium back into cells to reduce dangerous hyperkalaemia quickly.

q: Which of the following is an expected reaction from a client who has just been told by the physician that his tumor is malignant and has metastasis in to several organs?
opts: ["Crying uncontrollably","Criticizing medical care","Refusing to visit visitors","Asking for additional medical consultations"]
exp: Uncontrollable crying is a normal, expected acute grief response to devastating news.

q: Which of the following finding would indicate the presence of myocardial infarction?
opts: ["Increased creatinine kinase MB","Increased serum potassium","Increased blood urea nitrogen level","Decreased WBC level"]
exp: CK-MB is a cardiac-specific enzyme that rises with myocardial injury.

q: A patient is undergoing cardiac catheterization. Which of the following sensations reported by the patient during the procedure has the highest priority?
opts: ["Pressure at the insertion site","Urge to cough","Warm, flushed feeling","Chest pain"]
exp: Chest pain during catheterisation could signal ischaemia or complication — always the highest-priority symptom.

q: Electrocariaogram strip of a patient shows: P wave and QRS complexes --regular, PR-Interval - 0.14 seconds, QRS complexes measure- 0.08 seconds, overall hert beat rate- 82beats per minutes. This cardiac rhythm is\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_
opts: ["Sinus bradycardia","Sinus tachycardia","Normal sinus rhythm","First-degree heart block"]
exp: Normal PR (0.14s), normal QRS (0.08s), and a rate of 82 with regular P/QRS = normal sinus rhythm.

q: Which of the following pairing about Glasgow Coma Score is wrong?
opts: ["Spontaneous Eye Opening=6","No Verbal Response=1","Localizes to pain (Motor Activity)=5","Eye Opening to Verbal stimuli=3"]
exp: Spontaneous eye opening is scored 4 on the GCS, not 6 — that pairing is wrong.

q: The classic ECG changes in myocardial infarction (MI) are:
opts: ["T-wave inversion","ST-segment elevation","Development of an abnormal Q wave","All of the above"]
exp: T-wave inversion, ST elevation, and pathological Q waves are all classic evolving MI changes.

q: Tall-tented T waves and widened QRS are seen in:
opts: ["Hyponatremia","Hyperglycemia","Hyperkalemia","Hyperphosphatemia"]
exp: Tall tented T waves with widened QRS is the classic ECG picture of hyperkalaemia.

q: The depolarization stimulus for the normal heartbeat originates in the:
opts: ["Epicardium","His-bundle areas","Atrioventricular (AV) nodal","Sinoatrial (SA) node"]
exp: The SA node is the heart's natural pacemaker, initiating each normal beat.

q: Inferior wall ischemia produces changes in leads:
opts: ["II","III","aVF","All of the above"]
exp: Leads II, III, and aVF all view the inferior wall of the heart.

q: Which client is at greater risk for respiratory depression while receiving opioids for analgesia?
opts: ["An elderly chronic pain client with a hip fracture","A client with a heroin addiction and back pain","A young female client with advanced multiple myeloma","A child with an arm fracture and cystic fibrosis"]
exp: Chronic lung disease (cystic fibrosis) already compromises respiratory reserve, raising opioid depression risk.

q: The physician has ordered a placebo for a chronic pain client. You are newly hired nurse and you feel very uncomfortable administering the medication. What is the first action that you should take?
opts: ["Prepare the medication and hand it to the physician","Check the hospital policy regarding use of the placebo.","Follow a personal code of ethics and refuse to give it.","Contact the charge nurse for advice."]
exp: Before acting on personal discomfort, check what institutional policy actually says about placebo use.

q: A family member asks you, "Why can't you give more medicine? He is still having a lot of pain." What is your best response?
opts: ["\"The doctor ordered the medicine to be given every 4 hours.\"","\"If the medication is given too frequently he could suffer ill effects.\"","\"Please tell him that I will be right there to check of him.\"","\"Let's wait about 30-40 minutes. If there is no relief I'll call the doctor.\""]
exp: Explaining the safety rationale (risk of harm from over-frequent dosing) is honest and reassuring without overpromising.

q: Which are the Ventilator Parameters adjusted to maintain the optimum Minute Ventilation?
opts: ["FiO2","PEEP","Tidal Volume and Respiratory Rate","Both A and B"]
exp: Minute ventilation = tidal volume × respiratory rate, so these are the two parameters adjusted.

q: The mode of ventilation which allows the patient to breathe spontaneously at his or her own respiratory rate and depth between the ventilator breaths is
opts: ["Controlled Mandatory Ventilation","Synchronous Intermittent Mandatory Ventilation","Assist Control Mode","Pressure Control Mode"]
exp: SIMV allows spontaneous breaths between the set mandatory ventilator breaths.

q: Which of the following agent binds to the poison in the digestive system and prevents its absorption into the blood stream?
opts: ["lead","activated charcoal","sodim bicarbonate","all of the above"]
exp: Activated charcoal adsorbs many poisons in the gut, preventing systemic absorption.

q: In 1965, 1st specialized ICU was established:
opts: ["Medical ICU","Surgical ICU","Coronary Care Unit","Cardiac ICU"]
exp: The Coronary Care Unit is commonly cited as the first specialised critical care unit, established in the 1960s.

q: The unit in which comprehensive care of a critically ill patient which is deemed to recoverable stage is carried out.
opts: ["Critical Care Unit","Critical Care","Coronary Care Unit","Cardiac ICU"]
exp: A dedicated Critical Care Unit provides comprehensive care for critically ill but recoverable patients.

q: Critical Care is provided in the following areas except
opts: ["Accident & Emergency Units","Medical/Surgical ward","High Dependency Units","Intensive Care Units (ICU)"]
exp: A general medical/surgical ward is not a critical care area.

q: The role of Critical Care Nurse is:
opts: ["Care Provider","Educator","Advocate","All of the above"]
exp: Critical care nurses act as care providers, educators, and advocates all at once.

q: Critical care Nurse Role and responsibilities:
opts: ["Negotiating for patients who cannot speak for themselves and who require emergency intervention.","Requires Extensive Knowledge and a continual desire to learn.","Provides comprehensive care to select critically ill patients.","Constant and rapid assessment: close monitoring and respond to treatment."]
exp: Constant, rapid assessment and prompt response to changing status is the defining daily function of a CCN.

q: A 35 year old male is presented to ER with Necrotic Diabetic foot. The physician advised for admission. In which level of care the patient will be admitted?
opts: ["Level 0","Level 1","Level 2","Level 3"]
exp: A serious but single, localised problem needing close observation (not organ support) fits Level 1 care.

q: Which one of the following is critical care technology?
opts: ["Blood Pressure monitoring","Vital Signs","Ventilator","Medications"]
exp: A ventilator is a piece of life-support technology specific to critical care.

q: It is the most important component in Critical Care Nursing:
opts: ["Problem Solving","Decision making","Critical Thinking","Interpersonal relationship"]
exp: Critical thinking underlies every safe decision a critical care nurse makes.

q: The Critical Care Patient is:
opts: ["Physiologically Unstable","At Risk for Serious Complications","Require Intensive and complicated nursing support and/or advanced technology","All of the above"]
exp: All three features (instability, risk, need for advanced support) define a critical care patient.

q: Which one of the following is NOT a goal of Critical Care?
opts: ["Towards the survival of the critical ill patients and restoring quality of life","Restoring optimal physiological, psychological, social and spiritual potential","Helping the families of the critically ill patients in coping with crises","To manipulate the patient and the family"]
exp: Manipulating the patient/family is unethical and never a stated goal of critical care.

q: Defibrillation is used in:
opts: ["Pulseless Ventricular tachycardia & Ventricular fibrillation","Supra ventricular Tachycardia(SVT)","Pulseless Electrical activity(PEA)","First degree AV block"]
exp: Defibrillation is used for the two shockable rhythms: pulseless VT and VF.

q: In BLS survey ABCD, D stands for:
opts: ["Differential diagnosis","Defibrillation","Disability","Distal to Proximal"]
exp: In the ABCD survey, D stands for Defibrillation.

q: The depth of chest compressions will be:
opts: ["1 inch","2 inches","3 inches","4 inches"]
exp: Adult chest compressions should be about 2 inches (5cm) deep.

q: The monophasic shock energy is used:
exp: Monophasic defibrillators typically need higher energy, commonly cited around 200-300J (often fixed at 360J).

q: The drugs which are used during Basic Life Support (BLS):
opts: ["Epinephrine and Amiodarone","Atropine and Amiodarone","Atropine and Epinephrine","Epinephrine and Vasopressin"]
exp: Epinephrine and amiodarone are the standard ACLS arrest drugs referenced here.

q: Which one of the following drug is administered every 3-5 minutes of CPR?
opts: ["Epinephrine","Atropine","Amiodarone","Vasopressin"]
exp: Epinephrine is re-dosed every 3-5 minutes during resuscitation.

q: The required dose of Amiodarone in Refractory VF and Pulseless VT:
exp: The standard first dose of amiodarone in refractory VF/pulseless VT is 300mg IV.

q: When the delivery button is pushed, there will be a slight delay in firing because the machine is sensing the R wave in order to deliver the energy at the precise moment. This phenomenon is known as:
opts: ["Cardio Version","Synchronized Cardio Version","A Synchronized Cardio Version","Defibrillation"]
exp: Timing the shock to the R wave to avoid the vulnerable period is synchronized cardioversion.

q: Which of the following is NOT a reversible cause of Cardiac Arrest?
opts: ["Hypovolemia","Hypoxia","Hypertension","Tension pneumothorax"]
exp: Hypertension is not part of the reversible 4H/4T causes of arrest; the others (hypovolaemia, hypoxia, tension pneumothorax) are.

q: For wide regular rhythm the quantity of Energy in Joules is:
exp: A wide, regular tachycardia is typically cardioverted starting at 100J.

q: The vasoconstrictor effect of epinephrine is by:
opts: ["Increasing heart rate","Binding directly to alpha-1 adrenergic receptors of the blood vessels (arteries and veins)","Increasing heart muscle contractility","Increasing conductivity through the AV node"]
exp: Epinephrine constricts vessels by directly binding alpha-1 receptors on vascular smooth muscle.

q: The following drug is Second-line treatment for cardiogenic shock
opts: ["Epinephrine","Amiodarone","Deltacortil","Vasopression"]
exp: After first-line dobutamine/dopamine, epinephrine is commonly used as a second-line agent in cardiogenic shock.

q: First drug used to treat bradycardia:
opts: ["Epinephrine","Amiodarone","Deltacortil","Atropine"]
exp: Atropine is the first drug given for symptomatic bradycardia.

q: A 24 year old male is presented to ER with head injury. The patient has Tracheostomy intact. Heart Rate is below 50/min.Physician advised to administer Atropine 1 mg immediately. The only route of administering medication is Trachea now. Along with Atropine what other medications can be administered through Trachea;
opts: ["Amiodarone,","NaHCO3","Calcium","Adrenaline"]
exp: Endotracheal drugs follow "NAVEL" — adrenaline (epinephrine) can be given via the ET route along with atropine.

q: It is the monitoring of the concentration or partial pressure of carbon dioxide (CO2) in the respiratory gases.
opts: ["Capnography","Spirometry","Rebreathing","Oximetry"]
exp: Capnography monitors exhaled CO2 levels continuously.

q: Which of the following is ethical issue during CPR:
opts: ["Trained healthcare providers","Consent for Treatment","Family presence during CPR","Refusal and discontinuation of treatment"]
exp: Family presence during resuscitation is one of the most debated ethical issues in critical care.

q: Slow or Chronic onset disaster includes the following:
opts: ["Famine","Water","Vector-borne diseases","Person-to-person transmission diseases"]
exp: Famine develops gradually over time, unlike sudden-onset disasters like earthquakes.

q: South Asia, natural disaster (Earth Quake) which primarily affected Pakistan in which of the following year:
exp: The major Pakistan earthquake occurred in 2005.

q: In 2015, Flood has washed most of the Agricultural land in Chitral District of Khyber Pakhtoonkha (KP). Till now crops do not grow on the land. This phenomenon well described as:
opts: ["Deforestation","Desertification","Environmental degradation","Famine"]
exp: Long-term loss of land productivity after a disaster reflects broader environmental degradation.

q: For the victims of Army Public School (APS) Peshawar, Psychotherapy and counselling sessions were arranged for them. The purpose of this intervention was:
opts: ["To decrease anxiety level","To prevent from Post-Traumatic Stress Disorder","To facilitate in recovery","To avoid further deterioration"]
exp: Early counselling after mass trauma is specifically aimed at preventing PTSD from developing.

q: While working in disaster situation, the first thing which is to be encountered is:
opts: ["Make safety the first priority","Closely observe patient and family members in the event","Documentation of consent","Assess the patient and family for psychological function"]
exp: Scene/personal safety always comes first before any patient care in a disaster.

q: Below are the Patient and family-focused interventions EXCEPT
opts: ["Relieve anxiety and provide a sense of security","Allow family to stay with patient, if possible, to alleviate anxiety","Provide explanations and information","Documentation of consent"]
exp: Documenting consent is an administrative task, not a direct emotional-support intervention for patient/family.

q: Sorts patients by hierarchy based on the severity of health problems and the immediacy with which these problems must be treated.
opts: ["Triage","Tagging","Labelling","Emergency Severity Index"]
exp: Sorting patients by severity/urgency is the definition of triage.

q: The purpose of disaster planning is:
opts: ["To minimize the resulting loss of property, injuries, suffering and death that accompanies a disaster","Large number of people with different severity levels","Rapidly declining survival rates","Narrow window of opportunity for salvaged"]
exp: Disaster planning exists to minimise loss of life, injury, and property damage.

q: Four color code system is used in Disaster Triage, Red color among them represents:
opts: ["Medium priority","Ambulatory patients","High priority patients","Dead patients"]
exp: Red designates the highest-priority, most critical patients in the four-colour triage system.

q: In Triage Technique acronym START is used in which 'R' stands for:
opts: ["Rapid","Regular","Repeat","Rest"]
exp: START stands for Simple Triage And Rapid Treatment — the "R" is Rapid.

q: The volume of blood in the ventricles just before they contract. It denotes the overall fluid status of the body.
opts: ["Preload","After load","Cardiac Output","Contractility"]
exp: Preload is the ventricular volume just before contraction.

q: It is a flexible catheter, inserted into the femoral artery and passed into the descending aorta 1-2 cm below the left subclavian artery and slightly above the renal arteries.
opts: ["Pulmonary Artery Wedge Pressure","Pulmonary Artery Catheter","Intra-aortic balloon pump","None of the above"]
exp: This femoral-to-descending-aorta catheter positioned near the left subclavian is the IABP.

q: Which one of the following is the contraindication of Intra-aortic balloon pump insertion?
opts: ["Limb ischemia","Aortic dissection","Aortic aneurysm","Hematoma"]
exp: Aortic dissection is an absolute contraindication to IABP — inflation could worsen the tear.

q: The concept of triage, shock units and emergency Nursing was introduced in the following era:
opts: ["WWII & Korean War","Crimean War& WWII","WWI & Korean War","WWI&WWII"]
exp: Modern triage, shock units, and emergency nursing concepts are widely traced to WWII and the Korean War.

q: 1st specialized ICU (Coronary Care Unit) was established in:
exp: The first Coronary Care Unit is commonly dated to the mid-1960s.

q: A Nurse is counselling the family of a client with Road Traffic Accident (RTA). The family is screaming by saying that the client will be recovered or not? The following principles are important in Critical Care Nursing EXCEPT
opts: ["Anticipation","Early detection and prompt action","Collaborative practice","Stress elevation"]
exp: Deliberately elevating stress contradicts every principle of supportive critical care.

q: The Nurse in the ICU is providing food to the patient. The patient is unable to take it by his own. Here the Nurse is performing the role of:
opts: ["Comprehensive care provider","Care Provider","Educator","Advocate"]
exp: Physically feeding a patient who cannot feed themselves is direct hands-on care — the Care Provider role.

q: The client came to the Emergency Department with a complaint of uncontrolled Blood sugar for the last one week. The blood sugar checked on arrival which the Glucometer showed HI. The physician advised admission for further evaluation. In which level of care the patient will be admitted:
opts: ["Level 0","Level 1","Level 2","Level 3"]
exp: A single acute metabolic problem needing close monitoring fits Level 1 care.

q: A 45 years old male came to the ER with the complaint of Anuria for almost 10-15 hours. The client says that this condition suddenly happened to him. This condition is well defined as:
opts: ["Multiple Organ Failure","Organ dysfunction","Single organ failure","Acute Renal failure(ARF)"]
exp: Sudden cessation of urine output over hours is classic Acute Renal Failure.

q: It is a multidisciplinary health care specialty that is meant for patients with acute life threatening illness or injury:
opts: ["Critical care nursing","Critical care","Intensive care","All of the above"]
exp: "Critical care" is the general multidisciplinary specialty term being defined here.

q: Hemodynamic pressure monitoring provides information about the following parameters:
opts: ["Blood volume and fluid balance","Blood Volume and Heart Rate","Blood Volume and Stroke Volume","Fluid balance and Stroke Volume"]
exp: Hemodynamic monitoring reflects the patient's circulating blood volume and fluid balance status.

q: A client presented to ER with chest pain and excessive diaphrosis. The patient is diagnosed with myocardial infarction. The physician advises insertion of Central pressure monitoring. The purpose of central pressure monitoring in this case is:
opts: ["To assess the loss of cardiac function Volume is raised","The circulating Blood Volume is decreased","The circulating Blood Volume is normal","None of the above"]
exp: In MI, central pressure monitoring helps assess how well the heart is handling volume/function.

q: The following are the non-invasive method of hemodynamic monitoring EXCEPT
opts: ["Central venous pressure monitoring","NIBP","ECG","ECHO"]
exp: CVP monitoring requires an invasive central line, unlike NIBP, ECG, or ECHO.

q: A 55 year old male came to the ER with the complaint of severe Shortness of Breath. The physician advised for ABG's. The result was pH=7.20, PCO2=50, HCO3=24, PO2=80.A BSN student is looking at the values and makes the conclusion as:
opts: ["Respiratory Acidosis","Respiratory Alkalosis","Metabolic Acidosis","Metabolic Alkalosis"]
exp: Low pH with a high PCO2 and normal HCO3 is a respiratory acidosis.

q: Refer to Q#11 The Physician advised to monitor the Arterial Blood Pressure. Keep in mind the above scenario, what is the indication of inserting Arterial line:
opts: ["Patient is on inotropic drugs","Patient is on vasoactive drug","Patient requires frequent arterial blood sampling","All of the above"]
exp: Inotropes, vasoactive drugs, and frequent sampling needs are all standard reasons for arterial line placement.

q: Which of the following is NOT an indication of Central lines:
opts: ["Fluid resuscitation","Parenteral feeding","Measurement of central venous pressure","To monitor Blood Pressure"]
exp: Blood pressure is monitored via an arterial line, not the primary purpose of a central line.

q: The normal value of CVP is:
opts: ["5 to 10 cm H2O","6 to 8 cm H2O","8 to 10 cm H2O","7 to 8 cm H2O"]
exp: Normal CVP is generally cited as roughly 5-10 cmH2O.

q: The Nurse observes on the manometer that the value of CVP is 4 cm of H2O.The Nurse interpret it as following:
opts: ["The circulating Blood Volume is raised","The circulating Blood Volume is decreased","The circulating Blood Volume is normal","None of the above"]
exp: A CVP of 4 (below the normal range) suggests reduced circulating volume.

q: A serious disruption of the functioning of a society, causing widespread human, material or environment losses which exceed the ability of the affected society to cope with its own resources is called:
opts: ["Natural disaster","Man-made disaster","Disaster","Material Disaster"]
exp: This is the standard definition of a disaster.

q: Which one of the following is included in man-made disaster?
opts: ["Earth quake","Floods","Tsunami","Wars"]
exp: War is a human-caused (man-made) disaster, unlike the natural events listed.

q: In a Road Traffic Accident a large number of causalities came to the ER and most of them were independent and ambulatory. Which color code will the ER Nurse use for these patients:
opts: ["Red","Yellow","Green","Black"]
exp: Walking, stable casualties are triaged Green (minor/delayed).

q: A 65 year old female is put on Ventilator due to acute respiratory Failure (ARF).The ABG's result tell the following findings, PH=7.5,PCO2=50,HCO3=30,PO2=85.What would be the more likely diagnosis:
opts: ["Partial compensation","Complete compensation","No compensation","Both a & b"]
exp: pH still abnormal (7.5) despite compensatory changes in PCO2/HCO3 means only partial compensation has occurred.

q: Volume of air inspired or expired by a person from lungs in a minute:
opts: ["Tidal Volume","Minute Volume","Inspiratory Volume","Expiratory Volume"]
exp: Total air moved per minute is minute volume (tidal volume × respiratory rate).

q: 1. Which One of the following is NOT a cause of acute ventilation failure?
opts: ["Neurological impairment","Neuromuscular impairment","Primary respiratory impairment","Appendectomy"]
exp: An appendectomy itself doesn't directly cause acute ventilatory failure, unlike neurological, neuromuscular, or primary respiratory problems.

q: The volume of air inspired or expired in a single breath during regular breathing is called:
opts: ["Minute Volume","Tidal Volume","Stroke Volume","Respiratory Volume"]
exp: The volume of a single normal breath is tidal volume.

q: A 24 year old male patient is brought to the Emergency by his family with head injury. Initial survey was done by the Nurse in Triage. The Nurse informed physician that he has no respiratory efforts. Which mode of the ventilation will be used for this patient?
opts: ["Pressure controlled","Volume controlled","Assist Control Ventilation","Synchronized Intermittent Mandatory Ventilation (SIMV)"]
exp: With zero respiratory effort, Assist-Control ensures every breath is fully supported with a guaranteed backup rate.

q: The physician wrote order to wean off the patient from ventilator. The Nurse will adjust the ventilator setting to which of the following mode:
opts: ["Continuous Positive Airway Pressure (CPAP)","Assist Control Ventilation","Synchronized Intermittent Mandatory Ventilation (SIMV)","Pressure controlled"]
exp: SIMV is the classic mode used to gradually wean patients off full ventilator support.

q: A 20 year old male patient is put on Ventilator for 15 days due to no respiratory efforts. While doing the assessment, the Nurse observes that there is a sore on oral mucosa. Including this, what are the other complications of ventilator that the Nurse will notify?
opts: ["Baro-trauma","Tracheal damage","Aspiration","All of the above"]
exp: Oral sores, barotrauma, tracheal damage, and aspiration are all recognised ventilator-related complications.

q: Measurement of pressure, flow and oxygenation of blood within the cardiovascular system is called:
opts: ["Hemodynamics","Hemodynamic Monitoring","Hemodynamic pressure","None of the above"]
exp: Measuring pressure, flow, and oxygenation within the cardiovascular system is hemodynamic monitoring.

q: Any emergency that disrupts normal community function causing concern for the safety of its citizens including their lives and property.
opts: ["Manmade Disaster","Natural Disaster","Disaster","Flood"]
exp: This describes a disaster — an emergency overwhelming normal community function.

q: What is the role of a triage Nurse?
opts: ["The triage nurse collects data and classifies the illnesses and injuries to ensure that the patients most in need of care do not needlessly wait.","Systematic approach to manage emergent or urgent situations.","ED triage differs from disaster triage in that patients who are the most critically ill receive the most resources, regardless of potential outcome.","Provide additional interventions depending upon the stage of crisis."]
exp: The triage nurse's core job is prioritising care so the most urgent patients aren't kept waiting.

q: Which one of the following is NOT patient and family focused intervention:
opts: ["Relieve anxiety and provide a sense of security","Allow family to stay with patient, if possible, to alleviate anxiety","Provide explanations and information","Documentation of consent"]
exp: Documenting consent is administrative, not an emotional/support-focused family intervention.

q: Which is not the stage of shock?
opts: ["Progressive","Compensatory","Initial","Reversible"]
exp: The standard shock stages are initial, compensatory, progressive, and irreversible — "reversible" isn't one of them.

q: Which is NOT the type of shock?
opts: ["Cardiogenic","Hypovolemic","Anaphylactic","Aseptic"]
exp: Septic shock is real; "aseptic shock" is not a recognised type.

q: What is the primary cause of septic shock?
opts: ["Bleeding","Medication allergy","Infection(Blood)","Poison"]
exp: Septic shock is driven by infection (usually bloodstream).

q: What is the preferred treatment for anaphylactic shock?
opts: ["Epinephrine","Place the patient in sitting position and administer oxygen","Prevent the infection from occurring through patient teaching","Place a bag of ice on the area"]
exp: Epinephrine is the first-line, life-saving treatment for anaphylaxis.

q: What class of drug typically used to treat neurogenic shock?
opts: ["Beta blocker","Calcium channel blocker","Loop diuretics","Vasopressor"]
exp: Vasopressors are used to restore vascular tone lost in neurogenic shock.

q: What is the treatment of choice for hypovolemic shock?
opts: ["Have the patient drink a large bottle of water","Replace fluids intravenously as quickly as possible","Administer a vasopressor","Give epinephrine"]
exp: Rapid IV fluid replacement is the priority treatment for hypovolaemic shock.

q: Which of the following fluid volume loss is considered to be absolute hypovolemic shock?
opts: ["Ascites","Burns","GI bleed","Bowel obstruction"]
exp: GI bleeding represents true (absolute) loss of blood volume from the body.

q: Which is the most common type of shock?
opts: ["Cardiogenic","Hypovolemic","Anaphylactic","Septic"]
exp: Hypovolaemic shock is the most commonly encountered type overall.

q: Shock results from?
opts: ["Decreased blood flow to tissues","Increased blood flow to tissues","Adequate perfusion","All of the above"]
exp: Shock fundamentally results from inadequate blood flow/perfusion to tissues.

q: Which one of the following is NOT the cause of cardiogenic shock?
opts: ["CHF","Cardiomyopathy","MI","Spinal Cord Injury"]
exp: Spinal cord injury causes neurogenic shock, not cardiogenic shock.

q: In the state of shock, the intracellular environment becomes;
opts: ["Basic","Acidic","Alkaline","Both a & b"]
exp: Anaerobic metabolism during shock produces lactic acid, making cells acidic.

q: In the distributive shock, there occurs vasodilation that leads to a decrease in systemic vascular resistance which ultimately leads to the decrease in the following?
opts: ["Cardiac output(CO)","Stroke volume(SV)","Blood pressure(BP)","Mean arterial pressure(MAP)"]
exp: Falling SVR directly lowers mean arterial pressure (MAP = CO × SVR).

q: Physician has advised the patient to go for chest X-ray as the patient was in Shock. The purpose of doing X-ray in those patients is to confirm which type of shock?
opts: ["Hypovolemic shock","Obstructive shock","Cardiogenic shock","Hypovolemic shock"]
exp: Chest X-ray findings like pulmonary oedema/cardiomegaly help confirm cardiogenic shock.

q: A Nurse Asia Bano in the ICU at RMI taking care of a patient with shock. She is doing special monitoring of the patient. What elements come in the special monitoring of shock?
opts: ["Central venous pressure(CVP)","Cardiac output","Pulmonary catheter","All of the above"]
exp: CVP, cardiac output, and pulmonary artery catheter readings are all part of specialised shock monitoring.

q: An escape of blood from a blood vessel is called?
opts: ["Infection","Hemorrhage","Shock","Inflammation"]
exp: Blood escaping from a vessel is, by definition, haemorrhage.

q: You saw a person having cut in the right lower arm which is more in speed and volume. What actions will you take as a Student Nurse in the order?
opts: ["Direct pressure, pressure point, elevation, tourniquet","Direct pressure, elevation, pressure point","Elevation, direct pressure, pressure point, tourniquet","Direct pressure, pressure bandage, tourniquet"]
exp: Direct pressure first, then elevation, then a pressure point if bleeding continues, is the standard escalation before considering a tourniquet.

q: When a blood vessel is damaged, the first mechanism that happens to reduce the blood loss and maintain the homeostasis of the vessel.
opts: ["Increase peripheral perfusion","Vasoconstriction","Increase cerebral perfusion","Vasodilation"]
exp: Vasoconstriction is the body's immediate reflex to limit blood loss from an injured vessel.

q: A 14 year old boy came to the ER with epistaxis. According to the classification of the hemorrhage, epistaxis falls in which category?
opts: ["Internal Hemorrhage","Arterial Hemorrhage","External Hemorrhage","Venous Hemorrhage"]
exp: A nosebleed is visible, external bleeding.

q: A 23 year old young adult presented to ER Road Traffic accident (RTA).He has poly traumas and massive bleeding. The characteristic of the blood is dark red in color and steady in flow. Which type of hemorrhage is this?
opts: ["Internal Hemorrhage","Arterial Hemorrhage","External Hemorrhage","Venous Hemorrhage"]
exp: Dark red, steady-flowing blood (not bright red and spurting) indicates venous bleeding.

q: Refer to Q#19, the patient had undergone through craniotomy. Bleeding was notified on CT scan after one week of surgery which is known as;
opts: ["Primary Hemorrhage","Reactionary Hemorrhage","Secondary Hemorrhage","Venous Hemorrhage"]
exp: Bleeding appearing a week after surgery is delayed — secondary haemorrhage, usually from infection.

q: Hemorrhage in which there is a loss of blood from 500-1000 ml.
opts: ["Mild Hemorrhage","Moderate Hemorrhage","Severe Hemorrhage","Primary Hemorrhage"]
exp: A 500-1000ml loss is classified as moderate haemorrhage.

q: A Nurse in the ER observes that the patient has lost 100 ml of blood in 4 hours. According to the classification of hemorrhage it is;
opts: ["Acute Hemorrhage","Chronic hemorrhage","Primary Hemorrhage","Secondary Hemorrhage"]
exp: A small, slow ongoing loss over hours reflects chronic, low-grade bleeding.

q: A 20 year male presented to ER with motor bike accident. The Nurse is performing initial assessment of the patient in triage and finds out that the patient has delayed capillary refill, Cool, Moist skin, Decreasing blood pressure and increasing heart rate. What is the suspected medical diagnose of this patient?
opts: ["Shock","Hemorrhage","Acute Renal Failure(ARF)","Hypotension"]
exp: Cool moist skin, delayed cap refill, falling BP, rising HR together describe shock.

q: Refer to Q#23, what will be the actual Nursing diagnose for the above patient?
opts: ["Hypothermia related to ineffective tissue perfusion evidenced by hypotension","Risk for deficient fluid volume relate to large amount of blood loss evidenced by trauma","Ineffective tissue perfusion related to hypotension evidenced by excessive blood loss.","Risk for infection related to related to compromised circulation as evidence by blood loss"]
exp: The pathophysiology-matched diagnosis links hypotension directly to impaired tissue perfusion from blood loss.

q: The systemic agents which are used in hemorrhage is;
opts: ["Whole Blood","Platelet Rich Plasma","Cryoprecipitat.","All of the above"]
exp: Whole blood, platelet-rich plasma, and cryoprecipitate are all systemic products used to control haemorrhage.

q: A serious disruption of the functioning of a society, causing widespread human, material or environment losses which exceed the ability of the affected society to cope with its own resources.
opts: ["Natural disaster","Manmade disaster","Disaster","None of the above"]
exp: This is the standard definition of a disaster.

q: Which is TRUE about disaster?
opts: ["Disasters interrupt normal functioning of a community","Disasters exceed the coping mechanisms of the community","External assistance is needed to return to normal functioning of a community","All of the above"]
exp: All three statements accurately describe what defines a disaster.

q: A 37 year old male presented to the ER with chest pain, radiating to the left arm and diaphoresis. Upon history taking patient says that he has this problem for almost 3-4 hours and he has arrived here after a long drive. Assessing the patient's condition, the Emergency room Nurse diagnosis this patient as:
opts: ["Highly urgent","Urgent","Critically ill","Emergent"]
exp: Ongoing chest pain radiating with diaphoresis is an emergent, life-threatening presentation.

q: A Nurse in the ICU was looking at the cardiac monitor and observed that the patient is going in bradycardia and for that she was looking for prompt intervention. Including early identification of the patient condition, what are the Nurses responsibility working in ICU.
opts: ["Comprehensive Nursing Care","Initial Assessment","Maintain ABC","All of the above"]
exp: Comprehensive care, initial assessment, and maintaining ABCs are all core ICU nurse responsibilities.

q: The physician instructed the Nurse in the ICU to run a slow code as the family was not agreed for DNR. The approach used here to help the patient and family in the end of life is termed as:
opts: ["DNR","Life Support","Ethical Dilemma","Spiritual support"]
exp: Performing a deliberately less-aggressive code due to family/DNR disagreement is a classic ethical dilemma in critical care.

q: All of the following are the result of the sudden or acute impact of the disaster EXCEPT
opts: ["Food","Water","Famine","Vector-borne diseases"]
exp: Famine develops slowly over time and is not a sudden/acute disaster consequence.

q: Which one of the following is the common psychological disorder post disasters?
opts: ["Post-traumatic stress disorder(PTSD)","Depression","Psychosis","Neurosis"]
exp: PTSD is the most commonly cited psychological disorder following disaster exposure.

q: A 35 year old male came to ER with chest pain radiating to the left arm, diaphoresis and SOB. After assessment the triage Nurse puts the patient into the category of;
opts: ["Emergent","Urgent","Non urgent","Less urgent"]
exp: Classic cardiac chest pain symptoms place the patient in the emergent category.

q: In the mass causality post-earthquake in Balakot area of Pakistan in 2005.All the health volunteers from different health sector collectively categorizing patients based on the severity of injury with different color code. The color code Black represents;
opts: ["Minor injury","Deceased","Ambulatory","High priority"]
exp: Black designates deceased/expectant patients in mass-casualty triage.

q: It is the Nursing specialty in which Nurses care for patients in the emergency or critical phase of their illness or injury is called;
opts: ["Critical care Nursing","Emergency Nursing","Adult health Nursing","Triage Nursing"]
exp: Critical care nursing covers patients in the emergency or critical phase of illness broadly.

q: Amount of blood pumped by each side of the heart in one minute is called;
opts: ["Stroke volume","Cardiac output","Cardiac cycle","None of them"]
exp: The volume of blood pumped by the heart each minute is cardiac output.

q: A patient who is admitted in ICU with unstable hemodynamics, Arterial line catheter is passed for invasive blood pressure monitoring. His/her systolic pressure is 110mm of hg and diastolic pressure is 80 mm of hg. What would be the Mean Arterial Pressure (MAP)?
opts: ["73 mm of hg","83 mm of hg","50 mm of hg","90 mm of hg"]
exp: MAP = DBP + 1/3(SBP-DBP) = 80 + 1/3(30) = 90mmHg.

q: Which test is performed in order to check collateral circulation, before going to pass arterial line catheter in radial artery;
opts: ["Romberg test","Weber test","Whisper test","Allen test"]
exp: The Allen test checks ulnar collateral circulation before radial artery puncture.

q: 20, PCO2 55 mm hg, PO2 65 mm hg, HCO3 22 m mol. What does this mean?
opts: ["Respiratory alkalosis","Metabolic alkalosis","Respiratory acidosis","Metabolic acidosis"]
exp: Acidic pH with elevated PCO2 and roughly normal HCO3 indicates respiratory acidosis.

q: The purpose of inserting central venous pressure (CVP) into the major vein of body such as jugular vein, subclavian vein, and femoral is to monitor the pressure in;
opts: ["Right ventricle","Pulmonary artery","Left ventricle","Left atrium"]
exp: A CVP line placed via the great veins primarily reflects right ventricular filling pressure.

q: Central venous catheter line can be used for all of the following EXCEPT.
opts: ["Administer fluid","Administer medication","Obtain blood sample for lab investigations","Obtain blood for ABGs"]
exp: A central line samples venous, not arterial, blood — true ABGs require an arterial sample.

q: The nurse is checking the client's central venous pressure (CVP). The Nurse should place the zero of the manometer at the:
opts: ["Phlebostatic axis","PMI","Erb's point","Tail of Spence"]
exp: The phlebostatic axis is the standard reference (zero) point for CVP measurement.

q: Which one of the following is NOT a function of pulmonary arterial catheter (PAC) which is also called Swan GANZ catheter?
opts: ["Continuous cardiac output monitoring","Measurement of pulmonary artery pressure","Estimation of diastole filling of left ventricle","Measurement of right atrium pressure"]
exp: Right atrial pressure is more a CVP-line function; the PAC's defining roles are CO, PA pressure, and PCWP.

q: A patient who has severe shortness of breath(SOB) and his/her ABGs shows high level of carbon dioxide (CO2) 55mm hg and low level of (Sao2) 40mmhg, which shows type II respiratory failure. Which Approach is best to treat the patient?
opts: ["Delivering Oxygen through face mask","Delivering oxygen through CPAP machine","Delivering oxygen through BIPAP machine","None of them"]
exp: BiPAP delivers both inspiratory pressure support and PEEP, ideal for hypercapnic (Type II) respiratory failure.

q: The amount of pressure remaining in the lungs at the end of expiratory phase is;
opts: ["Residual volume","Expiratory pressure","Inspiratory pressure","Positive end expiratory pressure (PEEP)"]
exp: Pressure remaining in the lungs at end-expiration is PEEP.

q: If a patient has respiratory rate of 15B/M and tidal volume is 500ml.what would be the minute ventilation (MV)?
exp: Minute ventilation = 15 × 500ml = 7,500ml.

q: In which mode of ventilator the patient will initiate his/her own breath and does not depend on ventilator. Ventilator will only assess the patient efforts.
opts: ["AC mood","SIMV mood","Spontaneous mood","VCV mood"]
exp: In spontaneous mode, the patient triggers and controls their own breaths; the ventilator just supports/monitors.

q: FIO2 in ventilator means;
opts: ["Fraction of oxygen","Fraction of inspired oxygen","Fraction of expired oxygen","All are correct"]
exp: FiO2 stands for Fraction of Inspired Oxygen.

q: Arterial blood gases (ABGs) show respiratory alkalosis, and CO2 is very low that is 25 mm of Hg while on ventilator setting respiratory rate is set at 22b/m. What would you do with respiratory rate?
opts: ["Decrease respiratory rate","Increase respiratory rate","Do not change the rate","None of them"]
exp: With CO2 already too low (alkalosis), reducing the respiratory rate lets CO2 rise back toward normal.

q: Volume of air during normal breath is;
opts: ["Minute volume","Residual volume","Inspiratory reserve volume","Tidal volume"]
exp: Normal single-breath volume is tidal volume.

q: The normal value of physiological PEEP is;
exp: Physiological PEEP (to mimic normal airway pressure) is about 3-5 cmH2O.

q: While monitoring during ventilator support, which one of the following is NOT a responsibility of nurse;
opts: ["Check any indications of air leak","Do suctioning if needed","Arterial blood gas measurement","Change ventilator setting frequently"]
exp: Changing ventilator settings requires a physician/RT order, not independent nurse action.

q: The adverse effect of mechanical ventilator is;
opts: ["Cardiovascular effects","Pulmonary effects","Renal effects","All are correct"]
exp: Mechanical ventilation can affect the cardiovascular, pulmonary, and renal systems.

q: Is initiated by the patient, but controlled and ended by the ventilator.
opts: ["Mandatory breath","Assisted breath","Spontaneous breath","None of them"]
exp: A breath the patient starts but the ventilator finishes/controls is an assisted breath.

q: Which are the Ventilator Parameters adjusted to maintain the optimum oxygenation?
opts: ["FiO2 and PEEP","Tidal volume","Respiratory rate","Support pressure"]
exp: FiO2 and PEEP are the two parameters primarily adjusted to optimise oxygenation.

q: Disadvantages of endotracheal intubation includes all of the following EXCEPT
opts: ["Loss of the protective function of the upper airway","Loss of phonation (voice)","Damage to the subglottic area","Decreased airway resistance"]
exp: ETT actually increases (not decreases) airway resistance due to the narrow tube — that's the false statement.

q: Stretch of ventricular wall. Usually related to volume. (How full is the tank?)
opts: ["Afterload","Systolic pressure","Preload","Diastole"]
exp: Stretch of the ventricular wall related to filling volume is preload.

q: Which of the following drugs will decrease contractility and heart rate?
opts: ["Beta blockers","ACE inhibitors","Diuretics","Vasopressors"]
exp: Beta blockers reduce both heart rate and contractility.

q: A patient comes with severe chest pain, radiating to jaw and shoulder. His ECG shows ST elevations in lead II, lead III and lead AVF. What does it show?
opts: ["Posterior MI","Anterior MI","Septal MI","Inferior MI"]
exp: ST elevation in II, III, and aVF localises to the inferior wall.

q: Emergency venous access, volume resuscitation, nutritional support, administration of inotropes, and monitoring of central venous pressure is achieved by;
opts: ["Arterial line catheter","Central venous catheter","Double lumen catheter","Pulmonary arterial catheter"]
exp: A central venous catheter provides all these functions — access, resuscitation, nutrition, inotropes, and CVP monitoring.

q: What is the normal value of CVP in Millimeter of water?
opts: ["2-8 mm of H2o","2-5 mm of H2o","2-3 mm of H2o","2-4 mm of H2o"]
exp: Normal CVP is commonly cited as about 2-8 (mmHg, approximated here in the given unit).

q: Hemodynamic monitoring consist of;
opts: ["Invasive","Non invasive","Both of them","None of above"]
exp: Hemodynamic monitoring includes both invasive (arterial/CVP/PAC) and non-invasive (BP cuff, ECG) methods.

q: A Pulmonary Artery (PA) catheter has just been inserted. To confirm the placement, which one of the following is best action?
opts: ["Flush the line with Inj. Heparin","Place the bulky dressing over the insertion site","Call for chest X-Ray","Draw 30ml of blood back through the catheter to check patency"]
exp: A chest X-ray confirms correct catheter tip placement after insertion.

q: What position should the nurse place the head of the bed in order to obtain the most accurate reading of jugular vein pressure (JVP)?
opts: ["Raised 90 degrees","Raised 10 degrees","Raised 30 degrees","Supine position"]
exp: JVP is best assessed with the head of bed raised to about 30 degrees.

q: Which gas is used to inflate balloon during diastole in Intra-aortic Balloon pump (IABP) machine?
opts: ["Helium","Hydrogen","Chlorine","Iodine"]
exp: Helium is used because its low viscosity allows very rapid balloon inflation/deflation, and it's safer if it embolises.

q: The placement of Intra-aortic balloon is;
