opts: ["Ascending aorta","Descending aorta","Proximal aorta","Thoracic aorta"]
exp: The IABP sits in the descending aorta.

q: During inflation of Intra-aortic balloon the blood moves toward;
opts: ["Body and coronary arteries","Only upper extremities","Lower extremities","Whole body"]
exp: Balloon inflation during diastole pushes blood toward the body and augments coronary artery flow.

q: Which of the following is NOT a hemodynamic effects and benefits of intra-aortic balloon pump (IABP);
opts: ["Increase cardiac output","Decrease o2 demand","Increase coronary flow","Increase ventricular wall tension"]
exp: IABP reduces (not increases) ventricular wall tension — that is one of its therapeutic benefits.

q: During cardiac arrest, which mode of intra-aortic balloon pump (IABP) can provide very effective perfusion in conjunction with external compression?
opts: ["ECG","Pressure","Internal","Pacer A"]
exp: During CPR, ECG is unreliable due to compression artifact, so the IABP is switched to a pressure trigger.

q: A nurse is assessing Blood pressure of the patient, she knows that the invasive method of measuring Blood pressure is:
opts: ["Blood pressure cuff","Arterial line","Cardiac Monitor","PA catheter"]
exp: An arterial line is the invasive method of continuous blood pressure measurement.

q: A nurse is assisting in the procedure of CVP line insertion. Which of the following site is used for CVP line insertion:
opts: ["Carotid artery","Internal Jugular Vein","External Jugular Vein","Pulmonary vein"]
exp: The internal jugular vein is a standard, commonly used CVP line insertion site.

q: Mr. Amir (RN in Cardiac ICU) obtained cardiac output of a patient having 100 kg weight and report to the head nurse that the cardiac output is 3.5 Liter per minute. To confirm, whether the cardiac output is normal or abnormal, a more accurate measurement used is:
opts: ["Stroke volume","Heart rate","PCWP","Cardiac Index"]
exp: Cardiac index adjusts cardiac output for body size, giving a more accurate individual assessment.

q: Mr. Amir (RN in Cardiac ICU) obtained cardiac output of a patient and report to the head nurse that the cardiac output is 2.5 Liter per minute. Based upon the cardiac output, the nurse would anticipate the use of which of the following measure:
opts: ["Give fluid @ 50 mL per hour","Administer Furosemide","Start Dobutamine infusion","None of the above"]
exp: A low cardiac output (2.5L/min) suggests poor contractility, prompting an inotrope like dobutamine.

q: Which of the following measurement is used to assess cardiac contractility:
opts: ["CVP","SVR","PCWP","CO (Cardiac Output)"]
exp: Cardiac output is the standard bedside surrogate used to reflect overall contractile performance.

q: Preload can be measured by which of following:
opts: ["CVP","SVR","PA pressure","Cardiac Output"]
exp: CVP is the standard clinical surrogate for preload.

q: Mr. Noor (RN in Cardiac ICU) obtained CVP of a patient and report to the head nurse that CVP is currently 2. Based upon the value, which of the following measure should be taken:
opts: ["Start Dopamine","Start GTN infusion","Administer Lasix","Give N/S bolus"]
exp: A very low CVP (2) indicates hypovolaemia, treated with a fluid bolus.

q: While working in the Critical Care Unit, the bedside nurse knows that\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_refers to treat all patients equally and fairly:
opts: ["Beneficence","Non-Maleficence","Justice","Autonomy"]
exp: Treating every patient equally and fairly is the ethical principle of justice.

q: Which one of following is the determinant of Stroke volume:
opts: ["Preload","After Load","Contractility","All of the above"]
exp: Stroke volume is determined by preload, afterload, and contractility together.

q: While performing hemodynamic monitoring of the patient, the transducer should be kept at the Phlebostatic axis. The nurse knows that it is located at:
opts: ["Intersection of line between 2nd intercostal space and mid-axillary line","Intersection of line between 3rd intercostal space and mid-axillary line","Intersection of line between 4th intercostal space and mid-axillary line","Intersection of line between 5th intercostal space and mid-axillary line"]
exp: The phlebostatic axis sits at the 4th intercostal space, mid-axillary line.

q: A nurse is caring for a post- CABG patient. While performing hemodynamic monitoring, he noted that Pulmonary Artery pressure is 31/18. The nurse would understand which of the following abnormality as the cause of the this PA pressure reading:
opts: ["Left side heart failure","Right sided heart failure","Pulmonary infection","Pulmonary valve stenosis"]
exp: Elevated pulmonary artery pressures often reflect back-pressure transmitted from a failing left heart.

q: During insertion of Swan Ganz Catheter, the balloon should be inflated with air of:
opts: ["2 cc","3 cc","Less than 1.5 cc","More than 1.5 cc"]
exp: Swan-Ganz balloons are inflated with less than 1.5cc of air to avoid overinflation injury.

q: Which of the following is an indication of IABP:
opts: ["Cardiogenic shock","Severe aortic Regurgitation","Lower extremity ischemia","Dissecting Aortic Aneurysm"]
exp: Cardiogenic shock is a classic, well-established indication for IABP support.

q: Rapid deflation of Balloon of IABP will decrease:
opts: ["Pre-load","After Load","Contraction","Heart Rate"]
exp: Rapid deflation just before systole reduces the resistance (afterload) the heart must pump against.

q: During Ventricular Diastole the balloon of IABP should be:
opts: ["Deflated","Inflated","Standby","Auto filling"]
exp: The balloon inflates during diastole to augment coronary perfusion.

q: The balloon of IABP is positioned in the descending thoracic aorta 1 to 2 cm below:
opts: ["Coronary arteries","Right Subclavian Artery","Renal artery","Left Subclavian artery"]
exp: The balloon sits 1-2cm below the origin of the left subclavian artery.

q: Which of the following is the most appropriate indication for the use of IABP therapy in critical care?
opts: ["A 78 years old patient, 2 days post admission following an acute myocardial infraction who had developed cardiogenic shock","A 60 years old lady with hypotension and chest pain, 2 days after admission for fractured femur","A 45 years old patient with abdominal aortic aneurysm and with a history of Ischemic heart disease","A 25 years old patient with septic shock"]
exp: Post-MI cardiogenic shock is the textbook, most appropriate IABP indication among these options.

q: Inflation of the balloon causes:
opts: ["Increase myocardial oxygen demand","Increase Coronary blood flow","Decreasing Aortic Blood flow","All of the above"]
exp: Diastolic inflation pushes blood backward into the coronary arteries, boosting coronary flow.

q: The resistance (sum of all forces) which the left ventricle has to overcome to eject its blood is called:
opts: ["Preload","After Load","Stroke volume","Pulmonary Vascular Resistance"]
exp: The resistance the LV must overcome to eject blood is afterload.

q: The amount of blood which the heart pumps in one cardiac contraction is called:
opts: ["Pre-load","After Load","Stroke Volume","Cardiac Output"]
exp: The volume ejected in a single heartbeat is stroke volume.

q: The Left Ventricular End Diastolic Volume (LVEDV) is also called:
opts: ["Preload","Afterload","Cardiac Output","Cardiac Index"]
exp: LVEDV — the volume in the ventricle just before contraction — is another name for preload.

q: The Normal Cardiac Output ranges from\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_Liter per minute
exp: Normal cardiac output is roughly 4-8 L/min.

q: The value of Cardiac index ranges from\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_to\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_
exp: Normal cardiac index is roughly 2.6-4.2 L/min/m².

q: Which of the following Unit is used for the measurement of Cardiac Index?
opts: ["m/Min/L2","L/Min/m2","Min/L/m2","L/m2/Min"]
exp: Cardiac index is expressed in L/min/m².

q: Which of following trigger in the IABP machine should be used for a patient during Cardiac Pulmonary Resuscitation (CPR)?
opts: ["ECG","Pressure","Pacer A","Internal Trigger"]
exp: Pressure triggering is used during CPR since chest compressions make ECG triggering unreliable.

q: PCWP stands for:
opts: ["Pulmonary Capillary Wide pressure","Pulmonary Capillary Wedge Pressure","Post-Capillary Wedge Pressure","Pre Capillary Wedge Pressure"]
exp: PCWP stands for Pulmonary Capillary Wedge Pressure.

q: A patient came to the ER with Difficulty in breathing; He was intubated and placed on Mechanical ventilation. His ABGs report showed that his PCO2 is 55 mm Hg. To wash out CO2 which of the following parameter(s) should be adjusted:
opts: ["Fio2 and R/R","Vt and R/R","Vt and PEEP","PEEP and FiO2"]
exp: Washing out CO2 means increasing minute ventilation — adjusting tidal volume and respiratory rate.

q: To increase SpO2 in a mechanical ventilated patient, which of the following parameters should be adjusted:
opts: ["Fio2 and R/R","Vt and R/R","Vt and PEEP","PEEP and FiO2"]
exp: Oxygenation is improved primarily by increasing FiO2 and/or PEEP.

q: The mode of Mechanical Ventilation which does not allow the patient to take spontaneous breaths is:
opts: ["CMV (Control Mandatory Ventilation)","SIMV (Synchronous intermittent Mandatory Ventilation)","AC Mode (Assist Control Mode)","PCV (Pressure Control Mode)"]
exp: Control Mandatory Ventilation delivers only preset breaths with no room for spontaneous effort.

q: The mode of Mechanical Ventilation which has the risk of producing Hyper ventilation and Respiratory Alkalosis is:
opts: ["Controlled Mandatory Ventilation","Synchronous Intermittent Mandatory Ventilation","Assist Control Mode","Pressure Control Mode"]
exp: In Assist-Control, every patient trigger gets a full set breath, so a fast-breathing patient can easily hyperventilate.

q: Which of the following mode of Mechanical Ventilation cannot be used for weaning off patients from Mechanical Ventilation:
opts: ["Controlled Mechanical ventilation (CMV)","Synchronized intermittent mandatory ventilation (SIMV)","Pressure support ventilation (PSV)","Assist-control ventilation (ACV)"]
exp: Fully controlled ventilation (CMV) gives no way to assess or build the patient's own respiratory effort, so it can't be used for weaning.

q: The mode of Mechanical Ventilation in which there is a Preset Breath rate and tidal volume but the patient is able to initiate extra breath which is assisted by the ventilator is called:
opts: ["ACV","CMV","PSV","SIMV"]
exp: Preset rate/volume with the ability to trigger extra ventilator-assisted breaths describes Assist-Control Ventilation.

q: The mode of Mechanical ventilation in which there is Preset breath rate and tidal volume but the patient is able to take spontaneous breaths between the mandatory breaths. The spontaneous breaths above the preset Breath rate are not assisted by the ventilator:
opts: ["ACV","CMV","PSV","SIMV"]
exp: Preset mandatory breaths with unassisted spontaneous breathing in between is the definition of SIMV.

q: The ABGs of a ventilated patient showed that PH= 7.25, PCO2 = 55 mm HG and HCO3= 25 MEq/L. As a nurse what changes would you suggest in the Ventilator settings?
opts: ["Increase FiO2","Increase Respiratory Rate","Decrease Respiratory Rate","Decreasing Tidal Volume 36- Disease is a response to (A)Specific infective agents \\("]
exp: A low pH with high PCO2 (uncompensated respiratory acidosis) is corrected by increasing the respiratory rate to blow off more CO2.

q: A 2-year old was admitted for digoxin toxicity. BP is 94/60 mmhg, capillary refill time is 2 seconds and the ECG reveals 1st degree AV block with a heart rate of 60. The critical care nurse should:
opts: ["Prepare for cardioversion.","Administer vasovagal maneuvers.","Continue to monitor.","Administer atropine."]
exp: BP and cap refill are fine and 1st-degree block alone is benign — the correct action is simply to keep monitoring, not intervene.

q: It is a highly and well knowledgeable coordinated multidisciplinary health care specialty that is meant for patients with acute life threatening illness or injury.
opts: ["Critical care","Critical care nursing","Intensive care nursing","Multidisciplinary care"]
exp: This broad multidisciplinary specialty for acute life-threatening illness is critical care itself.

q: A client is undergoing a comphrensive admission assessment, the critical care nurse knows that, the single most significant indicator of cerebral perfusion and functioning is called
opts: ["Level of consciousness","Glasgow coma scale","Intra cranial pressure","Pupils dilatation"]
exp: Level of consciousness is the single most sensitive early indicator of cerebral perfusion/function.

q: A patient present to the Critical care unit after an exploratory laparotomy related to trauma. The patient is hypotensive and tachycardic. This is most likely due to which of the following?
opts: ["Hypovolaemia","Pain","Hypothermia","Hyperthermia"]
exp: Post-laparotomy hypotension with tachycardia in a trauma patient points first to hypovolaemia from blood/fluid loss.

q: A 50-year old man is admitted to the trauma resuscitation unit. He is intubated on arrival. What is the next step in the primary survey?
opts: ["Obtain a chest x-ray to determine tube placement","Listen to lung sounds to assess for ventilation.","Assess the patient's blood pressure and peripheral pulses.","Initiate large-bore intravenous catheter placement and replace fluid."]
exp: After intubation, the immediate next step is confirming effective ventilation by auscultating breath sounds.

q: A 16 year-old is admitted to the floor after a foot-ball injury. The patient has a femur fracture. An external fixation device was applied in the operating room. The patient is at an increased risk for development of which of the following complications:
opts: ["Post-traumatic stress disorder.","Deep venous thrombosis.","Pulmonary embolism.","Pressure sore."]
exp: Immobilisation with external fixation and long bone fracture markedly raises DVT risk.

q: A female patient tells you that when taking some antibiotics, she develops an itchy skin. This information should alert the critical care nurse that the patient may be more likely to experience what form of shock?
opts: ["Hypovolemic.","Cardiogenic","Anaphylactic","Septic"]
exp: A known drug allergy (itchy skin/hypersensitivity) raises risk of anaphylactic shock on re-exposure.

q: Which of the following should be the first monitor attached to the patient on arrival in the post anaesthesia care area (PACU):
opts: ["Pulse oximeter","Cardiac monitor","Non-invasive blood pressure","Temperature monitoring device"]
exp: Continuous cardiac monitoring is prioritised first on PACU arrival given arrhythmia risk post-anaesthesia.

q: Mr Abu, a 35year-old dispatch rider presents to your facility with diagnosis of Guillain-Barre syndrome. The critical care nurse knows the typical presentation of this condition is:
opts: ["Rapid ascending paralysis","Deficits of cranial nerves 1X and X","Respiratory arrest","Distal tingling of fingertips"]
exp: GBS classically presents with rapidly ascending paralysis starting distally and moving upward.

q: A patient arrives in the emergency department with a suspected cervical spinal cord injury. Which of the following is an assessment priority?
opts: ["Abdominal assessment","Cardiac assessment","Neurological assessment","Respiratory assessment"]
exp: In suspected cervical spine injury, protecting the airway/breathing (with spinal precautions) is the priority.

q: All of the following are interventions for the patient with ineffective thermoregulation EXCEPT:
opts: ["Adjust room temperature","Cover the patient with a warm blanket","Cover the patient's head with a hat or scarf","Use a heating pad"]
exp: A heating pad risks burns/uneven heating and isn't a standard safe thermoregulation intervention, unlike the other options.

q: Which of the following actions by the critical care nurse is the MOST effective means of preventing infection?
opts: ["Washing hands after client contact","Washing hands after removing gloves","Hand hygiene between clients","Hand hygiene before entry to a client's room and upon exit of a client's room"]
exp: Hand hygiene immediately before entering and after leaving a patient's room is the single most effective infection-control practice.

q: The critical care nurse knows that the physician is most likely to order which of the following laboratory tests to evaluate a client for hypoxia?
opts: ["Haematocrit","Sputum analysis","Arterial blood gas (ABG) analysis","Total haemoglobin"]
exp: ABG analysis directly measures oxygenation status, making it the test of choice for hypoxia.

q: A client is receiving a blood transfusion. The critical care nurse observes that the client is experiencing diarrhoea, abdominal pain, and chills. Which of the following actions should the nurse take FIRST?
opts: ["Assist the client to the bathroom.","Stop the transfusion.","Administer meperidine.","Get a warming blanket."]
exp: Diarrhoea, pain, and chills during a transfusion signal a reaction — stopping the transfusion is the first priority action.

q: An older adult client with a history of blood clots is in the emergency room with suspected deep vein thrombosis (DVT) of the left leg. The critical care nurse starts IV heparin as ordered. Which of the following is LEAST likely to be included in the care plan?
opts: ["Ambulation as tolerated","Warm, moist soaks applied to the affected area","Analgesics as ordered","Anti-embolism stockings"]
exp: Warm, moist soaks are avoided in active DVT as heat plus massage-like handling can dislodge a clot.

q: After receiving a report from the night nurse, which of the following clients should the critical care nurse see FIRST?
opts: ["A 31-year-old woman refusing sucralfate before breakfast","A 40-year-old man with left-sided weakness asking for assistance to the commode","A 52-year-old woman complaining of chills who is scheduled for a cholecystectomy","A 65-year-old man with a nasogastric tube who had a bowel resection"]
exp: A new complaint of chills in a pre-op surgical patient suggests possible infection/sepsis risk needing urgent assessment first.

q: Which of the following should the critical care nurse do next?
opts: ["Notify the physician that the client has become hypotensive, and obtain an order to administer IV fluids.","Place the client in semi-Fowler's position, and administer O2 at 4 L.","Administer a second dose of nitro-glycerine.","Document the results, and continue to monitor the client."]
exp: Given the scenario's stable-but-abnormal picture, documenting and continuing close monitoring is the appropriate next step.

q: As a critical care nurse working in ICU, you know that the MOST important equipment needed for patient care is:
opts: ["Bed side cardiac monitor","Arterial blood gases analyser","Mechanical ventilator","Defibrillator"]
exp: A mechanical ventilator directly sustains a critically ill patient's life-supporting respiratory function — the most essential ICU equipment.

q: This is done immediately upon arrival of patient into Critical care unit and it is done by obtaining information based on assessing the parameters using this acronym ABCDE.
opts: ["Preadmission assessment","Admission quick check","Comprehensive assessment","Ongoing assessment"]
exp: The ABCDE structured check done immediately on ICU arrival is the admission quick check.

q: The critical care nurse delivers a synchronised D.C. counter shock to a cardiac patient in a critical incidence, the nurse knows that the MOST appropriate term to describe this procedure is:
opts: ["CPR","Cardiac pacing","Defibrillation","Cardioversion"]
exp: A synchronised shock timed to the R wave used electively for a patient in a rhythm with a pulse is termed cardioversion.

q: The critical care nurse delivers a NON synchronised D.C. counter shock to a cardiac patient in a critical incidence, the nurse knows that the MOST appropriate term to describe this procedure is:
opts: ["CPR","Cardiac pacing","Defibrillation","Cardioversion"]
exp: An unsynchronised shock, used for pulseless/chaotic rhythms like VF, is defibrillation.

q: Mr john a 30-year old was admitted into critical care unit with diagnosis of severe tetanus via medical ward without proper review by the intensivist, as a critical care nurse this is typical of:
opts: ["Open ICU","Closed ICU","Level 1 ICU","Hybrid ICU"]
exp: Admission to critical care without prior review by the intensivist is characteristic of an Open ICU model.

q: Mrs john a 25-year old was admitted into critical care unit with diagnosis of severe tetanus via medical ward after properly been reviewed by the intensivist, as a critical care nurse this is typical of:
opts: ["Open ICU","Closed ICU","Level 1 ICU","Hybrid ICU"]
exp: Admission only after proper intensivist review reflects a Closed ICU model.

q: Majority of patients admitted into CCU tends to be highly vulnerable to nosocomial infections during their stay in the unit. As a critical care nurse you know this is mostly due to:
opts: ["The critical illness","Low immunity","Malnutrition","Lack of love ones"]
exp: Critical illness itself suppresses immune function, making low immunity the main driver of nosocomial infection risk in the CCU.

q: The clear cut defined admission and discharge procedure in critical care setting is known as:
opts: ["Admission procedure","Discharge procedure","Admission and discharge procedure","Operational policy"]
exp: A formally defined admission/discharge process is part of the unit's written operational policy.

q: Mrs Fatima, a post renal transplant patient in critical care unit is on continuous central venous pressure monitoring, as a critical care nurse this is only possible if she has which of the following in situ:
opts: ["Central venous catheter","Arterial catheter","Peripheral line","E.C.G."]
exp: Continuous CVP monitoring requires a central venous catheter in place.

q: The simple test used to help diagnose and monitor certain lung conditions by measuring how much air you can breathe out in one forced breath, it can also measure the volume of air inspired and expired by the lungs. The critical care nurse knows this test as:
opts: ["Stress ECG monitoring","Spiro-metery","Spiro-meter","Rest ECG monitoring"]
exp: Measuring forced exhaled air volume to assess lung function is spirometry.

q: Mr Simon, a 56 year has been on mechanical ventilator (IPPV mode) for the past 14 days. The critical care nurse knows that Mr Simon may likely experience:
opts: ["Alteration in cardiac function","Hyperventilation","Malnutrition","Renal failure"]
exp: Prolonged IPPV commonly causes cardiovascular effects from sustained positive intrathoracic pressure.

q: Mrs Samuel, a 32-year head injured patient in CCU complained of severe headache, nausea and vomiting and suddenly lost consciousness. The critical care nurse knows this is as a result of:
opts: ["Brain injury","Hypertension","Renal failure","Increase intra-cranial pressure"]
exp: Sudden headache, vomiting, and loss of consciousness in a head-injured patient signals rising intracranial pressure.

q: A critical care nurse while performing a suctioning procedure noticed some signs of hypercapnia in the patient. The MOST appropriate intervention to be taken is:
opts: ["Stop the procedure and inform the seniors","Stop and document the findings","Hyper oxygenate and intubate if possible","Reassure the patient"]
exp: Hypercapnia during suctioning signals hypoxia risk — hyperoxygenate (and intubate if needed) before continuing.

q: The critical care nurse while managing patient's airways knows that, the most appropriate head position to prevent falling of tongue thus preventing obstruction of the upper airway is:
opts: ["Left lateral position","Right lateral position","Sniffing position","Dorsal position"]
exp: The sniffing position aligns the airway axes and prevents the tongue from obstructing the airway.

q: The most appropriate nursing intervention performed by the critical care nurse to promote respiratory function for the critically-ill patient is:
opts: ["Adequate hydration","Frequent suctioning","Head up position","Turning and positioning every 2 hours"]
exp: Elevating the head of the bed improves lung expansion and ventilation in critically ill patients.

q: The following are components of medical record EXCEPT:
opts: ["Poor spelling","Medication chart","Nursing record","Vital signs"]
exp: Poor spelling is a documentation quality issue, not a structural component of the medical record itself.

q: Risk factor modification is an important component of patient education and rehabilitation after myocardial infarction. The critical care nurse knows one of the following as a major controlled risk factor:
opts: ["Age","Obesity","Excessive alcohol intake","Stress"]
exp: Obesity is a modifiable ("controllable") risk factor for MI, unlike age.

q: A description of pain that occurs with acute myocardial infarction is:
opts: ["Pain that worsens with leaning or sitting forward","Pain that decrease with swallowing","Abrupt onset of pain that resolves with decrease activities.","Pain that intensifies with a deep breath"]
exp: Classic MI pain has an abrupt onset and eases with rest/decreased activity, unlike pleuritic or positional pain.

q: The commonest thrombolytic agent used to establish reperfusion if there are no contraindications to its use in a patient with myocardial infarction is:
opts: ["Morphine sulphate","Aspirin","Pethidine","Streptokinase"]
exp: Streptokinase is the classic, widely used thrombolytic for reperfusion in MI.

q: The hallmark of myocardial infarction on an ECG is the present of:
opts: ["ST segment elevation","ST segment deviation","Prolong QRS complex","Shorten QRS complex"]
exp: ST-segment elevation is the hallmark ECG finding of acute MI.

q: It is a specially staffed and equipped area of a hospital that provides a level of care intermediate between intensive care and general ward care. The critical care nurse knows this is:
opts: ["High dependency unit","General ward","Post anaesthesia recovery unit","Critical care unit"]
exp: An intermediate level of care between ICU and general ward defines a High Dependency Unit.

q: The level of ICU that possess an active research programme with dedicated research staff is:
opts: ["Level 1","Level 11","Level 111","Level 1V"]
exp: A dedicated research programme is a feature of the highest (Level III) ICU classification.

q: Mr Moses was involved in ghastly motor accident leading to profound physio-metabolic changes involving multisystem. The critical care nurse always refers this condition as:
opts: ["Severe road traffic accident","Multi-organ dysfunction","Polytrauma","Head injury"]
exp: Multisystem physio-metabolic derangement from severe trauma is termed multi-organ dysfunction.

q: The groups of drugs relevant in cardiopulmonary resuscitation are
opts: ["Vasopressors and Antiarrhythmic:","Sedative and hypnotics","Antidiuretics","Antibiotics"]
exp: Vasopressors and antiarrhythmics are the core drug groups used during CPR.

q: The following are examples of arterial Vasodilators exempt
opts: ["Hydralazine","Minoxidil","Diazoxide","Nitro-glycerine"]
exp: Nitroglycerine is primarily a venodilator, not classified among the arterial vasodilators listed.

q: The use of resuscitative drugs is directed at the following EXCEPT: -
opts: ["Improving organ perfusion during cardiopulmonary resuscitation","Facilitating electrical defibrillation","Preventing the recurrence of malignant ventricular arrhythmias","Increasing tissue hypoxia and low blood pressure"]
exp: Resuscitative drugs aim to improve organ perfusion and prevent arrhythmias — increasing hypoxia/hypotension is never the goal.

q: Which of the following drugs is used in the management of hypertensive emergency?
opts: ["Tab amlodipine","Tab Lisinopril","Injection sodium nitroprusside","Tab furosemide"]
exp: IV sodium nitroprusside is a rapid-acting agent specifically used for hypertensive emergencies.

q: The critical care nurse knows one table spoonful is
exp: One tablespoon is standardly equal to 15ml.

q: The following are classes of bronchodilators except
opts: ["Xanthine derivatives","Beta-agonists","Anticholinergics","Beta blockers"]
exp: Beta blockers cause bronchoconstriction, so they are not classed among bronchodilators.

q: The following is truth about Xanthine Derivatives except
opts: ["Cause bronchoconstriction by constricting smooth muscles of the airways.","Relief of bronchospasm and greater airflow into and out of the lungs.","Also causes CNS stimulation.","Also causes cardiovascular stimulation"]
exp: Xanthine derivatives cause bronchodilation (relax airway smooth muscle), not bronchoconstriction — that statement is false.

q: As a critical care nurse, you are aware that the following are causes of low blood pressure EXCEPT:
opts: ["Dehydration","Blood loss","Increase salt intake","Heart problem"]
exp: Increased salt intake tends to raise, not lower, blood pressure.

q: Bronchodilators are classified into the following except
opts: ["Xanthine derivatives","Beta-agonists","Anticholinergics","Beta blockers"]
exp: Beta blockers are bronchoconstrictors, not a bronchodilator class.

q: The following are specialized cells and node that control heartbeat except
opts: ["Sinoatrial node","Atrioventricular node","Purkinje fibres","Pulmonary vein"]
exp: The pulmonary vein is a blood vessel, not part of the heart's electrical conduction system.

q: Which of the following statement is false?
opts: ["Hypertensive emergency are extreme elevations in BP that a accompanied by acute or progressing target-organ damage","Hypertensive emergencies require an immediate but gradual reduction in BP over a period of several minutes to several hours using intravenous antihypertensive agents.","Hypertensive crises are categorized as either hypertensive emergency or hypertensive urgency","Hypertensive urgency is associated with end organ injury or damage"]
exp: Hypertensive urgency (not emergency) is defined as severe BP elevation WITHOUT acute end-organ damage — the statement pairing urgency with organ injury is false.

q: John W. Travis developed a model which signifies right side shows a level of wellness that is achievement of optimum health and left side shows premature death in the year:
exp: Travis's Illness-Wellness Continuum model dates to 1972.

q: The following are the major factors affecting patients' health status, except;
opts: ["Focus","Smoking","Nutrition","Alcoholism"]
exp: "Focus" is not itself a recognised health-status factor, unlike smoking, nutrition, and alcohol use.

q: These are organized frame work structured for nurses to follow in order to avoid omission and commission and are scholarly descriptive of dynamic and change in nursing.
opts: ["Nursing Theories","Nursing Concepts","Nursing Assumptions","Nursing Models"]
exp: Structured frameworks nurses follow to guide practice and avoid errors are nursing theories.

q: Henderson conceptualizes the nurses' role as assisting sick or healthy individuals to gain independence in meeting............fundamental needs.
exp: Henderson described 14 fundamental needs nurses help patients meet.

q: Orem identifies three 3 types of nursing system;
opts: ["Wholly Compensatory, Partly Compensatory and Supportive-Educative Systems","Partial Compulsory, Partly Compensatory System and Supportive-Educative and Supportive-Educative Systems","Wholly Communicative, Partly Compensatory and Supportive-Educative Systems","Supportive Compensatory, Supportive-Educative Systems and instructive system."]
exp: Orem's three nursing systems are Wholly Compensatory, Partly Compensatory, and Supportive-Educative.

q: The critical care nurse knows that; the earliest record of Critical Thinking is said to be the teaching of
opts: ["Socrates","Plato","Hypocretes","Gulya"]
exp: Critical thinking is traditionally traced back to Socratic questioning.

q: When a Critical Thinking is applied to Nursing, it helps the students' brain function better and understands texts differently. As such critical thinking can be defined as
opts: ["The ability to think clearly and rationally about what to do and what to belief.","The ability to be critique and free thinker.","A means of being augmentative.","Being critical of other people."]
exp: Critical thinking is defined as thinking clearly and rationally about what to do or believe.

q: Mr. R. was admitted into Critical care unit with pain score of about 8/10, his pain signifies
opts: ["Mild pain","Very severe pain","Severe pain","Moderate pain"]
exp: A pain score of 8/10 falls in the severe pain range.

q: An interdisciplinary study of systems as they relate to one another within a larger more complex system, a general science of wholeness is referred as
opts: ["System theory","Maslow theory","Nightingale's theory","Henderson's theory"]
exp: Systems theory is the interdisciplinary study of how systems relate within a larger whole.

q: Competence evaluation can be performed in various ways, for example, using self-assessment, knowledge tests, peer evaluation, observation or portfolios. The best way to perform competence evaluation is
opts: ["To combine different evaluation methods.","Peer evaluation","Self-assessment","Observation or portfolios"]
exp: Combining multiple evaluation methods gives the most complete, reliable competence assessment.

q: Wong-Baker FACES scale is .......
opts: ["Used in children less than 3 years of age or older children who can't talk","Used like an APGAR scale","Used in children who can talk (usually 3 years and older)","All of the above."]
exp: The Wong-Baker FACES scale is designed for children roughly 3 years and older who can communicate.

q: The critical care nurse attempt to measures a patient's by using a special flexible ruler that indicates numbers and colour coding, this implies:
opts: ["Wong-Baker FACES Scale","Numeric Pain Rating Scale","Observation-FLACC Scale","Pain severity scale"]
exp: A flexible ruler with numbers/colour coding for measuring pain describes the Numeric Pain Rating Scale tool.

q: Mr Sani a patient occupying bed 3 in the ICU complained of a sudden, temporary flare of severe pain that occurs on a background of otherwise controlled pain. What type of pain does he experience according to situation?
opts: ["Breakthrough pain","Activity pain","Incidence pain","Nociceptive pain"]
exp: A sudden flare of pain breaking through otherwise controlled baseline pain is breakthrough pain.

q: The identification, analysis and responses to risk factors that are associated to a given profession is referred as;
opts: ["Risk management","Quality assurance","Quality control","None of the above"]
exp: Identifying and responding to profession-specific hazards is risk management.

q: Mrs John a 30-year old patient with acute Myocardial infarction is said to have developed awareness, education and growth, with the aid of health-illness continuum what level of health is your patient?
opts: ["Low level of wellness","Medium level of health","Premature death","High level of wellness"]
exp: Awareness, education, and growth reflect movement toward the high-wellness end of the health-illness continuum.

q: When the demand of self-care is greater than individual's capacity or ability to meet the patient's need in the critical care settings, this lead to care being compromised, this indicates:
opts: ["Self-care deficit","Self-insistencies","Self-incompetency","Self-compromise"]
exp: When self-care needs exceed the patient's ability to meet them, this is termed a self-care deficit.

q: Sister J. a senior critical care nurse always thinks about the practice/procedure before carrying it out. She also remembers past events as an experience to serve as pre-requisite for the future practice. This denotes that sister J. practices:
opts: ["Reflection Beyond Action","Reflection Before Action","Reflection in action","Reflection on Action"]
exp: Thinking through a procedure beforehand, informed by past experience, is reflection before action.

q: Matron B. the deputy manager of ICU requires a conscious effort to think about events and develop insights into them. What matron B. does is
opts: ["Reflective practice","Problem solving","Decision making","Skeptical nursing"]
exp: Deliberately thinking through events to build insight is the definition of reflective practice.

q: A Senior nursing officer KB was assigned to convey a critically ill client from Gombe to Bauchi, unfortunately the entrain Oxygen therapy disrupted as it got finished before reaching the destination. What would be the first process to cater the problem?
opts: ["Decision Making","Critical Thinking","Problem Solving","Seeking Help"]
exp: When something urgent needs an immediate fix (like restoring oxygen), rapid problem solving is the first practical response.

q: This occurs when air enters the pleura but cannot escape, resulting in one-way valve effect, thus produces shift in the mediastinum.
opts: ["Primary spontaneous pneumothorax","Secondary spontaneous pneumothorax","Tension pneumothorax","Traumatic pneumothorax"]
exp: A one-way air-trapping valve effect with mediastinal shift is the hallmark of tension pneumothorax.

q: In the management of poly-trauma patient acute resuscitation is considered as
opts: ["3 to 72 hours of management","0 to 3 hours of management","3 to 8 days of management","Beyond 8 days of management"]
exp: Acute resuscitation in polytrauma covers the first 0-3 hours of management.

q: The critical care nurse knows simultaneous injury of different regions of the body of which one or the combination of these injuries is life threatening, is popularly known as:
opts: ["Severe multiple injuries","Very severe trauma","Poly head injuries","Polytrauma"]
exp: Simultaneous life-threatening injuries across multiple body regions is the definition of polytrauma.

q: Critical care unit or ICU delirium Is often seen as a:
opts: ["Organic mental disorder characterised by impairment of sleeping, thinking and disturbances in perception and restlessness.","None organic mental disorder characterised by impairment of consciousness, disorientation and disturbances in perception and restlessness.","Organic mental disorder characterised by impairment of consciousness, disorientation and disturbances in perception and restlessness.","Organic thinking disorder characterised by impairment of consciousness, disorientation and disturbances in perception and rest."]
exp: ICU delirium is an organic (physiological) disturbance of consciousness, orientation, and perception.

q: The Intensive care nursing intervention on renal recipient is aimed at measures to maintain fluid balance, kidney functions, prevention of infection and assessment for possible graft rejection and other complications.
opts: ["Incorrect","Partially correct","Absolutely correct","Not at all"]
exp: Fluid balance, kidney function, infection prevention, and rejection monitoring are indeed all core goals of post-transplant ICU nursing care.

q: The following are the Early symptoms of pulmonary emphysema EXCEPT:
opts: ["Cough, Sputum production and Wheezing","Tachypnoea","Shortness of breath, which gets worse with activity","Delirium"]
exp: Delirium is a late/complication feature, not an early symptom of emphysema.

q: This denotes a disease of the alveoli characterized by irreversible damage to the alveoli making them less elastic, leading to decrease exchange of gasses causing hypoxemia.
opts: ["Pulmonary embolism","Pulmonary Emphysema","Pulmonary hypertension","Pulmonary Congestion"]
exp: Irreversible alveolar damage reducing elasticity and gas exchange describes pulmonary emphysema.

q: The critical care nurse knows taking care of a patient on mechanical ventilator by a critical care nurse comprises of
opts: ["2 responsibilities","4 responsibilities","3 responsibilities","1 responsibility"]
exp: Ventilator patient care is commonly organised into 2 broad nursing responsibility categories in this teaching framework.

q: Monro-Kellie doctrine, or hypothesis, is that the sum of volume of brain, CSF, and intracranial blood is constant. An increase in one should cause a decrease in one or both of the remaining two leading to:
opts: ["Normal intracranial pressure","Normal heamodynamic","Increase in the intracranial pressure","Lower intracranial pressure"]
exp: By the Monro-Kellie doctrine, added volume in a fixed cranial space raises intracranial pressure.

q: This disease condition consists of train of severe seizures with relatively short intervals or no interval between, the patient becomes exhausted and frequently hyperthermia. Death not uncommonly occurs during the attacks.
opts: ["Persistent convulsion","Status epilepticus","Poor epilepsy","Meningeal irritation"]
exp: Continuous seizures with exhaustion and hyperthermia describe status epilepticus.

q: The critical care nurse with his vast knowledge of respiratory system knows that the part of the respiratory system where gaseous exchange takes place is:
opts: ["Alveoli","Pharynx","Larynx","Trachea"]
exp: Gas exchange occurs at the alveoli.

q: A client who has an inhalation injury comes to ER. On the initial assessment, the nurse notes that the client is very confused and physically defensive. What do you think the client may be experiencing?
opts: ["Fear","Anxiety","Hypoxia","Pain"]
exp: Confusion and combativeness after inhalation injury is a classic sign of hypoxia.

q: The patient has vomiting that is very similar to coffee grounds. Where can the location of the bleeding be?
opts: ["Large Intestine (colon)","Small Intestine","Upper GI Tract","Rectum"]
exp: Coffee-ground emesis reflects digested blood from an upper GI source.

q: A patient who has been admitted with a gastric ulcer now develops severe epigastric pain that spreads over the entire abdomen. He is very pale and is lying on his side. What should be the nurse\'s immediate action?
opts: ["Notify the doctor and check the vitals.","Immediately administer opioids.","Perform deep palpations on the abdomen.","Reposition the patient in Semi-Fowler\\'s Position."]
exp: Sudden severe abdominal pain with pallor after a known ulcer suggests perforation — notify the doctor and check vitals immediately.

q: The patient has aphasia, decreased levels of consciousness & weakness in the right side of the body. There is a history of heart disease, ischemic attacks, and hyperlipidaemia. Based on the medical history, what type of stroke could it be?
opts: ["Haemorrhagic Stroke","Ischemic Stroke","Subarachnoid Haemorrhages","Intracerebral Haemorrhages"]
exp: Aphasia, altered consciousness, and hemiparesis with a cardiovascular risk history fits an ischaemic stroke.

q: A patient has been admitted with an ischemic stroke. She was given a recombinant tissue plasminogen activator (rtPA) in the ER. Which of the following medication she shouldn\'t be given?
opts: ["Aspirin","Labetalol","Sodium Nitroprusside","All of the above"]
exp: Aspirin (an anticoagulant-adjacent antiplatelet) is avoided after rtPA due to bleeding risk.

q: Which nursing intervention can help prevent sharp spikes in ICP and surgical haemorrhage?
opts: ["Give fluid restriction.","Help with breathing exercises.","Keep the head of the bed elevated.","Administer an antiemetic to prevent vomiting."]
exp: Preventing vomiting (which raises intrathoracic/intra-abdominal pressure) helps avoid ICP spikes and bleeding risk.

q: The patient has a risk of GI haemorrhage. For which of the critical symptoms should the patient be monitored?
opts: ["Hyperkalaemia and hypernatremia","Hematemesis and melena","Decreasing haemoglobin levels","Metabolic acidosis and hypovolemia"]
exp: Hematemesis and melena are the direct clinical signs of GI bleeding to monitor for.

q: Which of the following statements is true for acute liver damage?
opts: ["The main reason for acute liver failure is chronic liver damage.","Metabolic alkalosis is the common indicator of acute liver damage.","It might lead to cardiac dysrhythmias and respiratory failure.","None of the statements is true."]
exp: Acute liver failure can precipitate cardiac dysrhythmias and respiratory compromise from metabolic derangement.

q: A person with a 10-year history of severe alcoholism was admitted to the ER. He got the diagnosis of acute pancreatitis. Based on the recent diagnosis, what could be true about the patient?
opts: ["Should be started feeding","Requires to be monitored for hyperglycaemia","The patient is at the risk for hypovolemic shock","Should be placed on fluid restriction"]
exp: Severe alcoholism-related pancreatitis carries a significant risk of hypovolaemic shock from third-spacing and fluid loss.

q: Concerning pressure-control versus volume-control ventilation:
opts: ["Volume control will provide a set tidal volume.","Pressure control will provide a set tidal volume.","Lung compliance is irrelevant with pressure control.","Paediatric ventilation never uses pressure control ventilation."]
exp: Volume control ventilation guarantees a set tidal volume regardless of lung compliance.

q: The mode of ventilation which allows the patient to breathe spontaneously at his or her own respiratory rate and depth between the ventilator breaths is
opts: ["Controlled Mandatory Ventilation","Synchronous Intermittent Mandatory Ventilation","Assist Control Mode","Pressure Control Mode"]
exp: SIMV allows spontaneous breathing between the set mandatory breaths.

q: A 53-year-old patient who is receiving ibuprofen 400 mg twice a day, for chronic, low back pain develops lower-extremity oedema. The pain management nurse suspects that the oedema is caused by:
opts: ["A decrease in renal function","A low creatinine level.","An increase in glomerular filtration rate","An increase in plasma proteins."]
exp: NSAIDs can reduce renal blood flow/function, causing fluid retention and oedema.

q: The critical care nurse follows the recommended protocol for preventing constipation when starting a patient on opioids by
opts: ["Adding bulk fibre to the diet","Giving the patient enemas as needed.","Increasing fluids and exercise.","Using a bowel stimulant and stool softener."]
exp: A bowel stimulant plus stool softener is the standard combination to prevent opioid-induced constipation.

q: Which of the following is the initial analgesic drug of choice for a patient who is in severe pain, with a score of 9 out of 10, due to a fracture?
opts: ["IV paracetamol","Rectal NSAID","Splint","IV opiate"]
exp: IV opioid is the appropriate first-line choice for severe (9/10) acute fracture pain.

q: The action to be taken by critical care nurse before administering morphine 4.0 mg intravenously to a patient complaining of incisional pain is
opts: ["Assess the patient\\'s incision.","Clarify the order with the prescriber.","Assess the patient\\'s respiratory status.","Monitor the patient\\'s heart rate."]
exp: Assessing respiratory status is essential before giving IV morphine due to its respiratory depressant effect.

q: The critical care nurse knows brain death as:
opts: ["Reversible brain damage","Irreversible loss of all brain function","Reversible loss of brain function","Irreversible brain damage"]
exp: Brain death is defined as the irreversible loss of all brain function.

q: Which of the following drugs is not used to treat hypotension in the ICU?
opts: ["Dopamine","Noradrenaline","Hydralazine","Dobutamine"]
exp: Hydralazine lowers blood pressure — it treats hypertension, not hypotension.

q: The brain stem consist of the following parts
opts: ["Midbrain, hindbrain and forebrain","hindbrain, Ventricle and Pons","Midbrain, Pons and forebrain","Midbrain, Pons and Medulla"]
exp: The brainstem is made up of the midbrain, pons, and medulla.

q: The function of midbrain are
opts: ["Visual reflex and olfactory responses","Olfactory responses and gustatory function","Visual reflex and gustatory function","Visual reflexes and auditory responses"]
exp: The midbrain mediates visual (and auditory) reflexes.

q: The function of pons are
opts: ["Endocrine, sleep and arousal","Melanine, endocrine and arousal","Sex, sleep and pain transmission","Sleep, arousal and pain transmission"]
exp: The pons is involved in sleep, arousal, and pain transmission pathways.

q: The function of medulla oblongata are
opts: ["Parasympathetic control","Endocrine control","Renal control","Cardiorespiratory control"]
exp: The medulla oblongata controls core cardiorespiratory functions.

q: Which of the following is not a weak opioid?
opts: ["Hydrocodone","Hydromorphone","Codeine","Tramadol"]
exp: Hydromorphone is a strong (not weak) opioid, unlike codeine, tramadol, and hydrocodone.

q: Physical part of a computer are known as\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_
opts: ["Software","Hardware","Operating System","System unit"]
exp: The physical components of a computer are collectively called hardware.

q: \_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_is a place where processing takes place
opts: ["Box","CPU","Monitor","System unit"]
exp: The CPU is where processing takes place.

q: Processor is fixed on the
opts: ["Fan","Chipboard","Motherboard","Expansion slot"]
exp: The processor sits on the motherboard.

q: There are.........types of memory
opts: ["Two","Three","One","Four"]
exp: Computer memory is broadly classified into two types (RAM and ROM).

q: Which is used in security system and criminal investigations
opts: ["Fingerprint Reader","Robots","Sound card","Barcode Reader"]
exp: Fingerprint readers are used in security and criminal investigation systems.

q: \_\_\_\_\_ is the set of instruction given to the computer to perform a specific task
opts: ["Monitor","Hardware","Software","Printer"]
exp: The set of instructions telling a computer what to do is software.

q: A system software that helps Operating System to communicate with a device is called
opts: ["Operating system","Device Driver","Utility","Graphics Software"]
exp: A device driver lets the operating system communicate with hardware devices.

q: Pulmonary oedema is the.........
opts: ["Active movement of fluid across alveolar arterial membrane into the alveoli.","Active movement of fluid across alveolar venous membrane into the alveoli","Active movement of fluid across the capillary membrane into the alveoli","Active movement of fluid across the heart to the lungs."]
exp: Pulmonary oedema is fluid actively moving across the capillary membrane into the alveoli.

q: The primary cause of pulmonary oedema is......
opts: ["Myocardial infarction","Hypersensitivity of drugs","Fluid overload","Acute left ventricular failure"]
exp: Acute left ventricular failure is the primary driver of cardiogenic pulmonary oedema.

q: Pharmacotherapy for pulmonary oedema are the following except......
opts: ["Haematonics","Diuretics","Morphine","Digitalis"]
exp: Haematonics (used for anaemia) are not part of standard pulmonary oedema pharmacotherapy.

q: Three factors thought to be predispose to vascular thrombus are....
opts: ["Bed rest, Immobility and Surgery.","Reduction in blood flow, damage to the vessels wall and Increase in Blood coagulation","Obesity, Oral contraceptives and Oestrogen replacement","Trauma, Burns and Previous various thrombosis"]
exp: Virchow's triad — reduced blood flow, vessel wall damage, and hypercoagulability — predisposes to thrombosis.

q: The critical care nurse knows that one of the following is a thrombotic agent.
opts: ["Streptokinase","Heparin","Clexane","Warfarin sodium"]
exp: Streptokinase is a thrombolytic ("clot-busting") agent, unlike the anticoagulants listed.

q: Respiratory failure occurs in which of the following clinical conditions...
opts: ["PaO2 greater than 60mhg or Pa02 lower than 50mhg","Pa02 lower than 60mhg or PaO2 greater than 50mhg","PaO2 at 60mhg or PaO2 at 50mhg","PaO2 greater than 60mhg or PaO2 greater than 60mhg"]
exp: Respiratory failure is defined by PaO2 <60mmHg or PaCO2 >50mmHg.

q: The critical care nurse knows the term to describe accumulation of air within pleural space is called...
opts: ["Haemothorax","Pneumohemothorax","Tension pneumothorax","Pneumothorax"]
exp: Air accumulation in the pleural space is simply termed pneumothorax.

q: Pulmonary hypertension can be defined as...
opts: ["Mean pulmonary pressure greater than 30mhg","Mean pulmonary pressure less than 25mhg","Mean pulmonary arterial pressure greater than 25mhg at rest or 40mhg during exercise.","Mean pulmonary pressure less than 25mhg at rest or 40mhg during exercise."]
exp: Pulmonary hypertension is defined as mean pulmonary arterial pressure >25mmHg at rest (or >40 with exercise).

q: It is often called ICU psychosis but it is not, delirium is usually rapid within...
opts: ["30mins -1hr","Hours or few days","Weeks or months","Months to years"]
exp: ICU delirium typically has a rapid onset over hours to a few days.

q: The DSM provide criteria for diagnosis of ICU delirium which is divided into?
exp: DSM criteria classify delirium features into 5 domains.

q: There are ...............types of delirium
exp: Delirium is commonly categorised into 3 subtypes (hyperactive, hypoactive, mixed).

q: Respiratory failure can be classified base on onset as...
opts: ["Acute, Chronic and Acute on chronic respiratory failure","Mild, Moderate, and Severe respiratory failure","Sudden, weak, and Strong respiratory failure","Mild, acute, and chronic respiratory failure."]
exp: Respiratory failure is classified by onset as acute, chronic, or acute-on-chronic.

q: Failure to acknowledge the borrowed material is called (Take and use of others as one's own)
opts: ["Acknowledgement","Foot note","Index","Plagiarism"]
exp: Using someone else's work without acknowledgement is plagiarism.

q: A Blue print of Research work is called
opts: ["Research Problem","Research methods","Research tools","Research design"]
exp: A structured overall plan for conducting research is the research design.

q: Facts, figures and other relevant materials serving as bases for a study is called
opts: ["Sample","Method","Data","Theory"]
exp: Facts and figures serving as the basis of a study are data.

q: The concrete observable events which represent the abstract concepts or constructs are called
opts: ["Data","Sample","Variable","Proposition"]
exp: Concrete, observable representations of abstract concepts are variables.

q: The original source from which researcher collects information is
opts: ["Primary Source","Secondary Source","Both primary and secondary","Tertiary source"]
exp: The original, first-hand source of information is a primary source.

q: ............ is the raw material for Analysis
opts: ["Variables","Problem","Data","Sample"]
exp: Data is the raw material used for analysis.

q: The question which by its content structure or wordings leads the respondent in the direction of a certain answer is called
opts: ["Factual question","Opinion question","Leading question","Structural question"]
exp: A question worded to steer the respondent toward a particular answer is a leading question.

q: Questionnaire is filled by ...................
opts: ["Respondent","Everybody","Enumerator","Researcher"]
exp: A questionnaire is filled in by the respondent.

q: What is the primary site of action for torsemide?
opts: ["Loop of Henle -- thick ascending","Loop of Henle -- thin descending","Proximal convoluted tubule","Distal convoluted tubule."]
exp: Torsemide, a loop diuretic, acts primarily at the thick ascending limb of the Loop of Henle.

q: When is the use of spironolactone possibly contraindicated?
opts: ["In hypernatremia patients","In hyperkalaemia patients","In patients with refractory congestive heart failure","When also prescribing furosemide"]
exp: Spironolactone (a potassium-sparing diuretic) risks dangerous hyperkalaemia, so it is used cautiously in that setting.

q: Which of the following pairs is correct?
opts: ["Vasodilators: reduce preload","Arterial dilators: reduce cardiac output","Venodilators: increase blood volume returning to the heart","Arterial dilators: increase afterload"]
exp: Vasodilators reduce venous return (preload) as one of their core haemodynamic effects.

q: Which of the following is not a common potential side effect of Class II Antiarrhythmic drugs (e.g. propranolol, atenolol, carvedilol)?
opts: ["Bradycardia","Hypotension","Negative inotropes","Hepatotoxicity"]
exp: Hepatotoxicity is not a typical class effect of beta-blockers, unlike bradycardia, hypotension, and negative inotropy.

q: The best nutritional management of severely Obese client is
opts: ["Very high calorie diet.","Very low-calorie diet.","Very high carbohydrate diet.","Very high fat diet"]
exp: A very low-calorie diet under supervision is the standard nutritional approach for severe obesity.

q: The following are sign and symptoms of iron toxicity EXCEPT
opts: ["Hair lost","Apathy","Fatigue","Diarrhoea"]
exp: Hair loss is not a classic acute sign of iron toxicity, unlike GI symptoms and fatigue.

q: An intravenous solution that is used to provide free water and intracellular fluid hydration is
opts: ["Ringer's lactate solution","5 % dextrose in water","10 % dextrose in water","5 % dextrose in normal saline"]
exp: 5% dextrose in water provides free water for intracellular hydration since the dextrose is metabolised, leaving the water behind.

q: Bacterial food poisoning is caused by
opts: ["Vibrio chorale","Entamoeba histolytica","Salmonella species","Shigella sonei"]
exp: Salmonella species are a classic bacterial cause of food poisoning among these options.

q: Which of the following is the first symptom that bring a client with pernicious anaemia to seek medical attention?
opts: ["dark stool","sudden weight loss","tendency to bleed easily","feeling unusually fatigued"]
exp: Unusual fatigue from anaemia is typically the earliest symptom prompting a pernicious anaemia patient to seek care.

q: Which the following element is used in the treatment of diarrhoea reduce output and severity of diarrhoea
opts: ["oral iron","oral sodium","Oral potassium","Oral zinc"]
exp: Oral zinc supplementation is recommended to reduce the severity/duration of diarrhoea.

q: Normal body mass index (BMI) should not exceed
exp: A BMI above roughly 30 is generally considered the obesity threshold, so normal BMI is kept below this.

q: Symptoms associated with anaemia include
opts: ["Increased breathlessness","Decreased breathlessness","Increased activity intolerance","Moderate breathlessness"]
exp: Anaemia classically causes increased breathlessness due to reduced oxygen-carrying capacity.

q: The process by which water and diffusible substance move together across cell membrane response to fluid pressure is called
opts: ["Osmosis","Diffusion","Filtration","Active transport"]
exp: Filtration is the process where water and dissolved substances move together across a membrane under pressure.

q: Vitamin A deficiency can cause all of the following EXCEPT
opts: ["Night blindness","Keratomalacia","Dermoid","Xerosis"]
exp: Dermoid (a cyst) is unrelated to Vitamin A status, unlike night blindness, keratomalacia, and xerosis.

q: Characteristic of attitudes in psychology include the following except.
opts: ["Attitude origin","Attitude strength.","Attitude ambivalence.","Attitude accessibility."]
exp: "Attitude origin" is not a standard listed characteristic of attitudes (strength, ambivalence, accessibility are).

q: One of the following is not the core components of emotional intelligence.
opts: ["Self - awareness.","Self- esteem.","Self- management","Self- confidence."]
exp: Self-esteem is not typically listed as a core component of emotional intelligence (self-awareness, self-management, social skills are).

q: Your ability to not only understands your strengths and weaknesses, but to recognize your emotion and the effect they have on you and your team's performance is called.
opts: ["Self- esteem.","Self- confidence.","Self- awareness.","Self- management"]
exp: Recognising one's own emotions and their impact on performance is self-awareness.

q: Genetic and environment are the two factors that affect intelligence and it is also called......
opts: ["Gene- Physical tussles","Bio- physical tussles.","Nature- Nurture tussles","Inductive-Deductive"]
exp: The combined influence of genetics and environment on intelligence is called the nature-nurture debate/tussle.

q: Motivational type that drives people to achieve something they otherwise won't have been able to is ............
opts: ["Power- based motivation.","Incentive - based motivation.","Fear - based motivation.","Reduction of tension motivation"]
exp: Motivation driven by avoiding a negative outcome is fear-based motivation.

q: One of the following is an example of positive motivation.
opts: ["Employment","Transfer","Recognition.","Desire"]
exp: Employment/reward-type incentives represent positive motivation.

q: Process of motivation include the following except.
opts: ["Satisfied need","Tension.","Drive.","Better co-operation."]
exp: "Better co-operation" is an outcome, not a step in the motivation process itself (need, tension, drive are).

q: One of the following is not a key elements of emotion.
opts: ["Objective experience","Physiological response.","Behavioural response.","Subjective experience"]
exp: "Objective experience" isn't typically listed as a core element of emotion (physiological, behavioural, subjective are).

q: Interpersonal relationship theory was propounded by
opts: ["Hildegard Peplau","Martha Rogers.","Sigmoid Freud.","B.F. skinner"]
exp: Hildegard Peplau developed the interpersonal relations theory of nursing.

q: What is the order of the abdominal assessment?
opts: ["Inspection, percussion, auscultation, palpation","Inspection, auscultation, percussion, palpation","Inspection, palpation, percussion, auscultation","Inspection, percussion, palpation, auscultation"]
exp: Abdominal assessment order is inspection, auscultation, percussion, then palpation (to avoid disturbing bowel sounds before listening).

q: Which of the following statements is true for acute liver damage?
opts: ["The main reason for acute liver failure is chronic liver damage.","Metabolic alkalosis is the common indicator of acute liver damage.","It might lead to cardiac dysrhythmias and respiratory failure.","None of the statements is true."]
exp: Acute liver failure can precipitate dangerous cardiac arrhythmias and respiratory failure.

q: Which is the physiological altercation of acute pancreatitis?
opts: ["Hyperglycaemia","Premature activating of inactive digestive enzymes","Destruction of RBC","Respiratory issues"]
exp: Acute pancreatitis involves premature activation of the pancreas's own digestive enzymes, causing self-digestion.

q: The patient has acute liver disease. Which lab values are true with his condition?
opts: ["Increased ammonia levels","Decreased PT","Decreased AST and ALT","None of the above"]
exp: A failing liver cannot clear ammonia effectively, so ammonia levels rise.

q: Oesophageal varices can be caused due to \_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_.
opts: ["Portal hypertension","Inflammation","Superficial mucosal erosions","Pro-ulcer forces breaking down"]
exp: Portal hypertension drives the development of oesophageal varices.

q: Minute ventilation is equal to\...
opts: ["FiO2 X PEEP","FiO2/PEEP","Tidal Volume X Respiratory Rate","Tidal Volume/Respiratory Rate"]
exp: Minute ventilation = tidal volume × respiratory rate.

q: One of the following is a risk of keeping high PEEP
opts: ["Hypotension","Hypertension","Hyperthermia","Hypothermia"]
exp: High PEEP raises intrathoracic pressure, reducing venous return and risking hypotension.

q: Which of the following is not the main goal of mechanical ventilation in the ICU?
opts: ["Optimize patient comfort","Optimize exchange of carbon dioxide and oxygen","Get them through surgery with less discomfort","Decrease work of breathing"]
exp: "Getting through surgery" isn't a described core ICU ventilation goal — comfort, gas exchange, and reduced work of breathing are.

q: Which of the following is not a way to improve oxygenation?
opts: ["FiO2","PEEP","Inspiratory time","Respiratory rate"]
exp: Respiratory rate primarily affects ventilation/CO2 clearance, not oxygenation directly.

q: What is the injury to the alveoli caused by excessive pressure from the ventilator called?
opts: ["Barotrauma","Volume-trauma","Atelecto-trauma","Biotrauma"]
exp: Alveolar injury from excess ventilator pressure is barotrauma.

q: Which action should the nurse take when preparing patient-controlled analgesia for a postoperative patient?
opts: ["Caution the patient to limit the number of times he presses the dosing button.","Ask another nurse to double-check the setup before patient use.","Instruct the patient to administer a dose only when experiencing pain.","Provide clear, simple instructions for dosing if the patient is cognitively impaired"]
exp: Having a second nurse double-check PCA setup is a core safety practice before patient use.

q: Which of the following oxygen delivery device delivers an FiO2 of \>90%?
opts: ["Nasal cannula","Simple Face mask","Non- rebreathing face mask","Venturi Face mask"]
exp: A non-rebreather mask can deliver FiO2 above 90%, higher than the other listed devices.

q: Dietary therapy has not only become an essential adjunct of care but the bedrock of treatment in some critical illnesses like............., hypertension and chronic kidney disease.
opts: ["Head injury","Sepsis","Diabetes","Nutrition"]
exp: Dietary therapy is foundational in managing diabetes alongside hypertension and CKD.

q: Nutrition in Critical Care Nursing focus in exploring a link between a diet, health and illness.
opts: ["False","True","Not sure","Undecided"]
exp: Nutrition in critical care nursing does explore the diet-health-illness relationship — true.

q: .............is the study of how food and drink affects our bodies with special regard to the essential nutrients necessary to support human health.
opts: ["Nutrient","Diet","Food","Nutrition"]
exp: The study of how food affects the body and its essential nutrients is nutrition.

q: Which of the following statement is true about Nutrient?
opts: ["Nutrients may not be organic or inorganic","Nutrients may be inorganic only","Nutrients may be organic only","Nutrients may be organic or inorganic"]
exp: Nutrients can be either organic or inorganic in nature.

q: Any nutritious substance that people or animals eat, drink or that plants absorb in order to maintain life and growth.
opts: ["Carbohydrate","Food","Minerals","Nutrition"]
exp: Any substance eaten/drunk to sustain life and growth is generally termed food.

q: ............. are the kind and amount of food prescribed for a person or animal for a special reason.
opts: ["Nutrient","Diet","Food","Treatment"]
exp: The specific kind and amount of food prescribed for a person is their diet.

q: Proteins are made up of hundreds or thousands of smaller units called:
opts: ["Micronutrient","Fatty acids","Amino acids.","Glucose"]
exp: Proteins are built from smaller units called amino acids.

q: 21- Nurse Jamila was asked to explain animal sources of protein to a patient which are meat, fish, egg and ..........
opts: ["Fatty product","Non-diary product.","Diary product.","Wheat"]
exp: Common animal protein sources include meat, fish, egg, and dairy products.

q: A fat is changed to increase its shelf life. The process to make this change happen is called
opts: ["Saturation","Modification","Saponification.","Hydrogenation."]
exp: Hydrogenation is the process used to convert fats to extend shelf life (creating trans fats).

q: Artificial trans-fat is a type of fat that is added to some food products to improve taste, texture and extend their shelf life. However, trans fats can increase your risk of ............
opts: ["Diabetes","Lung diseases","Kidney diseases","Heart diseases"]
exp: Trans fats are strongly linked to increased heart disease risk.

q: The major functions of water include regulation of body temperature, production of body fluids, transportation of nutrients and ......
opts: ["Formation of oedema.","Dehydration.","Removal of waste products.","None of the above."]
exp: Water's functions include removing waste products from the body, alongside temperature regulation and transport.

q: Absorption refers to the movement of the nutrients, water and electrolyte from the
opts: ["the mouth to the small intestine to the anus.","the small intestine to large intestine.","the mouth to the stomach and small intestine.","lumen of the small intestine into the cell then into the blood."]
exp: Absorption is nutrients/water/electrolytes moving from the intestinal lumen into cells and then into the bloodstream.

q: Digestion of starch is initiated in the ........., facilitated by salivary amylase.
opts: ["Stomach","Mouth","Small intestine","Large intestine"]
exp: Starch digestion begins in the mouth via salivary amylase.

q: In the liver and muscles, most of the glucose is changed into glycogen by the process called:
opts: ["Glycogenolysis","Combustion","Conversion","Glycogenesis"]
exp: Converting glucose into stored glycogen is glycogenesis.

q: If blood glucose levels are low, then epinephrine and glucagon hormones are secreted to stimulate the conversion of glycogen to glucose. This process is called
opts: ["Glucose uptake","Glycogenolysis","Glycogenesis","Glucose formation"]
exp: Breaking glycogen back down into glucose is glycogenolysis.

q: Enteral feeding means nutrition taken
opts: ["food through the mouth via the gastrointestinal \\[GI\\] tract.","through a tube that goes directly to the stomach or small intestine.","through the mouth directly to the stomach or small intestine.","Intravenous line"]
exp: Enteral feeding delivered via a tube directly into the stomach/small intestine is the standard definition here.

q: It is a disease of the alveoli characterized by irreversible damage to the alveoli making them less elastic leading to hypoxia.
opts: ["Pulmonary oedema","Pulmonary Embolism","Pulmonary Emphysema","Pyeothorax"]
exp: Irreversible, less-elastic alveolar damage causing hypoxia describes pulmonary emphysema.

q: Nephrectomy is a surgical procedure to remove
opts: ["Nephron.","Healthy kidney.","Only one kidney.","All or part of a kidney."]
exp: Nephrectomy is surgical removal of all or part of a kidney.

q: Below are the most important considerations when intubating a traumatized patient, EXCEPT\...
opts: ["Payment","Patient","Equipment","Assistance"]
exp: "Payment" is clearly irrelevant to safe intubation, unlike patient status, equipment, and assistance.

q: One of these is not a clinical presentation of asthma
opts: ["Dyspnoea with prolonged expiratory phase","Productive cough with thick sputum.","Wheezing","upper limb vein distension"]
exp: Upper limb vein distension is not a recognised feature of asthma.

q: This refers to an asthma attack that doesn\'t improve with traditional treatments, such as inhaled bronchodilators. These attacks can last for several minutes or even hours
opts: ["Status asthmaticus","Intrinsic asthma","Extrinsic asthma","Mixed asthma"]
exp: An asthma attack unresponsive to standard bronchodilator treatment is status asthmaticus.

q: The individual is able to respond to stimuli, he may be able to exercise some form of will but exhibits some irrational thinking and reasoning; this is called
opts: ["Automatism","Confusion state","Delirium state","Lethargy state"]
exp: Responding to stimuli with some irrational thought but retained will describes a confusional state.

q: Patient appears sleepy but arausable. Speech and movement are minimum, a state referred as;
opts: ["Lethargy state","Acute confusional state","Delirium state","Comatose state"]
exp: Sleepy but arousable with minimal speech/movement describes lethargy.

q: A respiratory condition characterized by accumulation of air in the pleural cavity, (Space between the visceral and parietal pleura of the lung) is called;
opts: ["Pulmonary accumulation","Pulmonary oedema","Pneumothorax","Pyeothorax"]
exp: Air accumulation in the pleural space is pneumothorax.

q: The term used to describe an accumulation of both blood and air in the pleural space is known as
opts: ["Hydro-pneumothorax","Pyeo-pneumothorax","All of the above","Hemopneumothorax"]
exp: Combined blood and air in the pleural space is haemopneumothorax.

q: It Is the most common cause of pulmonary embolism.
opts: ["Fat embolism","Deep venous thrombosis","Air bubbles","Amniotic fluid embolus"]
exp: Deep venous thrombosis is by far the most common source of pulmonary embolism.

q: The critical care nurse knows that whenever caring for a critically-ill patient, it is very vital that, there are early and repeated discussions with patients' families to reduce family stress and improve consistency in communication. The nurse is practicing:
opts: ["Family involvement care","Family orientation","Family-focused care","Family advocate care"]
exp: Ongoing, structured communication with families to reduce their stress is family-focused care.

q: In what phase of care would the nurse listen for bowel sounds?
opts: ["Pre-arrival phase","Admission quick check","Comprehensive admission assessment","Resuscitation"]
exp: Listening for bowel sounds is part of the detailed comprehensive admission assessment, not the rapid initial check.

q: A 40-year-old woman is involved in an equestrian accident. She presents with chest pain, shortness of breath, and difficulty breathing. Her arterial oxygen saturation is 85% and her respiratory rate is 40b/minute. A chest tube is placed and the nurse observes a return of 300 mls blood into the system. What is the nurse's next line of action?
opts: ["Clamp the tube and notify surgeon immediately.","Call for a chest x-ray.","Place the patient on oxygen.","Explain to the patient what is going on to alley anxiety."]
exp: Sudden return of 300ml blood via the chest tube signals major haemorrhage — clamp and notify the surgeon immediately.

q: The primary aetiology of multi-organs dysfunction is believed to be related to:
opts: ["Inadequate tissue perfusion causing cell death.","Inflammatory/ immune response mechanism that activate the coagulation cascade.","History of chronic illness","A and B"]
exp: Multi-organ dysfunction arises from both direct hypoperfusion/cell death and an inflammatory/coagulation cascade response.

q: Which of the following is NOT true?
opts: ["Withholding life-sustaining treatment is ethically acceptable, but withdrawing such treatment is not.","To be truly autonomous, a patient must be fully informed and freely consenting.","Do Not Resuscitate order (DNR) is a medical order to withhold cardiopulmonary resuscitation (CPR).","A nurse's duty always to act in the best interests of his or her patient is based on the principle of beneficence."]
exp: Ethically, withholding and withdrawing life-sustaining treatment are considered equivalent — the statement claiming otherwise is false.

q: Which of the following macronutrients in parenteral nutrition may need to be increased in a patient who has large, nonhealing wounds?
opts: ["Carbohydrates","Lipids","Trace elements","Protein"]
exp: Protein needs rise substantially with large non-healing wounds to support tissue repair.

q: The critical care nurse has just administered insulin to a diabetic client. In which of the following ways should the nurse dispose of the needle?
opts: ["Re-cap the needle and discard it in the nearest puncture-resistant container.","Re-cap the needle and discard it in the nearest biohazard container.","Discard the needle in a puncture resistant container.","Break the needle and discard it in the nearest puncture-resistant container."]
exp: Needles should never be recapped (needlestick risk) — discard directly into a puncture-resistant sharps container.

q: Two critical care nurses are preparing to lift a client up in bed. Which of the following should the nurses do to help avoid injuring their backs?
opts: ["Bend from the waist.","Lift with the back, not with the legs.","Lower the head of the bed to about 30 degrees, if the client can tolerate it.","Make certain the bed is in a reasonably high position."]
exp: Keeping the bed at a high, comfortable working height protects staff backs during a manual lift.

q: The Critical Care Nurse manager knowns that, the art of getting an organizational activities done through other people using available resources is called.
opts: ["Leadership","Governance","Management","Delegation"]
exp: Achieving organisational goals through others using available resources is the definition of management.

q: He was the first to systematically describe management behaviour and also pioneered the identification of the principles and skills that underlie effective management.
opts: ["Henri Fayol","Max waber","F. Taylor","Gashinge et all."]
exp: Henri Fayol is credited as the pioneer of systematic management theory and principles.

q: This involves the way that experts view employees and how their views about man influences manager's approach to human management.
opts: ["Conceptual dimension","Human dimension","Technical dimension","Man dimension"]
exp: How managers view and relate to employees reflects the human dimension of management.

q: This consists of the manager's functional expertise and how their they carried out managerial activities in an organization.
opts: ["The human dimension","Conceptual dimension","The technical dimension","The man dimension"]
exp: A manager's functional expertise in carrying out activities is the technical dimension.

q: This stated that; work must be properly subdivided in order to reduce span of attention or efforts in an organization.
opts: ["Scalar chain","Division of labour","Remuneration","Unity of command"]
exp: Division of labour is the principle stating work should be subdivided to reduce effort/attention span needed.

q: The critical care nursing student will always exercise self-direction and self-control if he / she feels a sense of commitment to his job. This statement implies:
opts: ["Theory A","Theory b","Theory x","Theory y"]
exp: Theory Y assumes workers are self-directed and committed when they feel ownership of their work.

q: In order to achieve objective, organisations should apply the method of science to solve problems, with specific reference to operational research and advanced mathematical technique. This statement implies:
opts: ["Behavioural theory","System theory","Management by objective","Classical theory"]
exp: Applying scientific/mathematical methods to solve organisational problems reflects systems theory.

q: The ICU manager knows that some of her staffs prefers to be directed rather than to be allowed to use initiative or to exercise responsibility. This statement implies:
opts: ["Theory A","Theory b","Theory x","Theory y"]
exp: Preferring direction over independent initiative reflects Theory X assumptions about workers.

q: The following are all basic qualities of a good manager EXCEPT:
opts: ["Assertiveness","Tactfulness","Fearfulness","Decisiveness"]
exp: Fearfulness is the opposite of a good manager's needed qualities, unlike assertiveness, tact, and decisiveness.

q: The critical care nurse knowns this is often referred to as the process of meaningful interaction among human beings, more specifically, it is the process by which meanings are perceived and understanding is reached among human beings.
opts: ["Bulletin","News","Communication","Circular"]
exp: The meaningful exchange of understanding between people is the definition of communication.

q: Which one of the following is the primary imaging modality in ICU?
opts: ["Ultrasonography","Conventional X ray","CT scan","MRI"]
