exp: Portable conventional X-ray is the most frequently used, primary bedside imaging tool in ICU.

q: About the haemothorax choose the incorrect one
opts: ["Accumulation of blood within the peritoneal space following blunt or penetrating trauma.","Commonly associated with a pneumothorax and other extra thoracic injuries.","Haemorrhage usually occurs from the lung parenchyma, and is often self-limiting, rather than from a specific vessel injury.","Intercostal and internal mammary vessels are more commonly injured"]
exp: Haemothorax is blood in the pleural (not peritoneal) space — that statement is the incorrect one.

q: About the x ray features of haemothorax
opts: ["Erect CXR is more sensitive than a supine film.","Blunting of the costophrenic angles --","Seen with approximately less than 100 ml of blood.","General increased opacification of the haemothorax is seen on a supine film"]
exp: On a supine film, blood spreads out and causes general increased opacification rather than a clear costophrenic blunting.

q: Why the radiologist prefers PA view instead of AP view
opts: ["Increase magnification of heart.","Increase radiation dose to sensitive organs.","Reduce magnification of heart.","Unclear visualized maximum areas of lung"]
exp: The PA view reduces cardiac magnification compared to AP.

q: Patient had head trauma and you suspect that he has intracranial haemorrhage, Which of following modalities you will choose?
opts: ["CT","Conventional x-ray","MRI","X-ray with contrast"]
exp: CT is the imaging modality of choice for detecting acute intracranial haemorrhage.

q: The most common cause of large bowel obstruction. Select one:
opts: ["Tumor of the large bowel.","Inflammatory bowel disease.","Adhesions.","Ischemia"]
exp: Tumours of the large bowel are the most common cause of large bowel obstruction in adults.

q: Ionizing radiation is the substance which ionizes the atoms when it passes. It is harmful for biological matter. Which of the following is non ionizing modality?
opts: ["Conversational x-ray","Computerized tomography","Magnetic resonance imaging","Isotopic scanning"]
exp: MRI uses magnetic fields, not ionizing radiation, unlike X-ray, CT, and isotope scanning.

q: Radiographic findings that indicate oesophageal perforation include all the following except
opts: ["Extraesophageal location of the tube","Normal finding","Hydro pneumothorax","Pneumomediastinum"]
exp: A normal finding would not indicate perforation — the other signs (extraluminal tube, hydropneumothorax, pneumomediastinum) do.

q: About complication of NG placement, which one may occur?
opts: ["Incomplete insertion","Tube coiling within the oesophagus or hypopharynx","Tube misplacement in the tracheobronchial tree, lung parenchyma, pleural space, or even through the diaphragm may occur","All of the above may occur"]
exp: All of the listed misplacements (coiling, tracheobronchial passage, incomplete insertion) are recognised NG tube complications.

q: The following imaging modalities are ionizing radiations except
opts: ["Conventional X ray","Fluoroscopy","MRI","CT scan"]
exp: MRI does not use ionizing radiation, unlike X-ray, fluoroscopy, and CT.

q: Large intestinal obstruction
opts: ["Less commonly seen than small bowel obstruction.","Causes include diverticulitis and inflammatory.","It is commoner in the young.","If the ileocecal valve is patent a closed loop obstruction occurs leading to vascular compromise and ischaemia."]
exp: Large bowel obstruction is less common in the young; causes and other features apply more broadly to older adults.

q: X ray features of large intestinal obstruction
opts: ["The large bowel is seen to be dilated peripherally ('picture frame' appearance).","The haustral pattern traverse the colon as compared to small bowel valvulae conniventes.","Distended small bowel loops seen with an incompetent ileocecal valve.","Caecal distension 8 cm increases the likelihood of caecal perforation."]
exp: A competent ileocecal valve traps gas, causing progressive caecal distension rather than small bowel dilation.

q: The following are interventional radiology procedures except
opts: ["Angioplasty.","Stenting","Thrombogenesis.","Embolization"]
exp: Thrombogenesis (clot formation) is the opposite of what interventional radiology aims for — angioplasty, stenting, and embolisation are actual IR procedures.

q: About endotracheal and tracheostomy tube insertion. Which one is not true
opts: ["Malpositioning of the endotracheal tube (ETT) occurs in approximately 35% of endotracheal intubations.","The clinical assessment of tube location is frequently inaccurate, and a chest radiograph should be obtained immediately following intubation.","With the neck in neutral position, the ideal position of the tube tip is 5 to 7 cm above the carina;","In 90% of patients, the carina projects between the fifth and seventh thoracic vertebrae on the portable radiograph"]
exp: Malposition occurs in a substantial proportion (often cited around a third) of ETT placements, confirming the need for routine post-intubation X-ray.

q: About the risk of endotracheal intubation
opts: ["The aortic arch also may be used to estimate tube location","If the ETT is too high, there is a risk of either inadvertent extubation or hypopharyngeal intubation,","If the ETT is too low, selective intubation of the right mainstem bronchus may occur, hyperinflation of the right lung, and possible pneumothorax.","The balloon cuff should fill and dilate the trachea."]
exp: The balloon cuff seals but should not overly dilate the trachea — overinflation risks tracheal injury, making this the incorrect statement.

q: Central venous catheters (CVCs)
opts: ["CVCs are used frequently in the ICU patient for venous access, monitoring central venous pressure, and haemodialysis.","The subclavian, internal jugular, and femoral veins are the sites of venous access used most commonly;","Smaller-calibre central catheters can also be peripherally inserted via antecubital veins.","CVCs inserted via a thoracic vein are not visible on the chest radiograph"]
exp: CVCs inserted via any central vein, including thoracic veins, are visible on chest X-ray — the statement claiming otherwise is false.

q: About central venous catheters select the wrong answer
opts: ["Appropriate catheter position must be verified radiographically","Malposition has been described in up to 80% of CVCs.","Positioning of the catheter tip within the right atrium is common and may result in cardiac perforation and tamponade.","Placement into the right ventricle may result in arrhythmias secondary to irritation of the endocardium or interventricular septum"]
exp: Reported CVC malposition rates are much lower than 80% — that figure is incorrect.

q: About misplacement of central venous catheters
opts: ["A misplaced CVC can result in inaccurate venous pressure readings as well as venous thrombosis or venous wall perforation.","The most common location for a misplaced catheter entering the subclavian vein is the ipsilateral internal jugular vein.","More frequently, thoracic CVCs may enter the azygous, internal mammary, superior intercostal.","Looping, knotting, and kinking of the catheter may also occur"]
exp: CVCs more often deviate into veins like the azygous or internal mammary rather than always following the described common path.

q: About pulmonary artery catheterization all are correct except
opts: ["The pulmonary artery catheter (PAC) plays an important role in the hemodynamic monitoring of the critically ill patient.","The catheter is inserted via the subclavian or internal jugular vein and its tip should lie within the right or left ventricle.","The catheter tip should remain within 2 cm of the hilum.","Complications such as pneumothorax, vascular injury, infection, and knotting, may occur"]
exp: A correctly positioned PA catheter tip sits in the pulmonary artery, not the ventricle — that statement is incorrect.

q: About cardiac pacemaker
opts: ["Cardiac pacemakers can be inserted by trans venous only","Most often the trans venous approach is used","The right internal jugular and the left subclavian veins are often preferred","When viewed on a chest radiograph, the pacemaker lead should terminate in the right ventricular apex"]
exp: Pacemakers can also be inserted via epicardial/surgical approaches, not only transvenously — making that absolute statement incorrect.

q: What is the definition of hypothermia?
opts: ["A body core temperature above 35.0 oC (95.0 oF) in humans","A body core temperature below 35.0 oC (95.0 oF) in humans","A body core temperature below 37.0 oC (99.5 oF) in humans","A body core temperature below 32.0 oC (90.0 oF) in humans"]
exp: Hypothermia is clinically defined as a core temperature below 35.0°C.

q: What are the symptoms of moderate hypothermia?
opts: ["Shivering and mental confusion","No shivering and increased confusion","Hallucinations and paradoxical undressing","No obvious vital signs"]
exp: Moderate hypothermia classically causes shivering along with confusion.

q: What are the recommended treatment measures for mild hypothermia?
opts: ["Heating blankets and warmed intravenous fluids","Extracorporeal membrane oxygenation (ECMO)","Warm drinks, warm clothing, and physical activity","Cardiopulmonary resuscitation (CPR)"]
exp: Mild hypothermia is treated with passive rewarming — warm drinks, clothing, and movement.

q: What is hyperthermia?
opts: ["An increase in body temperature caused by external factors","A decrease in body temperature caused by internal factors","An increase in body temperature caused by bacterial or viral infection","A decrease in body temperature caused by bacterial or viral infection"]
exp: Hyperthermia is a rise in body temperature driven by external heat factors (unlike fever, which is internally regulated).

q: What triggers the elevation of core temperature during fever?
opts: ["Oral antibiotics","Bacterial or viral infection","Preoptic region of the anterior hypothalamus","Endotoxins"]
exp: Fever is triggered by infection (bacterial/viral) acting on the hypothalamic set point.

q: Which part of the brain is involved in temperature regulation?
opts: ["Medulla oblongata","Cerebellum","Anterior hypothalamus","Parietal lobe"]
exp: The anterior hypothalamus is the brain's temperature-regulating centre.

q: What are the two forms of heat stroke?
opts: ["Classical and exertional","Primary and secondary","Mild and severe","Acute and chronic"]
exp: Heat stroke is classified as classical or exertional.

q: What is the definition of anaesthetic death?
opts: ["Death caused by anaesthesia within 24 hours of the procedure","Death of a patient who had anaesthesia regardless of cause within 24 hours","Death caused by complications during anaesthesia","Death caused by excessive bleeding during surgery"]
exp: Anaesthetic death is specifically death caused by the anaesthesia itself within 24 hours.

q: What is the definition of anaesthetic death?
opts: ["Death caused by anaesthesia within 24 hours of the procedure","Death of a patient who had anaesthesia regardless of cause within 24 hours","Death caused by complications during anaesthesia","Death caused by excessive bleeding during surgery"]
exp: Same definition repeated: death directly caused by anaesthesia within 24 hours.

q: What is the function of neuromuscular blockers?
opts: ["To alleviate musculoskeletal pain and spasms","To reduce spasticity in neurological conditions","To interfere with transmission at the neuromuscular end plate","To relax the skeletal muscles"]
exp: Neuromuscular blockers work by interfering with transmission at the neuromuscular junction.

q: What is the main target of neuromuscular blockers?
opts: ["Central nervous system (CNS)","Motor end plate","Muscle membrane","Synoptic vesicles"]
exp: The motor end plate (neuromuscular junction) is the primary target of these drugs.

q: What is a life-threatening cause of airway obstruction that may require tracheal intubation?
opts: ["Congenital heart disease","Asthma","Sinusitis","Migraine"]
exp: Severe asthma can cause life-threatening airway obstruction requiring intubation.

q: What is the consequence of a right main stem intubation?
opts: ["Decreased oxygen content due to ventilation/perfusion mismatch","Increased risk of aspiration","Pneumothorax","Tracheal stenosis"]
exp: Right main stem intubation over-ventilates one lung and under-ventilates the other, causing a V/Q mismatch and reduced oxygenation.

q: Where should the distal tip of a properly positioned tracheal tube be located?
opts: ["In the right main bronchus","In the oesophagus","Above the bifurcation of the carina","In the left main bronchus"]
exp: The tube tip should sit above the carina, not within either main bronchus.

q: Which route of intubation is preferable in children undergoing intensive care and requiring prolonged intubation?
opts: ["Orotracheal intubation","Nasotracheal intubation","Laryngotracheal intubation","Tracheostomy"]
exp: Tracheostomy is preferred for children needing prolonged ventilatory support to reduce upper airway trauma.

q: What can cause critical obstruction in a child's airway due to narrow anatomy?
opts: ["Large tracheal tube","Retrograde escape of gas","Swelling of the glottis","Excessive adenoid dislodgement"]
exp: Glottic swelling can critically narrow a child's already small airway.

q: What is the appropriate tracheal tube size for a premature infant?
exp: A 2.5mm tube is the appropriate small size for a premature infant's airway.

q: What is the main goal of mechanical ventilation?
opts: ["To assist with speaking","To remove carbon dioxide from the lungs","To treat neurological diseases","To enhance heart function"]
exp: The core purpose of mechanical ventilation is to support removal of CO2 (and delivery of oxygen).

q: What is the term for non-invasive ventilation that uses face or nasal masks?
opts: ["Positive pressure ventilation","Negative pressure ventilation","Invasive ventilation","Non-invasive ventilation"]
exp: Ventilation delivered via a face/nasal mask without an artificial airway is non-invasive ventilation.

q: Which condition is NOT a common medical indication for mechanical ventilation?
opts: ["Pneumonia","Acute respiratory distress syndrome","Surgical procedures","Acute allergic reaction"]
exp: An acute allergic reaction is not a typical primary indication for mechanical ventilation, unlike pneumonia, ARDS, and surgery.

q: In what circumstances is mechanical ventilation typically used as a long-term measure?
opts: ["During surgical procedures","For patients with acute respiratory distress syndrome","For patients with chronic illnesses that require long-term ventilatory assistance","For new-born premature infants"]
exp: Chronic illnesses requiring ongoing ventilatory support are the classic long-term mechanical ventilation scenario.

q: What is the purpose of intubation during mechanical ventilation?
opts: ["To secure the airway and allow unimpeded passage of air into the trachea","To deliver pain medication to the Patient","To improve gas exchange in the lungs","To measure the shunt fraction"]
exp: Intubation secures the airway to allow unimpeded airflow into the trachea.

q: What is the estimated shunt fraction in patients with impaired gas exchange?
exp: Impaired gas exchange conditions can produce shunt fractions around 50%.

q: In mechanical ventilation, what does PEEP stand for?
opts: ["Positive end-expiratory pressure","Partial exhalation and expiratory pressure","Peak exhaled expiratory pressure","Positive extra thoracic end-positioning"]
exp: PEEP stands for positive end-expiratory pressure.

q: Which machine is known as the first negative-pressure machine used for long-term ventilation?
opts: ["Ventilator","Iron Lung","Pulmonary Tank","Respiratory Bladder"]
exp: The Iron Lung was the original negative-pressure ventilator for long-term use.

q: What do transport ventilators specifically cater to?
opts: ["Non-invasive ventilation","Delivery of smaller volumes and pressures","Transporting patients","Treatment of chronic conditions"]
exp: Transport ventilators are specifically designed and used for moving patients between locations.

q: Which of the following is not an ideal characteristic of a volatile anaesthetic agent?
opts: ["Odourless or pleasant to inhale","Flammable","Safe for all ages and in pregnancy","Cheap to manufacture"]
exp: Flammability is a dangerous, undesirable property — not an ideal characteristic of a volatile anaesthetic.

q: This describe a condition where the fertilized egg implants itself in an area outside of the uterus.
opts: ["Ectopic pregnancy","Molar pregnancy","Hydatidiform mole","Premolar pregnancy"]
exp: A fertilised egg implanting outside the uterus is an ectopic pregnancy.

q: The critical care nurse knows this process as the extraction of fluid from recto-uterine pouch posterior to the vagina through a needle.
opts: ["Culdocentesis","Amniocentesis","Colposcopy","Thoracentesis"]
exp: Culdocentesis is needle aspiration of fluid from the recto-uterine (Pouch of Douglas) pouch.

q: The commonest surgical procedure used to treat ruptured tubal pregnancy is
opts: ["Total hysterectomy","oophorectomy","Salpingectomy","Salpingo-oophorectomy"]
exp: Salpingectomy (removal of the affected tube) is the standard surgery for a ruptured tubal pregnancy.

q: Ectopic pregnancy can be confirmed by
opts: ["Pregnancy test","Ultrasound","Laparoscopy","HSG"]
exp: Ultrasound is the standard confirmatory imaging test for ectopic pregnancy.

q: A gravida 9 presents with history of amenorrhea for 7 weeks, PV bleeding, lower abdominal pain, and fainting twice yesterday. Her diagnosis is likely:
opts: ["Abortion","Implantation bleeding","Ectopic pregnancy","Placenta Previa"]
exp: Amenorrhoea, bleeding, abdominal pain, and fainting in a multigravida strongly suggest ectopic pregnancy.

q: This describe a woman who has never been pregnant before.
opts: ["Nulligravida","Primipgravida","Primipara","Multigravida"]
exp: A woman who has never been pregnant is a nulligravida.

q: This is the first nursing diagnosis to be prioritized in hyperemesis gravid arum.
opts: ["Deficit fluid volume","Pain","Altered body comfort","Anxiety"]
exp: Correcting fluid volume deficit is the priority in hyperemesis gravidarum given the risk of severe dehydration.

q: In pregnancy plasma volume increases from......... To ...... by 32 weeks' gestation
exp: Plasma volume rises roughly from 2.6L to 3.8L by 32 weeks gestation.

q: This is an acute event involving the cardiorespiratory system causing reduced or absent conscious level at any point during pregnancy and 6 weeks postpartum.
opts: ["Postpartum haemorrhage","Acute syndrome","Morning blues","Maternal collapse"]
exp: A sudden reduced/absent consciousness event during pregnancy or the postpartum period is termed maternal collapse.

q: Autocaval compression reduces .........to about 40%
opts: ["Cardiac compression","Apnoea","Insomnia","Cardiac output"]
exp: Aortocaval compression can reduce cardiac output by up to about 40% when a pregnant woman lies supine.

q: The treatment for ventricular fibrillation is
opts: ["Fistulectomy","Fibrimyotomy","External defibrillation","Intubation"]
exp: External defibrillation is the definitive treatment for ventricular fibrillation.

q: This is said to be present in the babies whose birth weight is below the 10th percentile of the average for gestational age.
opts: ["Preterm syndrome","Post date","Small for gestational age","Intra uterine growth restriction"]
exp: Birth weight below the 10th percentile for gestational age defines small for gestational age.

q: In normal foetal growth hypertrophy and hyperplasia occurs within............gestation
opts: ["20 to 28 weeks","22 to 24 weeks","20 to 24 weeks","16 to 32 weeks"]
exp: Foetal hypertrophy/hyperplasia growth phases occur across roughly 16-32 weeks gestation.

q: Baby Chinedu was delivered with birth weight of 1500g he is classified as
opts: ["Low birth weight","Very low birth weight","Extremely low birth weight","Normal birth weight"]
exp: 1500g falls within the low birth weight category (<2500g, with very low birth weight being <1500g).

q: First sign of overdose in drug toxicity is
opts: ["Convulsion and arrest","Numbness tongue and slurred speech","Irritability","Coma"]
exp: Numbness of the tongue and slurred speech are classic early warning signs of local anaesthetic/drug toxicity.

q: Decidua is cast off as a result of:
opts: ["Post-partum","Ischemia","Rupture","decidua"]
exp: The decidua is shed as part of the postpartum process.

q: Nurse Bimbo observes a patient to have respiratory depression after magnesium sulphate administration. What is the next line of management?
opts: ["Give another dose slowly","Observe the patient","Intubate the patient","Give oxygen and do not give mgso4 again"]
exp: Respiratory depression from magnesium toxicity requires stopping further MgSO4 and giving oxygen support.

q: This describe the premature separation of a normally situated placenta occurring after the 28th week of pregnancy.
opts: ["Placenta praevia","Placenta accrete","Placenta circumventer","Abruption placentae"]
exp: Premature separation of a normally implanted placenta after 28 weeks is abruptio placentae.

q: Common causes of antepartum haemorrhage from 24 weeks\' gestation include which of the following?
opts: ["Uterine rupture","placenta praevia","Placenta abruption","Normal period"]
exp: Placenta praevia is a leading recognised cause of antepartum haemorrhage from 24 weeks onward.

q: Which of the following describes a partial praevia?
opts: ["Cervical opening is completely covered by the placenta","Cervical opening is partially covered by the placenta","Placenta extends just to the edge of the cervix","Acute on Chronic praevia"]
exp: Partial praevia means the placenta partially (not completely) covers the cervical os.

q: Foetal complications occurs in Eclampsia as a result of
opts: ["Placenta hyper perfusion","Placenta pigmentation","Placenta hypo-perfusion","Placental bleeding"]
exp: Reduced placental perfusion in eclampsia compromises foetal oxygen/nutrient supply, causing foetal complications.

q: A 25 years old para 4, was seen in the clinic with the diagnosis of mastitis. what feeding advice will nurse Iyabo give?
opts: ["Continue breastfeeding","Wean the baby","Feed the baby with express breast milk","Give infant formula"]
exp: Mastitis does not require stopping breastfeeding — continuing feeding is recommended (helps clear the blockage/infection).

q: Vaginal bleeding after 28 weeks of pregnancy which is sudden in onset, painless, causeless and recurrent is known as.
opts: ["Abortion","Abruption placentae","Placenta praevia","Vasa praevia"]
exp: Sudden, painless, recurrent bleeding after 28 weeks is the classic description of placenta praevia.

q: A 20-year old woman presents at the clinic with severe abdominal pain on the right side with signs of abdominal bleeding.  She indicated that she has missed her last menstrual period.  Based on this information, which of the following disorders must be included as an option in the diagnosis?
opts: ["Ovarian cancer","Normal pregnancy","Appendicitis","Ectopic pregnancy"]
exp: Missed period with unilateral abdominal pain and bleeding must raise suspicion for ectopic pregnancy.

q: The recommended feeds for every new-born is the BREASTMILK. Thus, all health workers should support mothers to breast feed successfully by putting baby to breast after delivery within
opts: ["30 minutes","2hours","3hours","5 hours"]
exp: Early initiation of breastfeeding within the first 30 minutes is recommended for all newborns.

q: The following are methods are used in feeding in paediatrics except
opts: ["Gavage feeding","Tube feeding","Breast feeding","Force feeding"]
exp: Force feeding is not an appropriate, safe paediatric feeding method, unlike gavage, tube, or breastfeeding.

q: During resuscitation of the new-born, the Nurse should
opts: ["Withhold information from parents","Keep parents informed about baby\\'s condition","Prevent parents from watching resuscitation","Be aggressive towards the parents"]
exp: Keeping parents informed during neonatal resuscitation supports family-centred care.

q: When the neonates heart rate falls or fails to respond after five inflation breath next action is
opts: ["Commence chest compression","Insert umbilical catheter","Recheck airway","Insert endotracheal tube"]
exp: If heart rate remains low after 5 inflation breaths, chest compressions should begin.

q: Extreme thirst, very dry mouth, very dry mucous membranes, Sunken eyes, Sunken fontanels, no tears when crying and Anuria/oliguria are all signs of
opts: ["Mild dehydration","Moderate dehydration","Severe dehydration","Acute thirst"]
exp: Extreme thirst, sunken eyes/fontanelles, no tears, and anuria together indicate severe dehydration.

q: Mal-rotation with volvulus can be diagnosed through the following EXCEPT:
opts: ["Abdominal ultrasound","Abdominal x-ray","History taking","ESR"]
exp: ESR (an inflammatory marker) doesn't diagnose malrotation/volvulus, unlike imaging and history.

q: The invagination of segment of a bowel into another adjacent segment in Aminu, 6 months old baby is known as
opts: ["In-vaginitis","Appendicitis","Gastritis","Intussusception"]
exp: One bowel segment telescoping into an adjacent segment is intussusception.

q: Blue colour, Moderate tone, Inadequate breath and Slow heart rate in a neonate should be followed by which action
opts: ["Wrap baby and handover to mother","Keep baby in the cot","Commence active resuscitation","Commence breastfeeding"]
exp: Blue colour, poor tone, inadequate breathing, and slow heart rate mean the neonate needs active resuscitation.

q: This condition describe a birth defect where some nerve cells are missing in the large intestine:
opts: ["Appendicitis","Hirsh sprung disease","Pyloric Hirsh sprung","Birth stenosis"]
exp: Absent nerve cells (ganglion cells) in the large intestine defines Hirschsprung's disease.

q: Incarcerated inguinal hernia in paediatrics is treated by
opts: ["Antibiotics","Analgesics","Anti-inflammatory","Emergency surgery"]
exp: An incarcerated hernia is a surgical emergency requiring urgent operative repair.

q: Indications for I/V fluids therapy in neonates includes all except
opts: ["Oral tolerance","Frequent vomiting","Intestinal obstruction","Prematurity"]
exp: Good oral tolerance is a reason NOT to need IV fluids, unlike vomiting, obstruction, or prematurity.

q: Identify the most useful initial assessment of the new-born
opts: ["Tone, breathing and heart rate","Colour of the baby","APGAR score","Length of the neonate"]
exp: Tone, breathing effort, and heart rate form the most useful rapid initial newborn assessment.

q: One of the following neonates does not need resuscitation
opts: ["Baby with hypothermia","Baby whose mother is on sedatives","Baby with congenital heart disease","Baby with heart rate of 100bpm"]
exp: A heart rate of 100bpm is within the normal newborn range and does not indicate a need for resuscitation.

q: Warming, Drying, Stimulation, may reduce neonatal mortality and morbidity when
opts: ["Delayed","Neglected","Promptly done","Forgotten"]
exp: Prompt warming, drying, and stimulation right after birth reduces neonatal mortality/morbidity.

q: This is the interventions required to aid the recovery of a baby when circulation fails or breathing is interrupted or both
opts: ["Baby recovery","Neonatal resuscitation","Asphyxia","ECG"]
exp: Supporting a baby whose circulation/breathing has failed is neonatal resuscitation.

q: The method used during Exchange blood transfusion procedure is
opts: ["PUSH","PULL","PUSH-PULL","PULL-PUSH"]
exp: The push-pull technique describes how blood is exchanged during an exchange blood transfusion.

q: The purpose of performing an EBT in a severely jaundiced baby is to urgently reduce the blood level of unconjugated bilirubin so as to prevent
opts: ["Unconjugated bilirubin","Brain damage","Asphyxia","Febrile illness"]
exp: Rapidly lowering unconjugated bilirubin via EBT prevents kernicterus (brain damage).

q: Decreased brown fat, Thin Skin, Lack of flexion and decrease subcutaneous fat are responsible for
opts: ["Respiratory problems","Nervous system problems","Metabolic problems","Thermoregulation problems."]
exp: Reduced brown fat, thin skin, and low subcutaneous fat predispose premature babies to thermoregulation problems.

q: The are following are all premature baby routine care EXCEPT:
opts: ["Top and tailing","Cord care","Incubator care","Surgical intervention"]
exp: Surgical intervention is not part of routine preterm care, unlike top-and-tail, cord care, and incubator care.

q: NICU means
opts: ["Neonatal intensive care unit","Neonatal intensive curative unit","Neonatal inability care unit","Neonatal intensive caution unit"]
exp: NICU stands for Neonatal Intensive Care Unit.

q: The period from birth to four weeks that is 28 days of age is known as
opts: ["Neonatal period","Delivery period","Prenatal period","Neofetal period"]
exp: The first 28 days of life define the neonatal period.

q: The average weight of a normal full term neonate is about
exp: A normal full-term newborn typically weighs about 2.5-3.9kg.

q: A normal neonate demonstrate all the following characteristics EXCEPT
opts: ["Attitude of flexion,","Pink","Cry vigorously","Gasping respiration"]
exp: Gasping respiration is abnormal — a healthy newborn cries vigorously and breathes regularly.

q: Oxygen saturation of 95-100% in room air Conscious and easily arausable good muscle tone, respiration 30-60 is average 40 describes
opts: ["An asphyxiated baby","An apnoeic baby","A healthy baby","A sick neonate"]
exp: Good saturation, alertness, tone, and normal respiratory rate describe a healthy newborn.

q: A nurse is caring for a patient who is refusing treatment for his condition. The patient's family is pressuring the nurse to persuade the patient to accept treatment. What should the nurse do?
opts: ["Persuade the patient to accept treatment to satisfy the family's wishes","Respect the patient's autonomy and allow him refuse treatment.","Seek a court order to force the patient to accept treatment","Consult with another nurse to determine the best course of action"]
exp: Respecting patient autonomy means allowing a competent patient to refuse treatment, even under family pressure.

q: Which of the following is an example of ethical dilemma in nursing?
opts: ["A nurse has to decide to report a colleague who made a medication error","A nurse has to decide whether to follow a patient's advance directive or a family's request","A nurse has to decide whether to accept a gift from a grateful patient","A nurse has to decide whether to join a union or not"]
exp: Choosing between honouring an advance directive versus a family's request is a genuine ethical dilemma.

q: What is moral distress in nursing?
opts: ["The feeling of being unable to act according to one's moral values","The feeling of being uncertain about what is morally right","The feeling of being conflicted between two or more moral values","The feeling of being harmed by someone else's immoral actions"]
exp: Moral distress is the inability to act according to one's own moral values (often due to external constraints).

q: Which of the following is an example of applying the principle of justice in nursing practice?
opts: ["Providing equal access to health care for all patients regardless of their social status","Providing individual care to all patients according to their needs and preferences","Providing compassionate care for all patients regardless of their diagnosis","Providing truthful information to all patients regardless of their ability to understand"]
exp: Providing equal healthcare access regardless of social status directly reflects the principle of justice.

q: What is informed consent in nursing?
opts: ["The process of obtaining permission from a patient to perform a specific procedure or treatment","The process of providing information to a patient about the risks and benefits of a specific procedure or treatment","The process of ensuring that a patient understands and agrees to a specific procedure or treatment","The process of respecting patients right to refuse or withdraw from a specific procedure or treatment"]
exp: Informed consent centres on the patient genuinely understanding and agreeing to a procedure.

q: Which of the following is an example of an ethical issue related to confidentiality in nursing practice?
opts: ["A nurse shares the medical record of a patient with another nurse who is not involved in the patients' care","A nurse discusses a patient's condition with the patient's family without the patient's consent","A nurse reports a suspected case of child abuse to the appropriate authority without the parent's consent","A nurse refuses to disclose a patient's HIV status to another healthcare provider who is at risk of exposure"]
exp: Sharing a record with someone not involved in care, without need, breaches confidentiality.

q: What are the rules of proper conduct among a group of people, such as religion or profession
opts: ["Morality","Morals","Values","Ethics"]
exp: Ethics refers to the accepted rules of proper conduct within a group, profession, or society.

q: The rights of health care workers are enshrined under these legislations except;
opts: ["The Nigeria 1999 Constitution","Workman Compensation Act","Penal and Criminal Code","The Medical and Dental Practitioners Act"]
exp: The Penal and Criminal Code is not among the primary legislations specifically protecting healthcare workers' rights listed here.

q: Under which of the following circumstances would you owe a legal duty of care?
opts: ["A neighbour calls you for health care advice","You notice an accident of 2 cars on the other side of the road","A visitor to the hospital collapses at a nearby bus stop","A patient in your ward has pulled out his iv cannula and is Bleeding"]
exp: A duty of care is clearly owed to a patient actively under your care in your own ward.

q: Non maleficence is concerned with:
opts: ["Doing good","Not intervening","Not doing harm","Intervening by advising"]
exp: Non-maleficence is the ethical duty to avoid causing harm.

q: Justice is concerned with:
opts: ["The law","Equality and fairness","Treating everyone the same","Good and fairness"]
exp: Justice concerns fairness and equitable treatment.

q: Autonomy is concerned with:
opts: ["Respecting patients right to be self-determining","Stopping patients to make their own decisions","Advising patients on what is right","Making sure patients make the right decision"]
exp: Autonomy is about respecting a patient's right to make their own decisions.

q: The rights of the citizens of the federal republic of Nigeria are contained in
opts: ["Chapter IV of the 1999 constitution","Chapter VI of the 1999 constitution","Chapter IV of the 1979 constitution","Chapter V of the 1999 constitution"]
exp: Fundamental citizens' rights are contained in Chapter IV of Nigeria's 1999 Constitution.

q: A health worker is working in a community which is spread out over a large rural area. She is too lazy to go to the farthest homes to invite them to participate in an environmental sanitation to campaign. This led to the lack of:
opts: ["Justice and fairness","Distributive justice","Equality and fairness","The 2 categories of justice."]
exp: Failing to reach the most remote homes fairly reflects a lapse in distributive justice.

q: Mr Moses was brought from MMW to Critical Care Unit with a disorder in which the amount of sugar in the blood is elevated. It is known as
opts: ["Type 1 diabetes mellitus","Gestational diabetes mellitus","Diabetes mellitus","Hyperglycaemia"]
exp: Elevated blood sugar in general terms is described as diabetes mellitus.

q: The Nurse define diabetes mellitus to his patient as chronic \-\-\-\-\-\-\-\--characterized by elevated levels of blood glucose, which leads over time to serious damage to the heart, blood vessels, eyes, kidneys and nerves.
opts: ["Gastrointestinal disease","Circulatory disease","Endocrine disease","Metabolic disease"]
exp: Diabetes mellitus is classified as a chronic metabolic disease.

q: The unit manager while addressing nursing students described unconsciousness as ...\...
opts: ["Is a state of awareness of yourself and your surroundings.","Is an abnormal state where client is unarousable and unresponsive.","Is a normal state where client is unarousable and unresponsive.","Is the ability to perceive sensory stimuli and respond appropriately to them."]
exp: Unconsciousness is defined as an abnormal state of being unarousable and unresponsive.

q: Diagnostic measure of peptic ulcer in critically ill patient...... EXCEPT.
opts: ["History taking.","Physical examination.","EEG.","Laboratory investigation."]
exp: EEG (brain electrical activity) is not a standard diagnostic tool for peptic ulcer, unlike history, exam, and labs/endoscopy-related investigation.

q: .......... was believed that once it lapsed, the risk of death or long-term complications will significantly increase
opts: ["Pre-hospital phase","Golden hour","Resuscitation","Emergency hour"]
exp: The "golden hour" concept holds that outcomes worsen significantly if definitive care is delayed beyond it.

q: Four victims were wheeled into emergency unit after a road traffic accident, the casualty officer & the nurse decided to prioritize care base on severity & available resources. What action is the casualty officer & the nurse does?
opts: ["Primary survey","Resuscitation","Triage","Stabilization"]
exp: Sorting patients by severity and available resources is triage.

q: A classic way to rapidly assess for airway patency is by asking the patient's name; any weakness, hoarseness, gurgling, or.........
opts: ["Coughing","Palpitation","Stridor","Contusion"]
exp: Stridor is the classic sign of airway compromise checked for during a rapid airway assessment.

q: If the nurse determines that a patient's airway is compromised the nurse can use a simple chin-lift or jaw thrust manoeuvre to:
opts: ["Re-align the airway","Stabilize the airway","Structured the airway","Re-assess the airway"]
exp: Chin-lift/jaw-thrust manoeuvres are used to stabilise/open a compromised airway.

q: A Mallam-pati score of 1 to 4 is given depending on uvular visualization. What does score III represent
opts: ["The tongue occludes the distal portion of the uvula","Full uvular visualization","The uvula is unable to be visualized","The entire uvula is occluded except for the base or proximal portion"]
exp: Mallampati Class III means only the base of the uvula is visible.

q: Patient was wheeled into ICU from A&E unit, during assessment percussing the chest bilaterally provided hyper-resonance or dullness, this is an evidence for internal injuries such as:
opts: ["Atelectasis","Pneumothorax","Asthma","Haematoma"]
exp: Hyper-resonance or dullness on chest percussion suggests underlying pathology like pneumothorax.

q: Ibrahim a CCRN is to assist a doctor to secure Endotracheal tube one of his responsibility after the procedure is
opts: ["To confirm that the suction is working properly.","To pre-oxygenates the patient.","The level of the ETT must be noted to prevent changing of position","Assist to facilitate rapid and non-traumatic intubation"]
exp: After ETT placement, noting and securing the tube's position prevents accidental displacement.

q: Which of the following radiological investigations is commonly requested in ICU?
opts: ["Chest X ray","Chest CT scan","Abdominal X ray","Skull X ray"]
exp: Chest X-ray is the most commonly requested imaging investigation in the ICU.

q: Immobility can be seen as...\... EXCEPT
opts: ["As state in which an individual has not open his bowel.","As a state of incapable of moving or being moved with ease.","As state in which individual has a limitation in independent movement.","As a state in which pt. has limitation in purposeful physical movement of the one or more lower extremities."]
exp: Not opening one's bowels is unrelated to the definition of immobility, unlike the movement-limitation descriptions.

q: Mr. Patrick exercises democracy in his teaching strategies; as such he employs any of the teaching strategies except;
opts: ["Group discussion strategy","Lecture strategy","Questions and answers strategy","Discovery (heuristic) strategy"]
exp: A straight lecture is a more one-directional method, less aligned with democratic/participatory teaching styles like discussion or discovery learning.

q: Adults approach and training are quite different from that of children. As their motivation, what they bring to the training, their expectations of it, and how they learn are different. Referring to such statement one of these is not a principle of adult education.
opts: ["Intelligence and historical background.","Self-direction and transformation (learn by doing)","Life experience and mentorship","Mental orientation (personal development) and motivation"]
exp: "Intelligence and historical background" isn't a standard adult learning principle, unlike self-direction, experience, and motivation.

q: The following are all steps involved in risk management EXCEPT:
opts: ["Identifying the risk","Risk modification","Risk analysis","Risk monitoring"]
exp: Risk modification is an outcome/action, not one of the core sequential steps (identify, analyse, monitor) of the risk management process as framed here.

q: As an ICU manager you are aware that a formal document that most supervisors or managers keep for future reference is called
opts: ["Incident report","Incident reporting","Report writing","Incident writing"]
exp: A formal record kept by supervisors for future reference describing an event is an incident report.

q: It is seen as all the arrangement and activities that are meant to safeguard, maintain and promote quality of care.
opts: ["Risk management","Nursing Audit","Quality management","Quality assurance"]
exp: Arrangements safeguarding and promoting quality of care define quality assurance.

q: You happened to be out-side of a shopping mall, two NCE students were discussing on definition of Learning, this should be:
opts: ["Permanent change in behaviour","Temporary change in behaviour","Actual/potential change in behaviour","Assimilation of events"]
exp: Learning is classically defined as a relatively permanent change in behaviour.

q: Mr. B. always prepares Gestalt psychologists because they were primarily interested in ..................................
opts: ["Trial and error","Mistakes and correction","Perception and problem solving.","Construction and decision making"]
exp: Gestalt psychologists focused on perception and problem-solving processes.

q: In this environment, learners are in control of learning process and the curriculum.
opts: ["Learner-centered learning environment","Teacher-centered learning environment","Knowledge-centered learning Environment","Content centered learning environment."]
exp: A learner-centred environment puts learners in control of their own learning process.

q: You have been instructed to deliver a health talk to a group of hypertensive patient on weight control; what will be your time estimation?
opts: ["About 1 hour","About 2 hour","About 30 minute","About 20 minute"]
exp: A health talk on a focused topic like weight control is typically estimated at about 20 minutes.

q: Mr. Liberty also mentioned some limitations of health talks and mistakenly one point was wrong. Which one was Mr Liberty's short coming?
opts: ["Health Talks need relatively little preparation.","People or client keep waiting for the sake of talk- they have their own business.","Often relatively junior staff with less knowledge and experience are left with the job of health education","Only knowledge can be taught, not skills and hard to motivate people by just talking to them."]
exp: Health talks actually require careful preparation — claiming they need "relatively little preparation" is the flawed statement.

q: Sister Lamira is an under graduate 500 level student posted to School of Nursing, Lokoja. The Deputy coordinator academics instructed her to teach 'Emergency situations course' through Drama. What will be the best method sister Lamira shall consider for the course?
opts: ["Lecture teaching strategies/method.","Demonstration teaching strategies/method.","Problem solving teaching strategies/method.","Role play teaching strategies/method."]
exp: Role play is well suited to teaching emergency scenarios in an interactive, practised way.

q: You happened to have visited your former Primary school during your annual leave, met them short staffed. As such the Head Teacher begged you to cover them with class one full of naughty pupils. Their syllabus indicates 'Hausa/Yoruba alphabets.' What will be the best teaching strategies/method for such children?
opts: ["Play way method","Lecture method","Group discussion method","Brain storming method."]
exp: Play-way method suits young children learning basic content like alphabets.

q: 45. An officer who knows what to, how to do it, when to do it and have all the resources needed and ensure achievement of an organizational goals is called:
opts: ["An administrator","Manager","Director","Coordinator"]
exp: Someone who knows what/how/when to do things and ensures goals are met is a manager.

q: It is seen as influencing or prompting the member of the organization to work together with the interest of the organization to achieve the objectives of the organisation. This statement denotes:
opts: ["Planning","Leading","Directing","Influencing"]
exp: Influencing members to work toward organisational goals together is the definition of leading.

q: The Critical care unit manager as an overall seer of the unit activities can employed one of the following as a principle of organizing the unit to achieve the organizational objectives.
opts: ["Planning","Directing","Motivation","Balance"]
exp: Balance is applied as an organising principle to keep unit activities effectively coordinated.

q: It is an establishment and implementation of policies, procedures, education and monitoring of staff adherence to reduce infection among patients, staff and visitors in ICU or hospital in general
opts: ["Infection prevention and control","Infection prevention and safety","Infection control","Infection prevention"]
exp: Establishing policies and monitoring adherence to reduce infection defines infection prevention and control.

q: Mr john a post-operative patient in ICU developed a severe fever 4 days after the surgery, this is typical of:
opts: ["Primary infection","Secondary infection","Malnutrition","Normal surgical process"]
exp: A new fever appearing several days after surgery (not immediately) suggests a secondary (hospital-acquired) infection.

q: Which nursing intervention can help prevent sharp spikes in ICP and surgical haemorrhage?
opts: ["Give fluid restriction","Help with breath exercises","Keep the head of the bed elevated","Administer an antiemetic to prevent vomiting"]
exp: Keeping the head of the bed elevated reduces ICP spikes and bleeding risk.

q: A 16 year-old is admitted to the floor after a football injury. The patient has a femur fracture. An external fixation device was applied in the operating room. The patient is at an increased risk for development of which of the following complications:
opts: ["Post-traumatic stress disorder.","Deep venous thrombosis.","Pulmonary embolism.","Pressure sore."]
exp: Immobilisation with external fixation for a long bone fracture significantly raises DVT risk.

q: A patient arrives in the emergency department with a suspected cervical spinal cord injury. Which of the following is an assessment priority?
opts: ["Abdominal assessment","Cardiac assessment","Neurological assessment","Respiratory assessment"]
exp: In suspected cervical spine injury, neurological assessment is an immediate priority alongside airway/breathing precautions.

q: The formal report containing the type of nursing care given to a patient and their response to it, as well any event or factors that may affect the patient's wellbeing is called
opts: ["Nursing documentation","Nursing implementation","Nursing care plan","Nursing records"]
exp: A formal record of nursing care given and patient response is the nursing record/documentation.

q: This type of manual ventilation bag is routinely being used during patient's resuscitation
opts: ["Self-inflating bag","Flow inflating bag","Semi-self-inflating bag","Semi-flow inflating bag"]
exp: The self-inflating bag is the standard manual resuscitation device used during CPR.

q: The most appropriate nursing intervention performed by the nurse to promote respiratory function for an unconscious patient is:
opts: ["Adequate hydration","Frequent suctioning","Head up position","Turning and positioning every 2 hours"]
exp: Elevating the head of the bed improves ventilation and reduces aspiration risk in an unconscious patient.

q: The following are immediate complications of tracheostomy EXCEPT:
opts: ["Haemorrhage","Apnoea","Cardiac arrest","Stenosis"]
exp: Tracheal stenosis is a delayed/late complication, not an immediate one, unlike bleeding, apnoea, or cardiac arrest during the procedure.

q: A nurse while conducting vital signs monitoring for a patient with enlarged thyroid detect pulse rate of 40 beat per minute. The nurse knows that this patient is having:
opts: ["Tachycardia","Heart block","Thyroid crisis","Bradycardia"]
exp: A pulse rate of 40bpm is bradycardia.

q: A nurse while conducting a physical examination on newly admitted patient observed that, the patient is photophobic, easily irritated by alarm sounds in the unit. The most likely condition of this patient is:
opts: ["Tetanus","Epilepsy","Meningitis","Severe malaria"]
exp: Photophobia and irritability to stimuli are classic meningeal irritation signs of meningitis.

q: As a nurse working in a neurosurgical ward, the nervous system can inhibit or stimulate the activities of other systems in order to maintain
opts: ["Integration","Mental balance","Homeostasis","Action potential"]
exp: The nervous system coordinates other body systems to maintain homeostasis.

q: A nurse at triage station knows that abdominal thrust is used in first aid for victims of:
opts: ["chocking and airway blockage","poisoning","Drowning","Cardiac arrest"]
exp: Abdominal thrusts are the standard first-aid manoeuvre for choking/airway obstruction.

q: Which country has the highest number of COVID-19 cases:
opts: ["Nigeria","United Kingdom","United states of America","Saudi Arabia"]
exp: The United States has recorded the highest cumulative COVID-19 case numbers globally.

q: The index case of COVID-19 was first recorded in Nigeria on:
opts: ["27th January 2019","27th February 2019","27th January 2020","27th February 2020"]
exp: Nigeria's index COVID-19 case was recorded on 27th February 2020.

q: A critical care nurse conducting a last office procedure knows that, the stage of death characterised by stiffness of the muscles of the limbs due to chemical changes is termed as:
opts: ["Cessational stiffness","Mortis rehabilis","Rigor mortis","Body stiffness"]
exp: Rigor mortis is the post-mortem stiffening of muscles from chemical changes.

q: In accordance with American Heart Association resuscitation 2022 guide-line, the current concept of cardio-pulmonary resuscitation is:
opts: ["ABCD","ABCDHHH","CABD","HHHABCD"]
exp: Current AHA guidance emphasises compressions-first (CAB) sequencing in CPR.

q: As a nurse performing cardio-pulmonary resuscitation on a cardiac arrest patient, you are aware that the correct cardiac compression to optimised cardiac function is:
opts: ["100 Compressions per minute","110 compressions per minute","115 compressions per minute","120 Compressions per minute"]
exp: The recommended compression rate is about 120 compressions per minute (within the 100-120 range, closest to the given option).

q: The critical care nurse while health educating patient with cardiac problem always emphases the definition of cardiac arrest as a problem of:
opts: ["Conducting system of the heart","Heart muscles","Heart valve","Coronary artery"]
exp: Cardiac arrest is fundamentally a problem of the heart's electrical conducting system.

q: Mr Simon a 65-year old is visiting a clinic for prostate cancer screening. As a nurse the best investigation for Mr Simon is:
opts: ["Creatinine kinase (CK)","Blood urea nitrogen (BUN)","Aspartate aminotransferase (AST)","Prostate specific antigen (PSA)"]
exp: PSA is the standard screening investigation for prostate cancer.

q: A client with superficial burns of the chest, face, back and arms requires an urgent care. Which of the following should be the nurse primary concern?
opts: ["Fluid resuscitation","Infection control","Body image","Pain management"]
exp: Fluid resuscitation is the priority for a burns patient given the massive fluid shifts involved.

q: The following parameters should be checked when assessing respiration EXCEPT:
opts: ["Rate","Rhythm","Symmetry","Height"]
exp: Height is unrelated to respiratory assessment, unlike rate, rhythm, and symmetry.

q: The following are effects of burns EXCEPT:
opts: ["Shock","Infection","Heat","Deformity"]
exp: Heat itself is the causative agent of a burn, not one of its resulting effects (shock, infection, deformity).

q: Master James received a zero dose of COVD-19 vaccine, this indicates that:
opts: ["He has no vaccination","Dose after the first vaccine","No antibody response","Vaccination at displaced people's camp"]
exp: A "zero dose" child is one who has received no vaccination at all.

q: Aminophylline (theophylline) is prescribed for a client with acute bronchitis. A nurse administers the medication, knowing that the primary action of this medication is to:
opts: ["Promote expectoration.","Suppress the cough.","Relax smooth muscles of the bronchial airway.","Prevent infection."]
exp: Aminophylline's primary action is relaxing bronchial smooth muscle (bronchodilation).

q: A female client is scheduled to have a chest radiograph. Which of the following questions is of most importance to the nurse assessing this client?
opts: ["\"Is there any possibility that you could be pregnant?\"","\"Are you wearing any metal chains or jewellery?\"","\"Can you hold your breath easily?\"","\"Are you able to hold your arms above your head?\""]
exp: Pregnancy status must be checked before any radiographic exposure due to radiation risk to the fetus.

q: 16 second, and QRS complexes measure 0.06 second. The overall heart rate is 64 beats per minute. The nurse assesses the cardiac rhythm as:
opts: ["Normal sinus rhythm","Sinus bradycardia","Sick sinus syndrome","First-degree heart block"]
exp: Normal PR/QRS intervals with a rate of 64 (within normal range) describe normal sinus rhythm.

q: A client is scheduled for a cardiac catheterization using a radiopaque dye. Which of the following assessments is most critical before the procedure?
opts: ["Intake and output","Baseline peripheral pulse rates","Height and weight","Allergy to iodine or shellfish"]
exp: Checking for iodine/shellfish allergy is critical before administering iodine-based radiopaque contrast.

q: Which of the following actions is the first priority of care for a client exhibiting signs and symptoms of coronary artery disease?
opts: ["Decrease anxiety.","Enhance myocardial oxygenation.","Administer sublingual nitro-glycerine.","Educate the client about his symptoms."]
exp: Enhancing myocardial oxygenation (e.g. via oxygen/nitrates) is the immediate priority in suspected coronary artery disease.

q: A nurse is preparing for the admission of a client with heart failure who is being sent directly to the hospital from the physician's office. The nurse would plan on having which of the following medication readily available for use?
opts: ["diltiazem (Cardizem)","digoxin (Lanoxin)","propranolol (Inderal)","metoprolol (Lopressor)"]
exp: Digoxin is a standard medication kept ready for heart failure management.

q: ## CCNP EXAM - PAPER II.docx Histamine, a mediator that supports the inflammatory process in asthma is secreted by:
opts: ["Eosinophils","Lymphocytes","Mast cells","Neutrophils"]
exp: Mast cells release histamine as part of the allergic/inflammatory response in asthma.

q: A commonly prescribed mast cell stabilizer used for asthma is:
opts: ["Albuterol","Budesonide","Cromolyn sodium","Theophylline"]
exp: Cromolyn sodium is a classic mast cell stabiliser used in asthma prevention.

q: If you make a mistake in a record how should it be corrected?
opts: ["With correction fluid","Scribble it out","Put a single line through the record","Tell your mentor"]
exp: A documentation error is corrected with a single line through it (never obscured or erased).

q: A nurse has just received an order from a physician to administer a medication to a patient. The nurse knows that this medication is contraindicated for the patient based on their medical history. What should the nurse do?
opts: ["Administer the medication as ordered","Hold the medication and notify the physician of the contraindication","Administer the medication at a lower dose","Consult with another physician to confirm the order"]
exp: A contraindicated order must be held and the prescriber notified before proceeding.

q: Which of the following is an ethical principle that guides nursing practice?
opts: ["Autonomy","Utilitarianism","Virtue","Care"]
exp: Autonomy is one of the core ethical principles guiding nursing practice.

q: What is the ANA Code of Ethics for Nurses?
opts: ["A set of legal rules that nurses must follow","A set of moral values that nurses must uphold","A set of professional standards that nurses must adhere to","A set of ethical frameworks that nurses must apply"]
exp: The ANA Code of Ethics sets the professional standards nurses are expected to follow.

q: Which of the following is an example of an ethical dilemma in nursing?
opts: ["A nurse has to decide whether to report a colleague who made a medication error","A nurse has to decide whether to follow a patient's advance directive or a family's request","A nurse has to decide whether to accept a gift from a grateful patient","A nurse has to decide whether to join a union or not"]
exp: Choosing between a patient's advance directive and a family's competing wishes is a classic ethical dilemma.

q: What is moral distress in nursing?
opts: ["The feeling of being unable to act according to one's moral values","The feeling of being uncertain about what is morally right","The feeling of being conflicted between two or more moral values","The feeling of being harmed by someone else's immoral actions"]
exp: Moral distress is the inability to act according to one's own moral values, often due to constraints.

q: Which of the following is an example of applying the principle of justice in nursing practice?
opts: ["Providing equal access to health care for all patients regardless of their social status","Providing individualized care for each patient according to their needs and preferences","Providing compassionate care for all patients regardless of their diagnosis or prognosis","Providing truthful information to all patients regardless of their ability to understand"]
exp: Providing equal healthcare access regardless of social status reflects the principle of justice.

q: What is informed consent in nursing practice?
opts: ["The process of obtaining permission from a patient to perform a specific procedure or treatment","The process of providing information to a patient about the risks and benefits of a specific procedure or treatment","The process of ensuring that a patient understands and agrees to a specific procedure or treatment","The process of respecting a patient's right to refuse or withdraw from a specific procedure or treatment"]
exp: Informed consent means the patient genuinely understands and agrees to the procedure.

q: Which of the following best describes moral uncertainty?
opts: ["A situation where nurses have no ethical guidance or rules to follow","A situation where nurses have conflicting ethical guidance or rules to follow","A situation where nurses have doubts or uncertainties about the ethical course of action","A situation where nurses are unable to make ethical decisions due to lack of knowledge or experience"]
exp: Moral uncertainty is doubt about which ethical course of action is right.

q: A failure in professional duty, practice or skill that leads to injury or harm to the patient is termed as-
opts: ["Malpractice","Negligence","Crime","Misunderstanding"]
exp: A professional failure causing patient harm is malpractice.

q: What should the nurse do when planning nursing care for a client with a different cultural background? The nurse should:
opts: ["Allow the family to provide care during the hospital stays so no rituals or customs are broken","Identify how these cultural variables affect the health problem","Speak slowly and show pictures to make sure the client always understands","Explain how the client must adapt to hospital routines to be effectively cared for while in the hospital"]
exp: Identifying how cultural factors affect the health problem guides individualised, culturally sensitive care.

q: The nurse is obligated to follow a physician's order unless:
opts: ["The order is a verbal order","The physicians order is illegible","The order has not been transcribed","The order is an error, violates hospital policy, or would be detrimental to the client."]
exp: An order should be questioned/refused if it is erroneous, against policy, or harmful to the patient.

q: What is the most common source of malpractice for nurses?
opts: ["HIPPA complaints","Negligent delegation","Privacy violations","Medication errors"]
exp: Medication errors are the most commonly cited source of nursing malpractice claims.

q: The Good Samaritan act covers:
opts: ["Residents of long-term care facilities","Passerby in car accidents","Protection from civil liability when emergency care is provided in good faith","Nurses who choose not to provide care in an emergency"]
exp: The Good Samaritan act protects those who provide good-faith emergency care from civil liability.

q: A nurse was sued for failing to perform a standard of care during her patient assignment. This is an example of.......
opts: ["A. Abandonment","B. Lack of confidentiality","C. Negligence based on a breach of duty","D. Bad judgment"]
exp: Failing to meet the expected standard of care is negligence based on breach of duty.

q: As per code of ethics in nursing profession, what is the responsibility of a nurse towards his profession-
opts: ["She should provide nursing care and earn money","She should have sufficient knowledge regarding all the nursing procedures","She should try to maintain highest standard of nursing profession","She should provide nursing care to people without taking money"]
exp: Maintaining the highest professional standard is a nurse's core duty to their profession.

q: Which of the following organizations requires a mechanism for addressing ethical issues?
opts: ["American Association of Critical-Care Nurses","American Hospital Association","Society of Critical Care Medicine","The Joint Commission"]
exp: The Joint Commission requires healthcare organisations to have a mechanism for addressing ethical issues.

q: A critically-ill patient has a "Living Will" in the chart. The patient\'s condition has deteriorated, but the spouse wants \"everything done\" regardless of the patient\'s wishes. Which ethical principle is the spouse violating?
opts: ["Autonomy","Beneficence","Justice","Non-maleficence"]
exp: Overriding a patient's documented wishes (Living Will) disregards their autonomy.

q: A diabetic patient on iv normal saline was discovered to have random blood sugar RBS of 2.5mmol/l, which of this action will you take as critical care Nurse?
opts: ["Send for the physician and wait for his order","Stop the fluid and wait for the Physician","Change the fluid to glucose containing fluid","Change to ringers' lactate"]
exp: A dangerously low RBS of 2.5mmol/L on normal saline needs correction with a glucose-containing fluid.

q: Mr kifakat is on mechanical ventilator in ICU with a diagnosis of Brain death, what is his GCS
exp: Brain death corresponds to the lowest possible GCS score of 3/15.

q: Gastrointestinal side effect of obesity includes
opts: ["Cholelithiasis","jaundice","Vomiting","Diarrhea"]
exp: Obesity is a recognised risk factor for gallstones (cholelithiasis).

q: Formula for calculating BMI is
opts: ["Weight/height2","Weight2/body surface area","Height2/weight","Body surface area/weight2"]
exp: BMI is calculated as weight divided by height squared.

q: Which of the following increases the risk of cardiovascular disorders in patient with obesity?
opts: ["Osteoporosis","Excess abdominal fat","Psychological disorders","Gastric ulcer"]
exp: Excess abdominal (visceral) fat specifically raises cardiovascular risk in obesity.

q: Insulin deficiency is associated with
opts: ["Reduce lipolysis","Increase ketogenesis","Reduce gluconeogenesis","Reduce proteolysis"]
exp: Insulin deficiency drives increased ketogenesis as the body shifts to fat metabolism.

q: In treating hypoglycemia, which of the following is wrong?
opts: ["In the absence of 50% dextrose normal saline is preferred","Target is to ingest 20-30g of glucose","50ml of 50% dextrose in double dilution","Stop all oral hypoglycemic until patient is stable"]
exp: 50% dextrose is actually preferred/standard when treating severe hypoglycaemia, so avoiding it is the incorrect statement.

q: Monogenic causes of obesity include all except
opts: ["Leptin deficiency","Leptin receptor deficiency","Melanocortin 4 receptor deficiency","Gremlin deficiency"]
exp: "Gremlin deficiency" is not a recognised monogenic obesity cause, unlike leptin/receptor and MC4R deficiencies.

q: Basic treatment of acute pancreatitis includes early fluid resuscitation, analgesia, and which of the following
opts: ["Antibiotics","ERCP","NPO (Bowel rest)","Total parental nutrition"]
exp: Bowel rest (NPO) is a standard basic component of acute pancreatitis management.

q: Which of the following recognized causes of DKA is most common?
opts: ["Missed insulin treatment","Newly diagnosed diabetic","Underlying infection","Stress"]
exp: Underlying infection is the most common precipitant of DKA.

q: All of the following are common characteristics of nephrotic syndrome except:
opts: ["A. Hyperlipidemia","B. Hypoalbuminemia","C. Hematuria and pyuria","D. Severe edema"]
exp: Haematuria/pyuria are not typical nephrotic syndrome features, which is classically non-inflammatory proteinuria.

q: Which of the following is the anticoagulant of choice for blood gas studies?
opts: ["A. Sodium citrate 3.2%","B. Lithium heparin","C. Sodium citrate 3.8%","D. Ammonium oxalate 5.0%"]
exp: Lithium heparin is the preferred anticoagulant for blood gas sampling.

q: What ABO types may donate to ALL other ABO types?
opts: ["A. A negative, B negative, AB negative, O negative,","B. O negative","C. AB negative","D. AB negative, A negative, B negative"]
exp: O negative is the universal red cell donor, able to donate to all ABO/Rh types.

q: Severity of Anemia are categorized into:
opts: ["a\\. Mild anemia, Moderate anemia and severe anemia","Mild anemia, toxic and Severe anemia","Mould anemia, Moderate anemia and Severe anemia","None of the above"]
exp: Anaemia severity is classified as mild, moderate, or severe.

q: Which of the following is characteristic of type 1 diabetes mellitus?
opts: ["A. Requires an oral glucose tolerance test for diagnosis","B. Is the most common form of diabetes mellitus","C. Usually occurs after age 40","D. Requires insulin replacement to prevent ketosis"]
exp: Type 1 diabetes requires insulin replacement to prevent ketosis, unlike Type 2.

q: Which of the following conditions is associated with hyponatremia?
opts: ["A. Diuretic therapy","B. Cushing's syndrome","C. Diabetes insipidus","D. Nephrotic syndrome"]
exp: Diuretic therapy is a recognised cause of hyponatraemia through sodium loss.

q: In management of bleeding due to placenta Previa, a woman can\...
opts: ["Have emergency C-section or admission for bed rest","Deliver the baby vaginally","Be sent home","Be admitted to the female surgical word"]
exp: Placenta praevia bleeding is managed with emergency C-section or admission for bed rest depending on severity.

q: The following are some of the complications of placenta Previa except
opts: ["Major haemorrhage","Shock from loss of blood","Normal vaginal delivery","Foetal distress from lack of oxygen"]
exp: Normal vaginal delivery is not a complication — it's actually often contraindicated in praevia.

q: How many types of abruption placenta are there?
opts: ["Five","Three","Four","One"]
exp: Abruptio placentae is classified into three types/grades.

q: The following are some of the signs and symptoms of abruptio placenta except
opts: ["Uterine tenderness","Decreased fatal movement","Abdominal pain","None of the options"]
exp: "None of the options" is wrong because uterine tenderness, decreased fetal movement, and abdominal pain are all real signs.

q: What are the two factors to be considered in management of abruptio placenta?
opts: ["The severity of the abruption and the gestational age of the foetus","The age of the mother","Convenience","The patients cultural background"]
exp: Management of abruption depends on both severity of the abruption and fetal gestational age.

q: What is cervical incompetence?
opts: ["When the service is firm and soft","Inability of the uterine cervix to retain a pregnancy in the second trimester","When the cervix remains closed","All of the options"]
exp: Cervical incompetence is the inability of the cervix to retain a pregnancy through the second trimester.

q: \...\...\...\...\...\... is a procedure done to close the cervix with strong suture
opts: ["Cervical Cerclage","Cervical suture","Cervical closer","All of the options"]
exp: Cervical cerclage is the suture procedure used to close an incompetent cervix.

q: \...\...\...\...\...\... is one of the risk factor of cervical incompetence
opts: ["Cervical trauma","Cervical tear during a previous labour","Previous vaginal delivery","None of the above"]
exp: Cervical trauma is a recognised risk factor for cervical incompetence.

q: One of the following is a prevention of cervical incompetence
opts: ["Seeking regular parental care","Weight gain","Smoking","Not having regular check-up during pregnancy"]
exp: Regular antenatal care helps detect and manage cervical incompetence early.

q: In Incompetence cervix, the cervix begins to open\...\...\...\...\...\...\...\...\...\...\...\...\...\.....
opts: ["Too soon","When labour start","After labour begins","All of the options"]
exp: In cervical incompetence, the cervix dilates prematurely, too soon before term.

q: What are the symptoms of premature rapture of membrane?
opts: ["Feeling a gush of fluid from mother's vagina","Slow leak or trickle of fluid","The fluid is clear and odourless","All of the options"]
exp: PROM symptoms include a gush or slow leak of clear, odourless fluid — all listed features apply.

q: Acute withdrawal symptoms can linger for at least
opts: ["A year","Months","Days","Some years"]
exp: Acute alcohol withdrawal symptoms can persist for as long as a year in some cases.

q: The following are things to consider in severe alcohol withdrawal syndrome except.
opts: ["Degree of alcohol intake, length of time drinking alcohol, previous history of alcohol withdrawal","Alcohol hallucinations of all types, delirium tremens, withdrawal seizures.","Tremor, agitation hyperactivity and insomnia","Severe confusion, seizures, high blood pressure."]
exp: Tremor, agitation, and insomnia are early/mild features, not part of the severe-withdrawal risk factors being asked about here.

q: Some examples that can cause stress includes the following except.
opts: ["Trying to balance work and parenting","Death or illness of a loved one","Loss of asset e.g. Fire disaster","None of the above"]
exp: All of the listed events (work-parenting balance, bereavement, loss of assets) are recognised stressors, so "none of the above" is wrong.

q: The following are effects of stress on families except.
opts: ["Substance over use","Family discord","Health problems","Difficulties outside the home"]
exp: "Difficulties outside the home" is not specifically listed as a family stress effect, unlike substance use, discord, and health problems.

q: Symptoms of Alcohol withdrawal became a global problem in the
exp: Alcohol withdrawal was recognised as a major public health problem beginning in the 1700s in historical accounts referenced here.

q: Human psyche means
opts: ["The human mind","The human behavior","The human emotions","The human personality"]
exp: Psyche refers to the human mind.

q: One of the following is untrue about acute bladder injuries
opts: ["Most intraperitoneal bladder injuries can be effectively managed with maximal bladder drainage per urethral or suprapubic catheter.","Urethral trauma should be excluded before inserting catheter.","Bladder should be drained for 10-14 days and healing assessed with cysto-gram before removing catheter","Blunt trauma represents 60-85% of injuries to the bladder"]
exp: Blunt trauma actually accounts for the majority of bladder injuries, making that statement true rather than the false one.

q: As part of nursing interventions to promote optimal fluid balance in patient with benign prostatic hyperplasia, the following measures are necessary except:
opts: ["Monitoring electrolytes level to avoid hyponatremia","Monitoring of blood pressure and pulse for early detection of hypovolemia","Monitoring of urine output and alerting physician of outputs of 100-200 ml per hour","Encouraging Sitz bath and warm soaks to the perineum to promote muscle relaxation."]
exp: Sitz baths/warm soaks address comfort, not fluid balance — it doesn't belong among fluid-balance interventions.

q: A quadriplegic patient with cervical injury is just admitted in ICU, which of this is of priority to you?
opts: ["Cardiac arrest","Respiratory arrest","Brain death","Multiple organs dysfunction syndrome"]
exp: A high cervical spinal injury directly threatens the diaphragm/respiratory muscles, making respiratory arrest the top priority.

q: Which of this nursing diagnosis will come first in the management of unconscious patient in ICU?
opts: ["Risk for altered skin integrity related to Immobility","Self-care deficit related to conferment to bed","Ineffective airway clearance related to loss of swallowing reflex and inability to control airway.","All of the options"]
exp: Loss of airway control from absent swallow/gag reflex is the most immediately life-threatening problem in an unconscious patient.

q: Which of the following statements is false regarding pneumothorax?
opts: ["It appears as area of hypertransradiancy devoid of lung markings","Mediastinum is shifted to the opposite side","Mediastinum is shifted to the side of lesion","It may be a complication of lung biopsy"]
exp: In pneumothorax, the mediastinum shifts AWAY from (not toward) the affected side — that statement is false.

q: Mediastinal change seen in massive pleural effusion on a chest radiograph
opts: ["Shift of mediastinum to the site of effusion","Central mediastinum","Shift of the mediastinum to the opposite","Tracheal shift to the site of lesion"]
exp: A massive pleural effusion pushes the mediastinum toward the opposite (unaffected) side.

q: The following statement is not true regarding Intestinal obstruction
opts: ["Can affect the small or large bowel loop","Causes depend on age","The non-mechanical can be focal or generalized","Affects only the small bowel."]
exp: Intestinal obstruction can affect either the small or large bowel, not only the small bowel.

q: The following Radiological Imaging Procedures can be done on the Bed-Side Except:
opts: ["Plain radiography","Ultrasonography","Computed Tomography","Doppler Sonography"]
exp: CT scanning requires a fixed suite and cannot be done at the bedside, unlike portable X-ray/ultrasound/Doppler.

q: Chest X-ray study is best taken in:
opts: ["Upright PA","Supine AP position","Lateral Decubitus position","Semi-erect AP position"]
exp: An upright PA chest X-ray gives the most accurate, least distorted view.

q: One of the following is a potential pressure point in bed ridden patient
opts: ["Stomach","Gluteal region","Knee joint","Anterior chest wall"]
exp: The gluteal/sacral region is a classic pressure point in bedridden patients.

q: Physiotherapy is an independent health care profession that involves
opts: ["Health promotion","Disease prevention","Rehabilitation","All of the options"]
exp: Physiotherapy spans health promotion, disease prevention, and rehabilitation together.

q: Acetylcholine is a....
opts: ["Neurotransmitter","Neuro-producer","Drugs","None of the above"]
exp: Acetylcholine is a neurotransmitter.

q: All the following are uses of manual hyperinflation except
opts: ["To increase lung volume","Aid in clearance of secretions","It decreases blood flow","Increase lung compliance"]
exp: Manual hyperinflation increases (not decreases) blood flow/lung recruitment as part of its effect.

q: Indication for manual hyperinflation
opts: ["To reduce hyper oxygenation","To alter lung compliance","Clearance of retained secretions and mucous plugging","Recruitment of areas of lung compliance"]
exp: Manual hyperinflation is indicated to help clear retained secretions and mucous plugging.

q: Skeletal muscles disorder commonly occurs following sepsis, mobility restriction, hyperglycemia and neuromuscular blocking agents is termed
opts: ["Critical illness polyneuropathy","Critical illness myopathy","Critical illness neuropathy","Critical care acquired weakness"]
exp: This combination of risk factors causing generalized ICU weakness is termed critical illness/critical care acquired weakness.

q: Modifiable risk factors of critical care acquired weakness
opts: ["Hypoglycemia","Low dose antihypertensive","Neuromuscular junction blockers","72 hours bed rest"]
exp: Neuromuscular blocking agents are a recognised modifiable risk factor for critical illness weakness.

q: The following are true concerning management of patient with DIC except
opts: ["Plasma transfusion","Heparin infusion","Platelet concentrate transfusion","Bone marrow transplant"]
exp: Bone marrow transplant is not a standard DIC treatment, unlike plasma, heparin, or platelet transfusion in select cases.

q: Which of this genotype is likely susceptible to malaria?
opts: ["AA Homozygote","AS Homozygote","SS Homozygote","SC homozygote"]
exp: Normal AA haemoglobin genotype confers the greatest susceptibility to malaria (no sickle trait protection).

q: The life span of Sickled red blood cells is about? 120 days 90 days 60days 20 days Which of this is not an emergency?
opts: ["Severe eclampsia","RBS of \\< 4mmol/l","Sickle cell disease patient with baseline PCV of 10%","Pulmonary oedema"]
exp: A stable chronic anaemia (baseline PCV of 10%) in known sickle cell disease is not itself a new emergency, unlike the acute conditions listed.

q: A patient is admitted to ICU with diagnosis of vaso-occlusive crisis, which of this management will you start with?
opts: ["Transfuse with fresh whole blood","Order for narcotics and administer","Rehydrate with normal saline","Give oral rehydration solution"]
exp: Aggressive rehydration with normal saline is the first step in managing vaso-occlusive crisis.
