q: In the management of leukemia with pan-cytopaenia which of this is the best intervention?
opts: ["Give intravenous normal saline","Transfuse with blood with high PCV","Transfuse with fresh whole blood","Give platelet only"]
exp: Fresh whole blood transfusion helps correct the pancytopenia complications of leukaemia.

q: In the management of DIC which of this should the Nurse anticipate?
opts: ["Susceptible to infection","Anaemia","Risk for bleeding","Coagulation"]
exp: DIC carries a major risk of bleeding due to consumption of clotting factors/platelets.

q: Urine output of 0.5 to 1m/kg/hour is?
opts: ["Anuria","Polyuria","Normal urine output","Oliguria"]
exp: 0.5-1ml/kg/hr is within the normal expected urine output range.

q: Contemporary theories of leadership depend on several variables except:
opts: ["Organizational culture.","Values of the parents","Values of the followers.","Complexities of the situation."]
exp: "Values of the parents" is not a recognised leadership theory variable, unlike organisational culture, follower values, and situational complexity.

q: Which of this is not part of a manager function as resource manager?
opts: ["Personnel","Time","Capital projects","Supplies"]
exp: Personnel management falls under human resource/staff management, not specifically the 'resource manager' capital/supply/time function described here.

q: Which of this budget is not part types of budget?
opts: ["Personnel budget","Operational budget","Capital budget","Supplementary budget"]
exp: "Supplementary budget" is not one of the standard recognised budget types listed (personnel, operational, capital).

q: Caring for the staff members can be demonstrated through except:
opts: ["Offering sincere positive recognition for both individuals and teams.","Protecting the staff against treatment error to avoid dismissal","Praising and giving thanks for a job well done.","Spending time with staff members to reinforce positive work behaviors."]
exp: Shielding staff from accountability for errors is not genuine staff care — it undermines safety and standards.

q: Management can be seen as follows except
opts: ["An authority","A process","A profession","A factor of production"]
exp: Management is described as a process, profession, and factor of production — not as "an authority" per se.

q: Administrative Management Theory was theorized by?
opts: ["Abrahams law","Virginia Henderson","Henry Fayol","Florence nightingale"]
exp: Henry Fayol is credited with Administrative Management Theory.

q: In Theory X and Theory Y management which of this is correct?
opts: ["Theory X is negative","Theory X is Positive","Theory Y is negative","Theory X & Y are neutral"]
exp: Theory X assumes workers are inherently negative/unmotivated, requiring control.

q: Which of this is not a method of infection prevention in ICU?
opts: ["Use of personal towel to dry hand after washing","Use of ICU public towel to dry hand after washing","Use of public hand sanitizer in ICU","Use of personal hand sanitizer"]
exp: Shared/public towels risk cross-contamination, unlike personal towels or hand sanitiser use.

q: A nurse is caring for a patient who is refusing treatment for their condition. The patient's family is pressuring the nurse to persuade the patient to accept treatment. What should the nurse do?
opts: ["Persuade the patient to accept treatment to satisfy the family's wishes.","Respect the patient's autonomy and allow them to refuse treatment.","Seek a court order to force the patient to accept treatment.","Consult with another nurse to determine the best course of action."]
exp: Respecting the patient's autonomy to refuse treatment is the correct ethical response, even under family pressure.

q: Which ethical framework emphasizes caring as both an emotion and an action that guides moral decision making?
opts: ["Deontology","Utilitarianism","Virtue ethics","Care ethics"]
exp: Care ethics specifically frames caring as both an emotional and active guide to moral decisions.

q: A document that lists the medical treatment a person chooses to refuse if unable to make decisions is the:
opts: ["Durable power of attorney","Informed consent","Living will","Advance directives"]
exp: An advance directive is the document specifying treatments a person chooses to refuse if incapacitated.

q: The Critical Care Nurse Manager knows that, the art of getting an organizational activities done through other people using available resources is called.
opts: ["A. Leadership","B. Governance","C. Management","D. Delegation"]
exp: Achieving goals through others using available resources defines management.

q: He was the first to systematically describe management behavior and also pioneered the identification of the principles and skills that underlie effective management.
opts: ["A. Henri Fayol","B. Max waber","C. F. Taylor","D. Gashinge et all"]
exp: Henri Fayol pioneered systematic descriptions of management principles.

q: In mechanical ventilation, what does PEEP stand for?
opts: ["A. Positive end-expiratory pressure","B. Partial exhalation and expiratory pressure","C. Peak exhaled expiratory pressure","D. Positive extra thoracic end-positioning"]
exp: PEEP stands for Positive End-Expiratory Pressure.

q: This describe a woman who has never been pregnant before.
opts: ["A. Nulligravida","B. Primipgravida","C. Primipara","D. Multigravida"]
exp: A woman who has never been pregnant is a nulligravida.

q: Mr John a post-operative patient in ICU developed a severe fever 4 days after the surgery, this is typical of:
opts: ["A. Primary infection","B. Secondary infection","C. Malnutrition","D. Normal surgical process"]
exp: A new fever appearing days after surgery (not immediately) suggests a secondary (hospital-acquired) infection.

q: About Wallace rule of 9 which of this is not correct?
opts: ["Head and neck 9%","Perineum 1%","Anterior trunk 9%","Anterior upper limb 41/2%"]
exp: The anterior upper limb is counted as 9% (not 4.5%) under the Rule of Nines — 4.5% applies to a single lower limb segment or half a limb, making this option the incorrect one as framed.

q: Preoperative preparation of pressure ulcer
opts: ["Bowel preparation","Informed consent","Patient counselling","All of the options"]
exp: Preoperative pressure ulcer management encompasses bowel prep, consent, and counselling together.

q: Intrinsic factors in pressure ulcer
opts: ["Moisture","Advanced age","Malnutrition","Friction"]
exp: Advanced age is a patient-intrinsic risk factor for pressure ulcers, unlike moisture/friction which are extrinsic.

q: Immediate measures for the burn patient are all except:
opts: ["remove from source of injury","Irrigate with soap and water","remove all non-sticking clothing","cover with sheet or blanket"]
exp: Irrigating a burn with soap and water is not standard immediate first aid, unlike removing the source, clothing, and covering the wound.

q: The following are fluid resuscitation regimen for burns except:
opts: ["Evans","Warden","Minato","Brooke"]
exp: "Minato" is not a recognised burns fluid resuscitation formula, unlike Evans, Parkland/Baxter, and Brooke.

q: About prevention of pressure ulcers
opts: ["Clean and dry skin regularly","Remove particulate matter from beneath the patient","Pentazocine, Propofol to relieve spasms","Use of skin emollients for skin hydration"]
exp: Keeping skin clean and dry is a core pressure ulcer prevention measure.

q: Parenteral nutrition delivers the following nutrients EXCEPT
opts: ["carbohydrates","Protein","Lipids","Vitamin A"]
exp: Vitamin A is a micronutrient, not one of the core macronutrient categories (carbohydrate/protein/lipid) delivered in standard parenteral nutrition formulations here.

q: The decision to transfuse blood or blood component should be based on laboratory tests assessment as well as\....
opts: ["Clinical assessment","The use of red cells in surgery","Patient record of admission","Patient weight"]
exp: Transfusion decisions should combine lab results with the patient's overall clinical assessment, not labs alone.

q: Which one of the following test is used to monitor a Diabetes Mellitus patient?
opts: ["Creatinine test","Total protein","Alkaline phosphatase","Fasting Blood Sugar"]
exp: Fasting blood sugar is the standard test used to monitor diabetes control.

q: The following are Symptoms and Signs of anaemia except:
opts: ["weakness","Headache","dizziness","loss of weight"]
exp: Weight loss is a possible chronic finding but not a core classic acute sign like weakness, headache, or dizziness.

q: What type(s) of red cells is (are) acceptable to transfuse to an O-negative patient?
opts: ["A negative","B negative","AB negative","O negative"]
exp: O-negative patients can only safely receive O-negative red cells (the universal donor type, but O-negative recipients need matched O-negative).

q: Which is the major Hgb found in the RBCs of patients with sickle cell trait?
opts: ["Hgb S","Hgb F","Hgb A2","Hgb A"]
exp: Hgb S is the predominant abnormal haemoglobin present in sickle cell trait carriers.

q: All the following are risks of bedsore except
opts: ["Prolonged pressure on tissue","Immobility, compromised mobility","Correct application of pressure relieving devices","Poor lifting techniques"]
exp: Correct use of pressure-relieving devices reduces (not causes) bedsore risk — that is the exception among true risk factors.

q: These are all pressure area points except
opts: ["Occiput","Scapula","Thigh","Sacrum"]
exp: The thigh is not a classic bony pressure point, unlike occiput, scapula, and sacrum.

q: In medical management of bedsores all the following are carried out except
opts: ["Removing dead tissue","Cleaning the wound","Closing the edges as far as possible","None of the options"]
exp: Bedsore wounds are typically left open to heal (not surgically closed) in standard medical management.

q: On discharge patients with bedsores is being advised on the following
opts: ["To rest lying on the same position for a long time","Taking good care of their skin, maintaining good nutrition and fluid intake","Not exercising","To message the skin"]
exp: Good skin care, nutrition, and hydration are core discharge advice for bedsore prevention/healing.

q: How many points are there in Braden scale basic assessment tool to determine a patient risk for pressure ulcers
exp: The Braden scale pressure-ulcer risk assessment tool has 6 subscales/points.

q: Placenta Previa as one of the obstetric complication is when the placenta is at \....
opts: ["At the anterior upper part of the uterus","Posterior part of the uterus","Partially or completely covers the cervix","None of the options"]
exp: Placenta praevia is defined by the placenta partially or completely covering the cervix.

q: The main types of placenta Previa are
opts: ["Normal low implantation/partial and total placenta Previa","Partial, and normal low implantation","Partial placenta Previa","Total placenta Previa"]
exp: Placenta praevia types include low implantation, partial, and total praevia.

q: One of the treatment of placenta Previa without bleeding
opts: ["Emergency C-section","Deliver the baby vaginally","Admit mother","Promote bed rest"]
exp: Without active bleeding, bed rest is the standard conservative management approach.

q: If placenta fails to come away from uterine lining
opts: ["Hysterectomy is done","Laparotomy is done","The placenta is delivered vaginally","None of the options"]
exp: A retained placenta that fails to separate may require hysterectomy in severe cases.

q: The risk of placenta Previa can be reduced by
opts: ["Not smoking or using drugs","Report any vaginal bleeding","Report any abdominal trauma","All of the options"]
exp: Avoiding smoking, drug use, and prompt reporting of bleeding/trauma together reduce praevia-related risk.

q: These are complications of incompetence cervix except
opts: ["Premature birth","Pregnancy loss","Uterine rupture","Still birth"]
exp: Uterine rupture is not a typical complication of cervical incompetence itself, unlike preterm birth or pregnancy loss.

q: Premature rapture of membranes is when the membrane raptures
opts: ["During labour","Before labour begins","After labour","All of the options"]
exp: PROM is defined as membrane rupture occurring before labour begins.

q: A baby that is delivered 34 to 37 weeks due to premature rapture of membrane is cared for in\....
opts: ["I.C.U","N.I.C.U","Obstetric word","S.C.B.U"]
exp: A baby born 34-37 weeks due to PROM is typically cared for in the special care baby unit (SCBU).

q: What are the risk factors for premature rapture of the membrane?
opts: ["History of premature rapture of membrane","Vaginal bleeding","History of premature labour","All of the options"]
exp: History of PROM, vaginal bleeding, and prior preterm labour are all recognised risk factors.

q: What is the best thing one can do to prevent premature rapture of membrane?
opts: ["Avoid smoking cigarettes","Maintain a healthy pregnancy","Attend all prenatal clinic","All of the options"]
exp: Combining smoking avoidance, healthy pregnancy, and prenatal care together is the best preventive approach.

q: The following are types of pleural fluid based on the content except
opts: ["Hydrothorax","Pyeothorax","Exudate","Haemothorax"]
exp: Haemothorax is classified by cause/content differently — it's not grouped among the fluid-content-based pleural fluid types listed here (transudate/exudate/pus).

q: Which of the following chest X-ray features best describe typical free pleural fluid?
opts: ["Homogenous opacity sparing the cardiac and diaphragmatic outline","Homogenous opacity obliterating the cardiac and diaphragmatic outline with Meniscus sign","Non homogenous opacity with no meniscus sign","Homogenous opacity sparing the costophrenic angle"]
exp: A homogenous opacity obscuring the cardiac/diaphragmatic outline with a meniscus sign is the classic pleural effusion appearance.

q: Which of the following imaging modalities precisely describes the content of a pleural fluid?
opts: ["Computed Tomography (CT) scan","Plain radiography","Ultrasound scan","Fluoroscopy"]
exp: CT scanning best characterises the specific content/nature of pleural fluid.

q: The following are Non-Bedside Radiological imaging except:
opts: ["Doppler Ultrasonography","Intravenous urography (IVU)","Computed Tomography","Magnetic Resonance Imaging"]
exp: Doppler ultrasonography can actually be performed portably at the bedside, making it the exception among the fixed-suite-only imaging studies listed.

q: Which of the following Radiological Imaging Studies is routinely done in ICU setting?
opts: ["Magnetic Resonance","Barium Enema","Ultrasound scanning","Intravenous Urography"]
exp: Ultrasound scanning is the imaging study routinely and practically performed at the ICU bedside.

q: The following are advantages of Ultra sonographic study except:
opts: ["Real-time investigation","Widely available","Relatively cheap","It uses ionizing Radiation"]
exp: Ultrasound does NOT use ionising radiation — that is the false statement among its real advantages.

q: Which of the following conditions would indicate referral to cardio respiratory Therapist?
opts: ["Ebola","Incomplete SCI","COPD","DIC"]
exp: COPD is a classic condition warranting cardiorespiratory therapist referral.

q: Physiotherapy specialist who commonly works in the ICU setting is\....
opts: ["Orthopedics Physiotherapist","Pediatrics Physiotherapist","Cardiopulmonary Physiotherapist","Oncology and palliative care Physiotherapist"]
exp: Cardiopulmonary physiotherapists are the specialists most commonly working within ICU settings.

q: Manual hyperinflation
opts: ["Involve the use of expiratory pause of 5-10 seconds","Is used to provide fast and slow expiratory breath","Technique is not used in the management of patient's self-ventilating","Technique is used in the management of ventilated patients"]
exp: Manual hyperinflation is a technique specifically used in managing mechanically ventilated patients.

q: Active cycle of breathing technique ACBT is carried out in\....
opts: ["5 phases","4 phases","3 phases","2 phase"]
exp: ACBT is carried out in 3 phases (breathing control, thoracic expansion, forced expiration).

q: Manual clearing technique involves\....
opts: ["Positive expiratory pressure","Chest physiotherapy","Mechanical insufflations","Labour intensive and time consuming"]
exp: Chest physiotherapy is a manual technique used for secretion clearance.

q: Clinical presentation of critical care acquired weakness except
opts: ["Loss of sensation","Present about one week into the critical illness","Reduction of tendon reflex","Generalized limbs weakness"]
exp: Sensation is typically preserved in critical illness weakness (a predominantly motor problem), unlike the other listed motor features.

q: Intra-abdominal hypertension is:
opts: ["When IAP is consistently \\>12 mmHg with organ dysfunction","When IAP is \\>12 mmHg without organ dysfunction","When IAP is consistently \\<12 mmHg with organ dysfunction","When IAP is consistently \\> 5 mmHg without organ dysfunction"]
exp: Intra-abdominal hypertension is defined as sustained IAP >12mmHg WITHOUT necessarily needing organ dysfunction to be present.

q: Abdominal compartment syndrome is:
opts: ["When an intra-abdominal pressure is \\>20 mmHg with or without abdominal perfusion pressure \\<50 mmHg","When an intra-abdominal pressure is \\>20 mmHg with or without abdominal perfusion pressure greater than 50 mmHg","When an intra-abdominal pressure is 10-15 mmHg with normal abdominal perfusion pressure","None of the above"]
exp: Abdominal compartment syndrome is defined by IAP >20mmHg with or without reduced abdominal perfusion pressure.

q: Diagnosis of abdominal compartment syndrome (ACS)
opts: ["Imaging is very helpful in the diagnosis of ACS","Imaging is not helpful in the diagnosis of ACS","Chest x-ray may show increased lung volumes","Chest x-ray may show depressed hemi-diaphragm"]
exp: Imaging has limited specific diagnostic value for ACS — clinical/pressure measurement is key, making 'not helpful' the accurate statement.

q: Intra-abdominal pressure can be measured directly using\....
opts: ["Intra-gastric","Intra-colonic","Intra-vesical","Intra-abdominal"]
exp: Intra-vesical (bladder) pressure measurement is the standard method to indirectly measure IAP.

q: The following is a secondary abdominal compartment syndrome.
opts: ["Trauma","Septic shock","Ascites","Pancreatitis"]
exp: Septic shock (a non-abdominal originating cause) is a classic secondary cause of abdominal compartment syndrome.

q: The following are indication of monitoring intra-abdominal pressure except:
opts: ["Shock requiring resuscitation.","Damage control laparotomy","Major burns \\> 25%","Laparotomy closed with normal tension"]
exp: A laparotomy closed under normal tension is not itself a high-risk indication for IAP monitoring, unlike shock, damage control surgery, or major burns.

q: Goal for a child with respiratory infection:  \"The child will exhibit adequate oxygenation and \_\_\_ airway.  Keep O2 saturation \>92-93%.
opts: ["Patent","Potent","Cleared","Pertinent"]
exp: The goal wording specifically calls for a patent (open, clear) airway.

q: Obstruction of the airway in the patient with asthma is caused by all of the following except:
opts: ["Thick mucus","Swelling of bronchial membranes","Destruction of the alveolar wall","Contraction of muscles surrounding the bronchi"]
exp: Alveolar wall destruction is a feature of emphysema, not the airway obstruction mechanism in asthma.

q: On taking over in ICU from afternoon nurses, you discovered that a bottle of drug was not labelled and no information from handing over Nurse, what is the possible cause of this lapse?
opts: ["Poor communication","Lack of labeling from the pharmacy that dispense the drugs","Improper handing and taking over from previous shift","The patient relatives did not communicate to the Nurses"]
exp: Improper handover between shifts is the most likely cause of missing drug labelling/information.

q: You discovered that a patient's IV Cannula is issued but available cannulas are extreme sizes, what is the likely problem?
opts: ["Those are the sizes available in the pharmacy","They are the one used in ICU","Poor inventory","Poor communication"]
exp: Only having extreme cannula sizes available, without appropriate mid-range options, points to poor inventory management.

q: All the following are objectives of Health Talk Except
opts: ["Reaching the audience","Gaining attention","Misleading the audience","Being accepted"]
exp: Misleading the audience is the opposite of a health talk's real objectives (reaching, engaging, and gaining acceptance from the audience).

q: The following are hindrances to effective Health Talk Except
opts: ["Use of simple language","Pictures containing complicated diagrams","Pictures containing unfamiliar or strange subjects","Too much information is presented"]
exp: Using simple language helps (not hinders) effective health talks — it's the exception among the listed barriers.

q: A method of Teaching includes all the following Except....
opts: ["Explanation","Lecturing","Demonstration","Tertiary"]
exp: "Tertiary" isn't a recognised teaching method, unlike explanation, lecturing, and demonstration.

q: Which of this is not a major school of thought of management
opts: ["Modern approach","Economic thought","Classical thought","Human resource"]
exp: "Economic thought" isn't typically listed as a major management school, unlike classical, human resource, and modern approaches.

q: All the following are Rules of Management except
opts: ["Foresight","Organization","Equity","Coordination"]
exp: Equity is a principle (per Fayol), not one of the specific 'rules' category being distinguished here from foresight/organisation/coordination.

q: All the followings are Principles of Management except
opts: ["Command","Division of work","Scalar Chain","Unity of Direction"]
exp: Command is not one of Fayol's core numbered principles, unlike division of work, scalar chain, and unity of direction.

q: Fluid resuscitation requirements include the following except
opts: ["Current deficit","Ongoing losses","Maintenance requirements","Induction requirement"]
exp: "Induction requirement" is not a recognised category of fluid resuscitation need, unlike deficit, ongoing loss, and maintenance.

q: Mr Wazobia is a 5 yea rs' old who presented with diarrhea and vomiting, which of these fluid is the best choice?
opts: ["Oral Rehydration Solution","Normal Saline","Ringers lactate","5% Normal Saline"]
exp: Ringer's lactate is generally preferred for significant paediatric dehydration/fluid resuscitation over the other options given.

q: Which of these is not a characteristic of Sickle Red Cell
opts: ["Biconcave disc-shaped","Rigid","Life span of 20 days or less","Sticky surface, abnormal properties"]
exp: Sickle cells are crescent-shaped, not the normal biconcave disc shape of healthy red cells.

q: A sickle cell disease patient presented with chest pain, low Spo2, cough, dyspnea, which of these is the cause?
opts: ["Acute Asthmatic","Acute Chest Syndrome","Vaso-Occlusive Crisis","Acute Anaemia"]
exp: Chest pain, low SpO2, cough, and dyspnoea in sickle cell disease describe acute chest syndrome.

q: 10 year boys on admission with diagnosis of leukemia, which of these method/route of drugs administration will you avoid?
opts: ["Oral route","Intravenous route","Intramuscular route","Sublingual route"]
exp: Intramuscular injections are avoided in leukaemia patients due to bleeding/infection risk from low platelets/neutrophils.

q: Which one of these treatments of leukemia is still under investigation?
opts: ["Gene therapy","Cord blood and placenta transplant","Immunotherapy","Bone marrow transplant"]
exp: Gene therapy for leukaemia remains an area of ongoing investigation/research rather than standard established treatment.

q: A disorder characterized by the accumulation of malignant leucocyte cells in the bone marrow and peripheral blood is\....
opts: ["Thrombocytopenic","Erythrocytopenia","Leukocytopenia","Leukemia"]
exp: Malignant leucocyte accumulation in bone marrow/blood defines leukaemia.

q: Nursing diagnosis as it relates to acute pain in renal trauma setting involves the following except
opts: ["Assessing for level of pain, location of pain","Monitoring of pain level closely and reporting increase in severity to the physician.","Encouraging deep breathing exercise","Assessing patient's anxiety and fear"]
exp: Encouraging deep breathing is a general comfort measure, not specifically part of the pain-focused nursing diagnosis assessment listed.

q: Nursing care for patients with benign prostatic hyperplasia (BPH) includes the following except:
opts: ["Managing complications such as urinary tract infection","Assessment of menstrual irregularities","Relieving urinary retention","Promoting pain relief"]
exp: Menstrual assessment is irrelevant to BPH care, which affects only male patients.

q: Functional causes of urinary tract obstruction include:
opts: ["Bladder neck stenosis","Urethral stricture","Detrusor-sphincter dysynergia","Fecal impaction"]
exp: Detrusor-sphincter dysynergia is a functional (neuromuscular coordination) cause of urinary obstruction, unlike the mechanical causes listed.

q: Nursing intervention in patient with urinary retention includes all the following except:
opts: ["Performance of Credé's maneuver","Provision of appropriate catheter care when catheter is present","Teach self-catheterization and instruct in use and care of indwelling catheters","Administer intravenous furosemide to encourage voiding"]
exp: IV furosemide would worsen retention-related problems, not relieve retention — it does not belong among appropriate retention interventions.

q: Immediate attention by urologist is required in the following conditions except:
opts: ["Adult with urine output of 30-50mls/hour","Children with obstruction in a solitary kidney","Patient with obstruction and fever","Women with obstructive nephropathy"]
exp: A urine output of 30-50ml/hour in an adult is essentially normal and does not represent an urgent urological emergency.

q: Complications of penile trauma include all the following except:
opts: ["Peyronie's disease","Painful erection","Impotence","Penile angulation"]
exp: Peyronie's disease is a chronic condition, not a typical direct acute complication of penile trauma.

q: All the following are critical care considerations in bladder trauma except:
opts: ["Immediate priority to airway control, maintenance of ventilation, oxygenation and perfusion.","Trauma leader is important to coordinate management in multiply injured patient","Complete in depth assessment of patient is required after immediately treating life threatening injuries","Avoiding repeated assessment or survey"]
exp: Avoiding repeated assessment is unsafe — ongoing reassessment is essential in trauma care, making this the incorrect approach.

q: The following are nursing interventions in urological trauma except
opts: ["Checking specific areas like ribs and flank for swelling and marking with permanent marker for monitoring progression.","Collection of urine samples and ensuring proper labeling","Carrying out venous cut-down procedure to secure IV line","Preparing patient for Ultrasound scan and CT scan"]
exp: Venous cut-down isn't a standard urological trauma-specific nursing intervention, unlike the assessment/documentation tasks listed.

q: The method and process involved in facilitating peaceful ending of conflict and retribute defines
opts: ["Conflict","Psychological trauma","Conflict resolution","Fight"]
exp: The process of peacefully ending conflict is conflict resolution.

q: The following are examples of family stressors except.
opts: ["Marital discord","Financial issues","Land allocation","Child parent issues"]
exp: Land allocation is not a typically cited core family stressor, unlike marital discord, finances, and child-parent issues.

q: Suicide is when people \_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_ themselves with the aim of ending their life.
opts: ["Treat","Protect","Harm","Secure"]
exp: Suicide involves a person harming themselves with intent to end their life.

q: Family stress can show up as the following except.
opts: ["Missed commitment","Illness","Anger","Arguments"]
exp: Illness itself is a stressor cause, not a sign that family stress is showing up, unlike missed commitments, anger, or arguments.

q: One of the following is called the positive stress.
opts: ["Anxiety","Happiness","Worries","Eustress"]
exp: Eustress is the recognised term for positive, beneficial stress.

q: All are cognitive symptoms of psychological Trauma except.
opts: ["Loss of memory","Nightmares","Disorientation","Exhaustion"]
exp: Exhaustion is more a physical/emotional symptom than a cognitive one, unlike memory loss, nightmares, and disorientation.

q: Eustress stress means
opts: ["Energized individual","An episode of stress","Childhood stress","All of the options"]
exp: Eustress describes a state of positive energisation from stress.

q: The following are Caregiver stress management in the family except.
opts: ["Asking for and accepting help when you need it","Taking the training you need to help your loved one","Working hard to pay the family bills","Making time to take breaks every day"]
exp: Overworking to pay bills adds to caregiver stress rather than managing it — the opposite of a coping strategy.

q: The following are children stress management except.
opts: ["Talking about what's troubling you","Exercising every day","Eating nutritious foods, like protein and vegetables","Getting worried of predicaments"]
exp: Worrying about problems is the opposite of healthy stress management, unlike talking, exercise, and good nutrition.

q: The three types of eclampsia include the following except Ante partum Intra partum Pre partum Post-partum ANS; C The following are common symptoms of eclampsia except
opts: ["Seizures","Loss of consciousness","Agitations","Diabetes"]
exp: Diabetes is not a recognised symptom of eclampsia, unlike seizures, loss of consciousness, and agitation.

q: All are management of eclampsia fit except
opts: ["Secure and maintain clear airway","Prevent aspiration/injury by regular suctioning","Give oxygen via face mask","Early ambulation"]
exp: Early ambulation is inappropriate during an acute eclamptic emergency, unlike airway/oxygen/aspiration-prevention measures.

q: \_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_ is one of the principles of treatment of eclampsia
opts: ["Prevent further convulsion","Send mother home","Sedate the mother","All of the options"]
exp: Preventing further convulsions is a fundamental principle of eclampsia treatment.

q: The following are complications of eclampsia except
opts: ["Neurological deficit","Pulmonary oedema","Acute renal failure","Sepsis"]
exp: Sepsis is not a direct, classic complication of eclampsia itself, unlike neurological deficit, pulmonary oedema, and renal failure.

q: Severe eclampsia is a condition considered as follows except
opts: ["Complication of eclampsia","Severe form of pre-eclampsia","Can happen without signs of pre-eclampsia","It happens before pre-eclampsia"]
exp: Severe pre-eclampsia/eclampsia does not occur 'before' pre-eclampsia — pre-eclampsia precedes eclampsia, not the reverse.

q: \_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_ is a drug given intramuscularly to control convulsion
opts: ["Magnesium sulphate","Adrenalin","Corticosteroid","Diazepam"]
exp: Magnesium sulphate is the standard IM/IV drug used to control eclamptic convulsions.

q: If pre-eclampsia worsens and affect the brain causing seizures, the patient have develop \_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_
opts: ["Post-partum","Eclampsia","Ante partum","All of the options"]
exp: Pre-eclampsia progressing to seizures is by definition eclampsia.

q: The following is one of the criteria for diagnosing severe eclampsia
opts: ["Systolic blood pressure at 110 mmHg or diastolic blood pressure at 60 mmHg","Systolic blood pressure at 160 mmHg or higher or diastolic blood pressure of 100 mmHg or higher on 2 occasions at least 6 hours apart from bed rest","Systolic blood pressure at 130 mmHg or diastolic blood pressure at 100 mmHg on 2 occasions","All of the options"]
exp: Sustained severe BP readings (≥160/100) on two occasions are diagnostic criteria for severe pre-eclampsia/eclampsia.

q: Which of these is not associated with epiglottitis?
opts: ["Bacteria","Virus","Parasites","Foreign body"]
exp: Parasites are not a recognised cause of epiglottitis, unlike bacteria, viruses, and foreign bodies.

q: Regarding the newborn which of this is correct?
opts: ["Preterms are babies delivered before 40 weeks of delivery","All preterms are low birth weight","All preterms are small for gestational age","The term newborn may be small for gestational age"]
exp: A term (not preterm) baby can still be small for gestational age — the statement correctly reflects this distinction.

q: Regarding neonatal jaundice which of this is false
opts: ["Commoner in term babies","Bilirubin is an antioxidant","Bilirubin is neurotoxic","Conjugated bilirubin levels are important"]
exp: Neonatal jaundice is actually more common in preterm (not term) babies, making that statement the false one.

q: Complications of hyperbilirubinemia include the followings except
opts: ["Encephalopathy","Cerebral palsy","White teeth","Hearing loss"]
exp: "White teeth" is not a complication of hyperbilirubinaemia — in fact, kernicterus can cause dental enamel discolouration, the opposite of what's stated.

q: In fetal circulation which of this is false?
opts: ["There are multiple shunts","Two umbilical veins","The placenta serves excretory purposes","The lungs are filled with fluid"]
exp: There is only ONE umbilical vein (carrying oxygenated blood), not two, making that statement false.

q: A baby on phototherapy may develop the following except
opts: ["Fever","Rash","Fluid overload","Diarrhea"]
exp: Diarrhoea is not typically listed as a phototherapy side effect, unlike fever, rash, and fluid loss/overload issues.

q: Which of the following statements is false?
opts: ["A neonate is an infant","The neonatal period is the first week of life","Neonatology is part of perinatology","Mortality is high in the neonatal period"]
exp: The neonatal period is actually the first 28 days (four weeks), not just the first week — that statement is false.

q: Treatment acute chest syndrome includes the followings except
opts: ["Oxygen","Incentive spirometry","Analgesia","Chest stimulation"]
exp: "Chest stimulation" is not a recognised acute chest syndrome treatment, unlike oxygen, incentive spirometry, and analgesia.

q: Examples of sickle cell disease is/are except
opts: ["Hb SS","Hb SC","Hb AS","Hb Ac"]
exp: Hb AS is sickle cell TRAIT (a carrier state), not sickle cell disease itself, unlike SS, SC, and other true disease genotypes.

q: Which of the following is not a treatment modality for patient with epiglottitis?
opts: ["Tracheostomy","Steroids","Antibiotics","Observation"]
exp: Simple observation alone is inadequate for epiglottitis, which is a true airway emergency requiring active intervention.

q: All the following is correct about definition of disseminated intravascular coagulation except?
opts: ["It is a physiologic process","It is a pathologic process","There is a widespread activation of clotting cascade","There is a widespread formation of blood vessel throughout the body"]
exp: DIC is a pathological (not normal/physiologic) process of abnormal widespread clotting activation.

q: Concerning the following investigation in DIC, which is not correct?
opts: ["Prothrombin time is prolonged","Platelet count is reduced","Clotting factors are reduced","Fibrinogen is increased"]
exp: Fibrinogen is decreased (consumed), not increased, in DIC — making that statement incorrect.

q: The following are features of bronchial asthma except
opts: ["Chest pain","Shortness of breath","Angioedema","Inability to talk"]
exp: Angioedema is not a typical bronchial asthma feature, unlike chest tightness, breathlessness, and inability to speak in severe attacks.

q: The following drugs are useful in the management of bronchial asthma except
opts: ["B- agonists","Leukotrienes modifiers","Anticholinergics","Alpha blockers"]
exp: Alpha blockers are not used to treat bronchial asthma, unlike beta-agonists, leukotriene modifiers, and anticholinergics.

q: The following structures are involved in epiglottitis except
opts: ["Aryepiglottic folds","Arytenoid muscles","Uvula","Vocal cords"]
exp: The uvula is part of the soft palate, not directly involved in epiglottitis, unlike the epiglottic/laryngeal structures listed.

q: Respiratory treatment that involves percussion, vibration, and postural drainage to mobilize secretions.  A cupped hand or special device over the chest wall/rib cage is used to break up secretions is called?
opts: ["Postural Drainage","Chest Physiotherapy CPT)","Flutter Therapy","Multidirectional instability"]
exp: Chest physiotherapy (CPT) combines percussion, vibration, and postural drainage to mobilise secretions.

q: In patients with Cystic Fibrosis, abnormally thick mucus leads to obstruction of the secretory ducts of the \_\_ that can contribute to malnutrition and diabetes.
opts: ["Spleen","Bladder","Liver","Pancreas"]
exp: Thick mucus obstructing pancreatic ducts in cystic fibrosis contributes to malnutrition and diabetes.

q: Nurses and other healthcare providers often have difficulty helping a terminally ill patient through the necessary stages leading to acceptance of death. Which of the following strategies is most helpful to the nurse in achieving this goal?
opts: ["Reading books and other literature on the subject of thanatology.","Reflecting on the significance of death.","Reviewing varying cultural beliefs and practices related to death.","Taking psychology courses related to gerontology."]
exp: Reflecting on death's significance helps nurses process their own feelings, which is key to supporting patients through acceptance.

q: The most important nursing intervention to correct skin dryness is
opts: ["Ask the physician to refer the patient to a dermatologist, and suggest that the patient wear home-laundered sleepwear.","Encourage the patient to increase his fluid intake, use non-irritating soap when bathing the patient, and apply lotion to the involved areas.","Avoid bathing the patient until the condition is remedied, and notify the physician.","Consult the dietitian about increasing the patient's fat intake, and take necessary measures to prevent infection."]
exp: Increasing fluids, using gentle soap, and applying lotion directly addresses skin dryness at its source.

q: How can you Drag-and-drop and item in a folder
opts: ["to drag and drop, move the mouse pointer to that object and press the left mouse button once.","to drag and drop you press the left mouse button twice in quick succession.","to drag and drop just press the Right Mouse Button once.","to drag and drop a selected object, move the pointer to object you want to drag and drop, then press and hold the left mouse button, then move the object to where you want to drop it then release the left mouse button"]
exp: This describes the correct click-hold-drag-release technique for dragging and dropping an object.

q: \_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_ refers to the meaningful output or result obtained after processing data
opts: ["attribute","entity","information","data"]
exp: Information is defined as the meaningful output produced after processing raw data.

q: Which of the following conditions is most likely to directly cause peritonitis?
opts: ["Incarcerated hernia","Cholelithiasis","Perforated ulcer","Gastritis"]
exp: A perforated ulcer spills GI contents directly into the peritoneal cavity, directly causing peritonitis.

q: A client with gastric cancer may exhibit which of the following symptoms?
opts: ["Abdominal cramping","Feeling of fullness","Weight gain","Constant hunger"]
exp: A feeling of fullness (early satiety) is a classic symptom of gastric cancer.

q: Fistulas are most common with which of the following bowel disorders?
opts: ["Ulcerative colitis","Diverticulosis","Crohn's disease","Diverticulitis"]
exp: Crohn's disease characteristically causes transmural inflammation leading to fistula formation.

q: Which area of the alimentary canal is the most common location for Crohn's disease?
opts: ["Descending colon","Ascending colon","Sigmoid colon","Terminal ileum"]
exp: The terminal ileum is the most common site affected by Crohn's disease.

q: Medical management of the client with diverticulitis should include which of the following treatments?
opts: ["Administration of antibiotics","Reduced fluid intake.","Exercises to increase intra-abdominal pressure.","Increased fiber in the diet."]
exp: Antibiotics are a core treatment for the infective/inflammatory component of diverticulitis.

q: Which of the following tests should be administered to a client suspected of having diverticulosis?
opts: ["Abdominal ultrasound","Gastroscopy","Barium enema","Barium swallow"]
exp: Barium enema is used to visualise diverticula in the colon.

q: Which of the following complications is thought to be the most common cause of appendicitis?
opts: ["A fecalith","Bowel kinking","Internal bowel occlusion","Abdominal bowel swelling"]
exp: A fecalith (hardened stool) obstructing the appendix is the most common cause of appendicitis.

q: Which of the following factors can cause hepatitis A
opts: ["Eating contaminated food","Blood transfusions with infected blood.","Contact with infected blood.","Sexual contact with an infected person."]
exp: Hepatitis A spreads via the faecal-oral route, commonly through contaminated food.

q: The correct sequence for abdominal assessment is:
opts: ["Inspection, percussion, palpation, auscultation.","Inspection, auscultation, palpation, percussion.","Inspection, percussion, auscultation, palpation.","Inspection, palpation, auscultation, percussion."]
exp: Correct abdominal assessment order is inspection, auscultation, percussion, then palpation.

q: Peritonitis can occur as a complication of:
opts: ["Septicemia","Multiple organ failure","Hypovolemic shock","Peptic ulcer disease"]
exp: Perforated peptic ulcer disease is a classic cause of secondary peritonitis.

q: Patients with esophageal varices would reveal the following assessment:
opts: ["Increased blood pressure","Increased urinary output","Increased heart rate","Decreased respiratory rate"]
exp: Bleeding oesophageal varices cause blood loss, triggering a compensatory rise in heart rate.

q: Peptic ulcer disease may be caused by which of the following?
opts: ["Clostridium difficile","Helicobacter pylori","Candida albicans","Staphylococcus aureus"]
exp: H. pylori infection is a major recognised cause of peptic ulcer disease.

q: Nitrosocarcinogen production can be inhibited with the intake of:
opts: ["Carbohydrates","Fiber","Vitamin C","Vitamin E"]
exp: Vitamin C can inhibit the formation of carcinogenic nitroso compounds in the stomach.

q: Which of the following tests can be useful as a diagnostic and therapeutic tool in the biliary system?
opts: ["Ultrasonography","MRI","Endoscopic retrograde cholangiopancreatography (ERCP)","Computed tomography scan (CT scan)"]
exp: ERCP allows both diagnosis and therapeutic intervention (e.g. stone removal) in the biliary system.

q: To inhibit pancreatic secretions, which pharmacologic agent would you anticipate administering to a patient with acute pancreatitis?
opts: ["Nitroglycerin","Somatostatin","Pepcid","Pancrelipase"]
exp: Somatostatin suppresses pancreatic secretion, reducing pancreatic autodigestion in acute pancreatitis.

q: A patient with severe cirrhosis of the liver develops hepatorenal syndrome. Which of the following nursing assessment data would support this?
opts: ["Metabolic alkalosis","Decreased urinary concentration","Weight gain of less than 1kg per week","Oliguria and azotemia"]
exp: Oliguria with rising nitrogenous waste (azotaemia) is the hallmark of hepatorenal syndrome.

q: Which phase of hepatitis would the nurse incur strict precautionary measures at?
opts: ["Post-icteric","Icteric","Non-icteric","Pre-icteric"]
exp: The icteric (jaundice) phase is when infectivity/precautions are most critical in hepatitis.

q: You are caring for a 35 year old female patient in a hepatic coma. Which evaluation criteria would be the most appropriate?
opts: ["The patient demonstrates an increase in the level of consciousness.","The patient exhibits improved skin integrity.","The patient verbalizes decreased episodes of pain.","The patient experiences no evident signs of bleeding."]
exp: Improved level of consciousness is the most direct, relevant marker of recovery from hepatic coma.

q: The critical care nurse recognizes that an ideal plan for caregiver involvement includes;
opts: ["a caregiver at the bedside at all times.","An individually devised plan to involve caregivers with care and comfort measures.","allowing caregivers at the bedside at preset, brief intervals.","restriction of visiting in the ICU because the environment is overwhelming to caregivers."]
exp: An individualised plan tailored to each family's needs is the ideal approach to caregiver involvement.

q: Clients with chronic illnesses are more likely to get pneumonia when which of the following situations is present?
opts: ["dehydration and continuous loss of water","Living with many people in a small space","Severe periodontal disease","Malnutrition"]
exp: Overcrowded living conditions increase pneumonia risk through greater exposure to pathogens.

q: Which of the following is a priority goal for the client with COPD?
opts: ["Minimizing chest pain.","Treating infectious agents.","Maintaining functional ability.","Increasing carbon dioxide levels in the blood."]
exp: Maintaining functional ability is a central, realistic priority goal in chronic COPD management.

q: The nurse assesses the respiratory status of a client who is experiencing an exacerbation of COPD secondary to an upper respiratory tract infection. Which of the following findings would be expected?
opts: ["Normal breath sounds","Prolonged inspiration","Coarse crackles and rhonchi","Normal chest movement"]
exp: Coarse crackles and rhonchi are expected findings during a COPD exacerbation with infection.

q: A client has just returned to a nursing unit following bronchoscopy. A nurse would implement which of the following nursing interventions for this client?
opts: ["Administering atropine intravenously","Encouraging additional fluids for the next 24 hours","Administering small doses of midazolam (Versed)","Ensuring the return of the gag reflex before offering foods or fluids"]
exp: Confirming the gag reflex has returned prevents aspiration before resuming oral intake post-bronchoscopy.

q: A female client is scheduled to have a chest radiograph. Which of the following questions is of most importance to the nurse assessing this client?
opts: ["\"Are you wearing any metal chains or jewelry?\"","\"Are you able to hold your arms above your head?\"","\"Can you hold your breath easily?\"","\"Is there any possibility that you could be pregnant?\""]
exp: Pregnancy status must always be checked before any radiographic exposure due to fetal radiation risk.

q: Aminophylline (theophylline) is prescribed for a client with acute bronchitis. A nurse administers the medication, knowing that the primary action of this medication is to:
opts: ["Relax smooth muscles of the bronchial airway.","Suppress the cough.","Prevent infection.","Promote expectoration."]
exp: Aminophylline's primary action is relaxing bronchial smooth muscle (bronchodilation).

q: A patient was rushed to the clinic department due to hyperventilation. Which nursing intervention is the most appropriate for the client who is subsequently developing respiratory alkalosis?
opts: ["Administer low-flow oxygen therapy.","Administering sodium chloride I.V.","Preparing to administer sodium bicarbonate.","Encouraging slow, deep breaths."]
exp: Slow, deep breathing helps a hyperventilating patient retain CO2 and correct respiratory alkalosis.

q: A patients test revealed that her serum calcium is 2.5 mEq/L. Which assessment data does the nurse document when a client diagnosed with hypocalcemia develops a carpopedal spasm after the blood-pressure cuff is inflated?
opts: ["Paresthesia","Positive Chvosteks sign","Positive Trousseaus sign","Tetany"]
exp: Carpopedal spasm triggered by BP cuff inflation is specifically Trousseau's sign.

q: A patient with a very dry mouth, skin, and mucous membranes is diagnosed with dehydration. Which intervention should the nurse perform when caring for a patient diagnosed with fluid volume deficit?
opts: ["Obtaining the clients weight weekly at different times of the day.","Maintaining I V therapy at the keep vein open rate.","Monitoring arterial blood gas ABG results","Assessing urinary intake and output"]
exp: Tracking intake and output directly monitors fluid balance status in dehydration.

q: A female patient is diagnosed with hypomagnesemia, which nursing intervention would be appropriate?
opts: ["Instructing the client on the importance of preventing infection.","Instituting seizure precaution to prevent injury.","Teaching the client the importance of early ambulation.","Avoiding the use of tight tourniquet when drawing blood."]
exp: Seizure precautions are essential since hypomagnesemia increases neuromuscular excitability/seizure risk.

q: OS computer abbreviation usually means ?
opts: ["Order of Significance","Open Software","Operating System","Optical Sensor"]
exp: OS stands for Operating System.

q: Which of the following hypertensive emergence is not correct
opts: ["Low blood pressure over hours to days","Acute left ventricular failure","Eclampsia /preeclampsia","Disseminated intravascular coagulopathy"]
exp: A slow drop in BP over hours/days is not a hypertensive emergency — hypertensive emergencies involve rapid severe BP elevation, not low BP.

q: Concerning fulminant hepatic failure which is not a poor prognostic factor \_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_
opts: ["Age greater than 40years","Serum biliribium exceeding 300micromo/L","prothrombin time \\[PT\\]\\<1O","prothrombin \\[PT\\]\\>50 seconds"]
exp: A PT under 10 seconds difference (near normal) reflects preserved clotting function — a GOOD, not poor, prognostic sign.

q: Principles of radiation protection include all EXCEPT
opts: ["distance","time","space","Coning/collimation"]
exp: Space is not a standard cited radiation protection principle, unlike time, distance, and shielding/collimation.

q: What technical factor should always be checked before interpreting a frontal chest radiograph? \_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_
opts: ["Site","inspiration","Expiration","Expansion"]
exp: Checking adequate inspiration is a standard technical check before reading a chest film.

q: Barium Based contrast studies include all the following EXCEPT \_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_
opts: ["Barium swallow","Barium enema","Barium sulfate","Enterocysis"]
exp: Enterocysis (a small bowel study) is technically distinct from the barium swallow/enema/sulfate preparations being grouped here.

q: Management of uterine rupture is one of the following
opts: ["oxytocin should be administered","Simple repair","Uterine relaxation with tocolytic","Taking of history"]
exp: Simple surgical repair is the standard management approach for uterine rupture.

q: A 35year old para 7+2 \[6alive\] ,just had a spontaneous vaginal delivery of a live baby boy, she is lying on bed not talking, on examination her pulse rate is 100b/m, blood pressure 50/?, the possible cause is: \_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_
opts: ["Not regular in antenatal visit prenatally","Thromboembolism","Inadequate history of onset of labor","Inadequate history during antenatal booking"]
exp: Persistent hypotension/tachycardia postpartum with unclear labour history raises suspicion for occult bleeding tied to inadequate labour monitoring/history.

q: Concerning magnetic resonance imaging (MRI) it should not be used in patients with: \_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_
opts: ["Chest injuries","Head injury Patient","Spinal cord injury","Patient with metallic devices"]
exp: Patients with metallic implants/devices cannot safely undergo MRI due to the strong magnetic field.

q: Which of these technical factors should always be checked first before interpreting a frontal chest radiograph include: \_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_
opts: ["Check hemidiaphragm if the patient has taken an adequate deep breath","Cardiac silhouette frequency","Expiration and Inspiration","Check the identity information at the top of the film to make sure it is the correct film"]
exp: Confirming patient identity on the film is a fundamental first check before any interpretation.

q: Principles of treatment of a severely malnourished child include all EXCEPT
opts: ["Treat and prevent dehydration","Treat and prevent COVID -19","Treat and prevent hypothermia","Treat and prevent hypoglycaemia"]
exp: Treating/preventing COVID-19 is not part of the classic WHO severe malnutrition treatment principles (dehydration, hypothermia, hypoglycaemia).

q: One of these is NOT an output device \_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_
opts: ["Keyboard","Monitor","Printer","Projector"]
exp: A keyboard is an input device, not an output device.

q: One of these is NOT a characteristic of a computer \_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_
opts: ["Efficiency","Data","Portability","Accuracy"]
exp: "Data" itself is not a characteristic/property of a computer, unlike efficiency, portability, and accuracy.

opts: ["Data is the physical and tangible parts of a computer that we can see","Data is a set of instructions the computer follows to solve Problems","Data is the value of an attribute or characteristic belonging to an entity","Data is a group of Patients"]
exp: Data is defined as the value of an attribute/characteristic belonging to an entity.

q: The physical and tangible parts of a computer is called \_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_
opts: ["Software","Values","Tables","Hardware"]
exp: The physical, tangible components of a computer are called hardware.

q: Water leaves the body by all the following mechanism EXCEPT \_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_
opts: ["From feces eliminated from the intestine","Through metabolic reaction taking place in the cells","Through air expired from the lungs","Through sweat given off at the skin"]
exp: Water is lost via faeces, expired air, and sweat — metabolic reactions produce water rather than eliminate it, making that the exception.

q: The rate and depth of breathing has a regulatory influence on the: \_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_
opts: ["Protein metabolism of the body","Rate at which fats are broken down in the body","Acid/base balance of the body","Amount of water taken into the body"]
exp: Breathing rate/depth directly regulates CO2 elimination, controlling the body's acid-base balance.

q: Approximately one third of the body water exists in the: \_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_
opts: ["Blood","Transcellular fluid compartment","Kidneys and Urinary bladder","Extracellular fluid compartment"]
exp: About two-thirds of body water is intracellular and one-third extracellular — the extracellular compartment holds roughly a third.

q: An example of a strong acid found in the body is: \_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_
opts: ["Carbonic acid","Propionic acid","Acetic acid","Hydrochloric acid"]
exp: Hydrochloric acid (gastric acid) is a classic strong acid found in the body.

q: The two basic methods requirement for optional physiologic functions of all cells include\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_ and \_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_
opts: ["Hot and cold weather","Pit and osmolarity","Respiration and excretion","Oxygen and carbondioxide"]
exp: Respiration and excretion are the two basic processes required to maintain optimal cellular physiological function.

q: Concerning Mechanical ventilation, Phase variables are Parameters which control the Phases of a mechanical breath, which of the following is not a Phase
opts: ["Triggering","Cycling","Volume","Limit"]
exp: Volume is a set (target) parameter, not one of the four defined ventilator phase variables (trigger, limit, cycle, baseline).

q: These are all essential steps in the Management of myocardial infarction EXCEPT\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_
opts: ["Antibacterial therapy","Antiischeamic therapy","Anticoagulant therapy","Antiplatelet therapy"]
exp: Antibacterial therapy is not a core MI management step, unlike anti-ischaemic, anticoagulant, and antiplatelet therapy.

q: Indication for thrombolysis in myocardial infection includes the following EXCEPT \_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_
opts: ["ST elevation myocardial infarction","New onset left bundle branch block","Posterior myocardial infarction","Non ST elevation myocardial infarction"]
exp: Non-ST elevation MI is generally NOT an indication for thrombolysis, unlike STEMI, new LBBB, or posterior MI.

q: Concerning Mechanical ventilation, Phase variables are Parameters which control the Phases of a mechanical breath, which of the following is not a Phase
opts: ["Triggering","Cycling","Volume","Limit"]
exp: Same repeated logic: Volume is not one of the defined phase variables (trigger/limit/cycle) of a mechanical breath.

q: What is Computer Memory (Storage devices)?
opts: ["They are devices that are used for storing data and information permanently or temporarily","They are devices that perform all the processing in the computer.","They are devices that convert information from the form that the computer understands to a form that humans can understand.","They are devices that command the computer on how to execute programs to solve problems"]
exp: Storage devices are used to hold data/information either temporarily or permanently.

q: One of these is NOT an Input Device
opts: ["Microphone","Keyboard","Mouse","Digital Sphygmomanometer"]
exp: A digital sphygmomanometer is a medical measuring device, not a standard computer input device like the others listed.

q: Software designed and installed on a computer intended to solve specific problems or perform particular tasks is called?
opts: ["Application software","Utility","Spreadsheet","Open Source Software"]
exp: Software built for a specific task/problem is application software.

q: One of these is Not an Output Device
opts: ["Projector","Keyboard","Monitor","Printer"]
exp: A keyboard is an input, not an output, device.

opts: ["manages the resources on the computer and enable the hardware to function properly","is used for data analysis","is used to manage health information of patients","is used in making presentations"]
exp: System software manages computer resources and enables hardware to function properly.

q: A set of instruction and associated documentation that the computer follows to solve a problem is called \_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_?
opts: ["LAN","Devices","Memory","Software"]
exp: A set of instructions the computer follows, plus documentation, is software.

q: The devices that give out data and information from the computer is called \_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_?
opts: ["Universal Serial Bus","Network Topology","Sink/Receiver","Output devices"]
exp: Devices that give out data/information from the computer are output devices.

q: The devices that send data and control signals INTO the computer is called \_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_?
opts: ["Arithmetic Unit","Operating System","Input devices","Network"]
exp: Devices sending data/control signals into the computer are input devices.

q: The physical and tangible parts of a computer called \_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_?
opts: ["Values","Hardware","Software","Tables"]
exp: The physical, tangible parts of a computer are called hardware.

q: all of these are Computer hardware EXCEPT\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_?
opts: ["Software and programs","Input devices","Output devices","Memory/Storage devices"]
exp: Software/programs are not hardware — they are the exception among the physical components listed.

q: What is information?
opts: ["Information is the first step in data collection","Information is general Knowledge","Information is the meaningful result of processing data.","Information is raw data"]
exp: Information is the meaningful result produced from processing data.

q: What is an Entity?
opts: ["An Entity is any expression that can describe computer laboratory","An Entity is any person, object or idea about which data can be collected","An Entity is a collection of programs","An Entity is the equivalent of communication on a network"]
exp: An entity is any person, object, or idea about which data can be collected.

q: What is an attribute?
opts: ["An attribute is an object stored on a computer","An attribute is the smallest unit of a computer file","An attribute is a collection of related records","An attribute is a property or characteristic that can qualify or describe an entity"]
exp: An attribute is a property/characteristic that describes an entity.

q: What is Data?
opts: ["Data is a group of patients","Data is a set of instructions the computer follows to solve problems","Data is the value of an attribute or characteristic belonging to an entity","Data is the physical and tangible parts of a computer that we can see"]
exp: Data is the value of an attribute/characteristic belonging to an entity.

q: Which of these best describes a System?
opts: ["a group of components communicating with the user","a group of components presenting information","a group of components or elements organized for a common purpose or working together to achieve a goal","a group of components or elements creating software"]
exp: A system is a group of components organised together to achieve a common goal.

q: A network in which all the communicating computers are of the same or similar capacity is called a \_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_?
opts: ["Peer to peer network","Server-client network","Mainframe network","Cloud computing Network"]
exp: A network of similarly capable computers communicating as equals is a peer-to-peer network.

q: in a Computer Network the means of connection is \_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_?
opts: ["Speaker","Receiver/sink","Sender/source","Medium/channel"]
exp: The medium/channel is the means of connection in a computer network.

opts: ["an application used for accessing resources on a computer network.","an application for creating richly formated documents","an application for drawing images","an application for manipulating numerical data arranged in rows and columns"]
exp: A web browser is the application used to access resources on a computer network (the internet).

q: What is a Computer Network?
opts: ["A Computer Network is the interconnection of schools to give information","A Computer Network is the interconnection people to increase productivity","A Computer Network is the interconnection of two or more computers or computing devices through a medium for the purpose of sharing resources.","A Computer Network is the interconnection of two or more agents to achieve a goal"]
exp: A computer network is the interconnection of two or more computing devices to share resources.

q: Which of these best defines a computer
opts: ["A computer is an electronic device that accepts data processes data stores data and gives out information.","A computer is an electronic communication device.","A computer is a music playing calculation device.","A computer is an education device for presentation."]
exp: A computer is defined as an electronic device that accepts, processes, stores, and outputs data/information.

q: DB computer abbreviation usually means ?
opts: ["Database","Double Byte","Data Block","Driver Boot"]
exp: DB commonly stands for Database.

q: What is the primary goal of infection control practices in critical care?
opts: ["A) Inducing infection","B) Reducing hand hygiene","C) Preventing healthcare-associated infections","D) Administering antibiotics"]
exp: Preventing healthcare-associated infections is the primary goal of infection control in critical care.

q: Which of the following is a common sign of inadequate oxygenation in a critically ill patient?
opts: ["A) Increased blood pressure","B) Bradycardia (slow heart rate)","C) Cyanosis (bluish skin color)","D) Elevated body temperature"]
exp: Cyanosis (bluish skin) is a classic visible sign of inadequate oxygenation.

q: What is the primary purpose of a nasogastric (NG) tube in critical care?
opts: ["A) Monitoring cardiac hemodynamics","B) Administering pain medication","C) Providing nutrition and medications via the stomach","D) Measuring intracranial pressure (ICP)"]
exp: An NG tube's main critical care purpose is delivering nutrition and medication directly into the stomach.

q: In critical care, what is the primary role of a diuretic medication?
opts: ["A) Elevating blood pressure","B) Increasing heart rate","C) Promoting urine output","D) Reducing oxygen levels"]
exp: Diuretics work primarily by promoting urine output.

q: What is the primary goal of sepsis management in critical care?
opts: ["A) Inducing fever","B) Administering blood transfusions","C) Identifying and treating the infection promptly","D) Increasing heart rate"]
exp: Prompt identification and treatment of the underlying infection is the core goal of sepsis management.

q: Which of the following is a common method for preventing ventilator-associated pneumonia (VAP) in critical care?
opts: ["A) Administering sedatives","B) Frequent suctioning of the endotracheal tube","C) Decreasing hand hygiene","D) Avoiding handwashing"]
exp: Frequent endotracheal suctioning helps clear secretions and reduce VAP risk.

q: What is the primary purpose of the Glasgow Coma Scale (GCS) in critical care?
opts: ["A) Monitoring blood pressure","B) Assessing respiratory function","C) Evaluating neurological status and consciousness","D) Administering antibiotics"]
exp: The GCS evaluates neurological status and level of consciousness.

q: Which of the following is a common complication of central line placements in critical care?
opts: ["A) Decreased risk of infection","B) Airway obstruction","C) Catheter-related bloodstream infections","D) Improved wound healing"]
exp: Catheter-related bloodstream infection is a well-known complication of central line placement.

q: What is the primary goal of enteral nutrition in critical care?
opts: ["A) Inducing sleep","B) Providing comfort","C) Administering pain medication","D) Delivering nutrition through the gastrointestinal tract"]
exp: Enteral nutrition delivers nutrients directly through the GI tract.

q: In critical care, what is the primary purpose of a pulmonary artery catheter (PAC)?
opts: ["A) Monitoring brain activity","B) Administering anesthesia","C) Measuring intracranial pressure (ICP)","D) Assessing cardiac function and pressures"]
exp: A pulmonary artery catheter assesses cardiac function and intracardiac/pulmonary pressures.

q: Which of the following is a common cause of acute pancreatitis in critical care patients?
opts: ["Adequate fluid intake","Controlled blood glucose levels","Gallstones and alcohol consumption","Low cholesterol levels"]
exp: Gallstones and alcohol use are the two leading causes of acute pancreatitis.

q: What is the primary goal of a ventricular assist device (VAD) in critical care?
opts: ["To monitor blood glucose levels","To provide mechanical ventilation","To support heart function and circulation","To administer antibiotics"]
exp: A VAD mechanically supports heart function and circulation in severe heart failure.

q: Which of the following is a common cause of stress ulcers in critical care patients?
opts: ["Adequate nutrition","Controlled blood pressure","Severe illness and physiological stress","Elevated oxygen levels"]
exp: Severe illness and physiological stress are the classic drivers of stress ulcer development in critical care.

q: What is the primary purpose of a Swan-Ganz catheter in critical care?
opts: ["Monitoring blood pressure","Administering insulin","Measuring intracranial pressure (ICP)","Monitoring cardiac hemodynamics"]
exp: A Swan-Ganz (pulmonary artery) catheter is used to monitor cardiac hemodynamics.

q: In critical care, what is the primary role of a respiratory therapist?
opts: ["Administering anesthesia","Monitoring brain activity","Providing respiratory support and therapies","Administering chemotherapy"]
exp: Respiratory therapists provide respiratory support and therapies in critical care.

q: Which of the following file extensions is commonly associated with image files?
opts: [".txt",".pdf",".jpg",".docx"]
exp: .jpg is a standard image file extension.

q: What does a spreadsheet software like Microsoft Excel allow users to do?
opts: ["Browse the internet","Create and edit images","Create, organize, and analyze data in a tabular format","Manage emails"]
exp: Excel allows creating, organising, and analysing data in a tabular (spreadsheet) format.

q: What is a web browser primarily used for?
opts: ["Editing images","Creating spreadsheets","Browsing the internet","Programming"]
exp: A web browser is primarily used to browse the internet.

q: Which of the following is an example of an electronic spreadsheet software?
opts: ["Microsoft Excel","Adobe Premiere Pro","Notepad","Calculator"]
exp: Microsoft Excel is a well-known electronic spreadsheet application.

q: What is cloud computing?
opts: ["A type of software","A physical computer","A technology that allows accessing and storing data and programs over the internet","A type of computer virus"]
exp: Cloud computing allows accessing/storing data and programs over the internet rather than locally.

q: A 16-year old patient with cystic fibrosis is admitted with increased shortness of breath and possible pneumonia. Which nursing activity is most important to include in the patient's care?
opts: ["Perform postural drainage and chest physiotherapy every 4 hours.","Allow the patient to decide whether she needs aerosolized medications.","Place the patient in a private room to decrease the risk of further infection.","Plan activities to allow at least 8 hours of uninterrupted sleep."]
exp: Regular postural drainage and chest physiotherapy is essential to clear thick CF secretions and reduce infection/respiratory compromise.

q: If a person has AB- blood, who can donate blood to him?
exp: AB- can only safely receive Rh-negative blood of any ABO type, since anti-Rh antibodies would react to any Rh+ blood.

q: On what basis is the blood group classified?
opts: ["Antigen and antibody","Haemoglobin content","Rhesus Factor","Oxygen Content"]
exp: Blood groups are classified based on antigens present on RBCs and antibodies in plasma.

q: Other than transfusion, when is it necessary to take the Rh Factor into consideration?
opts: ["Cathertization","Spleen Rupture","Pregnancy","Blood Donation"]
exp: Rh factor compatibility is critical in pregnancy due to risk of maternal-fetal Rh incompatibility.

q: A person has blood group A means he/she has...................
opts: ["A antigen in RBC","A antigen in plasma","B antigen in RBC","B antigen in plasma"]
exp: Blood group A means the person has the A antigen on their red blood cells.

q: Which of the following is the correct sequence in which urine flows through the kidney toward the urinary bladder?
opts: ["Renal pelvis, major calyx, minor calyx, papillary duct, ureter.","Papillary duct, minor calyx, major calyx, renal pelvis, ureter.","Minor calyx, major calyx, papillary duct, renal pelvis, ureter.","Papillary duct, major calyx, minor calyx, ureter, renal pelvis."]
exp: Urine flows: minor calyx → major calyx → renal pelvis → ureter → bladder.

q: The bladder is lined with \_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_ epithelium.
opts: ["Simple columnar","Transitional","Stratified squamous","Simple cuboidal"]
exp: The bladder is lined with transitional epithelium, which allows stretching.

q: Each kidney receives blood from..................
opts: ["Afferent arteriole","Inter lobular veins","Renal artery","Renal vein"]
exp: Each kidney receives its blood supply from the renal artery.

q: The maximum volume of air contained in the lung by a full forced inhalation is called \_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_.
opts: ["Tidal volume","Vital capacity","Ventilation rate","Total lung capacity"]
exp: Maximum air the lungs can hold after full inhalation is total lung capacity.

q: Adduction is?
opts: ["Movement of a body part away from the mid-line","Movement of a body part towards the mid-line","Movement of a body part forwards","movement of a body part backwards"]
exp: Adduction is movement of a body part toward the midline.

q: Which enzyme digests nucleic acids?
opts: ["Pepsinogen","Pancreatic amylase","Trypsin","Nucleases"]
exp: Nucleases digest nucleic acids (DNA/RNA).

q: Endocrine system consist of the following glands EXCEPT:
opts: ["Thyroid Gland","Pancreas","Spleen","Thymus"]
exp: The spleen is a lymphatic organ, not an endocrine gland.

q: Protective functions of the skin are the following EXCEPT:
opts: ["Protect the joint","Protect against insects","Protect against harmful chemicals","Protect against water loss"]
exp: Protecting against insects is not a recognised protective skin function, unlike chemical/water-loss protection.

q: The layer of the skin that provides protection against bacteria, as well as chemical and mechanical injuries is the:
opts: ["Epidermis","Dermis","Sebum layer","Subcutaneous layer"]
exp: The epidermis is the outer barrier layer protecting against bacteria and injury.

q: Which of this part of alimentary canal accumulates lymphatic tissue?
opts: ["Jejunum","Ilium","Appendix","lymphatic nodes"]
exp: The appendix contains significant lymphatic tissue.

q: The followings are accessory organs of digestion EXCEPT:
opts: ["Salivary glands","Teeth","Lymph nodes","Gall bladder"]
exp: Lymph nodes are not accessory digestive organs, unlike salivary glands, teeth, and gall bladder.

q: Delirium Risk Factors are as follows EXCEPT
opts: ["Obesity","Preexisting dementia","Bone fractures","Infections"]
exp: Obesity is not a classic delirium risk factor, unlike dementia, fractures, and infections.

q: Communication may take a ................ to master
opts: ["Short time","Life time","Brief time","All of the above"]
exp: Effective communication is a skill that can take a lifetime to truly master.

q: If healthy behaviour is any activity undertaken by a person believing himself to be healthy, illness behaviour is any activity undertaken by a person who feels ................
opts: ["Well","Ill","Strong","Active"]
exp: Illness behaviour is activity undertaken by someone who feels ill.

q: Stress is the internal pressure while stressor is an:
opts: ["Attitude","Burden","Response","Tormentor"]
exp: A stressor is an external tormentor/trigger; stress is the internal response/pressure.

q: In the treatment of alcohol withdrawal there are three line management
opts: ["Supportive, Medical procedure and Medication","Supportive, Medication and Special procedure","Supportive group, medical management","Medication, Special therapy and Group therapy"]
exp: Alcohol withdrawal management follows supportive care, medical procedures, and medication together.

q: PTSD means
opts: ["Post treatment surgical disorder","Point to soul destruction","Post traumatic stress disorder","Post trauma sedation drugs"]
exp: PTSD stands for Post Traumatic Stress Disorder.

q: All are cognitive symptoms of psychological Trauma EXCEPT:
opts: ["Loss of memory","Nightmares","Disorientation","Exhaustion"]
exp: Exhaustion is more physical than cognitive, unlike memory loss, nightmares, and disorientation.

q: What are the three Elements of Attitude?
opts: ["Cognitive, Behavior, and Affective","Cognitive, Behavioral, and Effective","Cognitive, Behavioral, and Affective","Cognitive, Behave, and Affective"]
exp: The three elements of attitude are Cognitive, Behavioural, and Affective.

q: Divergent thinking is to \_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_ as convergent thinking is to \_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_.
opts: ["one solution, many solutions","many solutions, one solution","mathematical ability, verbal ability","extrinsic motivation, intrinsic motivation"]
exp: Divergent thinking generates many solutions; convergent thinking narrows to one.

q: The Hormone produced by adrenal glands that arouses the body is ...
opts: ["Glucagon","Prolactin","Adrenaline","Insulin"]
exp: Adrenaline (epinephrine) is the adrenal hormone that arouses the fight-or-flight response.

q: Positioning in critical care unit means the following EXCEPT
opts: ["To provide the best care","Each position carries some degree of risk to the patient","Magnified in risk for patients who cannot /may not communicate.","To conform with patients demand absolutely"]
exp: Positioning should be based on clinical need/risk assessment, not absolute compliance with patient demand.

q: Unconsciousness include all the following EXCEPT
opts: ["A state of abnormal cerebral activity","Unresponsive to internal and external environmental changes","Depression of cerebral function","Awareness of self and environment"]
exp: Awareness of self and environment is the opposite of unconsciousness, not a feature of it.

q: In assessment of unconscious patient which of this is the ideal sequence used?
opts: ["ABCDE","CABDE","BACDE","EACAB"]
exp: ABCDE is the standard structured assessment sequence for an unconscious patient.

q: The following are criteria for CCU admission EXCEPT
opts: ["Post cardiac arrest","Chronic renal failure","Myocardiac infarction","Prolong surgery"]
exp: Chronic renal failure alone is not typically an acute CCU admission criterion, unlike arrest, MI, or major surgery.

q: The ideal instrument for examining the pupils for size and reaction is..
opts: ["pen torch light","pupillary reaction light","Touch reaction light","Sluggish reacting light"]
exp: A pen torch light is the standard bedside tool for pupillary examination.

q: Airway obstruction is diagnosed via the following EXCEPT
opts: ["Gurgling","Snoring","Drooling of saliva","Apnoea"]
exp: Apnoea (absence of breathing) doesn't itself indicate airway obstruction sounds, unlike gurgling/snoring/drooling which reflect a partially obstructed airway.

q: Coughing during suctioning indicates stimulation of cough reflex centre at the
opts: ["Larynx","Pharynx","Trachea","Carina"]
exp: The carina is highly sensitive and its stimulation during suctioning triggers a strong cough reflex.

q: The following are symptoms of Tension pneumothorax EXCEPT
opts: ["Shortness of breath","Raised in Blood pressure","Decreased in Blood pressure","Acute chest pain"]
exp: Tension pneumothorax causes falling (not rising) blood pressure due to impaired venous return.

q: Indications for chest tube insertion with underwater-seal drainage are the following EXCEPT
opts: ["Traumatic haemopneumothorax","spontaneous pneumothorax","Emphysema","Pleuritic pains"]
exp: Emphysema alone isn't a typical indication for chest tube/underwater seal drainage, unlike pneumothorax or haemopneumothorax.

q: Modifiable Risk Factors for MI include all EXCEPT
opts: ["Advanced Age","Smoking","Hypertension","Diabetes"]
exp: Age is non-modifiable, unlike smoking, hypertension, and diabetes.

q: If the plan is to have one critical care unit in the hospital it should be located near:
opts: ["Theater unit","Surgical Intensive Care Unit","Centrally located","Accident and Emergency unit"]
exp: A single CCU is ideally located near the Accident & Emergency unit for rapid access to critical patients.
